1: Schistosomiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The condition is associated with several drugs, including Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is also linked to the use of Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol.

Ebola has been associated with multiple outbreak events across several countries, including repeated occurrences in the Democratic Republic of the Congo and Guinea Bissau, as well as isolated cases in Sierra Leone, Spain, Italy, Cameroon, and the United Republic of Tanzania. Outbreaks in the Democratic Republic of the Congo have been particularly severe, with events recorded in 2018, 2019, and 2020 involving thousands of confirmed cases and hundreds to over two thousand deaths. Notable outbreaks include one in 2019 with 2,025 cases and 1,357 deaths, another in 2019 with 3,291 cases and 2,193 deaths, and a 2020 event with 3,462 cases and 2,279 deaths. In Guinea Bissau, outbreaks occurred in 2014, with multiple events reporting hundreds of cases and over a hundred deaths. Additional outbreaks were documented in Sierra Leone in 2014, with over 2,000 cases and more than 1,200 deaths, and in 2000 in the United Republic of Tanzania, with 426 cases and 172 deaths. The virus has also been detected in other regions, including a single case in Spain in 2014 and one in Italy in 2015.

Ebola hemorrhagic fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including Remdesivir, Favipiravir, Galidesivir, and Teicoplanin, which are noted for their potential therapeutic or inhibitory effects. The disease is also linked to other conditions through biological and pathological relationships, such as being a subtype of viral infectious disease and being associated with severe systemic complications like shock and multiple organ failure. Certain drugs have been identified as contributing to or being associated with the pathogenesis of Ebola-related conditions, including Taurine, Alanine, and other compounds involved in inflammatory and metabolic pathways. Additionally, the disease is connected to broader disease categories such as viremia and cholestasis, indicating complex interactions with host physiology and drug metabolism.

Additionally, certain drugs are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis, particularly glucose-6-phosphate.

Yellow Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various countries, including Nigeria, Brazil, Congo, Ecuador, Angola, Cameroon, Mali, Burkina Faso, Egypt, and others, spanning from 1996 to 2022. Notable events include a large outbreak in Angola in 2016 with 3,137 cases and 345 deaths, a significant outbreak in Brazil in 2016 with 1,336 cases and 215 deaths, and a major outbreak in Egypt in 2012 with 732 cases and 165 deaths. Other outbreaks have occurred in regions such as French Guiana, South Africa, and multiple countries in Africa and South America, with case counts ranging from single cases to several hundred. Yellow Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Dengue Fever has been documented in numerous outbreaks across Asia, Africa, South America, and the Caribbean, with major events in Malaysia, India, Brazil, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and the Netherlands. Outbreaks have occurred in multiple years, including 1996, 1997, 2002, 2004, 2016, 2017, 2019, 2020, 2021, 2022, and 2023, with case numbers ranging from a few to over 7.6 million in a single outbreak reported in 2024. Notable events include a severe outbreak in the Netherlands in 2004 with over 40,000 cases and 507 deaths, a large outbreak in Pakistan in 2022 with over 50,000 cases, and a significant outbreak in Brazil in 2002 with over 317,000 cases. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its severe form, dengue hemorrhagic fever, is considered a subtype of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with Dengue Fever, including doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and various cholesterol derivatives.
6: Parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease is classified as a type of parasitic infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including Albendazole, Anthelmintics, Closantel, Ivermectin, Levamisole, Mebendazole, Netobimin, Niclosamide, Praziquantel, and Thiabendazole. Opisthorchiasis, which is a subtype of parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease, is linked to drugs such as Anthocyanins, Praziquantel, Reactive Nitrogen Species, Reactive Oxygen Species, and Turmeric. Hookworm disease is associated with Albendazole, Dichlorvos, Ivermectin, Oxantel pamoate, and tribendimidine. Schistosomiasis is treated with Metrifonate and Praziquantel, while intestinal schistosomiasis is associated with Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol. 7: Outbreaks of Yersinia Pestis have been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, with significant events occurring in Madagascar, Zambia, Mozambique, and other regions. In 2017, Madagascar experienced a major outbreak with multiple reports on August 1, August 17, August 23, and other dates, involving thousands of cases and hundreds of deaths, including a particularly severe episode with 2,348 confirmed cases and 202 deaths. Additional outbreaks were documented in Zambia in 1997 and 1999, with case counts ranging from 43 to 267, and in Mozambique in 1997 with 115 and 335 cases. Other recorded outbreaks occurred in Angola (1999), Congo (2005 and 2020), India (2002), United Republic of Tanzania (1998), Algeria (2003), and Ecuador (2010), with case numbers ranging from 10 to 436 and varying mortality rates. The pathogen Y. pestis has also been associated with infections in other contexts, including exposure to drugs such as arsenic, azathioprine, benzylpenicillin, ciprofloxacin, and vancomycin, which are often studied or used in conjunction with infectious conditions. Inflammation has been linked to various drugs, including allopurinol, losartan, and dexamethasone, with some agents like alanine and posaconazole implicated in disease pathogenesis. Chagas disease, which is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis, is associated with specific drugs such as benznidazole and nifurtimox, and is linked to the Chagas disease pathway. Parasitic infectious diseases, including those caused by Yersinia Pestis, are categorized under broader disease by infectious agent, and are associated with drugs like ivermectin and toosendanin, with dextrose also noted as a potential contributor to disease mechanisms.
8: Ebola has been associated with multiple outbreak events across several countries, including repeated occurrences in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Guinea Bissau, Sierra Leone, United Republic of Tanzania, Cameroon, and South Africa (KwaZulu Natal). Notable outbreaks include a large-scale event in the Democratic Republic of the Congo in 2018, with over 3,000 cases and more than 2,000 deaths, and another significant outbreak in 2019 with over 3,000 cases and more than 2,000 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in 2020 and 2021, with varying case numbers and fatalities, including a 2022 event with two confirmed cases and two deaths. In Guinea Bissau, multiple outbreaks were reported in 2014, with case counts ranging from 8 to 390 and death tolls from 3 to 267. The disease has also been documented in Tanzania in 2000 and 2019, and in Cameroon in 1996 and 2014. Ebola hemorrhagic fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including Remdesivir, Favipiravir, Galidesivir, Teicoplanin, and BH30sucMan, which are studied or used in the context of the disease. The condition is also linked to various environmental and chemical agents that may affect the immune system, such as arsenic, lead, mercury, ethanol, nicotine, and various pesticides and flame retardants. Additionally, Ebola is associated with pathways related to viral infectious disease processes, and it is considered a type of immune system disease, which itself is categorized as a disease of anatomical entity.

10: An outbreak of pneumonia occurred in Viet Nam and Indonesia on March 12, 2003, with 305 confirmed cases and 5 deaths. Pneumonia is classified as a lung disease and is associated with several drugs, including Amoxicillin-Potassium Clavulanate Combination, Ampicillin, Azithromycin, Doxycycline, Erythromycin, Vancomycin, and others. The drug Arginine is implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. Additionally, pneumonia is linked to various environmental and chemical exposures such as asbestos, coal, cocaine, soot, and vehicle emissions. The condition shares characteristics with other diseases, including epilepsy, which is categorized as a brain disease and resembles migraine.

Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and dozens of deaths, and in Brazil, where a major outbreak in 2002 involved over 300,000 cases. Additional outbreaks were documented in countries such as India, Pakistan, the Philippines, El Salvador, Cameroon, Bangladesh, and East Timor, with case counts ranging from a few to over 500,000, and death tolls varying from single digits to over 700. Notably, a large outbreak in 2024 was reported with 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths, while another in 2023 involved half a million cases. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and several drugs have been associated with its management, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, among others. The condition is also linked to the broader category of dengue disease, with dengue hemorrhagic fever recognized as a subtype.

Chikungunya virus disease has been reported in multiple outbreaks, including in the United Republic of Tanzania, where cases were recorded in 2016 and 2017, and in Congo, where a large outbreak in 2019 involved over 6,000 cases. Other notable events occurred in Chad, where a 2020 outbreak reported over 27,000 cases and one death, and in Reunion Island and France, with smaller but documented cases. The disease has also been observed in the United States, Spain, Italy, Argentina, and Egypt, with case numbers ranging from one to over 13,000. Chikungunya virus disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and the drug chloroquine has been associated with its treatment.

Malaria has been reported in outbreaks in countries such as Burundi, where a 1999 event involved over 600,000 cases and 51 deaths, and in Pakistan, where a 2022 outbreak included 3.4 million cases. Additional outbreaks were recorded in Ethiopia, the United Republic of Tanzania, and other regions, with some events reported without specific case or death counts. Malaria is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease, and its symptoms include fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, and various neurological and hematological manifestations. Several drugs are associated with its treatment, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemisinin, quinine, and various combinations such as atovaquone-proguanil and amodiaquine-artesunate. The disease is also linked to the biological pathway of malaria, and certain substances such as cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid are implicated in its pathogenesis.

Opisthorchiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. It is associated with several drugs, including anthocyanins, praziquantel, reactive nitrogen species, reactive oxygen species, and turmeric. 

Liver disease is categorized as a hepatobiliary disease. It is linked to a wide range of drugs, both as an associate and in disease pathogenesis. These include acetaminophen, acetylsalicylic acid, alcohol, antithyroid preparations, antibiotics such as cephalexin and doxycycline, chemotherapeutic agents like doxorubicin, environmental toxins such as diethylnitrosamine and tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, and various natural compounds including capsaicin, naringenin, and safflower oil. Additionally, hepatitis B e antigens and toluene are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis.

Fibrosis is associated with a broad spectrum of drugs, including alendronate, amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate combination, asbestos, bezafibrate, carboplatin, doxepin, emodin, enalapril, gentamicins, ketoconazole, melatonin, methapyrilene, methylmercury compounds, naringin, and various natural extracts such as panax notoginseng and salvia. Other agents include oxygen, taurine, thioacetamide, and tobacco smoke pollution.

Cholangiocarcinoma is classified as a bile duct adenocarcinoma. It is associated with drugs such as 1,2,3-trichloropropane, 2-aminofluorene, azoxymethane, carmustine, chlorodiphenyl, diethylnitrosamine, dimethylnitrosamine, melatonin, methotrexate, nitroglycerin, pentachlorophenol, phenobarbital, reactive nitrogen species, reactive oxygen species, tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, thalidomide, thioacetamide, furan, methylethylnitrosamine, nitromethane, and propylene dichloride.

Schistosomiasis is also classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with metrifonate and praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is linked to praziquantel, quercetin, and resveratrol.

Jaundice is associated with numerous drugs, including acetylsalicylic acid, alprazolam, amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate combination, atorvastatin, azathioprine, benzbromarone, carbamazepine, chlorpromazine, ciprofloxacin, cyproterone acetate, diflunisal, disulfiram, ethambutol, fluconazole, fluoxymesterone, ganciclovir, heparin, isoniazid, ketoconazole, lamotrigine, methotrexate, nifedipine, pioglitazone, pravastatin, roxithromycin, tamoxifen, thalidomide, thioridazine, turmeric, ursodeoxycholic acid, vitamin E, and various toxic substances such as aluminum phosphide and cefuroxime axetil.

An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. These events highlight a significant and sustained burden of the disease in the region during that period.

Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which in turn is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, and various antiprotozoals. Other compounds linked to treatment or study include Allopurinol, Artemisinin, and several experimental agents such as 2,5-bis(2-(2-propoxy)-4-(2-pyridylimino)aminophenyl)furan and 2-(2''-dichloroacetamidobenzyl)-3-(3'-indolylquinoline). The disease is also associated with biological pathways related to leishmaniasis, and its pathogenesis involves mechanisms such as reactive oxygen species and neopterin. Additionally, several immunosuppressive and chemotherapeutic agents, including Azathioprine, Cyclosporine, Methotrexate, and various chemotherapy protocols like MOPP-ABV and VIP-E, are noted as being associated with the condition, suggesting a link between immune modulation and disease susceptibility or progression.
14: Granulomatous amebic encephalitis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Similarly, primary amebic meningoencephalitis is also categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease.

Outbreaks of Zika virus have been reported across multiple countries, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, India, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, and Brazil, with case counts ranging from 1 to 8,750. Notable outbreaks occurred in French Guiana in 2015 and 2016, with 8,750 and 6 cases respectively, and in Brazil in 2015 and 2016, with 14 and 3,174 cases. Zika virus is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological agents associated with Zika virus include chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide.

Outbreaks of Dengue Fever have been documented in numerous regions, including Malaysia, Cameroon, Bangladesh, East Timor, Barbados, El Salvador, Brazil, India, the Netherlands, Pakistan, Burkina Faso, and others, with case numbers ranging from 1 to over 7.6 million. Major outbreaks occurred in the Netherlands in 2004, with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths, and in Pakistan in 2019 and 2022, with 47,120 and 48,906 cases respectively. In 2024, a large outbreak was reported with 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with this condition include 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, F2-isoprostanes, hydroxychloroquine, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, rolitetracycline, silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease.

Outbreaks of Yellow Fever have been recorded in Ghana, Cameroon, Ecuador, Brazil, Mali, United Republic of Tanzania, Angola, Egypt, Congo, Sierra Leone, and other countries, with case counts ranging from 1 to 459 and death tolls from 1 to 116. Notable events include a 2012 outbreak in Egypt with 459 cases and 116 deaths, and a 2016 outbreak in Angola with 164 cases and 37 deaths. Yellow Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. 

Outbreaks of Chikungunya virus disease have occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania, the United States, Congo, Cameroon, France, Chad, Reunion, St Martin, Italy, Spain, Argentina, India, and Egypt, with case numbers ranging from 1 to 27,540. A significant outbreak in Chad in 2020 involved 27,540 cases and 1 death, while another in Egypt in 2018 involved 13,978 cases. Chikungunya virus disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with this condition include chloroquine.

Measles outbreaks have been reported globally across multiple years and regions, including significant events in Egypt (2018 and 2019), Ethiopia (2022), Madagascar (2018), Mexico (2020), Brazil (2018), Sudan (W Equatoria) (2022), Pakistan (2022), the Cook Islands (2019), the Central African Republic (North) (2019), the United States (2015), Japan (2018), Thailand (2023), Ecuador (2015 and 2023), the Netherlands (2022), Somalia (2022), South Africa (KwaZulu Natal) (2022), Kyrgyzstan (2014), Chile (2023), Guadeloupe (1997), and Burundi (2020), with case counts ranging from single cases to over 40,000 in a single outbreak. The condition is also associated with several drugs, including Benznidazole, Nifurtimox, Nitrofural, and 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, among others, which are either studied in relation to the disease or used in its treatment.

19: Cutaneous leishmaniasis, mucocutaneous leishmaniasis, and leishmaniasis are all classified as types of leishmaniasis, with leishmaniasis further categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Pentamidine, Pentoxifylline, Miltefosine, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, Paromomycin, and various antiprotozoals. Additional drugs linked to treatment or study include Allopurinol, Indomethacin, Omeprazole, Zinc sulfate, WR279396, liposomal amphotericin B, mammea A-BB, methyl gallate, oil of Chenopodium, pistagremic acid, and several experimental compounds such as 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, and 5,7-dihydroxy-8-(2-methylbutanoyl)-6-(3-methylbutyl)-4-phenyl-chroman-2-one. The biological pathway associated with leishmaniasis is the leishmaniasis pathway itself.
20: An outbreak of HIV infection-AIDS occurred in Pakistan on April 25, 2019, with 876 confirmed cases. Malaria has been reported in multiple locations and time periods, including an outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, another in Burundi on May 25, 1999, with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, a further outbreak in Ethiopia reported on November 1, 2024, and a significant event in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, involving 3,400,000 cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, oliguria, pallor, pruritus, hemoglobinuria, and neurologic manifestations. The disease is linked to biological pathways involving malaria itself. Several drugs are associated with malaria treatment, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemether, artesunate, quinine, mefloquine, atovaquone, proguanil, sulfonamides, and various combinations such as amodiaquine-artesunate and atovaquone-proguanil. Additionally, some drugs are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, including cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. HIV infection is associated with the pathway of human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection. Dermatitis is classified as a skin disease and is associated with numerous drugs, including mustard gas, dinitrofluorobenzene, chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, cisplatin, docetaxel, imiquimod, warfarin, and others, with gemcitabine specifically noted to play a role in its pathogenesis. Onchocerciasis is classified as a filariasis and is associated with drugs such as ivermectin, diethylcarbamazine, albendazole, and abamectin. Skin atrophy is also categorized as a skin disease. The broader category of disease is linked to pathogenic roles of homocysteine, isoprostanes, mannitol, tocopherol, and ubidecarenone.
21:
Anemia is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including acute abdominal pain, chest pain, cyanosis, diarrhea, fever, jaundice, pallor, purpura, retinal and vitreous hemorrhage, and various forms of anoxia and respiratory alkalosis. Additional manifestations include cardiac output abnormalities, hearing loss, intellectual disability, and complications such as hydrops fetalis and fetal distress. The condition is classified as a hematopoietic system disease and shares clinical similarities with celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, malaria, and thymus cancer. Several drugs are associated with anemia, including anticonvulsants, cyclosporine, dasatinib, methotrexate, and various chemotherapeutic agents such as ifosfamide and daunorubicin. Some drugs, like aspirin, caffeine, and phenacetin combinations, are also linked to the condition. Notably, alanine is implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. Environmental and chemical exposures, such as cadmium, lead, and air and water pollutants, are associated with anemia, as are dietary factors like beta carotene and calcium. Certain drugs, including interferons and valproic acid, are also noted in this context.

Cancer is categorized as a disease of cellular proliferation. It is associated with a broad spectrum of drugs, including chemotherapeutics such as cisplatin, doxorubicin, and cyclophosphamide, as well as agents like metformin, temsirolimus, and various environmental toxins such as benzene, tobacco smoke, and organoselenium compounds. Some drugs, including azathioprine and disulfiram, are linked to the disease through their role in pathogenesis. Key pathogenic factors include amines, glycine, kynurenic acid, ethyl arachidonate, and trimethionine. The disease is also associated with exposure to pollutants such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, flame retardants, and volatile oils.

Immune system diseases are classified as diseases of anatomical entity and are associated with a variety of drugs, including azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, and mercury compounds, as well as environmental agents such as arsenic trioxide, lead, and various volatile oils and pesticides. Other contributing factors include ethanol, nicotine, and pollutants like trichloroethylene and perfluorooctane sulfonic acid. The disease is also linked to compounds such as chrysene, ochratoxin A, and sodium arsenite.
22: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999, Pakistan in 2022 with 3.4 million confirmed cases, and Ethiopia in 2024. The disease is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, coma, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, unconsciousness, neurological manifestations, oliguria, pallor, pruritus, hemoglobinuria, hypergammaglobulinemia, cerebellar ataxia, birth weight abnormalities, fetal hypoxia, and consciousness disorders. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or study of malaria, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, atovaquone-proguanil, and sulfonamides. Some drugs, such as chloroquine diphosphate, desmethylastemizole, and ferroquine, are noted for their role in disease pathogenesis, particularly in relation to cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid metabolism. The disease is also linked to the malaria pathway, and its treatment involves a variety of antimalarial agents, including artemether, primaquine, and pyrimethamine.
23: Parasitemia is associated with several drugs, including Artesunate, Chloroquine, Quinine, cobaltiprotoporphyrin, and the fanasil–pyrimethamine drug combination, with creatinine implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. Chagas disease is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis and is linked to the biological pathway of Chagas disease. It is treated with drugs such as 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, Benznidazole, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Nifurtimox, and Nitrofural. Symptoms of anemia, which is associated with Chagas disease, include acute abdominal pain, fatigue, pallor, fever, jaundice, purpura, retinal hemorrhage, skin manifestations, cardiac output abnormalities, and various neurological and systemic manifestations such as hypoxia, hypergammaglobulinemia, and proteinuria. The condition resembles celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, malaria, and thymus cancer, and is categorized as a hematopoietic system disease. Megacolon and megaesophagus, both associated with Chagas disease, are classified as colonic and esophageal diseases, respectively, with specific drug associations including dibutylphthalate and diethylnitrosamine. Right bundle branch block is recognized as a type of heart conduction disease. Chagas cardiomyopathy is managed with digoxin and diuretics. Additionally, various drugs such as angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, heparin, imatinib mesylate, interferons, and others are associated with anemia, some of which also play a role in disease pathogenesis, including alanine.
24:
Strongyloidiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. It is associated with several drugs, including Abamectin, Dichlorvos, Levamisole, Metrifonate, and Thiabendazole, which are used in its treatment or studied in relation to the condition.

Hepatitis B is a viral infectious disease that presents with a range of symptoms, including jaundice, fever, skin manifestations, purpura, proteinuria, hypergammaglobulinemia, brain death, intellectual disability, Down syndrome, and other systemic features. It is linked to multiple drugs, both as therapeutic agents and in the context of disease pathogenesis. Drugs associated with hepatitis B include Abacavir, Adefovir, Adefovir dipivoxil, Lamivudine, Entecavir, Tenofovir, Emtricitabine, Interferon alfa, Peginterferon alfa-2a, and various antivirals such as Acyclovir, Ganciclovir, and Zidovudine. Additionally, drugs like Hepatitis B Vaccines, Hepatitis B Surface Antigens, and Hepatitis B Core Antigens are used in prevention and diagnosis. Some compounds, including dextrose, unspecified form, and hepatitis B antigens, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. The condition is associated with the Hepatitis B biological pathway and resembles liver cancer, membranous glomerulonephritis, and primary biliary cholangitis in clinical or pathological features.

Parasitic infectious disease is categorized as a disease by infectious agent. The broader category of Parasitic Diseases is associated with drugs such as Abamectin, Arsenamide, Disintegrins, Ivermectin, and toosendanin, with dextrose, unspecified form, noted as playing a role in disease pathogenesis.
25: An outbreak of pneumonia occurred in Viet Nam and Indonesia on March 12, 2003, with 305 confirmed cases and 5 deaths. Pneumonia is classified as a lung disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including Amikacin, Ampicillin, Azithromycin, Ceftriaxone, Dexamethasone, Methotrexate, and Ofloxacin, among others. Several of these drugs are also linked to the disease's pathogenesis, with arginine identified as a contributing factor. The condition is further associated with environmental and chemical agents such as asbestos, dust, and vehicle emissions.
26:
Dengue Fever has been associated with multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia in 1997 with over 19,000 cases and 50 deaths, Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, and Pakistan in 2019 with 47,120 cases and 75 deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in countries such as Cameroon, Bangladesh, Mayotte, East Timor, Barbados, El Salvador, Egypt, India, France, and the Netherlands, with case counts ranging from a few to over 58,000. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and hinokiflavone. It is also linked to the broader category of dengue disease, which includes dengue hemorrhagic fever as a subtype.

Cholera has been documented in numerous outbreaks globally, including in the Federal Republic of Nigeria with over 12,000 cases and 1,193 deaths in 1996, and in the United Republic of Tanzania with 60,000 cases and 3,100 deaths in 2009. Other significant outbreaks occurred in Angola, Congo, Iraq, Mozambique, and Panama, with case numbers ranging from dozens to over 30,000. The disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway of Vibrio cholerae infection. The cholera vaccine CVD 103-HgR is noted as a drug associated with the condition.

Japanese Encephalitis has been reported in Malaysia, India, Thailand, and Australia, with notable outbreaks in India in 2005 involving 1,145 cases and 296 deaths, and in Australia in 2022 with 37 cases and 3 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with outbreaks in Southeast Asia and the Pacific region.

Hemorrhagic fever has been recorded in multiple outbreaks in the United Republic of Tanzania, Congo, Sudan (W Equatoria), Gabon, and other regions, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 300 and death tolls reaching up to 80 in some instances. The disease has been linked to outbreaks in Central and West Africa, particularly in the Congo Basin and surrounding areas, and is associated with high mortality rates in some events. It is also reported in South Africa (KwaZulu Natal) and other African countries.

Leishmaniasis is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including amphotericin B, antimony, meglumine antimoniate, miltefosine, and various natural compounds such as 5,7-dihydroxy-8-(2-methylbutanoyl)-6-(3-methylbutyl)-4-phenyl-chroman-2-one and oil of Chenopodium. The disease is linked to the biological pathway of leishmaniasis.

Schistosomiasis and its subtype intestinal schistosomiasis are classified as parasitic helminthiasis infectious diseases. The condition is associated with drugs such as praziquantel, metrifonate, quercetin, and resveratrol.

Hemolysis is associated with a wide range of drugs and substances, including acetaminophen, chloroquine, amiodarone, aspirin, benzylpenicillin, and various toxins such as aflatoxins, mercury compounds, and viper venoms. It is also linked to environmental and chemical agents like cadmium chloride, dichlorodifluoromethane, and tobacco smoke pollution.
27:
Cholera has been associated with multiple documented outbreaks across various regions, including significant events in the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 2009, with over 16,000 and 60,000 cases respectively, and in Mozambique in 1998 and 2004, with more than 26,000 and 15,000 cases reported. Outbreaks have also occurred in Nigeria, Pakistan, Congo, Cameroon, Iraq, and other countries, with case counts ranging from a few to over 30,000 and death tolls reaching several hundred in some instances. The causative agent, *Vibrio cholerae*, has been linked to outbreaks in countries such as Cameroon, Zambia, Egypt, and Iraq, with notable events including a large outbreak in Zambia in 2002 involving over 22,000 cases and 609 deaths. Cholera is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway of *Vibrio cholerae* infection. The disease is managed with the cholera vaccine CVD 103-HgR, and several drugs are associated with its treatment or study, including ciprofloxacin, doxycycline, and oral rehydration solutions. Additionally, cholera is recognized as a disease caused by an infectious agent and is part of the broader category of bacterial infectious diseases.

Diarrhoea has been reported in multiple outbreaks, including a notable event in Angola in 1998 involving 560 cases and 12 deaths. It is classified as a gastrointestinal system disease and is associated with various drugs that either treat it or contribute to its pathogenesis, such as loperamide, omeprazole, and antibiotics like ciprofloxacin and metronidazole. The condition is linked to the pathogenic roles of amino acids like alanine and aspartic acid, as well as chlorides. Diarrhoea is also associated with several pharmaceutical agents, including antipsychotics, chemotherapeutics, and herbal drugs, and is influenced by substances such as lipopolysaccharide from *Escherichia coli* and tcdA protein from *Clostridium difficile*. The disease is part of a broader category of infectious diseases and is often studied in the context of gastrointestinal dysfunction.
28: Ehrlichiosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. Human monocytic ehrlichiosis is a subtype of ehrlichiosis. Lyme disease is also categorized as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the drugs Ceftriaxone and Doxycycline. Rickettsioses NOS is associated with the drugs 3-(5-tert-butylisoxazol-3-yl)-2-((3-chlorophenyl)hydrazono)-3-oxopropionitrile and Ciprofloxacin.

An outbreak of diarrhoea occurred in Angola on May 26, 1998, resulting in 560 confirmed cases and 12 deaths. Diarrhoea is classified as a gastrointestinal system disease and includes a subtype known as osmotic diarrhoea. Numerous drugs are associated with diarrhoea, including 1-arabinofuranosylcytosine-5'-stearylphosphate, Abemaciclib, Acetazolamide, Ambenonium Chloride, Azacitidine, Cefuroxime, Clindamycin, Clomipramine, Curcumin, Cyclophosphamide, Cyclosporins, Cytarabine, D-JNKI-1, Drugs, Chinese Herbal, Enkephalin, D-Penicillamine (2,5)-, Ethinylestradiol, Flucytosine, Fluorouracil, Gemeprost, Granisetron, Indomethacin, Irbesartan, Itraconazole, Lamivudine, Lincomycin, Linezolid, MRK 003, Minocycline, Morphine, Nelfinavir, ON 01910, Okadaic Acid, Omeprazole, Pantoprazole, Praziquantel, Procainamide, Quercetin, Quinidine, Rifampicin, Roflumilast, Rosuvastatin Calcium, SR 48968, Saccharin, Ticlopidine, Tioguanine, Vincristine, Vorinostat, Zinc, carbidopa-levodopa drug combination, and cesium chloride. Additionally, certain drugs are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis, including alanine and aspartic acid.

Malaria outbreaks have been reported in multiple countries across different time periods. In 1998, an outbreak occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania, and another in Burundi on May 25, 1999, with 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks were recorded in Ethiopia and Pakistan, with the latter reporting 3,400,000 cases on January 1, 2022. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, vomiting, diarrhea, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, eye hemorrhage, pallor, purpura, purpura fulminans, oliguria, hypergammaglobulinemia, cerebellar ataxia, consciousness disorders, fetal hypoxia, birth weight abnormalities, neurologic manifestations, hemoglobinuria, and jet lag syndrome. Several drugs are associated with malaria treatment, including Amodiaquine, Artemether, Artemisinin, Artemotil, Artesunate, Astemizole, CDRI-97-78, Calcium, Chloroquine, Chlorpromazine, Ciprofloxacin, Cyproheptadine, Dapsone, Diterpenes, Clerodane, Doxycycline, Halofantrine, Heme, Hydrazones, Hydroxychloroquine, Imipramine, Insecticides, Ketotifen, Lumefantrine, Maloprim, Mefloquine, Primaquine, Proguanil, Protriptyline, Pyrimethamine, Quinazolines, Quinidine, Quinine, Testosterone, Tetracycline, Triazoles, Trimipramine, amodiaquine-artesunate combination, atovaquone-proguanil combination, borrelidin, chloroquine diphosphate, desmethylastemizole, fanasil-pyrimethamine combination, ferroquine, frenolicin B, isodiospyrol A, mefloquine-sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, mururin A, palmarumycin JC2, poliothrysoside, and sulfonamides. Some of these drugs also play a role in the disease’s pathogenesis, involving cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. The disease is linked to the malaria pathway and shares characteristics with other parasitic protozoa infectious diseases.
30: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks, including one in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, and another in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3.4 million confirmed cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a range of symptoms such as fever, chills, coma, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, pallor, retinal hemorrhage, and neurological manifestations. The disease is linked to several biological pathways, including the malaria pathway itself. Treatment involves a variety of drugs, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin-based combinations, mefloquine, primaquine, and sulfonamides. Certain drugs are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, including cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. Malaria resembles other conditions such as celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, thymus cancer, and anemia.

Visceral Leishmaniasis has been associated with multiple outbreak events in Pakistan during 2002, including a major outbreak on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional events on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. These outbreaks highlight a significant burden of the disease in the region during that period.

                        Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which in turn is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Numerous drugs are associated with the treatment or study of the disease, including Amphotericin B, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, Antimony compounds, and various antiprotozoals. Specific compounds such as 2,5-bis(2-(2-propoxy)-4-(2-pyridylimino)aminophenyl)furan, 2-(2''-dichloroacetamidobenzyl)-3-(3'-indolylquinoline), and several natural products like Desmodium gangeticum extract and methyl gallate have also been linked to the condition. Additionally, drugs such as Allopurinol, Artemisinin, Ketoconazole, and Thalidomide are noted in association with the disease. The biological pathways involved include those related to leishmaniasis, and the disease is associated with reactive oxygen species and neopterin.
32: Intestinal infectious disease is classified as a type of intestinal disease. Trichinosis is categorized as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and has been associated with the use of Mangifera indica extract and mangiferin. Edema is linked to a wide range of substances and drugs, including acetylcysteine, aconitine, alcohols, anesthetics, azathioprine, bisphenol A, bleomycin, cadralazine, chromium, cisplatin, cloxacillin, crotalid venoms, diclofenac, dinitrochlorobenzene, dinoprostone, FCC13, GR 73632, gossypol, heparin, L-826266, lansoprazole, lithium carbonate, masoprocol, metamfetamine, N(6)-(3-iodobenzyl)-5'-N-methylcarboxamidoadenosine, phosphoramidon, pyrazolanthrone, SR 140333, sodium lauryl sulfate, terazosin, tetradecanoylphorbol acetate, theophylline, tizanidine, triamcinolone acetonide, valproic acid, vehicle emissions, bradykinin derivatives, calcium channel blockers, centipedic acid, demethoxycurcumin, glycyrol, honokiol, hydrochlorothiazide-triamterene, kaempferol, kaempferol-3-O-galactoside, pinitol, and qingre zaoshi liangxue fang.

Infection is associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, the COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, clarithromycin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, cytarabine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, doxorubicin, epirubicin, etoposide, everolimus, exisulind, the FEC protocol, the LNH 87 protocol, lithium, the MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, metronidazole, netilmicin, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, sirolimus, the TAC protocol, tobramycin, triamcinolone, vancomycin, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin.

Cutaneous leishmaniasis is associated with drugs such as 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, allopurinol, amphotericin B, antimony, antimony sodium gluconate, antiprotozoals, gentamicins, indomethacin, itraconazole, ketoconazole, meglumine, meglumine antimoniate, miltefosine, omeprazole, paromomycin, pentamidine, pentoxifylline, sodium chloride, WR279396, zinc sulfate, liposomal amphotericin B, and mammea A-BB. It is classified as a type of leishmaniasis.

Mucocutaneous leishmaniasis is associated with amphotericin B, antimony, antimony sodium gluconate, antiprotozoals, meglumine antimoniate, pentamidine, and pentoxifylline. It is also classified as a type of leishmaniasis.

Leishmaniasis is associated with drugs including 5,7-dihydroxy-8-(2-methylbutanoyl)-6-(3-methylbutyl)-4-phenyl-chroman-2-one, amphotericin B, antimony, antimony sodium gluconate, antiprotozoals, arsenic compounds, atovaquone, chromans, marbofloxacin, maytansine, meglumine antimoniate, miltefosine, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, N-butyl-(1-(4-methoxy)phenyl-9H-beta-carboline)-3-carboxamide, nelfinavir, nitric oxide, pentamidine, tafenoquine, trifluralin, fenarimol, mammea A-BB, methyl gallate, oil of Chenopodium, and pistagremic acid. It is linked to the biological pathway of leishmaniasis and is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease.

Hypertension is characterized by symptoms such as albuminuria, angina pectoris, anoxia, birth weight, body weight, cardiac output, low, decerebrate state, dizziness, edema, eye manifestations, fetal distress, fetal hypoxia, headache, hearing loss, noise-induced, heart murmurs, hemifacial spasm, hypotension, orthostatic, intermittent claudication, microvascular angina, nocturia, obesity, abdominal, oliguria, overweight, polyuria, post-exercise hypotension, proteinuria, psychophysiologic disorders, reflex, abnormal, retinal hemorrhage, sleep apnea syndromes, sleep apnea, obstructive, snoring, and Williams syndrome. It is associated with drugs including 1H-indene-2-carboxylic acid, 1-(1,3-benzodioxol-5-yl)-3-(2-(carboxymethoxy)-4-methoxyphenyl)-2,3-dihydro-5-propoxy-, (1S,2R,3S)-, A 192621, AM 281, azilsartan, barbiturates, carbon monoxide, cathinone, cilnidipine, ciprofloxacin, clozapine, droperidol, drugs, Chinese herbal, fluorescein, GABA-B receptor agonists, indinavir, iodine, irbesartan, isosorbide dinitrate, isosorbide mononitrate, LR B-081, lead acetate, levodopa, lipoic acid, MK 761, manidipine, medetomidine, metformin, methyldopa, N-(2-cyclohexyloxy-4-nitrophenyl)methanesulfonamide, naloxone, naphazoline, naproxen, norgestrienone, olanzapine, ozone, PS1145, pargyline, piperaxon, polychlorinated biphenyls, ramiprilat, rilmenidine, sibutramine, sildenafil citrate, sodium cyanide, tramazoline, urapidil, verapamil, methyl arachidonylfluorophosphonate, prolinedithiocarbamate, and stannous mesoporphyrin. Certain drugs, such as 1-naphthaleneacetic acid, 2,3-epoxypropyl acrylate, 5,6-epoxy-8,11,14-eicosatrienoic acid, 11-dehydro-thromboxane B2, cotinine, cytidine-5'-triphosphate, phosphatidylinositols, prostaglandins a, and undecaphosphotungstate, are involved in the disease’s pathogenesis. Hypertension is classified as a type of artery disease and resembles abdominal obesity-metabolic syndrome 1, obesity, and type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Tuberculosis is associated with drugs including 9,9-dimethylcarbazine, amikacin, aminosalicylic acid, amithiozone, bedaquiline, capreomycin, chenodeoxycholic acid, ciprofloxacin, cycloserine, ethambutol, ethionamide, gentamicins, iron, isoniazid, kanamycin, menaquinone, OPC-67683, ofloxacin, pitolisant, prednisolone, pyrazinamide, pyridoxine, pyrroles, rifampicin, streptomycin, terizidone, tretinoin, verapamil, viomycin, isofon, and isoniazid/pyrazinamide/rifampin. Some drugs, such as lopinavir, phosphorus, and phosphates, are involved in the disease’s pathogenesis. Tuberculosis is linked to the biological pathway of tuberculosis and is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease.
34:
Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998 and July 1999, and in Ethiopia in November 2024. A major outbreak was also reported in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and encompasses several subtypes, including Plasmodium falciparum malaria, Plasmodium vivax malaria, and Plasmodium malariae malaria, all of which are considered forms of malaria.

The disease is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, coma, unconsciousness, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, pallor, retinal hemorrhage, eye hemorrhage, cerebral ataxia, hypergammaglobulinemia, oliguria, purpura, and fetal hypoxia. Key drugs used in the treatment or management of malaria include Amodiaquine, Artesunate, Atovaquone, Chloroquine, Mefloquine, Primaquine, Pyrimethamine, Quinine, and sulfonamides. Specific drug combinations such as amodiaquine-artesunate, atovaquone-proguanil, and mefloquine-sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine are also used. Some drugs are associated with the disease's pathogenesis, including Glucose-6-Phosphate, Cholesterol, Glutamic acid, and bilirubin. The condition is linked to the biological pathway known as Malaria. Fever is a common symptom and is associated with various drugs, including Artesunate, Dexamethasone, and Thioridazine, with Hydroxyindoleacetic Acid implicated in its pathogenesis.
35:
Dengue Fever has been associated with multiple documented outbreaks across various regions and time periods, including significant events in Malaysia (1996 and 1997), Sri Lanka (2017), Brazil (2002 and 2008), the Netherlands (2004), India (1996, 2003, and 2021), Pakistan (2019 and 2021), and more recently in Bangladesh (2022), Cape Verde (2009), and multiple countries in Asia, Africa, and South America (1998). Outbreaks have also been reported in Cameroon, El Salvador, the Philippines, Ecuador, and Reunion (France), with case counts ranging from a few to over 80,000, and fatalities reaching up to 750 in 2023. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its severe form, dengue hemorrhagic fever, is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease. Biomedical studies indicate that several substances are associated with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, F2-isoprostanes, hydroxychloroquine, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, rolitetracycline, silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A.

Zika virus outbreaks have been reported in multiple countries, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Brazil, Argentina, and India. Notable outbreaks occurred in French Guiana (2015 and 2016), Brazil (2015 and 2016), and India (2016 and 2021), with case numbers ranging from 1 to over 8,700. Some outbreaks were associated with fatalities, such as in the Republic of Panama (1 death) and El Salvador (2 deaths). The virus is linked to a range of clinical manifestations and has been reported in both endemic and imported cases, including in the Caribbean and parts of Europe.

Chikungunya virus disease has been documented in outbreaks across the United States, France, Italy, Spain, Argentina, India, Egypt, Reunion Island, and the United Republic of Tanzania, with notable events in 2005, 2006, 2013, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2018, and 2019. The disease has been reported in Chad (2020), with over 27,000 cases and one death, and in Congo (2019), with 6,149 cases. It is classified as a viral infectious disease, and the drug chloroquine is associated with its management. Outbreaks have occurred in both tropical and temperate regions, including in Europe, highlighting its global spread potential.

42: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, and July 14, 1999, as well as in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, where 3,400,000 cases were reported. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is further categorized into subtypes such as Plasmodium falciparum malaria and Plasmodium vivax malaria, both of which are associated with distinct drug treatments. Common symptoms of malaria include fever, chills, jaundice, vomiting, diarrhea, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, hemoglobinuria, pallor, purpura, and neurological manifestations. The disease is also linked to complications such as hemolysis, fetal hypoxia, and reduced birth weight. Several drugs are associated with malaria treatment, including chloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, mefloquine, primaquine, and atovaquone-proguanil combinations. Some drugs, such as chloroquine and primaquine, are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, particularly through interactions with glucose-6-phosphate, cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. Biological pathways associated with malaria include the malaria pathway itself. The disease shares characteristics with other parasitic protozoa infectious diseases and is influenced by various environmental and pharmacological factors.

An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a leishmaniasis and belongs to the category of parasitic protozoa infectious diseases. Treatment options include a range of drugs such as Amphotericin B, Antimony, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, and liposomal amphotericin B, among others. The disease is associated with the Leishmaniasis pathway.

Malaria outbreaks have been reported in multiple regions, including the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, Ethiopia in 2024, and Pakistan in 2022 with 3,400,000 cases. Malaria is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with the Malaria pathway. Common symptoms include fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, and neurological manifestations. Treatment involves drugs such as Chloroquine, Artemisinin, Quinine, Primaquine, Pyrimethamine, Atovaquone, and combinations like atovaquone-proguanil and amodiaquine-artesunate. Certain drugs, including cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. Malaria resembles anemia and other hematopoietic system diseases.

Outbreaks of Ebola have been documented in multiple countries over several decades, with significant events occurring in the Republic of Congo, Guinea Bissau, Sierra Leone, United Republic of Tanzania, Mali, Angola, and the United States. Notable outbreaks include one in Congo in 1996, with 20 confirmed cases and 13 deaths, and another in 2007, involving 372 cases and 166 deaths. A major outbreak in Congo in 2018 was followed by multiple subsequent events, including one in May 2018 with 58 cases and 27 deaths, and another in August 2018 with 53 cases and 29 deaths. Further outbreaks in Congo in 2019 and 2020 were particularly severe, with case counts exceeding 1,000 and death tolls reaching over 2,000 in several instances. In 2014, outbreaks were reported in Guinea Bissau, Sierra Leone, and Mali, with the Sierra Leone outbreak involving over 800 cases and 539 deaths. Additional events occurred in 2020 and 2021, including a small outbreak in Congo with one confirmed case and one death. The virus responsible for these outbreaks is Ebola, a member of the filovirus family.

Ebola hemorrhagic fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. Several drugs have been associated with its treatment or study, including BH30sucMan, Favipiravir, Galidesivir, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Remdesivir, and Teicoplanin. Additionally, HIV infection is linked to the biological pathway of human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection, which may be relevant in understanding co-infections or immune system interactions in the context of Ebola.
47:
Outbreaks of Zika virus have been documented across multiple countries, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, India, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, and others, with case counts ranging from 1 to 8,750. Notable outbreaks occurred in French Guiana in 2015 and 2016, with 8,750 and 6 cases respectively, and in Republic of Panama in 2015 and 2016, with 4 and 5 cases. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological agents associated with Zika virus include chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide.

Outbreaks of Dengue Fever have been reported in numerous regions, including Malaysia, Sri Lanka, People's Republic of Bangladesh, Mayotte, East Timor, Barbados, El Salvador, India, Cape Verde, The Netherlands, Vietnam, Pakistan, France, Spain, Burkina Faso, Thailand, Honduras, Reunion (France), Uruguay, Ecuador, Brazil, São Tomé and Príncipe, and others, with case numbers ranging from 2 to over 7.6 million. Major outbreaks occurred in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, in Pakistan in 2019 with 47,120 cases and 75 deaths, and in Brazil in 2008 with 120,570 cases and 48 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its severe form, dengue hemorrhagic fever, is a subtype of dengue disease. Drugs associated with dengue disease include 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, F2-isoprostanes, hydroxychloroquine, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, rolitetracycline, silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A.

Outbreaks of Yellow Fever have been recorded in Brazil, Federal Republic of Nigeria, French Guiana, Cameroon, United Republic of Tanzania, Ecuador, Angola, Chad, China, Benin, Ghana, Mali, Sierra Leone, Egypt, and other countries, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 1,100. Notable outbreaks include one in Angola in 2015 with 1,132 cases and 168 deaths, and in Brazil in 2017 with 723 cases and 237 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with viral infectious diseases include tilorone, 6-phosphonylmethoxyethoxy-2,4-diaminopyrimidine, cidofovir, melatonin, tobacco smoke pollution, ergosterol-5,8-peroxide, proxigermanium, and putranjivain A.

Outbreaks of Chikungunya virus disease have occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania, United States, Congo, Cameroon, France, Reunion, St Martin, Italy, Spain, Argentina, India, and Egypt, with case numbers ranging from 1 to over 27,000. A significant outbreak in Chad in 2020 involved 27,540 cases and 1 death, while outbreaks in Reunion in 2005 and 2006 involved 3,115 and 1,722 cases respectively. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with chikungunya include chloroquine.

51: Chagas disease is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including Benznidazole, Nifurtimox, Nitrofural, and various compounds such as 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, and N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl. The condition is linked to the biological pathway associated with Chagas disease. Furthermore, parasitemia, a condition related to the presence of parasites in the blood, is associated with the same drugs and also involves creatinine in its disease pathogenesis.
52: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998 and July 1999, and in Ethiopia in November 2024. A major outbreak was also documented in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, diarrhea, vomiting, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, oliguria, pallor, pruritus, hypergammaglobulinemia, hemoglobinuria, cerebellar ataxia, birth weight abnormalities, fetal hypoxia, eye hemorrhage, consciousness disorders, and neurologic manifestations. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or study of malaria, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, atovaquone-proguanil, and sulfonamides. Some drugs, such as chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine, are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, along with metabolic factors like cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. The disease is linked to the biological pathway known as Malaria.

Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Brazil, the Netherlands, India, and several countries in Africa, South America, and Southeast Asia, with case counts ranging from a few to over half a million and death tolls reaching several hundred. Notable outbreaks occurred in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, in the Netherlands in 2004 with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths, and in 2023 with a large-scale outbreak reported in unspecified locations involving 500,000 cases and 750 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, among others. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease.

Typhoid fever has been documented in outbreaks in the Republic of Tajikistan in 1996, with over 7,500 cases reported, and in Pakistan in 2016 with 5,274 cases. It is categorized as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the use of antibiotics such as chloramphenicol and ciprofloxacin. The disease is linked to the biological pathway of Salmonella infection.

Visceral Leishmaniasis has been reported in multiple outbreaks in Pakistan, with one event in 2002 involving 200,000 cases. It is classified as a leishmaniasis and is associated with a variety of drugs, including amphotericin B, miltefosine, antimony compounds, paromomycin, and ketoconazole, among others.

Leptospirosis has been observed in outbreaks in the United Republic of Tanzania, India, Malaysia, and Canada, with case numbers ranging from 6 to 413 and death tolls up to 48. It is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease.

Tuberculosis is associated with a broad range of drugs, including isoniazid, rifampicin, ethambutol, pyrazinamide, and bedaquiline, and is linked to the pathway of tuberculosis. It is also associated with the role of lopinavir, phosphorus, and phosphates in disease pathogenesis. Tuberculosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease.

Respiratory tract infections are associated with numerous drugs, including amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate, azithromycin, ceftriaxone, and various antivirals and anti-inflammatories, with some drugs such as clozapine implicated in disease pathogenesis.

57:
Dengue Fever has been associated with multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and dozens of deaths, and Sri Lanka, which experienced a large outbreak in 2017 with over 80,000 cases and 215 fatalities. Additional outbreaks were reported in Brazil, India, Pakistan, Egypt, the Netherlands, and other countries, with case counts ranging from a few to over 300,000, and death tolls varying from single digits to several hundred. The disease has also been documented in East Timor, Barbados, El Salvador, France, and the Caribbean, among other locations. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its pathogenesis is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver. A severe form of the disease, dengue hemorrhagic fever, is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease.

Rift Valley Fever has been reported in multiple countries, including Egypt, Tanzania, Madagascar, Yemen, Saudi Arabia, and South Africa, with outbreaks documented from 1999 to 2022. Notable events include a large outbreak in Egypt in 2007 with over 600 cases and 211 deaths, and another in 2008 with 698 cases and 222 deaths. In Tanzania, outbreaks occurred in 2007 and 2018, with high mortality rates, including a 2007 event with 264 cases and 109 deaths. The disease has also been reported in Mayotte, Niger, and China, with varying case numbers and fatalities. Rift Valley Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its management has been associated with the use of galidesivir and ribavirin.

Chikungunya virus disease has been reported in numerous countries, including Reunion Island, India, Tanzania, Congo, Chad, France, Italy, Spain, and Argentina. Major outbreaks occurred in Reunion Island in 2005 and 2006, with over 3,000 and 1,700 cases respectively, and in Chad in 2020 with 27,540 cases. Smaller outbreaks were recorded in the United States, Cameroon, and other regions. The disease is also classified as a viral infectious disease, and its treatment has been associated with chloroquine. Infections in general are linked to a broad range of drugs, including antibiotics, antivirals, chemotherapeutics, and immunosuppressants such as azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, methotrexate, and dexamethasone.
58: Chagas disease is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, Benznidazole, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Nifurtimox, and Nitrofural, which are studied or used in its treatment. The disease is linked to the Chagas disease pathway.
59:
Dengue Fever has been associated with multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and dozens of deaths. Outbreaks have also been documented in Sri Lanka, Brazil, the Netherlands, India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and several countries in Africa and South America, with case counts ranging from a few to over 800,000 in some instances. Notably, large-scale outbreaks were reported in 2002 in Brazil and the Netherlands, and in 2022 and 2023 in Bangladesh and other regions, with severe outcomes including hundreds of deaths. The disease is caused by the Dengue virus and is classified as a viral infectious disease. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and various bioactive compounds such as hinokiflavone and podocarpusflavone A, which are studied in relation to the condition.

Hemorrhagic fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks, primarily in the United Republic of Tanzania, Congo, Sudan (W Equatoria), and other African regions, with case numbers ranging from a few to over 100 and death tolls reaching up to 80 in some instances. Outbreaks have occurred in the 1990s, 2000s, and 2010s, with notable events in 2000, 2002, 2003, 2004, 2007, 2012, and 2016. The disease has been documented in regions including Cuvette Ouest, Gabon, and the Republic of the Congo, with high fatality rates in certain outbreaks. The virus responsible is associated with severe clinical outcomes, including high mortality.
60:
Infection is associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, the COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, clarithromycin, cocaine, cyclosporine, cytarabine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, doxorubicin, etoposide, everolimus, exisulind, fludarabine, itraconazole, the LNH 87 protocol, lithium, the MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, metronidazole, netilmicin, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, sirolimus, the TAC protocol, tetrandrine, tobramycin, topotecan, triamcinolone, the VIP-E protocol, vancomycin, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin.

Scabies is classified as a mite infestation and is associated with drugs such as benzyl benzoate, ivermectin, lindane, permethrin, piperonyl butoxide, butyl phosphorotrithioate, diethyl maleate, and sulfur.

Strongyloidiasis is categorized as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with drugs including abamectin, dichlorvos, levamisole, metrifonate, and thiabendazole.
61:
Outbreaks of Zika virus have been reported across multiple countries between 2015 and 2021, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, India, Brazil, Argentina, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Sint Maarten, Cape Verde, Bonaire and Aruba, Trinidad and Tobago, Costa Rica, Colombia and Venezuela, France, and others. Notable events include a large outbreak in French Guiana in November 2015 with 8,750 confirmed cases, a significant outbreak in Brazil in January 2016 with 3,174 cases and 38 deaths, and a case in India in July 2021 involving 70 confirmed cases. Additional outbreaks were documented in various regions with smaller case counts, including single cases in several countries and clusters of 1 to 10 cases in others.

Zika virus is associated with several clinical conditions, including zika virus congenital syndrome, which is classified as a syndrome. Microcephaly, a condition characterized by an abnormally small head, is recognized as a congenital nervous system abnormality and has been linked to Zika virus infection. Several drugs have been associated with microcephaly, including acitretin, carbamazepine, valproic acid, ethanol, phenytoin, phenobarbital, anticonvulsants, cocaine, cytarabine, and others. Guillain-Barré syndrome, an autoimmune disease of the peripheral nervous system, has also been associated with Zika virus infection, and several drugs such as cisplatin, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, and corticosteroids have been linked to this condition. Zika virus is classified as a viral infectious disease and falls under the broader category of diseases caused by infectious agents. It is also associated with various drugs, including tilorone, cidofovir, and others, which are studied in the context of viral infections.
62:
Elephantiasis is classified as a form of lymphedema. Drugs associated with its management include quartz, smectite, and mica.

Filarial elephantiasis is a subtype of filariasis. Treatment is associated with the use of albendazole, diethylcarbamazine, and ivermectin.

Basal cell carcinoma is linked to multiple drugs, including arecoline, arsenic, azathioprine, doxorubicin, ficusin, HhAntag691, imiquimod, and methotrexate. It is also associated with the biological pathway of basal cell carcinoma.

Superficial basal cell carcinoma is a subtype of basal cell carcinoma.

Pituitary adenoma is a benign neoplasm of the pituitary gland. It is associated with a range of drugs, including 2-amino-5-nitrothiazole, batimastat, bromocriptine, cabergoline, caffeine, clomifene, combined oral contraceptives, diethylstilbestrol, domperidone, estradiol, estrone, metronidazole, n-nitrosobutylurea, octreotide, resveratrol, risperidone, tamoxifen, temozolomide, toremifene, urethane, valproic acid, and various estrogens and estrogen derivatives. The condition is linked to the biological pathways of apoptosis, cell cycle regulation during mitosis, ErbB signaling, insulin signaling, and transcriptional misregulation in cancer.

Diabetes mellitus is a glucose metabolism disease. It is associated with numerous drugs, including 2,4,5,2',4',5'-hexachlorobiphenyl, 8-(4-tolylsulfonylamino)quinoline, 8-(methylsulfonylamino)quinoline, acetylsalicylic acid, Agent Orange, air pollutants, allergens, alloxan, arachidonic acids, arsenic, atrazine, chlorides, chlorpromazine, chlorpyrifos, chlorthalidone, clozapine, copper, dichlorvos, dieldrin, hexachlorobenzene, insecticides, magnesium, methylprednisolone, molybdenum, nitrogen dioxide, phenformin, polychlorinated biphenyls, polychlorinated dibenzodioxins, potassium, pravastatin, prednisone, quetiapine fumarate, repaglinide, resveratrol, rosiglitazone, serotonin, sulpiride, trifluoperazine, voglibose, alachlor, aminoacetone, dimethylarsinous acid, glucocorticoids, nimbidiol, osthol, oxychlordane, pesticides, pregnadiene-3,16-dione, and triphenyltin. Additionally, certain substances such as alanine, chemokine CX3CL1, and pyrimidine are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis.

Dengue Fever is a viral infectious disease caused by the dengue virus, with documented outbreaks across multiple countries and regions over several decades. Notable outbreaks include a large-scale event in Malaysia in 1997, reporting 11,328 cases and 28 deaths, and a significant outbreak in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths. Additional major outbreaks occurred in Brazil in 2002 (317,787 cases, 57 deaths), the Netherlands in 2004 (with multiple events involving over 40,000 cases and more than 500 deaths), and India in 1996 and 2003 with thousands of cases. More recent outbreaks were reported in Bangladesh in 2022 (7,687 and 52,807 cases), Pakistan in 2019 and 2022 (47,120 and 48,906 cases), and East Timor in 2005 and 2022. A particularly severe outbreak was reported in 2024 with 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths, though the affected country was not specified. The disease is associated with several drugs, including hydroxychloroquine, doxycycline, and silver, and is linked to biological pathways involving 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, and F2-isoprostanes. It is classified as a viral infectious disease and is also known as dengue disease, with dengue hemorrhagic fever recognized as a subtype.

Chikungunya virus disease is a viral infectious disease caused by the chikungunya virus, with documented outbreaks in multiple countries. Notable events include a large outbreak in Chad in 2020 with 27,540 cases and one death, and significant outbreaks in Reunion Island in 2005 and 2006, with 3,115 and 1,722 cases respectively. Other outbreaks occurred in Tanzania in 2016 and 2017 (1,792 and 453 cases), Congo in 2019 (6,149 cases), and Egypt in 2018 (13,978 cases). Smaller outbreaks were reported in France, Italy, Spain, and the United States, with cases ranging from 1 to 183. The disease is associated with the drug chloroquine and is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Yellow Fever is a viral infectious disease caused by the yellow fever virus, with outbreaks reported in multiple countries across Africa, South America, and other regions. Notable outbreaks include events in Angola in 1995, 1997, and 2016 (359, 2, and 164 cases respectively), and in Brazil in 2000, 2002, 2003, 2005, 2017, and 2018 (with case counts ranging from 3 to over 700). In 2021, outbreaks were reported across 33 countries in five WHO regions, with 203 cases and 40 deaths in January and 300 cases and 66 deaths in December. Other outbreaks occurred in Cameroon, Ghana, Guinea Bissau, Egypt, and Mali, with case numbers ranging from 1 to over 700. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Zika virus is a viral infectious disease caused by the Zika virus, with outbreaks reported in multiple countries, particularly in the Americas and the Pacific. Major outbreaks occurred in French Guiana in 2015 and 2016 (8,750 and 6 cases), Brazil in 2015 and 2016 (14 and 3,174 cases), and Cape Verde in 2015 (165 cases). Smaller outbreaks were reported in El Salvador, Panama, Ecuador, Mexico, and the United States, with case counts ranging from 1 to 46. Additional cases were reported in India in 2016 and 2021 (1 and 70 cases), and in the Maldives in 2016. The disease is associated with outbreaks in the Caribbean and Central America, including Sint Maarten, St Lucia, and the Dominican Republic.

Malaria is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease caused by Plasmodium species, with documented outbreaks in multiple countries. Notable events include a large outbreak in Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, and a significant outbreak in Pakistan in 2022 with 3,400,000 cases. Outbreaks were also reported in Ethiopia and the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999. The disease is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, and neurological manifestations. It is linked to several drugs, including chloroquine, artemether, artesunate, and quinine, and is associated with the biological pathway of malaria. The disease is also linked to the role of cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid in disease pathogenesis.

Measles outbreaks have been reported across multiple countries and time periods, including significant events in Egypt (2018 and 2019), Ethiopia (2022), Madagascar (2018), the Cook Islands (2019), the Central African Republic (2019), Sudan (2022), Pakistan (2022), Mexico (2020), Brazil (2018), Japan (2018), the United States (2015), Thailand (2023), Ecuador (2015 and 2023), the Netherlands (2022), Kyrgyzstan (2014), Chile (2023), Somalia (2022), South Africa (2022), Guadeloupe (1997), and the Republic of Ireland (2000). Outbreaks have also been documented in the European Region (2019) and across Africa, Europe, and the USA (2011). These events have involved case counts ranging from a single case to over 40,000, with associated deaths reported in several instances. Measles is classified as a viral infectious disease, and the disease is associated with the Measles pathway. 

Malaria outbreaks have been recorded in the United Republic of Tanzania (1998 and 1999), Burundi (1999), Ethiopia (2024), and Pakistan (2022), with the latter reporting 3,400,000 cases. The disease presents with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, coma, unconsciousness, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, pallor, purpura, retinal hemorrhage, hemoglobinuria, hypergammaglobulinemia, cerebellar ataxia, and neurological manifestations. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or management of malaria, including chloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, mefloquine, primaquine, amodiaquine, atovaquone, proguanil, and sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine. Malaria is associated with the biological pathway known as "Malaria," and its pathogenesis involves interactions with key metabolic substances such as glucose-6-phosphate, adenosine phosphate, and bilirubin. The disease shares a relationship with other conditions, including hypoglycemia and shock, both of which are linked to drug interactions and metabolic disturbances. Hypoglycemia is associated with drugs like chloroquine, sulfonylurea compounds, and pentamidine, which affect glucose metabolism, while shock is linked to drugs such as mefloquine, cocaine, and epinephrine, with taurine implicated in its pathogenesis. These associations highlight the complex interplay between malaria and various physiological and pharmacological systems.
