Opportunistic mycoses such as mucormycosis and candidiasis are also recognized, with mucormycosis treated using Amphotericin B and liposomal amphotericin B, and candidiasis managed with a broad range of antifungals including Amphotericin B, Fluconazole, Itraconazole, and Voriconazole. Aspergillosis and its invasive form are similarly classified as opportunistic mycoses and are treated with drugs such as Amphotericin B, Itraconazole, Voriconazole, and corticosteroids.

Tuberculosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with a wide array of drugs, including Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol, Streptomycin, and newer agents like Bedaquiline and OPC-67683. Some drugs, such as Lopinavir, Phosphorus, and phosphates, are noted to play a role in the disease’s pathogenesis. The condition is linked to the tuberculosis pathway.

Lymphoma is classified as a hematologic cancer and is associated with various antineoplastic agents, including anthracyclines, alkylating agents like Cyclophosphamide and Cisplatin, and targeted therapies such as Idelalisib and Rituximab. Other agents like benzene, radiation, and certain environmental pollutants are also linked to its development.

Dilated cardiomyopathy is classified as an intrinsic cardiomyopathy and is associated with a range of symptoms including dyspnea, chest pain, edema, syncope, and heart murmurs. It is linked to several drugs, including Amiodarone, Doxorubicin, Digoxin, and diuretics, with Creatinine and Malonaldehyde implicated in its pathogenesis. Chronic obstructive pulmonary disease is classified as an obstructive lung disease and resembles idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis. It is associated with symptoms such as dyspnea, cough, fatigue, and hypoxia, and is treated with bronchodilators, corticosteroids, and phosphodiesterase 4 inhibitors. Environmental factors such as tobacco smoke, asbestos, and heavy metals are implicated in its pathogenesis, along with drugs like Ampicillin and Arginine.

Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999 with over 600,000 cases and 51 deaths, Pakistan in 2022 with 3.4 million confirmed cases, and Ethiopia in 2024. The disease is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, coma, diarrhea, vomiting, pallor, purpura, retinal hemorrhage, and neurological manifestations such as unconsciousness and cerebellar ataxia. It is also linked to complications like hemoglobinuria, hypergammaglobulinemia, oliguria, and fetal hypoxia.

Treatment for malaria involves several drugs, including artemisinin, artesunate, chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, mefloquine, primaquine, atovaquone-proguanil, and amodiaquine-artesunate combinations. Other drugs associated with malaria include dapsone, doxycycline, sulfonamides, and various antimalarial agents such as quinine and quinidine. Some drugs are noted to play a role in disease pathogenesis, including cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. Additionally, certain substances like hydroxyindoleacetic acid are implicated in the disease’s biological mechanisms.

Malaria is associated with the biological pathway known as malaria, and its causative agent, Plasmodium vivax malaria, is a subtype of malaria. The disease shares characteristics with other parasitic protozoa infectious diseases, and its management often involves antimalarial drugs, insecticides, and supportive therapies.

Outbreaks of Yersinia Pestis have been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including Ecuador in 2010 with 17 confirmed cases, and Madagascar, which experienced several outbreaks between 1997 and 2021, with case counts ranging from 10 to over 2,000 and death tolls varying from 8 to 202. Additional outbreaks occurred in Zambia, Mozambique, Angola, Congo, India, Algeria, and the United Republic of Tanzania, with case numbers ranging from 10 to 436 and deaths reported in several instances. The disease has been particularly persistent in Madagascar, where multiple outbreaks were documented in 2017 alone, including events on August 1, August 6, August 17, and August 23, with cumulative cases exceeding 2,000 and over 200 deaths.

Yersinia Pestis is associated with the disease category plague, which includes subtypes such as bubonic plague, pneumonic plague, and septicemic plague. 96: Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Brazil, India, the Netherlands, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Egypt, Thailand, and several countries in South America, Africa, and Southeast Asia, with case counts ranging from a few to over 760,000 and death tolls reaching up to 750. Notable outbreaks occurred in 1997 in Malaysia with 11,328 cases and 28 deaths, in 2002 in Brazil with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths, in 2004 in the Netherlands with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths, and in 2022 and 2023 in Bangladesh and other regions with tens of thousands of cases. Additional outbreaks were documented in East Timor, Burkina Faso, France, Spain, and other countries, with some events involving significant mortality. The disease is caused by the Dengue virus and is classified as a viral infectious disease. It is also associated with a more severe form known as dengue hemorrhagic fever. Symptoms such as fever, fatigue, and asthenia are commonly observed, and several drugs have been associated with the condition, including hydroxychloroquine, doxycycline, and various antivirals. The condition is also associated with exposure to environmental agents such as particulate matter and smoke.

Anemia is a hematopoietic system disease and resembles conditions such as celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, malaria, and thymus cancer. It presents with symptoms including fatigue, pallor, fever, jaundice, and various neurological and gastrointestinal manifestations. Treatment involves drugs such as folic acid, iron, and erythropoietin, with alanine implicated in its pathogenesis.

Thrombocytopenia is a blood platelet disease and is associated with drugs including clopidogrel, warfarin, and various chemotherapeutics, with aspartic acid implicated in its pathogenesis. Lymphadenopathy is associated with the drug AZD8848.

The disease is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, Doxycycline, F2-Isoprostanes, Hydroxychloroquine, Hydroxyeicosatetraenoic Acids, Rolitetracycline, Silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease.
126:
Leptospirosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and has been associated with multiple outbreak events across different regions. Notable outbreaks occurred in India in October 1997, with 413 confirmed cases and 48 deaths, and in the United Republic of Tanzania in August 2022, involving 20 cases and 3 deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in Tanzania in June 2004, with 141 cases and 6 deaths, and in Malaysia in September 2000, with 37 confirmed cases. A smaller outbreak was also documented in Canada in September 2000, involving 6 cases.

Myocarditis has been reported in outbreaks in Sri Lanka in March 2005 and in Greece in April 2002, the latter involving 39 cases and 3 deaths. Myocarditis is categorized as an extrinsic cardiomyopathy. It is associated with a range of drugs, including acetylcysteine, amphotericin B, doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, antipsychotics, corticosteroids, and various other compounds such as 4-hydroxy-2-nonenal, dopamine, and fructus schizandrae, radix ginseng, radix ophiopogonis drug combination. Additionally, certain substances like homocysteine, isoprostanes, mannitol, tocopherol, and ubidecarenone are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis.
127: Melioidosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease.
128: Chagas disease is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, Benznidazole, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Nifurtimox, and Nitrofural, which are studied or used in its treatment. The disease is linked to the Chagas disease pathway.
129:
Tetanus neonatorum is classified as a form of tetanus, a primary bacterial infectious disease. The condition is associated with several drugs, including Baclofen, Barbiturates, Diazepam, Labetalol, Magnesium sulfate, Midazolam, Pancuronium, Propofol, Tetanus Toxin, and Vecuronium bromide, which are used in its management or observed in related clinical contexts.

Anemia is categorized as a hematopoietic system disease and shares clinical similarities with celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, malaria, and thymus cancer. It is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including acute abdominal pain, fever, jaundice, diarrhea, anemia-related fatigue, cardiac output abnormalities, neurological manifestations, retinal and vitreous hemorrhages, and various systemic and metabolic disturbances such as hypergammaglobulinemia, alkalosis, and proteinuria. Several drugs are associated with anemia, including acetaminophen, aspirin, various chemotherapeutic agents like cyclophosphamide and doxorubicin, antiretrovirals, diuretics, and environmental toxins such as benzene and lead. Additionally, phenylhydrazine and arsenic compounds are implicated in its pathogenesis, with alanine identified as a contributing factor in disease development.

Hypoglycemia is classified as a glucose metabolism disease. It is associated with numerous drugs, including acetaminophen, chloroquine, insulin secretagogues like glipizide and glyburide, antidiabetic agents such as metformin and tolbutamide, and various other compounds including lithium, warfarin, and valproic acid. Some of these drugs are also implicated in the pathogenesis of the condition, with glutamic acid identified as a contributing factor. The condition is also linked to dietary factors such as dietary fats and triglycerides, and to substances like sodium-4,5-dihydroxy-1,3-benzene disulfonate and aluminum phosphide.

Fever is associated with a broad spectrum of drugs, including antibiotics such as ampicillin and co-trimoxazole, antipsychotics like amisulpride, anticonvulsants such as carbamazepine, and immunomodulators like sirolimus and tacrolimus. It is also linked to environmental agents such as lipopolysaccharide from E. coli and liposomal amphotericin B. Certain drugs, including nicotine, caffeine, and ergot alkaloids, are implicated in the pathogenesis of fever, with hydroxyindoleacetic acid identified as a contributing factor.
130:
An outbreak of myocarditis occurred in Sri Lanka on March 17, 2005, and another in Greece on April 25, 2002, involving 39 confirmed cases and 3 deaths. Myocarditis is classified as an extrinsic cardiomyopathy. It is associated with a range of drugs, including amphetamines, cocaine, anthracyclines, cyclophosphamide, hydroxychloroquine, and various antipsychotics and antidepressants. Additionally, several substances such as lipopolysaccharide from E. coli O55-B5, malonaldehyde, and 4-hydroxy-2-nonenal have been linked to its pathogenesis. 

Chagas disease, also known as American trypanosomiasis, is categorized as a form of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with drugs such as benznidazole, nifurtimox, and nitrofural, and is linked to the Chagas disease pathway. The condition is also associated with parasitemia, which involves drugs like artesunate, chloroquine, and quinine. 

Dilated cardiomyopathy is an intrinsic cardiomyopathy and is associated with symptoms such as dyspnea, chest pain, edema, syncope, and heart murmurs. It is linked to drugs including angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, beta-blockers, diuretics, and digoxin, with some agents such as creatinine and malonaldehyde implicated in its pathogenesis. 

Right bundle branch block is classified as a type of heart conduction disease. Myocardial infarction is considered a form of coronary artery disease and is associated with drugs such as cocaine, ethanol, and various antineoplastic agents, with endothelins and phosphates playing a role in its pathogenesis. 

Hypertrophy is associated with drugs including aminobenzoates, bisphenol A, and various environmental agents such as flame retardants and tobacco smoke pollution, with phosphorus implicated in its development. Chronic disease is linked to drugs like acetylsalicylic acid and corticosteroids. Inflammation is associated with a broad range of agents, including pollutants, antioxidants, and anti-inflammatory compounds, with alanine and posaconazole involved in its pathogenesis. Fibrosis is associated with numerous drugs and environmental toxins, including carbon tetrachloride, methotrexate, and asbestos, with creatinine also implicated in its pathogenesis.
131:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various countries, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and dozens of deaths. Outbreaks have also been documented in Sri Lanka, Brazil, India, the Netherlands, and several other nations, with case counts ranging from a few to over half a million, and death tolls reaching several hundred. Notable large-scale outbreaks occurred in 2002 in Brazil and The Netherlands, and in 2023 in an unspecified country with 500,000 confirmed cases and 750 deaths. More recent outbreaks were reported in 2024 with over 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths, indicating a growing global burden. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, among others. It is also linked to dengue hemorrhagic fever, a more severe form of the disease. The condition is known to be associated with a range of biological pathways and is recognized as a significant public health concern in tropical and subtropical regions.

Zika virus outbreaks have been reported in multiple countries, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Panama, Mexico, French Guiana, Brazil, and India, with case numbers ranging from a few to over 8,000 in a single outbreak. Notable events include a large outbreak in French Guiana in 2015 with 8,750 cases and a significant outbreak in Brazil in 2016 with 3,174 cases and 38 deaths. The virus has also been detected in the United States, the Netherlands, and other regions, often with limited case counts. Zika virus is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide. It is linked to congenital syndrome, a condition that includes microcephaly, which is itself associated with various teratogenic agents such as alcohol, valproic acid, and mercury. The virus is also connected to other congenital nervous system abnormalities and eye diseases like chorioretinitis. Outbreaks have been reported in both endemic and non-endemic regions, highlighting its potential for global spread.
132: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, Ethiopia in 2024, and Pakistan in 2022, where 3.4 million cases were confirmed. The disease is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, diarrhea, vomiting, retinal and eye hemorrhage, pallor, purpura, hypergammaglobulinemia, cerebellar ataxia, neurological manifestations, oliguria, hemoglobinuria, and fetal hypoxia. In terms of treatment, several drugs are associated with malaria, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, mefloquine, artemisinin, artesunate, artemether, lumefantrine, atovaquone, proguanil, pyrimethamine, sulfonamides, doxycycline, tetracycline, and various combinations such as amodiaquine-artesunate and mefloquine-sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine. Additionally, certain compounds like astemizole, desmethylastemizole, and triazoles are noted for their role in disease pathogenesis, while cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin are implicated in the disease's biological mechanisms. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease, and the condition is linked to the malaria pathway.
133:
Outbreaks of West Nile Disease have been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including Canada, the United States, the Republic of Moldova, Israel, and Brazil. Notable events include a large outbreak in the United States in 2002 with over 3,500 confirmed cases and 211 deaths, as well as significant outbreaks in Canada during the same year, with multiple events reporting hundreds of cases. Additional outbreaks occurred in 1996 in the Republic of Moldova and in 2000 in Israel, with varying case numbers and mortality. West Nile Disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and it has been associated with the drugs Active Hexose Correlated Compound and Minocycline.

Outbreaks of Zika virus have been documented in numerous countries, including Brazil, French Guiana, Ecuador, Panama, Mexico, the United States, India, and several Caribbean and Pacific islands. A major outbreak occurred in French Guiana in 2015 with 8,750 confirmed cases, while other events reported smaller clusters, such as in the Netherlands, El Salvador, and the Dominican Republic. Some outbreaks were associated with fatalities, including one in the Republic of Panama with one death. Zika virus is classified as a viral infectious disease, and it has been associated with the drugs Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and Niclosamide.

Dengue Fever has been reported in a wide range of countries, including Brazil, India, Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Egypt, Pakistan, the Netherlands, and several African and Southeast Asian nations. Major outbreaks occurred in 1996 and 1997 in Malaysia, with over 11,000 cases in 1997, and in 2002 in Brazil with over 317,000 cases. A severe outbreak in Sri Lanka in 2017 involved over 80,000 cases and 215 deaths, while another in Pakistan in 2019 involved over 47,000 cases and 75 deaths. A large outbreak in 2023 was reported in an unspecified location with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and it is associated with the drugs 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, Doxycycline, F2-Isoprostanes, Hydroxychloroquine, Hydroxyeicosatetraenoic Acids, Rolitetracycline, Silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease.

Microcephaly is classified as a congenital nervous system abnormality and has been associated with a range of drugs and substances, including Acitretin, Anticonvulsants, Bisphenol A, Busulfan, Carbamazepine, Cocaine, Cytarabine, Estradiol, Ethanol, Fluorouracil, Glyphosate, Mercury, Methylnitrosourea, Phenobarbital, Phenytoin, Propofol, Sotalol, Tretinoin, Trimethadione, Valproic acid, Vitamin A, ipsapirone, nonylphenol, and succinylaminoimidazole carboxamide riboside.

Guillain-Barré syndrome is classified as an autoimmune disease of the peripheral nervous system and has been associated with drugs such as Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Inhibitors, Antilymphocyte Serum, Cisplatin, Cladribine, Colchicine, Cyclophosphamide, Cyclosporine, Cytarabine, Fibrinolytic Agents, Gemcitabine, Penicillamine, Pravastatin, Simvastatin, and corticosteroids.

St. Louis encephalitis is classified as a viral infectious disease.
134:
Urticaria is classified as a skin disease and has been associated with a wide range of drugs, including acetaminophen, allobarbital, aminophenazone, amoxicillin, amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate combination, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, azithromycin, bacampicillin, benzydamine, brodimoprim, bromhexine, cefixime, cefoperazone, ceftriaxone, cetirizine, chlorpheniramine, cimetidine, cinnamaldehyde, clopidogrel, co-trimoxazole, dapsone, diacerein, diltiazem, doxorubicin, etomidate, flurbiprofen, furosemide, glafenine, hydrocortisone, iodine, iohexol, itraconazole, ketorolac, mefloquine, meperidine, mofebutazone, montelukast, netilmicin, norfloxacin, penicillamine, procainamide, ramipril, rifampicin, salicylic acid, sulfadiazine, sulfasalazine, tolmetin, vancomycin, corticosteroids, and noramidopyrine.

Filariasis is categorized as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole.

Loiasis is a form of filariasis and is associated with the drugs diethylcarbamazine and ivermectin.

Mansonelliasis is also classified as a filariasis.

Onchocerciasis is a type of filariasis and is associated with the drugs abamectin, albendazole, diethylcarbamazine, and ivermectin.

Fatigue is associated with numerous drugs, including abemaciclib, acetazolamide, ascorbic acid, atenolol, benzatropine, benzodiazepines, budesonide, capecitabine, chloroquine, clomipramine, clonidine, clozapine, contraceptives, oral, cyclopentolate, cyclophosphamide, dexamethasone, didanosine, digoxin, disulfiram, docetaxel, doxorubicin, etoricoxib, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, guanfacine, indapamide, isoniazid, ketamine, lamivudine, lenalidomide, letrozole, methimazole, mitomycin, norethisterone, ondansetron, PC protocol, paclitaxel, phenytoin, pilocarpine, potassium chloride, semaxanib, sorafenib, testosterone, thalidomide, thiopental, ursodeoxycholic acid, vitamin A, zonisamide, sodium arsenate, and tetrahydrofuran. Additionally, uric acid is implicated in the pathogenesis of fatigue.

Mental fatigue is associated with flavonols.

Myalgia unspecified is associated with drugs such as 6-chloronicotinic acid, acids, chlorpyrifos, cinacalcet, lead, PC protocol, permethrin, petroleum, pyridostigmine bromide, quercetin, rocuronium, rosuvastatin calcium, simvastatin, succinylcholine, vitamin D, and hmg coa reductase inhibitors.
135:
Cholera has been documented in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including significant events in the United Republic of Tanzania, Pakistan, Somalia, Mozambique, Congo, and Sierra Leone, with case counts ranging from dozens to over 38,000 and death tolls reaching several hundred. Notable outbreaks occurred in 1996, 1997, 1998, 2000, 2001, 2002, 2003, 2004, 2010, 2012, 2015, 2017, 2021, and 2022, indicating recurring transmission in vulnerable areas. The disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway of Vibrio cholerae infection. The cholera vaccine CVD 103-HgR is recognized as a drug associated with the disease.

Typhoid fever has been reported in outbreaks in the Republic of Tajikistan, Pakistan, Algeria, and other regions, with confirmed cases ranging from 910 to over 7,500. The disease is also classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is linked to the biological pathway of Salmonella infection. Drugs associated with typhoid fever include Chloramphenicol and Ciprofloxacin.

V. cholerae, the causative agent of cholera, has been involved in outbreaks in countries such as Cameroon, Zambia, Mexico, Niger, Congo, Sierra Leone, United Republic of Tanzania, Egypt, Philippines, Iraq, Dagestan, South East Asia, Azerbaijan, and Madagascar, with case numbers varying from a few to over 22,000 and deaths ranging from 1 to over 80. The disease is similarly categorized as a primary bacterial infectious disease.
136: Helicobacter pylori infection is associated with a range of drugs, including Amoxicillin, Clarithromycin, Metronidazole, Tetracycline, Omeprazole, Lansoprazole, Pantoprazole, Rabeprazole, Proton pump inhibitors, Ranitidine, Bismuth, Bismuth subsalicylate, Ranitidine bismuth citrate, Bismuth tripotassium dicitrate, and various other agents such as Cetraxate, Ciprofloxacin, Lymecycline, Furazolidone, Tinidazole, Zinc, Octreotide, Curcumin, Cyanocobalamin, Fructosamine, Malonaldehyde, Cocaine, Nickel, beta-artecyclopropylmether, jinghua weikang, naphthalenediimide, and trichorabdal A. Some of these drugs are noted to play a role in the disease's pathogenesis, particularly cholesterol. The infection is also linked to the epithelial cell signaling pathway in Helicobacter pylori infection. Melioidosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease.
137:
Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, with 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths, and another outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998. Additional outbreaks occurred in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases reported in the latter. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is further subdivided into specific forms, including Plasmodium falciparum malaria and Plasmodium vivax malaria, both of which are subtypes of malaria.

The disease is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, diarrhea, vomiting, pallor, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, hemoglobinuria, and neurological manifestations such as cerebellar ataxia and consciousness disorders. It is also linked to complications like fetal hypoxia, birth weight reduction, and hypergammaglobulinemia. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or management of malaria, including artemisinin, artesunate, chloroquine, mefloquine, primaquine, amodiaquine, and atovaquone-proguanil combinations. Some drugs, such as chloroquine diphosphate, mefloquine-sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, and fanasil-pyrimethamine combinations, are specifically noted for their role in disease pathogenesis, particularly in relation to glucose-6-phosphate metabolism. Other drugs, including dapsone, sulfonamides, and tetracycline, are also associated with malaria treatment.

Biological pathways linked to malaria include the malaria pathway itself, indicating its central role in disease mechanisms. Additionally, certain substances such as cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. Malaria is also associated with various other drugs, including antibiotics like ciprofloxacin, doxycycline, and vancomycin, as well as immunosuppressants such as cyclosporine, azathioprine, and methotrexate, which may be used in related conditions or co-infections.
138: An outbreak of Diarrhoea occurred in Angola on May 26, 1998, reporting 560 confirmed cases and 12 deaths. Diarrhoea is classified as a gastrointestinal system disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including the 1-UFT protocol, amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate combination, ceftizoxime, cefuroxime, clindamycin, gentamicins, imipenem, rifampicin, and others such as cyclosporine, tacrolimus, and morphine. Additionally, several drugs are noted to play a role in the disease’s pathogenesis, including alanine, aspartic acid, and chlorides. The condition is also linked to parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease through its association with necatoriasis, and the term "Hookworm disease" is connected to the use of drugs such as albendazole, dichlorvos, ivermectin, oxantel pamoate, and tribendimidine.
139:
Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The condition is associated with the use of drugs including acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole.

Onchocerciasis is a form of filariasis and is associated with the use of several drugs, including abamectin, albendazole, diethylcarbamazine, and ivermectin.
140:
A range of diseases are associated with various drugs, both in terms of therapeutic association and involvement in disease pathogenesis. The drug 1-Methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine and its analogs, along with other compounds such as 2,2',4,4',5-brominated diphenyl ether, 2,5-hexanedione, and several others, are associated with nervous system disease, while sorbitol and tryptophan are implicated in its pathogenesis. Similarly, homocysteine, isoprostanes, mannitol, tocopherol, and ubidecarenone are linked to disease pathogenesis. In the context of adult T-cell leukemia, arsenic trioxide, bortezomib, docosahexaenoic acids, emodin, peretinoin, resveratrol, ritonavir, zidovudine, zoledronic acid, and sodium arsenite are associated with the condition, and the disease is linked to the pathways of Human T-cell leukemia virus 1 infection and viral carcinogenesis. It is classified as a subtype of t-cell lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma. T-cell adult acute lymphocytic leukemia is further categorized as a form of adult acute lymphocytic leukemia. Paraparesis, spastic, is associated with drugs such as bicuculline, cytarabine, methadone, morphine, and nicorandil. 

Rheumatoid arthritis presents with a broad spectrum of symptoms, including arthralgia, ataxia, back pain, fatigue, fever, pain, paresthesia, and various neurological and systemic manifestations. It is associated with drugs such as acetylsalicylic acid, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, azathioprine, methotrexate, and several others, while calcifediol and pyrrole-2-carboxylate are implicated in its pathogenesis. The condition is linked to the pathway of rheumatoid arthritis and is classified as a type of arthritis, with similarities to systemic lupus erythematosus and systemic scleroderma. Inflammation is associated with a wide array of drugs, including acarbose, diclofenac, and various solvents and environmental agents, with alanine and posaconazole playing roles in its pathogenesis. Tropical spastic paraparesis is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with prednisone, zidovudine, and thiocyanate, with the Human T-cell leukemia virus 1 infection pathway implicated. 

Multiple sclerosis is characterized by symptoms such as ataxia, diplopia, paresthesia, muscle spasticity, urinary incontinence, and various neurological deficits. It is associated with drugs including amantadine, azathioprine, fingolimod hydrochloride, glatiramer acetate, and vitamin D, while hydrogen is involved in its pathogenesis. The disease is linked to the cell adhesion molecules pathway and is classified as an autoimmune disease of the central nervous system.
141: An outbreak of pneumonia occurred in Viet Nam and Indonesia on March 12, 2003, with 305 confirmed cases and 5 deaths. Pneumonia is classified as a lung disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including amikacin, amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate combination, vancomycin, liposomal amphotericin B, and various beta-lactam antibacterials, as well as environmental agents such as air pollutants, asbestos, arsenic, and coal ash. The drug arginine is implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. Additionally, pneumonia is linked to several other conditions, including eosinophilic pneumonia and chronic eosinophilic pneumonia, which are subtypes of pneumonia. The condition is also associated with exposure to various toxic substances such as mustard gas, paraquat, and particulate matter, and is influenced by factors like iron, zinc, and lipopolysaccharides.
142:
Outbreaks of Zika virus have been reported across multiple countries, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, India, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, and others, with case counts ranging from 1 to 8,750. Notable outbreaks occurred in French Guiana in 2015 and 2016, with 8,750 and 6 cases respectively, and in Republic of Panama in 2015 and 2016, with 4 and 5 cases. In India, outbreaks were reported in 2016 and 2021, with 1 and 70 cases respectively. Zika virus is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological agents associated with Zika virus include chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide.

Outbreaks of Yellow Fever have been documented in numerous countries, including Cameroon, Brazil, Ghana, Sierra Leone, Congo, United Republic of Tanzania, Ecuador, Guinea Bissau, Angola, Egypt, Mali, Burkina Faso, Belgium, and others, with case numbers ranging from 1 to 700,000. Notable outbreaks include a large-scale event in Cameroon in 2011 with 700,000 cases and 35 deaths, and a significant outbreak in Brazil in 2017 with 1 case. In 2021, a multi-country outbreak across 33 countries in five WHO regions reported 203 cases and 40 deaths. Yellow Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Outbreaks of Dengue Fever have occurred in multiple regions, including Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Mayotte, Barbados, El Salvador, Brazil, Egypt, India, Cape Verde, The Netherlands, France, Pakistan, and others, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 7.6 million. Major outbreaks include a 2023 event with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths, a 2017 outbreak in Sri Lanka with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, and a 2024 outbreak with 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its hemorrhagic form is a subtype of dengue disease. Drugs associated with dengue disease include 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, F2-isoprostanes, hydroxychloroquine, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, rolitetracycline, silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A.

Outbreaks of Chikungunya virus disease have been reported in the United Republic of Tanzania, United States, Congo, Cameroon, France, Reunion, Italy, Spain, Argentina, and Egypt, with case numbers ranging from 1 to 27,540. A major outbreak occurred in Chad in 2020 with 27,540 cases and 1 death. Chikungunya virus disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with this condition include chloroquine.

Outbreaks of filariasis have been reported in various regions, and the condition is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. Drugs associated with filariasis include acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole.
143: Outbreaks of Salmonellosis have been reported in multiple countries, including the United States in 2004, 2015, and France in 2017, with confirmed case counts of 32, 124, and 35, respectively. Salmonellosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is also recognized as a gastrointestinal system disease. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including Ampicillin, Cefixime, Cefotaxime, Ceftriaxone, Chloramphenicol, Ciprofloxacin, Gentamicins, Ofloxacin, Pefloxacin, Sultamicillin, Tobramycin, quinocetone, trimethoprim sulfamethizole, zosterin, and plant extracts. Additional drugs associated with Salmonellosis include Cinnamaldehyde, while other drugs such as 6-hydroxy-5-((p-sulfophenyl)azo)-2-naphthalenesulfonic acid disodium salt, Acetylsalicylic acid, Amoxicillin-Potassium Clavulanate Combination, Clioquinol, Cocaine, Colchicine, Domperidone, Erythromycin, Fluorouracil, Fluoxetine, Loperamide, Methotrexate, Metoclopramide, Nitric Oxide, Peptichemio, Procarbazine, Tetraethyl Lead, and deoxynivalenol have also been linked to the disease.
144:
Influenza is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including 1'-methylspiro(adamantane-2,3'-pyrrolidine)maleate, acetylsalicylic acid, Active Hexose Correlated Compound, amantadine, azithromycin, camostat, isoquercitrin, masoprocol, oseltamivir, oseltamivir acid, ozone, probenecid, pseudoephedrine, quercetin, resveratrol, rimantadine, Ro 64-0802, selenium, sulfur dioxide, vitamin E, agathisflavone, amentoflavone, benzylsulfonylargininyl-proline-(4-amidinobenzyl)amide, pyrrolidine dithiocarbamic acid, and robustaflavone. The disease is biologically linked to the Influenza A pathway.

Schistosomiasis is associated with the drugs metrifonate and praziquantel. It is categorized as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease.

Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is also associated with the drugs praziquantel, quercetin, and resveratrol.
145: Schistosomiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. It is associated with several drugs, including Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a form of schistosomiasis, is also linked to Praziquantel, as well as Quercetin and Resveratrol. Schistosoma mansoni infection involves a broader range of drugs, such as Praziquantel, 3,7-dimethyloctan-1-ol, Acetylcysteine, Artemether, IA 4, Nitric Oxide, Oxamniquine, and others, with triglycerides playing a role in disease pathogenesis. The condition is also associated with gastrointestinal hemorrhage, which involves numerous drugs including Acetaminophen, Aspirin, Dexamethasone, Warfarin, and others, with cyclic GMP implicated in disease pathogenesis. Infection, a broader category, is linked to drugs such as Azathioprine, Ciprofloxacin, Dexamethasone, Vancomycin, and others. Chronic Hepatitis C is associated with drugs like Interferon alfa, Ribavirin, Sofosbuvir, Ledipasvir, and others. Fibrosis is linked to a wide range of agents including Bleomycin, Ethanol, Dexamethasone, Methotrexate, and various natural compounds, with several drugs contributing to disease pathogenesis through mechanisms involving Aspartic acid, acylcarnitine, and other metabolites. Portal hypertension, which is a subtype of hepatic vascular disease, is associated with drugs such as Propranolol, Spironolactone, Octreotide, and others, including Nitric Oxide. Hepatocellular carcinoma, a form of liver carcinoma, is linked to drugs such as Sorafenib, Doxorubicin, Methotrexate, and others, with Aspartic acid, acylcarnitine, and tumor-associated antigen 72 involved in disease pathogenesis. It is associated with biological pathways including Hepatitis B, Hepatitis C, MicroRNAs in cancer, and Viral carcinogenesis. Inflammation is associated with drugs such as Epigallocatechin gallate, Dexamethasone, and others, with Alanine and Posaconazole implicated in disease pathogenesis. Ascites is linked to drugs including Furosemide, Spironolactone, and others, such as Propranolol and Vincristine.
146:
Chagas disease is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, Benznidazole, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Nifurtimox, and Nitrofural. The disease is linked to the Chagas disease pathway.

Leishmaniasis is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It is associated with a range of drugs, including 5,7-dihydroxy-8-(2-methylbutanoyl)-6-(3-methylbutyl)-4-phenyl-chroman-2-one, Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Antiprotozoals, Arsenic compounds, Atovaquone, Chromans, Marbofloxacin, Maytansine, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, N-butyl-(1-(4-methoxy)phenyl-9H-beta-carboline)-3-carboxamide, Nelfinavir, Nitric Oxide, Pentamidine, Tafenoquine, Trifluralin, fenarimol, mammea A-BB, methyl gallate, oil of Chenopodium, and pistagremic acid. The disease is linked to the Leishmaniasis pathway.

Sleeping sickness is also classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with drugs such as Melarsoprol, Miltefosine, Pafuramidine, Pentamidine, Phenylbutazone, Piclamilast, and octadecyl-(2-(N-methylpiperidino)ethyl)phosphate. The condition is linked to the African trypanosomiasis pathway.
147:
An outbreak of pneumonia occurred in Viet Nam and Indonesia on March 12, 2003, with 305 confirmed cases and 5 deaths. Pneumonia is classified as a lung disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including Amoxicillin-Potassium Clavulanate Combination, Azithromycin, Cefixime, Cefpodoxime proxetil, Linezolid, Vancomycin, and various corticosteroids. Additionally, arginine has been identified as playing a role in the disease's pathogenesis.

An outbreak of diarrhea was reported in Angola on May 26, 1998, involving 560 cases and 12 deaths. Diarrhea is categorized as a gastrointestinal system disease and is associated with drugs such as Fluorouracil, Octreotide, Mesalazine, and various antibiotics, with alanine and aspartic acid implicated in its pathogenesis.

Salmonellosis outbreaks were documented in the United States in 2004, 2015, and 2017, with case counts of 32, 124, and 35, respectively. The disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the drug cinnamaldehyde.

Malaria outbreaks have been reported in multiple countries over several years, including Burundi in 1999 (616,034 cases, 51 deaths), United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Pakistan in 2022 (3,400,000 cases), and Ethiopia in 2024. Malaria is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with symptoms such as fever, chills, jaundice, coma, diarrhea, and retinal hemorrhage. It is linked to drugs including chloroquine, artemisinin, quinine, atovaquone-proguanil, and sulfonamides, with cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin playing roles in its pathogenesis. The disease is also associated with the malaria pathway and resembles conditions such as celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, anemia, and thymus cancer.

Anemia is classified as a hematopoietic system disease and shares characteristics with malaria, celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, and thymus cancer. It is associated with symptoms including fatigue, jaundice, pallor, fever, and various systemic manifestations. Drugs linked to anemia include iron sucrose, cyclophosphamide, and various chemotherapeutic agents, with alanine implicated in its pathogenesis.

Infection is associated with a broad range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, and various antibiotics, antifungals, and immunosuppressants.
148: Lymphogranuloma venereum is classified as a commensal bacterial infectious disease. Chlamydia is also categorized as a commensal bacterial infectious disease, and trachoma shares the same classification. Chlamydia infection is associated with several drugs, including Azithromycin, Ciprofloxacin, Doxycycline, Erythromycin, INP 0341, INP 0400, Melitten, Mercuric chloride, Minocycline, and Trovafloxacin. HIV infection is linked to the biological pathway of Human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection.
149:
Outbreaks of Zika virus have been reported across multiple countries between 2015 and 2021, with notable events in French Guiana, Brazil, Ecuador, and the Republic of Panama, where case counts ranged from a few to over 8,000. Significant outbreaks occurred in French Guiana in November 2015 with 8,750 confirmed cases, and in Brazil in January 2016 with 3,174 cases and 38 deaths. Other countries affected include El Salvador, Guatemala, Mexico, Colombia, Venezuela, Honduras, Dominican Republic, St Lucia, Sint Maarten, Trinidad and Tobago, Cape Verde, India, Papua New Guinea, the Netherlands, and the United States, with case numbers generally low but occasionally reaching double digits. Some outbreaks were associated with fatalities, such as in the Republic of Panama (1 death) and El Salvador (2 deaths). The virus has also been reported in regions including the Caribbean, South America, and parts of Asia, with cases documented in 2015, 2016, and later in 2017 and 2021.

Zika virus infection is associated with a range of drugs, including antibiotics such as Ceftriaxone, Ciprofloxacin, and Vancomycin, antivirals like Itraconazole, immunosuppressants such as Cyclosporine and Methotrexate, chemotherapeutic agents including Cytarabine and Doxorubicin, and other compounds like Lithium, Silver sulfadiazine, and Cocaine. These drugs are either used in the treatment or management of the infection or are studied in relation to the disease, though their specific roles in pathogenesis are not detailed.
150:
An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan in January 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28 of the same year, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. These events highlight a significant and sustained burden of the disease in the region during that period.

Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which in turn is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a broad range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Meglumine antimoniate, Pentamidine, Miltefosine, Antimony and its derivatives, and various antiprotozoals. Other compounds with reported associations include Artemisinin, Isosorbide dinitrate, Ketoconazole, Paromomycin, and several experimental agents such as 2,5-bis(2-(2-propoxy)-4-(2-pyridylimino)aminophenyl)furan and 2-(2''-dichloroacetamidobenzyl)-3-(3'-indolylquinoline). The biological pathways linked to the disease include those associated with leishmaniasis, and the condition is also connected to the activity of reactive oxygen species and neopterin.
151: Chagas disease, also known as American trypanosomiasis, is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including Benznidazole, Nifurtimox, Nitrofural, and various experimental compounds such as 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, and N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl. The disease is linked to biological pathways involving Chagas disease itself. Additionally, death associated with Chagas disease has been observed in the context of exposure to a wide range of toxic substances, including arsenic, cadmium, lead, mercury, benzene, polychlorinated biphenyls, pesticides, carbon disulfide, asbestos, tobacco smoke pollution, and various other environmental and chemical pollutants. Furthermore, certain amino acids are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis.

Zika virus has been associated with multiple outbreak events across various countries, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, Brazil, Argentina, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Cape Verde, India, and others, with case counts ranging from 1 to 8,750. Notable outbreaks occurred in French Guiana in 2015 and 2016, with 8,750 and 6 cases respectively, and in Brazil in 2016 with 3,174 confirmed cases and 38 deaths. Zika virus is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological agents associated with Zika virus include Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and Niclosamide.

Yellow Fever has been reported in numerous outbreaks across Brazil, Ghana, Federal Republic of Nigeria, Congo, South Africa (KwaZulu Natal), Cameroon, China, Ecuador, Angola, Chad, Egypt, and other regions, with case numbers ranging from 1 to 4,189 and death tolls from 1 to 238. Significant outbreaks occurred in Angola in 2016 with 3,137 cases and 345 deaths, in Nigeria in 2019 with 4,189 cases and 23 deaths, and in Brazil in 2017 with 110 cases and 30 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Dengue Fever has been documented in widespread outbreaks in Malaysia, Sri Lanka, People's Republic of Bangladesh, Mayotte, East Timor, Barbados, El Salvador, Brazil, India, France, Pakistan, Egypt, Portugal, and other countries, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 500,000. Notable events include a 2023 outbreak with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths, a 2024 outbreak with 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths, and a 2017 outbreak in Sri Lanka with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with Dengue Fever include 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, Doxycycline, F2-Isoprostanes, Hydroxychloroquine, Hydroxyeicosatetraenoic Acids, Rolitetracycline, Silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of Dengue disease.

Chikungunya virus disease has been reported in outbreaks in the United Republic of Tanzania, United States, Congo, Cameroon, France, Reunion, Italy, Spain, Argentina, India, Egypt, and other locations, with case numbers ranging from 1 to 27,540. Notable outbreaks occurred in Chad in 2020 with 27,540 cases and 1 death, and in Reunion in 2005 with 3,115 cases. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with Chikungunya include Chloroquine.

West Nile Disease has been observed in outbreaks in Canada, United States, Republic of Moldova, Israel, Brazil, Portugal, Romania and Turkey, and other regions, with case counts ranging from 1 to 3,587 and death tolls from 1 to 211. Significant outbreaks occurred in the United States in 2002 with 3,587 cases and 211 deaths, and in Canada in 2002 with 141 cases and 2 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with West Nile Disease include Active Hexose Correlated Compound and Minocycline.

Japanese Encephalitis has been reported in outbreaks in Malaysia, India, Thailand, and Australia, with case numbers ranging from 37 to 1,145 and death tolls from 3 to 296. Notable events include a 2005 outbreak in India with 1,145 cases and 296 deaths, and a 2022 outbreak in Australia with 37 cases and 3 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease.

The disease is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It is linked to symptoms such as fever, chills, jaundice, vomiting, coma, and others, and is treated with drugs including chloroquine, artemisinin, quinine, and atovaquone-proguanil. 

Parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease is categorized as a parasitic infectious disease and is treated with drugs such as albendazole, mebendazole, ivermectin, praziquantel, and other anthelmintics. 

Tuberculosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway of Tuberculosis. It is treated with drugs including isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol, streptomycin, and bedaquiline, and some drugs such as lopinavir and phosphorus are implicated in its pathogenesis. 

Non-Hodgkin lymphoma is classified as a lymphoma and is associated with drugs such as cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone, as well as environmental agents like dioxins, herbicides, and insecticides. 

Leukemia is classified as a hematologic cancer and is associated with drugs such as methotrexate, cytarabine, imatinib, and doxorubicin, as well as environmental factors like pesticides, insecticides, and radioactive fallout. Steroids are implicated in its pathogenesis. 

HIV infection is associated with the biological pathway of human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection. 

Noma is classified as an ulcerative stomatitis. 

Gingivitis is classified as a gingival disease and is associated with drugs such as chlorhexidine, lidocaine, and prednisone.

159: An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which in turn is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, Antimony, and various antiprotozoals. Other compounds linked to treatment or study include Allopurinol, Artemisinin, Isosorbide dinitrate, Ketoconazole, and several organotin compounds. The disease is also associated with biological pathways related to leishmaniasis. Additionally, infections involving Visceral Leishmaniasis have been observed in conjunction with other drugs such as Cyclophosphamide, Methotrexate, and Doxorubicin, which may be used in the context of immunosuppressive or oncological treatment.

Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998, another in Burundi in May 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, an outbreak in Ethiopia in November 2024, and a large-scale outbreak in Pakistan in January 2022 involving 3.4 million cases. The disease is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, oliguria, pallor, pruritus, hypergammaglobulinemia, hemoglobinuria, cerebellar ataxia, birth weight abnormalities, fetal hypoxia, eye hemorrhage, and neurologic manifestations. Several drugs are associated with malaria treatment, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, atovaquone, proguanil, lumefantrine, pyrimethamine, primaquine, dapsone, sulfonamides, and various drug combinations such as amodiaquine-artesunate and atovaquone-proguanil. Some drugs, such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. The disease is linked to the malaria biological pathway.

Visceral Leishmaniasis has been reported in multiple outbreaks in Pakistan, including events in January 2002, May 2002, and June 2002, with case counts ranging from 738 to 200,000. It is classified as a type of leishmaniasis. Treatment involves drugs such as amphotericin B, miltefosine, pentamidine, paromomycin, antimony compounds (including antimony sodium gluconate and antimony potassium tartrate), meglumine antimoniate, ketoconazole, allopurinol, and various experimental agents. The disease is also associated with reactive oxygen species and other compounds such as isosorbide dinitrate and val-Glu-Pro-Ile-Gly-Tyr.

Additionally, exposure to environmental agents such as bisphenol A, particulate matter, and lipopolysaccharide from E. coli has been linked to the condition.

Malaria outbreaks have been reported in multiple regions, including the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999 with over 616,000 cases and 51 deaths, Pakistan in 2022 with 3.4 million cases, and Ethiopia in 2024. The disease is caused by parasitic protozoa and is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, coma, diarrhea, and neurological manifestations. Key drugs used in treatment include artemether, artesunate, chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, and various antimalarial combinations. The disease is linked to biological pathways involving malaria itself and is influenced by factors such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin in its pathogenesis.

Lassa Fever is a viral infectious disease that has been reported in multiple countries across West Africa and beyond, including Angola, Benin, Federal Republic of Nigeria, Ghana, Sierra Leone, Guinea Bissau, and several European nations such as Germany, the United Kingdom, and Sweden. Outbreaks have occurred in various years, with notable events including a large outbreak in Nigeria in 2023 involving 4,702 cases and 152 deaths, and another in 2018 with 1,849 cases and 114 deaths. Smaller outbreaks have also been documented in countries like Kenya, Tanzania, and The Netherlands, with case counts ranging from 1 to 38 and death tolls from 1 to 73. The disease is associated with the drug Ribavirin, which is used in its treatment. Lassa Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is caused by a virus belonging to the Arenaviridae family.

Malaria is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease caused by Plasmodium species and is prevalent in tropical and subtropical regions. Outbreaks have been reported in countries such as Burundi, Ethiopia, Pakistan, and the United Republic of Tanzania, with significant case numbers recorded in Pakistan in 2022, where over 3.4 million cases were confirmed. The disease is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, and neurological manifestations. It can also lead to complications such as hemoglobinuria, oliguria, and fetal hypoxia. Treatment involves several antimalarial drugs, including Artemether, Artesunate, Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, Mefloquine, Primaquine, and combinations such as atovaquone-proguanil and amodiaquine-artesunate. The disease is linked to biological pathways related to malaria infection and is influenced by factors such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin in its pathogenesis.

Other notable outbreaks occurred in Iraq, where several events were reported between 1998 and 2007, with case numbers ranging from 20 to over 30,000, and in Cameroon, with outbreaks in 2018, 2021, and 2023. Cholera is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the pathogen Vibrio cholerae infection. Outbreaks of acute respiratory illness have been reported in multiple locations, including China in February 2003 with 300 confirmed cases and 5 deaths, and again on February 12, 2003, with 305 cases and 5 deaths. A separate outbreak occurred in Greece on May 3, 2002, involving 51 cases and 3 deaths. Meningitis has been associated with numerous outbreaks across various countries and time periods, including large-scale events in Burkina Faso in 2001, 1997, and 2003, with case counts ranging from 7,751 to over 30,000 and death tolls reaching up to 4,498. Significant outbreaks were also documented in Nigeria, with major events in 1996 and 2015, and in Egypt, where multiple outbreaks occurred between 1996 and 2000, with one event in 1999 reporting 22,000 cases and 1,600 deaths. Other notable outbreaks occurred in Ghana, Chad, Sudan, South Africa (KwaZulu Natal), and the Republic of Moldova, among others, with case numbers and fatalities varying widely. Nipah virus outbreaks have been recorded in Malaysia in 1999, and in India and Bangladesh in multiple years, including 2001, 2004, 2018, 2021, 2023, and 2024, with case counts ranging from 1 to 19 and death tolls from 1 to 17 per outbreak.

Meningitis is classified as a central nervous system disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including ampicillin, ceftriaxone, vancomycin, dexamethasone, and corticosteroids, which are used in its treatment. Bacterial meningitis is a subtype of meningitis and shares the same drug associations. The disease is also linked to the TCR signaling pathway. Acute respiratory illness is not explicitly linked to specific drugs or pathways in the provided data, but it is a condition that has been associated with significant outbreaks in Asia. Nipah virus infection is not described in terms of drug associations or biological pathways in the current data, but it is a viral disease with severe clinical outcomes. Other diseases such as encephalitis, ischemia, vasculitis, and cerebellar ataxia are associated with various drugs and biological pathways, including TCR signaling, vascular and nervous system disease categories, and specific pathogenic mechanisms involving amino acids and other metabolites.
172: Rickettsioses NOS is associated with the drugs 3-(5-tert-butylisoxazol-3-yl)-2-((3-chlorophenyl)hydrazono)-3-oxopropionitrile and Ciprofloxacin, which are observed or studied concurrently with the condition. Additionally, the condition fever of unknown origin is associated with the drugs Amphotericin B, Everolimus, and liposomal amphotericin B, which are used or studied in its context.
173: Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia in 1997 with 11,328 cases and 28 deaths, Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, Brazil in 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths, and the Netherlands in 2004 with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in countries such as Cameroon, East Timor, Pakistan, Egypt, India, and Bangladesh, with case counts ranging from dozens to hundreds of thousands and death tolls varying from a few to over 750. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver. It is also linked to the condition dengue hemorrhagic fever, which is a subtype of dengue disease.
174:
Outbreaks of typhoid fever have been reported in multiple regions, including a significant event in the Republic of Tajikistan in August 1996 with 7,516 confirmed cases, and another in August 1996 with 4,000 cases. A later outbreak occurred in Pakistan in November 2016, involving 5,274 confirmed cases, and a separate outbreak in Algeria in January 1996 resulted in 910 cases and one death. Typhoid fever is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway of Salmonella infection. The condition is commonly managed with the drugs chloramphenicol and ciprofloxacin, which are often studied or used in conjunction with the disease.

Cholera outbreaks have occurred globally across various time periods and locations, including a large outbreak in the Republic of Panama in November 2010 with 60,240 cases and 1,415 deaths, and another in the same country in October 2010 with 3,342 cases and 259 deaths. Additional major outbreaks were reported in Federal Republic of Nigeria in March 1996 (6,117 cases, 487 deaths) and in 1996-03-01 (1,487 cases, 99 deaths), in United Republic of Tanzania in December 1997 (35,591 cases, 2,025 deaths) and in November 2015 (9,871 cases, 150 deaths), and in Angola in June 2006 with 43,076 cases and 1,642 deaths. Other notable events include outbreaks in Iraq in 2003 and 2007, in Mozambique in 2004, in Sierra Leone in 2012, and in Benin in 2021. Cholera is also classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is linked to the biological pathway of Vibrio cholerae infection. The cholera vaccine CVD 103-HgR is associated with the disease, often used in prevention and control efforts.
175:
Parasitic diseases are classified as a type of disease by infectious agent and are further categorized as parasitic infectious diseases. These conditions are associated with several drugs, including Abamectin, Arsenamide, Disintegrins, Ivermectin, toosendanin, and Dextrose in unspecified form, which plays a role in disease pathogenesis. Specific parasitic diseases such as schistosomiasis and its subtype intestinal schistosomiasis are linked to drugs like Metrifonate, Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol. Schistosomiasis is also recognized as a form of parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease, and intestinal schistosomiasis is a subtype of schistosomiasis.

Migraine is a brain disease that resembles restless legs syndrome and is characterized by a wide range of symptoms, including headache, dizziness, nausea, vomiting, visual disturbances such as scotoma and optical illusions, sensory disorders like paresthesia and hyperesthesia, neurological manifestations such as aphasia, hemiplegia, and ophthalmoplegia, as well as systemic symptoms like pain, amnesia, and sleep-wake transition disorders. It is associated with numerous drugs, including Acetaminophen, Ibuprofen, Propranolol, Topiramate, Valproic acid, and various serotonin receptor agonists, as well as substances like nicotine, caffeine, and certain foods such as aspartame. The condition is also linked to drugs that may contribute to its pathogenesis, such as ergot alkaloids and nitroglycerin.

Diabetes mellitus is a glucose metabolism disease and is associated with a broad range of drugs, including Metformin, Glipizide, and Sulfonylurea Compounds, which are used in its management. It is also linked to substances that may play a role in its pathogenesis, such as Alanine, chemokine CX3CL1, and pyrimidine. Additional drugs associated with diabetes include antipsychotics, environmental toxins like Agent Orange and DDT, and dietary components such as omega-3 fatty acids and flavonoids.

Parkinson’s disease is a synucleinopathy and shares clinical and pathological features with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis and restless legs syndrome. It is characterized by symptoms such as tremor, bradykinesia, rigidity, postural instability, gait disturbances, dysarthria, dyskinesia, and non-motor manifestations including fatigue, sleep disorders, REM sleep behavior disorder, hallucinations, cognitive impairment, and urinary bladder dysfunction. The disease is associated with drugs that may contribute to its pathogenesis, such as gamma-aminobutyric acid, and with various agents including 1-methyl-4-phenyl-1,2,3,6-tetrahydropyridine, Manganese chloride, and mancozeb.

Parkinsonian syndrome is associated with a range of drugs, including Levodopa, Risperidone, and various antipsychotics, as well as environmental toxins such as maneb and particulate matter. It is also linked to compounds like coenzyme Q10, ginsenoside Re, and resveratrol, which may play a role in its pathogenesis. The condition is associated with the neurotransmitter gamma-aminobutyric acid and is influenced by drugs such as haloperidol, thioridazine, and clozapine.
176:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including Malaysia, where outbreaks occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and several deaths, and in 2022 with over 7,000 cases in Bangladesh and over 52,000 cases in another outbreak. Significant outbreaks were also documented in Brazil, with over 300,000 cases in 2002 and over 120,000 in 2008, and in the Netherlands, where multiple outbreaks between 2002 and 2004 resulted in tens of thousands of cases and hundreds of deaths. Other notable events include outbreaks in India, Cameroon, El Salvador, Cape Verde, East Timor, Thailand, Pakistan, and Reunion (France), with case counts ranging from a few to over 500,000 and death tolls reaching into the hundreds. Additional outbreaks were reported in countries such as Egypt, Vietnam, Honduras, Uruguay, Ecuador, Burkina Faso, and Chad, with varying case numbers and mortality rates.

Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. It is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, among others. The condition is also linked to dengue hemorrhagic fever, which is a more severe form of the disease. Other related conditions include thrombocytosis, a blood platelet disorder, and hypotension, which is associated with a range of drugs such as antihypertensives, vasopressin, and various antibiotics. Fever, a common symptom of Dengue Fever, is associated with drugs like acetohexamide, amodiaquine, and hydroxyindoleacetic acid, which may play a role in its pathogenesis.
177:
Leptospirosis has been reported in multiple outbreaks across different regions, including an event in the United Republic of Tanzania on August 8, 2022, with 20 confirmed cases and 3 deaths, another in India on October 27, 1997, involving 413 cases and 48 deaths, a case in Malaysia on September 3, 2000, with 37 confirmed cases, a prior outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania on June 17, 2004, with 141 cases and 6 deaths, and a smaller outbreak in Canada on September 21, 2000, with 6 confirmed cases. Leptospirosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease.

Malaria has been documented in several outbreaks, including one in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, another in Burundi on May 25, 1999, with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, a report from Ethiopia on November 1, 2024, and a large outbreak in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, involving 3,400,000 cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal and eye hemorrhage, pallor, purpura, oliguria, hypergammaglobulinemia, cerebellar ataxia, birth weight abnormalities, fetal hypoxia, and neurologic manifestations. Several drugs are associated with malaria treatment or study, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, atovaquone-proguanil combination, and sulfonamides, among others. Some drugs are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. The disease is linked to the malaria pathway and is associated with the use of insecticides.
178: Parasitic diseases are associated with several drugs, including Abamectin, Arsenamide, Disintegrins, Ivermectin, toosendanin, Iron, Metronidazole, Albendazole, Diethylcarbamazine, Acetylsalicylic acid, and Levamisole, which are used in their treatment. Dextrose, unspecified form, is implicated in the pathogenesis of these diseases. Parasitic diseases are classified as a type of disease by infectious agent, and parasitic protozoa infectious disease is a subtype of parasitic infectious disease. Onchocerciasis is a form of filariasis, which itself is categorized as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease.
179:
Leptospirosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. Outbreaks have been documented in multiple countries, including Tanzania, where events occurred in 2004 and 2022 with 141 and 20 confirmed cases respectively, and in India in 1997 with 413 cases and 48 deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in Malaysia in 2000 with 37 cases and in Canada in 2000 with 6 cases.

Rabies is categorized as a viral infectious disease. Outbreaks have been recorded in France in 2004 with one death, and in unspecified locations in 1997 and 2024, though details on the latter are limited. The drug cinnabarin is associated with rabies.

Anthrax is recognized as a primary bacterial infectious disease. Outbreaks have occurred in multiple regions, including Ghana in 1997 with 185 cases and 26 deaths, Ethiopia in 2000, the Soviet Union in 1998 with 15 cases and 2 deaths, and the United States in 2001 with multiple events involving 3 to 23 cases and 1 to 5 deaths. A significant outbreak was reported in Zimbabwe in 2023 with 684 cases and 4 deaths. The drugs Ciprofloxacin and Omadacycline are associated with anthrax.

Typhoid fever is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. Outbreaks have occurred in the Republic of Tajikistan in 1996 with over 7,500 cases, in Pakistan in 2016 with 5,274 cases, and in Algeria in 1996 with 910 cases and one death. The drugs Chloramphenicol and Ciprofloxacin are associated with typhoid fever, and the disease is linked to the Salmonella infection pathway.

Rift Valley Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. Outbreaks have been reported in numerous countries, including Egypt, where multiple events occurred between 2007 and 2022 with case counts ranging from 12 to over 600 and death tolls from 11 to 222. Other outbreaks occurred in Madagascar, Niger, United Republic of Tanzania, Saudi Arabia, Yemen, South Africa, Cameroon, and China. The drugs Galidesivir and Ribavirin are associated with Rift Valley Fever.

Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Outbreaks have been reported in Burundi in 1999 with over 616,000 cases and 51 deaths, in Pakistan in 2022 with 3.4 million cases, and in Ethiopia and Tanzania in multiple years. Symptoms include fever, chills, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, coma, and neurological manifestations. The drugs Amodiaquine, Artemether, Artemisinin, Artesunate, Atovaquone, Chloroquine, Ciprofloxacin, Doxycycline, and others are associated with malaria, and the disease is linked to the Malaria pathway. Cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin are implicated in its pathogenesis.

Fever is associated with a wide range of drugs, including acetaminophen, ibuprofen, corticosteroids, and various antibiotics, and is also linked to the role of hydroxyindoleacetic acid in disease pathogenesis. The disease "disease" itself is associated with drugs that play a role in pathogenesis, including homocysteine, isoprostanes, mannitol, tocopherol, and ubidecarenone.
180: Liver cirrhosis is classified as a type of liver disease. Numerous drugs have been associated with liver cirrhosis, including 1-Naphthylisothiocyanate, 3,5-diethoxycarbonyl-1,4-dihydrocollidine, 3-methylquercetin, Betulinic Acid, Cholic Acid, Colchicine, Contraceptives, Oral, Dietary Fats, Doxorubicin, EMD 527040, Endotoxins, Epalrestat, Ethanol, Etretinate, Fenofibrate, Floxuridine, Fluorouracil, Gallic Acid, Genistein, Halofuginone, Hyaluronic acid, Losartan, Menthol, Mercaptopurine, Mitogens, Nimodipine, Nizatidine, Octreotide, Particulate Matter, Plant Extracts, Scopolamine, Simvastatin, Tea, Thiabendazole, Thiamethoxam, Titanium dioxide, Ursodeoxycholic acid, Ursolic acid, Zinc protoporphyrin, arsenic acid, arsenite, chloroquine diphosphate, chromium hexavalent ion, ochratoxin A, perhexiline maleate, platycodin D, propylene dichloride, schizandrin B, tetramethylpyrazine, trofopar, and glycerin, which plays a role in the disease's pathogenesis. Hepatocellular carcinoma is a type of liver carcinoma and is associated with drugs such as 2,4-dinitrotoluene, 2-Methoxyestradiol, 2-amino-3,8-dimethylimidazo(4,5-f)quinoxaline, 2-amino-9H-pyrido(2,3-b)indole, 3-(4-methylsulfonylphenyl)-4-phenyl-5-trifluoromethylisoxazole, Androgens, Anti-Bacterial Agents, Azathioprine, Bezafibrate, Butylated hydroxytoluene, Chloroquine, Ciprofibrate, Cisplatin, Daunorubicin, Dichloroacetic acid, Dinitrobenzenes, Docetaxel, Ethanol, Gallium maltolate, Gefitinib, Glu-P-2, Irinotecan, Iron, Malachite Green, Meloxicam, Menaquinone, Methionine, N-(2-(3-chloro-5-(trifluoromethyl)-2-pyridyl)ethyl)-alpha,alpha,alpha-trifluoro-o-toluamide, N-ethyl-N-hydroxyethylnitrosamine, Norinyl, Octreotide, PIAF regimen, Pentachlorophenol, Permethrin, Phenytoin, Tetradecanoylphorbol Acetate, Topotecan, Ursodeoxycholic acid, Vanadium, Vitamin K, aflatoxin b1, aroclor 1260, beta-hexachlorocyclohexane, cadmium telluride, dibutylnitrosamine, estrogens, nitrobenzene, sodium arsenite, trichloroacetic acid, and triglycerides, with Aspartic acid, acylcarnitine, ogt 719, purine, and tumor-associated antigen 72 implicated in its pathogenesis. The condition is linked to biological pathways including Hepatitis B, Hepatitis C, Hepatocellular carcinoma, MicroRNAs in cancer, and Viral carcinogenesis. Fibrosis is associated with drugs such as 3,4,5,3',4'-pentachlorobiphenyl, 6-chloro-2,3,4,9-tetrahydro-1H-carbazole-1-carboxamide, 6-diphenylmethyl-5-(5-isopropyl-2-methoxybenzylamino)-1-azabicyclo(2.2.2)octane-3-carboxylic acid, AM 281, AM 1241, AZM551248, Acetaminophen, Alendronate, Amiodarone, Aniline, Atorvastatin, Aurothioglucose, Azathioprine, BIIB 723, Bisphenol A, Bromocriptine, Butylated hydroxytoluene, Cyproterone acetate, Dexamethasone, Dextropropoxyphene, Ergot alkaloids, Famotidine, Mefenamic acid, Meperidine, Methapyrilene, Methotrexate, Methylmercury Compounds, Mitomycin, Monocrotaline, N-(oxo-5,6-dihydrophenanthridin-2-yl)-N,N-dimethylacetamide hydrochloride, Naproxen, Naveglitazar, Nifuroxazide, Olmesartan, Particulate Matter, Phenobarbital, Phenytoin, Propoxur, Puerarin, Silicon dioxide, Tacrolimus, Telmisartan, Tretinoin, Trichostatin A, Ursodeoxycholic acid, Y-27632, arachidonyl-2-chloroethylamide, ganyanping, naringin, and platycodin D. Cholangiocarcinoma is classified as a bile duct adenocarcinoma and is associated with drugs such as 1,2,3-trichloropropane, 2,2-bis(bromomethyl)-1,3-propanediol, 2,3',4,4',5-pentachlorobiphenyl, 2-aminofluorene, 2-nitrotoluene, 3,4,5,3',4'-pentachlorobiphenyl, Anabolic Agents, Azoxymethane, Carmustine, Chlorodiphenyl (54% Chlorine), Diethylnitrosamine, Dimethylnitrosamine, Melatonin, Methotrexate, Methylene chloride, Nitroglycerin, Pentachlorophenol, Phenobarbital, Reactive Nitrogen Species, Reactive Oxygen Species, Tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, Thalidomide, Thioacetamide, furan, methylethylnitrosamine, nitromethane, and propylene dichloride. Melanoma presents with symptoms including abdominal pain, anorexia, anoxia, blindness, cachexia, chills, choroid hemorrhage, colic, diarrhea, diplopia, dysphonia, edema, eye hemorrhage, eye pain, fatigue, fever, hyperalgesia, hyphema, muscle weakness, nausea, night blindness, paresis, paresthesia, pruritus, retinal hemorrhage, scotoma, tinea pedis, vision disorders, low vision, vitreous hemorrhage, and vomiting. It is associated with drugs such as 3-phenylpropionic acid, 4-((3-bromophenyl)amino)-6,7-dimethoxyquinazoline, 4-phenyl-5-(4-nitrocinnamoyl)-1,3,4-thiadiazolium-2-phenylamine, 6-hydroxy-2,5,7,8-tetramethylchroman-2-carboxylic acid, Acitretin, Apigenin, Artesunate, Astemizole, Bosentan, CVD protocol, Carmustine, Cinnamaldehyde, Curcumin, Cyclophosphamide, Cysteinyldopa, Diethylstilbestrol, Docetaxel, Eugenol, Folic acid, Fosbretabulin, Levamisole, Lometrexol, Maneb, N-(2-(diethylamino)ethyl)-2-iodobenzamide, Nelfinavir, Nicotine, Novobiocin, Oblimersen, PD-0325901, PP regimen, Paclitaxel, Paclitaxel docosahexaenoic acid, Pamidronate, Peginterferon alfa-2b, Pimozide, Plitidepsin, Polylactic Acid-Polyglycolic Acid Copolymer, Resveratrol, Semaxanib, Sirolimus, Temozolomide, Thiostrepton, Tretinoin, Triprolidine, Vitamin D, Zinc, detorubicin, hinokiflavone, pyrrole-2,3,5-tricarboxylic acid, and sodium arsenite, with arginine playing a role in its pathogenesis. The condition is linked to the Melanoma pathway and resembles meningioma, ocular cancer, penile cancer, rectum cancer, sarcoma, skin cancer, vaginal cancer, vitiligo, and vulva cancer. Skin melanoma is classified as a type of skin cancer and is associated with boron. Clonorchiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. Carcinogenesis is associated with drugs such as 1,3-butadiene, 4-vinyl-2,6-dimethoxyphenol, Benzalkonium Compounds, Benzo(a)pyrene, Cyproterone acetate, Diethylnitrosamine, Geraniol, Limonene, N-Nitrosopyrrolidine, N-nitroso(di-n-propyl)amine, N-nitrosoperhydroazepine, N-nitrosopiperidine, Pentachlorophenol, Perillyl alcohol, Reactive Oxygen Species, chromium hexavalent ion, dimethoxon, norathyriol, phosphates, plastochromanol 8, protein-bound polysaccharide K, Basidiomycetes, and usnic acid, with carbon implicated in its pathogenesis. Primary sclerosing cholangitis is classified as a type of sclerosing cholangitis. Sclerosing cholangitis presents with symptoms including abdominal pain, Down Syndrome, fatigue, hypergammaglobulinemia, jaundice, obstructive jaundice, and pruritus. It is associated with drugs such as 3,5-diethoxycarbonyl-1,4-dihydrocollidine, 24-norursodeoxycholic acid, Bezafibrate, Cisplatin, Danazol, Floxuridine, Fluorouracil, Fu-CF protocol, Linezolid, Methimazole, Minocycline, N-formylmethionyl-leucyl-tyrosine, Paclitaxel, Quinacrine, Sulfasalazine, Teicoplanin, Tobacco Smoke Pollution, Tretinoin, Trinitrobenzenesulfonic Acid, and Ursodeoxycholic acid, with calcium playing a role in its pathogenesis, and resembles ulcerative colitis. Clear-cell metastatic renal cell carcinoma is associated with arsenic.
181: Schistosomiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with several treatment drugs, including Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is treated with Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol. Urinary schistosomiasis, another form of the disease, is managed with Metrifonate. Urinary tract infections, which may be related or co-occurring, are associated with a broad range of antibiotics and antimicrobial agents such as Amoxicillin, Ciprofloxacin, Vancomycin, and many others, and are classified as urinary system diseases.
