It is classified as a brain disease and shares clinical similarities with migraine. Pharmacologically, epilepsy is linked to multiple drugs: several anticonvulsants and other agents such as diazepam, valproic acid, lamotrigine, gabapentin, pregabalin, and phenytoin are used in its management. Additionally, certain drugs are implicated in the pathogenesis of epilepsy, including choline, which plays a role in disease mechanisms. Other substances associated with epilepsy include antibiotics like ciprofloxacin and imipenem-cilastatin, antipsychotics, stimulants, and neurotoxic agents such as methylmercury compounds and phosphinothricin. The condition is also associated with metabolic and nutritional factors, including deficiencies in folic acid and pyridoxal phosphate.

Cysticercosis is classified as a form of taeniasis and is treated with drugs such as albendazole, praziquantel, dexamethasone, and other steroids. Taeniasis itself is a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease, indicating a broader category of parasitic infections involving tapeworms.
224: Parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease is classified as a parasitic infectious disease and is associated with multiple drugs, including Albendazole, Anthelmintics, Closantel, Ivermectin, Levamisole, Mebendazole, Netobimin, Niclosamide, Praziquantel, and Thiabendazole. Filariasis, a subtype of parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease, is linked to the drugs Acetylsalicylic acid and Levamisole. Schistosoma mansonii infection is associated with drugs such as 3,7-dimethyloctan-1-ol, Acetylcysteine, Artemether, IA 4, Nitric Oxide, Oxamniquine, and Praziquantel, and its pathogenesis involves triglycerides. Lymphedema, classified as a lymphatic system disease, is associated with the drugs Melphalan and Sirolimus, and its pathogenesis involves caprolactone; it is also linked to biological pathways including AKT phosphorylation of nuclear targets, focal adhesion, RAF/MAP kinase cascade, and signaling to p38 via RIT and RIN. Hydrocele, categorized as a male reproductive system disease, is associated with Phenol and Tetracycline. HIV infection is associated with the biological pathway of human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection. Fever is associated with a broad range of drugs, including 4,4'-diphenylmethane diisocyanate, Amoxicillin, Amphotericin B, Azithromycin, Azlocillin, Carmofur, Carvedilol, Ceftazidime, Chlorambucil, Clozapine, Cocaine, Cyclosporine, Cytarabine, DP 155, Docetaxel, Enkephalin, Leucine-2-Alanine, Flurbiprofen, Foscarnet, Glucosides, Glycine betaine, Ifosfamide, Insecticides, Irinotecan, Isotretinoin, Lincomycin, Lipopolysaccharides, Mefenamic acid, Melphalan, Mesalazine, Methylprednisolone, Meticillin, Nelfinavir, Nizatidine, Perphenazine, Phenacetin, Phenazopyridine, Phenmetrazine, Phenoxymethylpenicillin, Ritanserin, SCH 23390, Succinylcholine, Sulfasalazine, Tegafur, Trichloroethylene, carbabenzpyride, corticosteroids, glaucine, indoleacrylic acid, stronger neominophagen C, and ukrain, and its pathogenesis involves Hydroxyindoleacetic Acid.
225:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and dozens of deaths, and in Sri Lanka in 2017 with over 80,000 cases and 215 fatalities. Additional outbreaks were documented in Brazil, Pakistan, Bangladesh, the Netherlands, India, and other countries, with case counts ranging from a few to over half a million, and death tolls reaching several hundred in some instances. The disease has also been reported in countries such as Cameroon, El Salvador, Egypt, France, Indonesia, and the Caribbean, indicating a widespread global distribution. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its pathogenesis is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver. It is also linked to dengue hemorrhagic fever and dengue shock syndrome, both of which are considered subtypes of dengue disease.

Japanese Encephalitis has been reported in outbreaks in Malaysia, India, Thailand, and Australia, with case numbers ranging from 133 to 1,145 and death tolls from 15 to 296. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and has been documented in both endemic and emerging regions. The outbreaks have occurred in diverse geographic locations, including Southeast Asia and the Pacific, highlighting its regional and seasonal patterns.

Diarrhoea has been reported in Angola in 1998, with 560 confirmed cases and 12 deaths. The condition is classified as a gastrointestinal system disease and is associated with several drugs, including arsenic, atropine, neostigmine, and various antibiotics, some of which may contribute to its pathogenesis. The disease is also linked to the presence of specific metabolites such as alanine and aspartic acid.
226:
Hepatitis B is a viral infectious disease that presents with symptoms including fever, jaundice, proteinuria, purpura, skin manifestations, brain death, intellectual disability, hypergammaglobulinemia, and Down syndrome. It is associated with several drugs, including acyclovir, adefovir, lamivudine, tenofovir, emtricitabine, interferon alfa, peginterferon alfa-2a, and various nucleoside analogs, some of which are used in combination therapies. Certain drugs, such as hepatitis B antigens and core antigens, play a role in the disease’s pathogenesis, along with substances like acids and dextrose. The disease is linked to the hepatitis B biological pathway and is classified as a viral infectious disease. It resembles liver cancer, membranous glomerulonephritis, and primary biliary cholangitis in clinical or pathological features.

Tungiasis is classified as a parasitic ectoparasitic infectious disease.

Tetanus is a primary bacterial infectious disease. It is managed with drugs such as diazepam, magnesium sulfate, baclofen, midazolam, propofol, pancuronium, vecuronium bromide, labetalol, and tetanus toxin, which are used in treatment or management of symptoms.

Infection, as a general category, is associated with a broad range of drugs including antibiotics such as benzylpenicillin, ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, and vancomycin, antivirals like acyclovir and ritonavir, chemotherapeutic agents such as methotrexate and doxorubicin, immunosuppressants like cyclosporine and prednisone, and other agents including arsenic, cocaine, and silver sulfadiazine.
227:
Rift Valley Fever has been associated with multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Madagascar in 2008 with 418 cases and 17 deaths, Egypt in 2007 and 2008 with over 600 cases and more than 200 deaths in some instances, and repeated outbreaks in the United Republic of Tanzania, Yemen, Saudi Arabia, and other countries between 1999 and 2022, with case counts ranging from single cases to over 600 and death tolls reaching up to 109 in a single event. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological interventions associated with Rift Valley Fever include the drugs Galidesivir and Ribavirin.

Malaria has been reported in numerous outbreaks, including a large-scale event in Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, another in Pakistan in 2022 with 3.4 million cases, and ongoing occurrences in Ethiopia, United Republic of Tanzania, and other regions. The disease is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Symptoms associated with malaria include fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, and neurological manifestations, among others. Several drugs are associated with malaria treatment, including Artemisinin, Chloroquine, Quinine, Mefloquine, Atovaquone, Proguanil, and various combinations such as amodiaquine-artesunate and atovaquone-proguanil. Additionally, certain drugs are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, including cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. The disease is linked to the biological pathway known as Malaria.
228: Malaria has been associated with multiple outbreak events across several countries, including a significant outbreak in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which reported 616,034 cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998 and July 1999, and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. Another outbreak was reported in Ethiopia on November 1, 2024. The disease is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a range of clinical symptoms, including fever, chills, coma, unconsciousness, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, oliguria, pallor, pruritus, hypergammaglobulinemia, hemoglobinuria, cerebellar ataxia, birth weight abnormalities, fetal hypoxia, eye hemorrhage, consciousness disorders, neurologic manifestations, and jet lag syndrome. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or study of malaria, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, lumefantrine, atovaquone, proguanil, pyrimethamine, sulfonamides, and various drug combinations such as amodiaquine-artesunate and atovaquone-proguanil. Other agents linked to malaria include dapsone, doxycycline, tetracycline, triazoles, imipramine, cyproheptadine, ketotifen, and various natural compounds like diterpenes, quercetin, resveratrol, and borrelidin. The disease is also associated with biological pathways related to malaria itself, and certain substances such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin are implicated in its pathogenesis.
229:
Dengue Fever has been associated with multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and dozens of deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in countries such as Brazil, the Netherlands, India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, and the Philippines, with case counts ranging from a few dozen to over half a million. Notably, large-scale outbreaks were documented in 2002 in Brazil and in 2022 and 2023 in Bangladesh and other regions, with one event in 2024 reporting over 7.6 million cases. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, which are either studied in relation to the disease or observed in concurrent use. The condition is also linked to biological compounds such as F2-Isoprostanes, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, and various flavonoids like hinokiflavone and podocarpusflavone A.

Leptospirosis has been reported in multiple outbreaks, including in India, Malaysia, Canada, and Tanzania, with case numbers ranging from 6 to over 400 and fatalities reaching up to 48. The disease is categorized as a primary bacterial infectious disease. Outbreaks have occurred in both tropical and temperate regions, often following environmental exposures such as flooding or contact with contaminated water.

Meningitis has been documented in numerous outbreaks across Africa, Asia, and Europe, with particularly severe events in Burkina Faso, Nigeria, Egypt, and Ghana. Major outbreaks occurred in the 1990s and 2000s, with case counts exceeding 20,000 and death tolls reaching thousands in some instances. The disease is classified as a central nervous system disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including antibiotics such as ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, ampicillin, vancomycin, and meropenem, as well as corticosteroids and other agents like dexamethasone and ibuprofen. Bacterial meningitis, a subtype of meningitis, is specifically linked to antimicrobial agents such as amoxicillin, benzylpenicillin, and linezolid.

Zika fever has been reported in outbreaks across Latin America, the Caribbean, and parts of Asia and Africa, with notable events in Brazil, French Guiana, Panama, and India. Cases have ranged from single instances to over 8,000 in a single outbreak. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide, which are either used in treatment or studied in relation to the condition.

Kidney failure is associated with a broad spectrum of drugs, including acetaminophen, lithium, methimazole, and various antibiotics such as amikacin and gentamicins, some of which contribute to disease pathogenesis through mechanisms involving compounds like 8-epi-prostaglandin F2alpha and calcium phosphate. The condition is classified as a kidney disease. Jaundice is linked to numerous drugs, including anticonvulsants, antibiotics, antivirals, and statins, with some agents such as isoniazid, methotrexate, and amiodarone known to induce hepatotoxicity. The condition is associated with substances like bilirubin and perhexiline maleate, and is often a clinical manifestation of underlying liver dysfunction.
230:
Measles is a viral infectious disease caused by the measles virus, with documented outbreaks occurring in multiple countries across different time periods. Notable outbreaks include a large-scale event in Ethiopia in May 2022, reporting 16,814 cases and 182 deaths, and another in Madagascar in October 2018 with 19,539 cases and 39 deaths. Additional significant outbreaks were recorded in Egypt (2018 and 2019), Brazil (2018), Mexico (2020), Pakistan (2022), the Cook Islands (2019), the Central African Republic (2019), Sudan (2022), Japan (2018), the United States (2015), Thailand (2023), and the European Region (2019), with case counts ranging from single cases to over 40,000. The disease is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, rash, conjunctivitis, and neurological manifestations such as coma and unconsciousness. Biologically, measles is linked to the measles pathway and is classified as a viral infectious disease. Treatment and management involve antiviral agents and supportive care, with drugs such as ribavirin and immunoglobulins being used in some contexts.

Ebola hemorrhagic fever is a viral infectious disease caused by the Ebola virus, with multiple outbreaks reported primarily in Central and West Africa. Major outbreaks occurred in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, including events in 2018, 2019, and 2020, with case counts ranging from 53 to over 3,400 and death tolls exceeding 2,000 in several instances. Additional outbreaks were documented in Guinea Bissau (2014 and 2021), Sierra Leone (2014), and other regions such as Angola, the United Republic of Tanzania, and Cuvette Ouest region. The disease is characterized by severe hemorrhagic symptoms, high fever, and significant mortality, with case fatality rates often exceeding 50%. Ebola is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with the Ebola virus pathogenesis. Antiviral treatments such as Remdesivir, Favipiravir, Galidesivir, and Teicoplanin have been studied or used in clinical settings. The disease is also linked to the Ebola hemorrhagic fever pathway and has been reported in multiple countries across decades, indicating persistent transmission risks in endemic regions.

Malaria is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease caused by Plasmodium species, with widespread outbreaks reported globally. Notable events include a large outbreak in Pakistan in January 2022, involving 3.4 million cases, and a significant outbreak in Burundi in May 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths. Other outbreaks have been documented in Ethiopia, the United Republic of Tanzania (in 1998 and 1999), and more recently in 2024. The disease presents with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice, coma, retinal hemorrhage, and neurological complications such as cerebellar ataxia and unconsciousness. It is also associated with conditions like hemoglobinuria, oliguria, pallor, and purpura. Malaria is linked to the malaria pathway and is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Treatment involves antimalarial drugs such as chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemisinin-based combination therapies (e.g., artesunate, amodiaquine), quinine, and others. The disease's pathogenesis is influenced by factors such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin, and it is associated with various drugs used both therapeutically and in research.
231:
Leprosy is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is characterized by a wide range of symptoms, including ageusia, blindness, edema, eye manifestations, facial paralysis, fever, gait disorders, hyperesthesia, hypesthesia, muscle weakness, muscular atrophy, neuralgia, neurologic manifestations, oral manifestations, paralysis, paresthesia, abnormal reflexes, sensation disorders, skin manifestations, somatosensory disorders, and vision disorders. The condition is associated with several drugs, including chloroquine, clofazimine, dapsone, prednisolone, reactive oxygen species, rifampicin, thalidomide, and triptolide. Biological pathways linked to leprosy include antigen processing and presentation, cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity, protein processing in the endoplasmic reticulum, and Toll-like receptor signaling pathway.

Lepromatous leprosy, a form of leprosy, is categorized as a subtype of leprosy and is associated with the same drugs as leprosy, including clofazimine, dapsone, ethionamide, ofloxacin, prednisolone, prednisone, rifampicin, and thalidomide.
232: Rabies outbreaks have been reported in multiple instances, including an event in France on August 26, 2004, which resulted in one death. Additional outbreaks were recorded in unspecified locations on May 6, 1997, and April 10, 2024. Rabies is classified as a viral infectious disease. The drug cinnabarin is associated with rabies, indicating its use or study in relation to the condition.
233:
Blastomycosis is classified as a primary systemic mycosis and is associated with several antifungal drugs, including Amphotericin B, Fluconazole, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, and Miconazole.  

Inflammatory bowel disease is categorized as an intestinal disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including Azathioprine, Corticotropin, Curcumin, Dexamethasone, Dextran Sulfate, Glucosamine, Heparin, Leukotriene B4, Mercaptopurine, Mesalazine, Methotrexate, Metronidazole, Nabumetone, Naproxen, Olsalazine, Omeprazole, Polaprezinc, Prednisolone, Prednisone, Rifaximin, Rutin, Sulfasalazine, Tioguanine, Tobacco Smoke Pollution, Trinitrobenzenesulfonic Acid, Zileuton, and capsiate. The drug Arachidonic Acid plays a role in the disease’s pathogenesis. Biological pathways linked to inflammatory bowel disease include antigen processing and presentation, autophagy, cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, the JAK-STAT signaling pathway, and the disease itself.  

Cryptococcosis is classified as an opportunistic mycosis and is associated with antifungal agents such as Amphotericin B, Fluconazole, Flucytosine, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, amphotericin B, deoxycholate drug combination, and tebuconazole.  

Fungal infectious disease is defined as a disease caused by infectious agents and is associated with a broad spectrum of drugs, including Albaconazole, Amiodarone, Amphotericin B, Caspofungin, Clotrimazole, Dimyristoylphosphatidylcholine, ER 30346, Fluconazole, Flucytosine, Griseofulvin, Iron, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, Methotrexate, Micafungin, Miconazole, Natamycin, Nystatin, Posaconazole, Sertaconazole, Terbinafine, Voriconazole, amphotericin B, deoxycholate drug combination, borrelidin, climacostol, dimyristoylphosphatidylglycerol, liposomal amphotericin B, and palmarumycin JC2.

234: An outbreak of diarrhoea occurred in Angola on May 26, 1998, reporting 560 confirmed cases and 12 deaths. The disease is also linked to other gastrointestinal disorders, including cryptosporidiosis, which is considered a subtype of coccidiosis. 235:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including Malaysia, where outbreaks occurred in 1997 with over 11,000 cases and 28 deaths, and again in 1997 with 19,544 cases and 50 deaths. Significant outbreaks were also documented in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, in Brazil in 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths, and in The Netherlands in 2004 with over 40,000 cases and 507 deaths. Other notable events include outbreaks in Pakistan in 2019 with 47,120 cases and 75 deaths, in India in 1996 with over 8,000 cases and 319 deaths, and in East Timor in 2005 with multiple incidents involving over 100 cases and several deaths. A large-scale outbreak was reported in 2023 in an unspecified country with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths, and another in 2024 with 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in countries such as Cameroon, Barbados, El Salvador, Egypt, France, Indonesia, South America, and others, with varying case counts and mortality rates.

Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. It is also recognized as a subtype of dengue disease, which itself is a broader category of viral infection. The condition has been associated with a range of drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, Doxycycline, F2-Isoprostanes, Hydroxychloroquine, Hydroxyeicosatetraenoic Acids, Rolitetracycline, Silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A, which are either studied in relation to the disease or observed in concurrent use. Additionally, filariasis, a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease, is associated with the drugs Acetylsalicylic acid and Levamisole, though this relationship is not directly linked to Dengue Fever.
236: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999, Pakistan in 2022 with 3.4 million confirmed cases, and Ethiopia in 2024. The disease is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, coma, unconsciousness, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, retinal and eye hemorrhage, pallor, purpura, oliguria, hypergammaglobulinemia, cerebellar ataxia, and neurological manifestations. It is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Key drugs used in its treatment include chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, lumefantrine, mefloquine, primaquine, pyrimethamine, atovaquone, and various combinations such as atovaquone-proguanil and mefloquine-sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine. Some drugs are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, including cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. The disease is linked to the Malaria biological pathway.
237: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths, and another outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998. Additional outbreaks were documented in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with the latter reporting 3,400,000 cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is also considered a subtype of parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The disease is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, coma, unconsciousness, jaundice, hemoglobinuria, retinal hemorrhage, oliguria, pallor, pruritus, purpura, vomiting, diarrhea, and neurologic manifestations. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or study of malaria, including Amodiaquine, Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, Artemisinin, Artesunate, Mefloquine, Primaquine, Pyrimethamine, Atovaquone, Proguanil, and Quinine. Some of these drugs, such as chloroquine diphosphate and mefloquine-sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, are used in combination therapies. The disease is also linked to specific biological pathways, notably the malaria pathway. Furthermore, malaria shares clinical and pathological similarities with other conditions, including anemia, celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, and thymus cancer. The pathogenesis of malaria involves metabolic factors such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin.

An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis. The disease is associated with a range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Miltefosine, Meglumine antimoniate, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Paromomycin, Pentamidine, Allopurinol, and various antiprotozoals. Other compounds linked to treatment or study include 2,5-bis(2-(2-propoxy)-4-(2-pyridylimino)aminophenyl)furan, "2-(2''-dichloroacetamidobenzyl)-3-(3'-indolylquinoline)", Desmodium gangeticum extract, and reactive oxygen species. 

Diarrhoea was reported in Angola on May 26, 1998, with 560 confirmed cases and 12 deaths. Diarrhoea is categorized as a gastrointestinal system disease. It is associated with multiple drugs, including Atropine, Cimetidine, Cisplatin, Metformin, and various antidiarrheal agents, and is linked to the pathogenesis of amino acids such as Alanine and Aspartic acid, as well as Chlorides. The condition is also associated with compounds like lipopolysaccharide from Escherichia coli O111 B4 and organophosphorus compounds.
239: Outbreaks of diarrhoea have been documented in multiple regions, including an event in Angola in May 1998 involving 560 confirmed cases and 12 deaths. Cholera, caused by *Vibrio cholerae*, has been associated with numerous outbreaks across various countries and time periods, including significant events in the Republic of Panama in November 2010 with 60,240 cases and 1,415 deaths, in United Republic of Tanzania in June 2009 with 98,424 cases and 4,276 deaths, and in Sierra Leone in September 2012 with over 18,000 cases and 271 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in countries such as Mozambique, Congo, Somalia, Nigeria, and Iraq, with case counts ranging from a few to tens of thousands and death tolls varying accordingly. *V. cholerae* has also been linked to outbreaks in Zambia, Cameroon, Egypt, Pakistan, and other regions, with some events reporting high mortality and others limited to a few cases. The disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway of *Vibrio cholerae* infection. Diarrhoea is recognized as a subtype of gastrointestinal system disease, and acute diarrhoea is considered a form of diarrhoea. In terms of pharmacological associations, several drugs are noted to be associated with diarrhoea, including amoxicillin, metronidazole, omeprazole, lansoprazole, and various antibiotics and antivirals. Additionally, certain drugs such as alanine and aspartic acid are implicated in the pathogenesis of diarrhoea. The cholera vaccine CVD 103-HgR is specifically associated with the management of cholera. Vomiting is linked to a range of drugs, including metoclopramide, scopolamine, and various chemotherapeutic agents, with alanine also noted as a contributing factor in its pathogenesis.
240: Infection is associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, clarithromycin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, etoposide, everolimus, exisulind, FEC protocol, fludarabine, itraconazole, LNH 87 protocol, lithium, MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, metronidazole, netilmicin, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, TAC protocol, tetrandrine, tobramycin, topotecan, triamcinolone, VIP-E protocol, vancomycin, and asulacrine, as well as liposomal doxorubicin. These drugs are often observed or studied concurrently with infection.

Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The condition is associated with the use of the drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole.

Trypanosomiasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with the use of antimony, arsenic compounds, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, pentamidine, and borrelidin.
242:
Parasitemia is associated with several drugs, including Artesunate, Chloroquine, Quinine, cobaltiprotoporphyrin, and the fanasil-pyrimethamine drug combination, which are often studied or used in its treatment. Additionally, Creatinine is implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. 

Trypanosomiasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as Antimony, Arsenic compounds, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, Pentamidine, and Borrelidin.
243:
Human granulocytic anaplasmosis is classified as a type of ehrlichiosis and is also categorized as a hematopoietic system disease. It shares clinical similarities with celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, malaria, and thymus cancer. The condition is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, fatigue, headache, abdominal pain, jaundice, purpura, skin manifestations, neurologic manifestations, and anemia-related signs such as pallor, reticulocytosis, and hemoglobinuria. Specific symptoms include acute pain, cyanosis, diarrhea, vitreous hemorrhage, and eye manifestations. 

Several drugs are associated with the disease, including acetaminophen, amiodarone, carbamazepine, hydrochlorothiazide, methyldopa, tacrolimus, and various chemotherapeutic agents such as CHOP, EPOCH, and FAM protocols. Some drugs are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis, including alanine, homocysteine, isoprostanes, mannitol, tocopherol, and ubidecarenone. Additionally, certain antibiotics such as amikacin, ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, and vancomycin are associated with the condition, and creatinine has been linked to its pathogenesis. The disease is also associated with exposure to environmental agents such as air pollutants, petroleum, and plant resins.
244:
Epilepsy is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including seizures, confusion, memory disorders, amnesia (both anterograde and retrograde), transient global amnesia, aphasia, coma, stupor, unconsciousness, sleep disorders such as sleep apnea and parasomnias, motor disturbances like dystonia, chorea, athetosis, and myoclonus, as well as cognitive and behavioral manifestations such as intellectual disability, learning disorders, and psychomotor disorders. Additional symptoms include fever, lethargy, hallucinations, illusions, and various neurological conditions such as cerebral hypoxia, neuroacanthocytosis, and spinocerebellar ataxias.

Several drugs are associated with epilepsy, either as treatments or as agents involved in its pathogenesis. Anticonvulsants, including carbamazepine, phenytoin, valproic acid, levetiracetam, pregabalin, zonisamide, and phenobarbital, are commonly used in management. Other drugs linked to epilepsy include clonazepam, diazepam, midazolam, nitrazepam, and dextroamphetamine. Some drugs, such as cisplatin, penicillamine, and erythromycin, are associated with the condition, while others like choline are implicated in its disease mechanisms. The drug combination of carbidopa and levodopa, as well as agents like didepil, are also noted in relation to epilepsy.

Epilepsy is classified as a type of brain disease and shares clinical and pathological similarities with migraine, particularly in terms of neurological symptomatology and potential underlying mechanisms.
245:
Outbreaks of Zika virus have been documented across multiple countries between 2015 and 2021, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, Brazil, Argentina, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Cape Verde, India, and others. Notable events include a large outbreak in French Guiana in November 2015 with 8,750 confirmed cases, and a significant outbreak in Brazil in January 2016 with 3,174 cases and 38 deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in various regions with case counts ranging from 1 to 70, including a 2021 outbreak in India with 70 cases. Zika virus is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological agents associated with Zika virus include Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and Niclosamide.

Outbreaks of Dengue Fever have occurred in numerous countries across Asia, Africa, South America, and the Caribbean, with major events reported in Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Brazil, Pakistan, The Netherlands, Cameroon, Cape Verde, East Timor, India, and others. Notable outbreaks include a 1997 event in Malaysia with 11,328 cases and 28 deaths, a 2017 outbreak in Sri Lanka with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, and a 2023 outbreak in an unspecified location with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths. A particularly severe outbreak in 2024 affected an unspecified region with 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with Dengue Fever include 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, Doxycycline, F2-Isoprostanes, Hydroxychloroquine, Hydroxyeicosatetraenoic Acids, Rolitetracycline, Silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of Dengue Fever.

Outbreaks of Chikungunya virus disease have been reported in countries including the United Republic of Tanzania, United States, Congo, Cameroon, France, Chad, Reunion, St Martin, Italy, Spain, Argentina, and Egypt. Notable events include a 2020 outbreak in Chad with 27,540 cases and one death, a 2019 outbreak in Congo with 6,149 cases, and a 2005 outbreak in Reunion Island with 3,115 cases. Chikungunya virus disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with Chikungunya include Chloroquine.

Ulcerative colitis presents with symptoms such as acute abdomen, abdominal pain, arthralgia, colic, constipation, diarrhea, dyspepsia, eye manifestations, fever, flatulence, nausea, vomiting, purpura, skin manifestations, and failure to thrive. Leprosy is characterized by symptoms such as ageusia, blindness, edema, eye manifestations, facial paralysis, fever, gait disorders, hyperesthesia, hypesthesia, muscle weakness, muscular atrophy, neuralgia, neurologic manifestations, oral manifestations, paralysis, paresthesia, abnormal reflexes, sensation disorders, skin manifestations, somatosensory disorders, and vision disorders. The disease is linked to pathways such as Antigen processing and presentation, Cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, Natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity, Protein processing in endoplasmic reticulum, and Toll-like receptor signaling pathway. Crohn’s disease presents with symptoms including acute abdomen, abdominal pain, arthralgia, body weight changes, colic, constipation, diarrhea, dyspepsia, edema, eructation, failure to thrive, fever, low back pain, nausea, oral manifestations, pain, postoperative pain, pruritus ani, psychophysiologic disorders, purpura, skin manifestations, tetany, and vomiting. It is associated with drugs such as Acetaminophen, Ampicillin, Azathioprine, Aztreonam, BIRB 796, Budesonide, Chlordiazepoxide, Ciprofloxacin, Clidinium, Fusidic acid, Heparin, Hydrocortisone, Immunosuppressants, Linezolid, Mercaptopurine, Mesalazine, Methotrexate, Methylprednisolone, Metronidazole, Prednisolone, Prednisone, Steroids, Sulfasalazine, Thalidomide, Tioguanine, and corticosteroids, with Malonaldehyde contributing to its pathogenesis. It is classified as a type of inflammatory bowel disease and resembles ankylosing spondylitis, celiac disease, colon cancer, ileum cancer, jejunal cancer, psoriatic arthritis, sclerosing cholangitis, and ulcerative colitis. Crohn’s colitis is a subtype of Crohn’s disease. Lepromatous leprosy is a form of leprosy and is associated with drugs including Clofazimine, Dapsone, Ethionamide, Ofloxacin, Prednisolone, Prednisone, Rifampicin, and Thalidomide.
248:
Dengue Fever has been associated with numerous outbreaks across multiple countries and time periods, including large-scale events in Malaysia in 1996 and 1997 with over 2,000 and 11,000 cases respectively, and in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths. Significant outbreaks have also been reported in Brazil in 2002 and 2008, with over 300,000 and 120,000 cases, respectively, and in India during multiple years, including 1996 and 2003, with case counts ranging from several thousand to over 8,000. Other notable outbreaks occurred in the Netherlands in 2004, with more than 58,000 cases and over 600 deaths, and in Mayotte in 2020 with 3,533 cases and 16 deaths. Additional outbreaks have been documented in East Timor, Barbados, El Salvador, Egypt, France, Thailand, Pakistan, and other regions, with case numbers varying from a few to tens of thousands and death tolls ranging from single digits to over 700. A particularly severe outbreak was reported in 2024 with 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths, though the specific country was not specified. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is further categorized into subtypes such as dengue hemorrhagic fever and dengue shock syndrome, both of which are considered forms of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, among others. Additionally, compounds such as F2-Isoprostanes, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, and various flavonoids have been linked to the disease. 

Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome has been documented in the Republic of Panama in 2000, with 12 confirmed cases and 3 deaths. It is classified as a viral infectious disease, and the drug nitrates have been identified as playing a role in its disease pathogenesis.
249: An outbreak of filariasis was reported, and the condition is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. 275:
An outbreak of Staphylococcal infection occurred in Japan on June 19, 1997, and another on July 10, 2000, with the latter reporting 13,809 confirmed cases.

Staphylococcal infection is classified as a bacterial infectious disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including amoxicillin, ampicillin, ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, clindamycin, nafcillin, and others, which are used in its treatment or studied in relation to its management. The disease is also linked to the role of calcium in its pathogenesis. Additionally, Staphylococcal infection falls under the broader category of diseases caused by infectious agents.
276: Intestinal schistosomiasis is classified as a type of schistosomiasis, which in turn is a subtype of parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The condition is associated with the use of drugs including Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol. Schistosomiasis is also linked to the drug Metrifonate, and urinary schistosomiasis, a form of schistosomiasis, is associated with Metrifonate as well.
277:
An infection is classified as an integumentary system disease and has been associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, carboplatin, ciprofloxacin, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, dexamethasone, doxorubicin, methotrexate, vancomycin, and others. Additionally, certain drugs such as aminolevulinic acid, apomorphine, bexarotene, bleomycin, and hydroxychloroquine are linked to skin disease, with ceramides implicated in the disease pathogenesis. The condition is also associated with exposure to toxic agents like mustard gas, styrene, and 2-chloroethyl ethyl sulfide. 

Onchocerciasis and loiasis are both classified as filariasis, with onchocerciasis being associated with drugs such as abamectin, albendazole, diethylcarbamazine, and ivermectin, while loiasis is linked to diethylcarbamazine and ivermectin. 

Orthostatic hypotension is associated with various drugs, including angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, beta blockers, antipsychotics, antidepressants, and antihypertensives such as losartan and doxazosin. The pathogenesis of this condition involves norepinephrine, and several drugs, including apomorphine, dopamine agonists, and certain antipsychotics, are known to influence this pathway.
278:
Tuberculosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including Amikacin, Amoxicillin-Potassium Clavulanate Combination, Bedaquiline, Capreomycin, Ciprofloxacin, Cycloserine, Ethambutol, Ethionamide, Gentamicins, Isoniazid, Kanamycin, Pyrazinamide, Rifampicin, Streptomycin, and Terizidone, among others. Some drugs, such as Lopinavir, Phosphorus, and phosphates, are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. The condition is linked to the tuberculosis pathway. 

Borderline leprosy is a subtype of leprosy and is associated with drugs including Clofazimine, Dapsone, Prednisolone, and Rifampicin. 

Granuloma is associated with numerous drugs, such as Acetaminophen, Allopurinol, Aluminum, Amoxicillin-Potassium Clavulanate Combination, Dexamethasone, Diltiazem, Indomethacin, Methadone, Methylprednisolone Acetate, Naloxone, Nitrofurantoin, Ofloxacin, Phenylbutazone, Phenytoin, Prednisolone, Pyrazinamide, Quinidine, Quinine, Sulfasalazine, and Zymosan, among others. 

Necrosis is associated with a broad range of drugs, including Acetaldehyde, Amiodarone, Azathioprine, Cyclosporine, Dexamethasone, Dizocilpine maleate, Dopamine, Glutathione, Nelfinavir, Oxaliplatin, Propoxur, Propylthiouracil, Puromycin Aminonucleoside, Reactive Oxygen Species, S-Nitroso-N-Acetylpenicillamine, Silver, Sodium selenite, Tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, and Zoledronic acid, with Magnesium also implicated in its pathogenesis. 

Buruli ulcer disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. 

Edema is associated with drugs such as Atenolol, Bicuculline, Clofibrate, Cocaine, Crizotinib, Curcumin, Cytarabine, Dibenzothiophene, Edaravone, Estramustine, Etoricoxib, Felodipine, Gentamicins, Glycopyrrolate, Hispidulin, Icariin, Iron Dextran, Ketamine, MK-886, MK-8825, Mannitol, Mefenamic acid, Naproxen, Nicardipine, Nimesulide, Phenol, Propofol, Rofecoxib, Ruthenium Red, Semaxanib, Silver, Sulfasalazine, Thiazolidines, Ticlopidine, Verlukast, and various others. 

Vasculitis is a vascular disease and is associated with drugs including Aceclofenac, Amphetamine, Azathioprine, Betaxolol, Carbimazole, Cefotaxime, Ciprofloxacin, Clindamycin, Cocaine, Crack Cocaine, Cytarabine, Dapsone, Dinoprostone, Enalapril, Fluoxetine, Flurbiprofen, Food Additives, Gemcitabine, Glimepiride, Heparin, Hydrochlorothiazide, Imatinib Mesylate, Imiquimod, Isoniazid, Lisinopril, Melphalan, Mercuric chloride, Metamfetamine, Minocycline, Nifedipine, Nitrofurantoin, Nitroprusside, Paroxetine, Penicillamine, Phenprocoumon, Phenylbutazone, Pramipexole, Prednisolone, Procainamide, Quinidine, Quinine, Ramipril, Ribavirin, Rifampicin, Tacrolimus, Tamoxifen, Temozolomide, Tretinoin, Warfarin, and corticosteroids. 

Systemic vasculitis is associated with drugs such as Amphetamines, Cladribine, Cocaine, Diflunisal, Methotrexate, Methyldopa, Phenytoin, Propylthiouracil, and glucocorticoids. 

Leprosy, a primary bacterial infectious disease, presents with symptoms including ageusia, blindness, edema, eye manifestations, facial paralysis, fever, gait disorders, hyperesthesia, hypesthesia, muscle weakness, muscular atrophy, neuralgia, neurologic manifestations, oral manifestations, paralysis, paresthesia, abnormal reflexes, sensation disorders, skin manifestations, somatosensory disorders, and vision disorders. It is associated with drugs such as Chloroquine, Clofazimine, Dapsone, Prednisolone, Reactive Oxygen Species, Rifampicin, Thalidomide, and Triptolide. The disease is linked to biological pathways including antigen processing and presentation, cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity, protein processing in the endoplasmic reticulum, and Toll-like receptor signaling pathway. 

Lepromatous leprosy, a subtype of leprosy, is associated with drugs including Clofazimine, Dapsone, Ethionamide, Ofloxacin, Prednisolone, Prednisone, Rifampicin, and Thalidomide.
279: An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan in January 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks in May and June of the same year, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with multiple therapeutic agents, including Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, and various antiprotozoals. Other drugs linked to its treatment include liposomal amphotericin B, artemisinin, and several natural compounds such as mammea A-BB and amarogentin. The disease is also associated with the Leishmaniasis pathway.
280: Leishmaniasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, Atovaquone, Nelfinavir, Nitric Oxide, Tafenoquine, Marbofloxacin, Maytansine, Trifluralin, and various compounds such as 5,7-dihydroxy-8-(2-methylbutanoyl)-6-(3-methylbutyl)-4-phenyl-chroman-2-one, methyl gallate, fenarimol, mammea A-BB, pistagremic acid, oil of Chenopodium, and several other antiprotozoals, arsenic compounds, chromans, and N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine derivatives. The disease is linked to the biological pathway associated with leishmaniasis.
281:
An outbreak of Plasmodium occurred in Barbados on February 9, 2007, with 280 confirmed cases. Malaria has been reported in multiple locations, including the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998, Burundi on May 25, 1999, with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, Ethiopia in November 2024, and Pakistan on January 1, 2022, where 3,400,000 cases were confirmed. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with symptoms such as fever, chills, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, pallor, purpura, retinal hemorrhage, and neurological manifestations. Key drugs associated with malaria treatment include chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, primaquine, atovaquone, doxycycline, and sulfonamides. Some of these drugs, such as chloroquine and quinine, are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, along with cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. The disease is linked to the Malaria pathway and shares characteristics with conditions such as anemia, celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, and thymus cancer.

Hepatitis is associated with a broad range of drugs, including azathioprine, acetaminophen, methotrexate, and various antivirals, and is classified as a liver disease. Hepatitis C is specifically linked to the Hepatitis C pathway and is categorized as a viral infectious disease. It is associated with drugs such as ribavirin, interferon alfa, sofosbuvir, ledipasvir, daclatasvir, and others, with alanine implicated in its pathogenesis. Hepatitis B is also a viral infectious disease and is associated with symptoms like jaundice, fever, proteinuria, and purpura. It is linked to drugs such as entecavir, tenofovir, interferon alfa, and hepatitis B vaccines, with hepatitis B antigens and acids playing a role in its pathogenesis. Hepatitis B resembles liver cancer, membranous glomerulonephritis, and primary biliary cholangitis.

Syphilis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as arsenic, doxycycline, and metamfetamine. HIV infection is linked to the Human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection pathway. Anemia is categorized as a hematopoietic system disease and resembles celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, malaria, and thymus cancer. It presents with symptoms including fatigue, pallor, jaundice, diarrhea, fever, and neurological manifestations, and is associated with drugs such as iron supplements, erythropoietin, and various chemotherapeutics. Alanine is also implicated in its pathogenesis. Infection is associated with a wide range of drugs, including antibiotics, antivirals, and chemotherapeutic agents, such as ceftriaxone, doxorubicin, and cyclophosphamide.
282:
An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. These events highlight a significant and sustained burden of the disease in the region during that period.

Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis. Several drugs are associated with its treatment or study, including Allopurinol, Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Antimony potassium tartrate, Antimony sodium tartrate, Antiprotozoals, Artemisinin, Desmodium gangeticum extract, Isosorbide dinitrate, Ketoconazole, Meglumine, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Neopterin, Organotin Compounds, Paromomycin, Pentamidine, Reactive Oxygen Species, Thalidomide, Val-Glu-Pro-Ile-Gly-Tyr, amarogentin, amphotericin B, deoxycholate drug combination, and liposomal amphotericin B. Additionally, various other compounds such as 2,5-bis(2-(2-propoxy)-4-(2-pyridylimino)aminophenyl)furan, 2-(2''-dichloroacetamidobenzyl)-3-(3'-indolylquinoline), and numerous environmental and pharmaceutical agents including amiodarone, aniline, anticonvulsants, arsenic trioxide, azathioprine, BCG vaccine, CEP 33779, carbamazepine, cefaclor, cefodizime, ciprofloxacin, curcumin, dextran sulfate, diuron, environmental pollutants, ferric chloride, geraniol, guanidines, hydroxyacetylaminofluorene, irinotecan, L 695256, lead acetate, manganese chloride, mequindox, methoxychlor, methyldopa, minocycline, pentachlorophenol, phenytoin, protein kinase inhibitors, quinidine, resveratrol, sulfasalazine, tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, tiaguanine, trastuzumab emtansine, trinitrobenzenesulfonic acid, urethane, vincristine, vitamin E, bromfenvinphos, ceric oxide, chromium trioxide, dimethylglycine, lead nitrate, nickel chloride, sodium arsenite, and tiazofurin have been linked to the condition.
283:
Lassa Fever has been documented in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Angola, Sierra Leone, Nigeria, Benin, Ghana, Guinea Bissau, and various European nations such as Germany, the Netherlands, the United Kingdom, and Sweden. Notable outbreaks include a significant event in Nigeria in 2018 with over 1,800 cases and 114 deaths, another in 2023 with 4,702 cases and 152 deaths, and a 1996 outbreak in Sierra Leone with 799 cases and 148 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in 2016 in Benin with 71 cases and 23 deaths, and in 2022 in Ghana with one case and one death. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Ribavirin is associated with the treatment of Lassa Fever.

Ebola hemorrhagic fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks, primarily in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Sierra Leone, and Guinea Bissau. Major outbreaks include a 2018 event in the Congo with 308 cases and 189 deaths, a 2019 outbreak with 1,945 cases and 1,302 deaths, and a 2020 event with 3,462 cases and 2,279 deaths. In Sierra Leone, outbreaks in 2014 involved over 3,000 cases and 1,552 deaths. Additional cases were reported in 2014 in Guinea Bissau and in 2021 in the Congo with 12 cases and 6 deaths. Ebola hemorrhagic fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. Antiviral drugs such as Favipiravir, Galidesivir, Remdesivir, and Teicoplanin are associated with the disease.

Marburg virus disease has been reported in Angola, the Congo, and the United Republic of Tanzania. A major outbreak in Angola in 2005 resulted in 374 cases and 329 deaths. Smaller outbreaks occurred in the Congo in 2000 and in Tanzania in 2007, each with one confirmed case.

Hemorrhagic fever has been documented in multiple regions, including the United Republic of Tanzania, Congo, Sudan, Gabon, and the Republic of the Congo. Outbreaks in Tanzania have included cases with high mortality, such as one in 2000 with 160 cases and 55 deaths. In Congo, multiple outbreaks occurred between 1996 and 2012, with case counts ranging from 14 to 61 and death tolls from 4 to 45. Additional outbreaks were reported in 2003 and 2004 in Sudan and in 2001 in Gabon and the Republic of the Congo.

Myocarditis has been reported in Sri Lanka and Greece, with a 2005 outbreak in Sri Lanka and a 2002 event in Greece involving 39 cases and 3 deaths. The condition is classified as an extrinsic cardiomyopathy. Drugs associated with myocarditis include amphetamines, anthracyclines, antipsychotics, and various chemotherapeutic agents. Some drugs, such as lithium and nicotine, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis.

Dilated cardiomyopathy is classified as an intrinsic cardiomyopathy. It is associated with symptoms such as dyspnea, chest pain, edema, syncope, and heart murmurs. Drugs linked to the condition include angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, anthracyclines, cocaine, and digitalis glycosides. Some drugs contribute to pathogenesis through mechanisms involving creatinine and malonaldehyde.

Thrombocytopenia is classified as a blood platelet disease. It is associated with drugs such as chemotherapy agents, anticoagulants, and antibiotics. Some drugs, including aspartic acid, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. The condition is linked to the tight junction pathway.

Encephalitis is classified as a brain disease. It is associated with drugs such as antivirals, anticonvulsants, and immunosuppressants. The TCR signaling pathway is linked to the disease.

Disseminated intravascular coagulation is classified as a thrombophilia. It is associated with drugs such as anticoagulants, antiplatelets, and chemotherapeutic agents.

Hypoproteinemia is associated with drugs such as chemotherapeutics and environmental toxins.

Dehydration is associated with drugs such as diuretics, chemotherapy agents, and antibiotics.

Cerebellar ataxia is classified as a hereditary ataxia. It is associated with drugs such as anticonvulsants, antipsychotics, and chemotherapeutic agents. Calcium is implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis.

Lymphopenia is classified as a type of leukopenia. It is associated with drugs such as chemotherapy agents, immunosuppressants, and environmental toxins. Creatinine and zinc are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis.

Viremia is associated with drugs such as antivirals and immunomodulators. Alanine is implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis.

Hepatic infarction is classified as a hepatic vascular disease.

Dyspnea is associated with drugs such as antihypertensives, antipsychotics, and chemotherapeutic agents.

Hematuria is associated with drugs such as anticoagulants, antibiotics, and chemotherapeutic agents.

Myocarditis is associated with drugs such as antipsychotics, anticonvulsants, and chemotherapeutic agents. Some drugs, such as creatinine and malonaldehyde, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis.
284:
Infection is associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, clarithromycin, clindamycin, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, cytarabine, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, doxorubicin, epirubicin, etoposide, everolimus, exisulind, FEC protocol, fludarabine, itraconazole, LNH 87 protocol, lithium, MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, metronidazole, netilmicin, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, sirolimus, TAC protocol, tetrandrine, tobramycin, topotecan, triamcinolone, vancomycin, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin.

285: Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole are associated with this condition, indicating their use or study in relation to filariasis.
286: Chagas disease is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, Benznidazole, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Nifurtimox, and Nitrofural, which are studied or used in its treatment. 288: Leptospirosis has been reported in multiple outbreaks across different regions, including an event in the United Republic of Tanzania in August 2022 with 20 confirmed cases and 3 deaths, another in India in October 1997 involving 413 cases and 48 deaths, a case in Malaysia in September 2000 with 37 confirmed cases, a prior outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania in June 2004 with 141 cases and 6 deaths, and a smaller outbreak in Canada in September 2000 with 6 confirmed cases. Leptospirosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease.
289:

                        Thrombocytopenia is categorized as a blood platelet disease and is associated with a range of drugs, such as 1-(5-(4-chlorophenyl)-4-(4-pyridinyl)-1H-pyrazol-3-yl)-4-methylpiperazine, Amrinone, Aspartame, Auranofin, BMS 181339, Bivalirudin, CMF regimen, Carbamazepine, Ciprofloxacin, Clorazepate Dipotassium, Cocaine, Colchicine, DAV regimen, Dacarbazine, Dalteparin, Danazol, Daunorubicin, Desmopressin, Dexamethasone, Diclofenac, Doxifluridine, EPOCH protocol, Epinephrine, Ethambutol, Etoposide, Fluindione, GDC-0152, Heme, Ibuprofen, Isoniazid, Lepirudin, Methylprednisolone, Mitoxantrone, Nadroparin, Nedaplatin, PAVE protocol 2, PROMACE-CytaBOM protocol, Peginterferon alfa-2b, Phenytoin, Procarbazine, Pyrazinamide, Ranitidine, Ribavirin, Rifampicin, Streptozocin, Tamoxifen, Temozolomide, Venlafaxine Hydrochloride, Vorinostat, and granulocyte colony-stimulating factor.
