290: Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with the drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole. Infection, a broad category of disease, is linked to a wide range of drugs including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, bortezomib, the COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, clarithromycin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, cytarabine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, doxorubicin, epirubicin, everolimus, the FEC protocol, fludarabine, itraconazole, the LNH 87 protocol, the MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, sirolimus, the TAC protocol, tetrandrine, tobramycin, topotecan, triamcinolone, the VIP-E protocol, vancomycin, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin. Schistosomiasis is associated with the drugs metrifonate and praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is linked to praziquantel, quercetin, and resveratrol. Trachoma is classified as a commensal bacterial infectious disease. Hookworm disease is associated with the drugs albendazole, dichlorvos, ivermectin, oxantel pamoate, and tribendimidine.
291:
Skin disease is classified as an integumentary system disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including Amcinonide, Amiodarone, Arsenic, Beraprost, Beta Blocking Agents, Bleomycin, Busulfan, Chloroquine, Codeine, Colchicine, Contrast Media, Cyanamide, Cyclophosphamide, Cyproterone acetate with ethinyl estradiol, Dabrafenib, Dexamethasone, Disulfiram, Doxycycline, Everolimus, Folic acid, Fuel Oils, Geraniol, Hydrocortisone butyrate, Hydroxyurea, Indomethacin, Leucovorin, Mechlorethamine, Melphalan, Mepivacaine, Metamfetamine, Methyl aminolevulinate, Monobenzone, N-(4-chlorophenyl)-1-((3-(furan-2-yl)phenyl)carbonyl)piperidine-3-carboxamide, Paclitaxel, Penicillamine, Phenelzine, Plant Preparations, Pyridoxine, Selenium, Sodium aurothiomalate, Tazarotene, Thalidomide, Tocainide, Triamcinolone Acetonide, Triclosan, Turmeric, Vemurafenib, alternariol, chloroquine diphosphate, and glyderinine. Some of these drugs, such as Ceramides, are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. 

Cutaneous leishmaniasis and mucocutaneous leishmaniasis are both classified as types of leishmaniasis, which itself is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Cutaneous leishmaniasis is associated with drugs including 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, Allopurinol, Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Antiprotozoals, Gentamicins, Indomethacin, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, Meglumine, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Omeprazole, Paromomycin, Pentamidine, Pentoxifylline, Sodium chloride, WR279396, Zinc sulfate, liposomal amphotericin B, and mammea A-BB. Mucocutaneous leishmaniasis is associated with Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Antiprotozoals, Meglumine antimoniate, Pentamidine, and Pentoxifylline. Leishmaniasis is also linked to the biological pathway associated with the disease.

Psoriasis is a skin disease that presents with symptoms such as arthralgia, back pain, chills, diarrhea, Down Syndrome, edema, facial pain, fatigue, fever, flushing, hypergammaglobulinemia, monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance, nausea, neck pain, neurogenic inflammation, oral manifestations, pain, pruritus, pruritus ani, pruritus vulvae, psychophysiologic disorders, purpura, skin manifestations, tetany, and tinea pedis. It is associated with drugs including 1 alpha,24-dihydroxyvitamin D3, 12,20-dihydroxy-5,8,10,14-eicosatetraenoic acid, Adapalene, Amcinonide, Anthralin, Azaribine, Azathioprine, Azelaic acid, Benoxaprofen, Bergapten, Betamethasone, Calcipotriol, Cholesterol, LDL, Clobetasone butyrate, Colchicine, Cyclosporine, Dermatologicals, Desonide, Etretinate, Fatty Acids, Fluocinonide, Folic acid, Free Radicals, Fumarates, Hydroxychloroquine, Imiquimod, Laurocapram, Leukotriene B4, Lithium, Lithium carbonate, Methotrexate, Minocycline, Mycophenolic acid, Oxymatrine, Phenylbutazone, Prednisolone, R 115866, Razoxane, Sotrastaurin, Steroids, Sulfasalazine, Tacrolimus, Tamibarotene, Tazarotene, Tetracycline, Tofacitinib, Trioxsalen, Uridine, betamethasone-17,21-dipropionate, and corticosteroids. The drug Cyclic GMP is involved in the disease's pathogenesis. Psoriasis resembles psoriatic arthritis, rheumatoid arthritis, skin cancer, systemic lupus erythematosus, systemic scleroderma, and vitiligo.

Eczema is associated with a broad range of drugs, including 2-(6-((3-(4-(diphenylmethoxy)piperidino)propyl)amino)imidazo(1,2-b)pyridazin-2-yl)-2-methylpropionic acid, Agent Orange, Air Pollutants, Alitretinoin, Amcinonide, Benomyl, Bromothalonil, Carbamazepine, Carbaryl, Chloral hydrate, Dexamethasone, Diethylhexyl Phthalate, Dimethoate, Dinitrobenzenes, Formaldehyde, Halcinonide, Hydrocortisone, Lead, Maneb, Methoxsalen, Nickel sulfate, Nitrogen Dioxide, Nitrogen Oxides, Particulate Matter, Phenylmercuric acetate, Phylloquinone, Picryl Chloride, Polymyxin B, Prednicarbate, Tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, Thiram, Tretinoin, Trioxsalen, Vehicle Emissions, Zineb, alclometasone dipropionate, betamethasone benzoate, betamethasone-17,21-dipropionate, diazolidinylurea, diisobutyl phthalate, diisodecyl phthalate, diisononyl phthalate, dinocap, mancozeb, methyldithiocarbamate, mono-(2-ethylhexyl)phthalate, mono-isobutyl phthalate, monoethyl phthalate, propachlor, propineb.

Atopic dermatitis, a form of allergic contact dermatitis, presents with symptoms including edema, hypergammaglobulinemia, neurogenic inflammation, pruritus, pruritus ani, psychophysiologic disorders, respiratory sounds, skin manifestations, sleep disorders, and tinea pedis. It is associated with drugs such as Antigens, Fungal, Benzalkonium Compounds, Betamethasone Valerate, Biological Products, Butylated hydroxytoluene, CX 659S, Calcipotriol, Capsaicin, Chlorhexidine, Chlorquinaldol, Coal tar, Cyclic adenosine monophosphate, Cyclophosphamide, Dichlorophen, Diethylhexyl Phthalate, Dinitrochlorobenzene, Fluocinonide, Formaldehyde, Glucosamine, Glutaral, Kathon 886, Ketoconazole, Oxazolone, Parathion, Particulate Matter, Phenylmercuric acetate, Pimecrolimus, Plant Extracts, Prednicarbate, Prednisolone, Propylene Glycols, Quaternium-15, Sorbic acid, Steroids, Tacrolimus, Thalidomide, Thiocarbamates, Tobacco Smoke Pollution, Trolamine, Vehicle Emissions, astragalin, corticosteroids, diisononyl phthalate, imidazolidinyl urea, lauryl gallate, mono(2-ethyl-5-hydroxyhexyl) phthalate, monobutyl phthalate, naphthalan, oleanolic acid 3-acetate, and sphingosine phosphorylcholine. The drugs Dextrose, unspecified form and Linoleic acid are involved in the disease's pathogenesis. Atopic dermatitis resembles psoriasis and vitiligo.

Erythema is associated with drugs including 3,5-dichloro-2,4-6-trifluoropyridine, Carboplatin, Carrageenan, Climbazole, Croton Oil, Cytarabine, Dimethyl sulfoxide, Dinoprostone, Epirubicin, Etomidate, Fentanyl, Fluorescein, Fluorouracil, Freund's Adjuvant, Glycopyrrolate, Imiquimod, Iodamide, Isotretinoin, Meperidine, Mepyramine, Mesalazine, Methylprednisolone aceponate, Methylprednisolone hemisuccinate, Mometasone furoate, N-((4-dimethylaminophenyl)methyl)-N-(4-isopropylphenyl)-7-methoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydronaphthalen-1-carboxamide, Nicotine, Oxaliplatin, Phenytoin, Pimecrolimus, Protein kinase inhibitors, Pyridoxine, Sodium lauryl sulfate, Sulfisoxazole, Temozolomide, Thiopental, Triethylenetetramine, Trinitrobenzenesulfonic Acid, Vancomycin, Vincristine, Yohimbine, benzanthrone, chromium hexavalent ion, delta-aminolevulinic acid methyl ester, icilin, n-tetradecane, p-Methoxy-N-methylphenethylamine, pyrazinobutazone, rhododendrol, tridecane, and trimellitic anhydride.

Inflammation is associated with drugs including 2,3,7,8-tetrachlorodibenzofuran, 4-(5-benzo(1,3)dioxol-5-yl-4-pyridin-2-yl-1H-imidazol-2-yl)benzamide, 15(S)-15-methylprostaglandin E1, Acarbose, Active Hexose Correlated Compound, Air Pollutants, Allergens, Apamin, Arachidonic Acid, Candesartan, Chlordan, Clofibrate, Crotalid Venoms, Dinitrochlorobenzene, Estradiol, Febuxostat, Ferrous sulfate, Genistein, Glucaric acid, Hydrochloric acid, Indole-3-carbinol, Lead acetate, Lipopolysaccharides, Lycopene, Metronidazole, MitoTEMPO, Olvanil, Penicillamine, SK&F 98625, Silver Compounds, Sitagliptin phosphate, Tamoxifen, Tobacco Smoke Pollution, Toluene, Tretinoin, Tributyltin, Turmeric, Vitamin D, Vitamin E, allyl alcohol, bis(2-chloroethoxy)methane, boldine methine, brevetoxin, catalpol, cyanoginosin LR, lead nitrate, neoechinulin A, nitrosoperoxycarbonate, perthamide C, and yangonin. The drugs Alanine and Posaconazole are involved in the disease's pathogenesis.

Ulcer is associated with drugs including 2,3-dibromopropanol, 4-(5-(4-chlorophenyl)-3-(trifluoromethyl)-1H-pyrazol-1-yl)benzenesulfonamide, 5,7-dihydroxy-2-methyl-8-(4-(3-hydroxy-1-methyl)-piperidinyl)-4H-1-benzopyran-4-one, 7,14-dihydroxydocosa-4,8,10,12,16,19-hexaenoic acid, 8-(bis(2-methylphenyl)methyl)-3-phenyl-8-azabicyclo(3.2.1)octan-3-ol, Acetic acid, Acetylcysteine, Acetylsalicylic acid, Allicin, Cholesterol, Corticotropin, Crocin, Cyclosporine, Dextran Sulfate, Diclofenac, Domperidone, Epinephrine, Ethanol, Famotidine, Formaldehyde, Histamine, Indomethacin, Iridoid Glycosides, Ketamine, Ketorolac, Lansoprazole, Mesalazine, Metoclopramide, Muraglitazar, Obeticholic acid, Omeprazole, Pentosan Sulfuric Polyester, Phenylbutazone, RN 1734, Reserpine, Rifampicin, Sirolimus, Smoke, Sulfasalazine, Thalidomide, Ticlopidine, Topiramate, Trinitrobenzenesulfonic Acid, Turmeric, gypenoside, icilin, perilla seed oil, qingre zaoshi liangxue fang, rentiapril, and sinomenine.
292:
Ebola has been associated with multiple outbreaks across several countries, including repeated events in the Republic of Congo and Guinea Bissau, as well as significant outbreaks in Sierra Leone and the United Republic of Tanzania. Notable outbreaks occurred in 2014 in Guinea Bissau and Sierra Leone, with case counts ranging from dozens to over 3,000, and in 2018–2020 in the Republic of Congo, with case numbers exceeding 2,000 in several instances. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. In terms of pharmacological interventions, several drugs have been associated with Ebola, including BH30sucMan, Favipiravir, Galidesivir, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Remdesivir, and Teicoplanin.

Typhoid fever has been documented in outbreaks in the Republic of Tajikistan in 1996, with over 7,500 cases reported, and in Pakistan in 2016, involving more than 5,000 cases. It is categorized as a primary bacterial infectious disease, and its pathogenesis is linked to the Salmonella infection pathway. Treatment options include the use of Chloramphenicol and Ciprofloxacin.

HIV infection-AIDS was reported in Pakistan in 2019, with 876 confirmed cases. The disease is associated with the Human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection pathway.

Cholera has been reported in numerous outbreaks across Africa, Asia, and South America, including major events in the Republic of Panama, Federal Republic of Nigeria, United Republic of Tanzania, and Congo, with case counts ranging from a few dozen to over 36,000. Notably, a severe outbreak occurred in Zambia in 2022, with over 36,000 cases and 1,210 deaths. The disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease, and its pathogenesis is linked to Vibrio cholerae infection. The cholera vaccine CVD 103-HgR is associated with treatment.

Malaria has been reported in outbreaks in the United Republic of Tanzania, Burundi, and Ethiopia, with a particularly large outbreak in Pakistan in 2022 involving 3.4 million cases. The disease is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Symptoms include fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, coma, jaundice, purpura, and neurological manifestations. Several drugs are associated with treatment, including Artemether, Artesunate, Chloroquine, Quinine, and various combinations such as atovaquone-proguanil and amodiaquine-artesunate. Additionally, certain drugs play a role in disease pathogenesis, including Cholesterol, Glutamic acid, and bilirubin. The disease is linked to the Malaria pathway.
293:
Outbreaks of Zika virus have been reported across multiple countries between 2015 and 2021, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Brazil, Argentina, India, and others. Notable events include a large outbreak in French Guiana in November 2015 with 8,750 confirmed cases, and significant outbreaks in Brazil in 2015 and 2016 with thousands of cases. Some outbreaks were associated with deaths, such as one in the Republic of Panama in March 2016 with one death, and another in the United States in March 2016 with two cases and one death. Additional cases were reported in India in 2016 and 2021, with 70 cases recorded in July 2021. Zika virus is classified as a viral infectious disease. In terms of pharmaceutical associations, drugs such as Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and Niclosamide are observed to be associated with the condition.

Outbreaks of Yellow Fever have occurred in various regions, including Ghana, Federal Republic of Nigeria, Cameroon, Ecuador, Angola, Congo, Brazil, South Africa (KwaZulu Natal), Guinea Bissau, Sierra Leone, Burkina Faso, Sudan (W Equatoria), United Republic of Tanzania, and Ethiopia. Notable events include a large outbreak in Nigeria in August 2021 with 1,312 cases and two deaths, and a significant outbreak in Congo in March 2016 with 453 cases and 45 deaths. Other major outbreaks occurred in Angola in 2016 with 164 cases and 37 deaths, and in Brazil in 2017 with 35 cases and 20 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. In the context of viremia, several drugs are associated with the condition, including Active Hexose Correlated Compound, Adefovir dipivoxil, Corticosterone, Indinavir, Lamivudine, Mycophenolic acid, Piperonyl butoxide, Ribavirin, Tenofovir, Zidovudine, and resmethrin, with Alanine noted as playing a role in disease pathogenesis.
294: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998 and July 1999, and in Ethiopia in November 2024. A major outbreak was also documented in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and shares clinical and pathological similarities with conditions such as celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, anemia, and thymus cancer. The disease is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, pallor, retinal hemorrhage, coma, and neurological manifestations. Key drugs used in its treatment include Artemether, Artesunate, Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, Primaquine, Mefloquine, Atovaquone-proguanil combination, and Amodiaquine-artesunate combination. Other drugs are associated with the disease either through concurrent use or involvement in its pathogenesis, including cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. The disease is linked to the Malaria pathway, and its management often involves antimalarial agents such as sulfonamides, tetracycline, and various quinoline derivatives.
295:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple countries across Asia, Africa, South America, and the Caribbean, with outbreaks documented in Cameroon, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Mayotte, East Timor, Barbados, El Salvador, Brazil, Egypt, the Netherlands, India, France, and others, spanning from 1995 to 2024. These outbreaks have varied in scale, with case counts ranging from a few to over 500,000, and fatalities reaching up to 750 in some instances. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is a subtype of dengue disease. It is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and various flavonoids such as hinokiflavone and podocarpusflavone A. The disease shares characteristics with other viral infectious diseases and is linked to biological pathways involving malaria.

Chikungunya virus disease has been reported in Tanzania, the United States, Congo, France, Reunion, Italy, Spain, Argentina, and Egypt, with outbreaks occurring between 2005 and 2018. Case numbers have ranged from 1 to over 27,000, with fatalities reported in some instances. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with the drug chloroquine. It shares similar characteristics with other viral infectious diseases.

Zika virus outbreaks have been documented in El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, the Netherlands, St Lucia, the United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, India, Honduras, the Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Cape Verde, Brazil, and others, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 8,700. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide. It has also been linked to neurological complications and congenital abnormalities in some outbreaks.

Yellow Fever has been reported in Brazil, French Guiana, Cameroon, the Netherlands, Angola, China, United Republic of Tanzania, Guinea Bissau, Mali, Benin, Egypt, Ecuador, and other regions, with outbreaks occurring from 1996 to 2020. Case numbers have varied from 1 to over 1,700, with fatalities reaching up to 238. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with outbreaks in regions with high mosquito vector activity.

Rift Valley Fever has been reported in Madagascar, Tanzania, Niger, Egypt, Mayotte, Yemen, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, and Cameroon, with outbreaks occurring between 1999 and 2022. Case counts have ranged from 1 to over 698, with fatalities reaching up to 222. The disease is associated with drugs such as galidesivir and ribavirin and is classified as a viral infectious disease.

West Nile Disease has been reported in Canada, the United States, Republic of Moldova, Israel, Brazil, Portugal, Romania, and Turkey, with outbreaks occurring between 1996 and 2015. Case numbers have ranged from 1 to over 3,500, with fatalities reaching up to 211. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as active hexose correlated compound and minocycline.

Malaria has been reported in Tanzania, Burundi, Ethiopia, and Pakistan, with outbreaks documented from 1998 to 2022. Case counts have reached up to 616,034, with fatalities reported in some instances. The disease is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including chloroquine, artemether, artesunate, quinine, and various antimalarials. It presents with symptoms such as fever, chills, vomiting, jaundice, coma, and neurological manifestations. The disease is linked to the biological pathway of malaria and is influenced by factors such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin in its pathogenesis.
296: Syphilis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is considered a subtype of syphilis, with primary syphilis being a specific stage within the disease spectrum. The condition has been associated with the use of several drugs, including arsenic, doxycycline, and metamfetamine, which are observed in clinical or research contexts alongside the disease.
297:
An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a leishmaniasis, a group of diseases caused by parasitic protozoa. Several drugs are associated with its treatment, including Amphotericin B, Antimony, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Paromomycin, Pentamidine, and various antiprotozoals. Other compounds such as Allopurinol, Artemisinin, and reactive oxygen species are also linked to its management. Additionally, the disease is associated with pathways related to antigen processing and presentation, cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity, protein processing in the endoplasmic reticulum, and Toll-like receptor signaling.

Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, Ethiopia in 2024, and Pakistan in 2022 with 3,400,000 cases. Malaria is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, and neurological manifestations. Several drugs are linked to its treatment, including Artemether, Artesunate, Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, Quinine, Mefloquine, Primaquine, and Atovaquone-proguanil combination. Other drugs such as Amodiaquine, Dapsone, and various triazoles are also associated with its management. The disease is further linked to the role of certain substances in its pathogenesis, including cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. The biological pathway associated with malaria includes the malaria pathway itself.
298: Ebola has been associated with multiple outbreaks across several countries, including repeated events in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Sierra Leone, Guinea Bissau, and other regions such as Tanzania, Cameroon, Mali, Angola, South Africa, and Italy. Outbreaks have occurred from as early as 1996 to 2021, with significant case and death counts reported, particularly in the Congo, where several outbreaks involved thousands of cases and hundreds to over two thousand deaths. Notable events include a large outbreak in 2014 in Sierra Leone with 1,975 cases and 1,069 deaths, and multiple severe outbreaks in the Congo between 2018 and 2020, including one with over 3,400 cases and 2,280 deaths. Ebola is classified as a viral infectious disease. In terms of pharmaceutical associations, several drugs have been linked to Ebola, including Remdesivir, Favipiravir, Galidesivir, Teicoplanin, and BH30sucMan, among others. Additionally, a broad range of other drugs, such as arsenic, azathioprine, cyclophosphamide, methotrexate, vancomycin, and various chemotherapeutic agents, are noted as being associated with infection, though their specific roles in Ebola pathogenesis are not detailed.
299:
An outbreak of pneumonia was reported in Viet Nam and Indonesia on March 12, 2003, involving 305 confirmed cases and 5 deaths. Pneumonia is classified as a lung disease and is associated with several drugs, including Amoxicillin-Potassium Clavulanate Combination, Azithromycin, Clarithromycin, Co-Trimoxazole, and Prednisone. The condition is also linked to the pathogenic role of arginine in its disease mechanisms.

Outbreaks of typhoid fever occurred in multiple locations, including the Republic of Tajikistan in 1996 with 7,516 cases and 4,000 cases, and in Pakistan in 2016 with 5,274 cases. Another outbreak was reported in Algeria in 1996, involving 910 cases and 1 death. Typhoid fever is categorized as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the use of drugs such as Chloramphenicol and Ciprofloxacin. It is also linked to the Salmonella infection pathway.

Malaria outbreaks were documented in various regions, including Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Ethiopia in 2024, and Pakistan in 2022 with 3,400,000 cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and presents symptoms such as fever, chills, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, and neurological manifestations. It is associated with drugs including Chloroquine, Artemisinin, Quinine, and Atovaquone-Proguanil, and its pathogenesis involves the role of cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. The disease is linked to the Malaria pathway.

Visceral leishmaniasis outbreaks occurred in Pakistan in 2002, with multiple events reporting 200,000 cases and one event reporting 738 cases. The disease is classified as a leishmaniasis and is associated with drugs such as Amphotericin B, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, and Antimony compounds. It is also linked to the use of organotin compounds and reactive oxygen species.

Pancreatitis is associated with a range of symptoms including abdominal pain, vomiting, jaundice, diarrhea, and ecchymosis. It is linked to drugs such as Amiodarone, Indomethacin, Isoniazid, and Metformin, with potassium playing a role in its pathogenesis. The condition is associated with the pathways of bile secretion, gastric acid secretion, pancreatic secretion, and protein digestion and absorption. It is classified as a pancreas disease and resembles sclerosing cholangitis.

Systemic lupus erythematosus presents with symptoms such as fatigue, fever, joint pain, skin rashes, proteinuria, and neurological manifestations. It is associated with drugs including Hydroxychloroquine, Azathioprine, and Prednisone, and its pathogenesis involves the role of angiostatins, malonaldehyde, nitrite, nucleotides, prostaglandins, and purine. The disease is classified as a connective tissue disease and resembles systemic scleroderma.

Cutaneous lupus erythematosus is a subtype of lupus erythematosus and is associated with drugs such as Hydroxychloroquine, Dapsone, and Prednisone. Oral ulcers are linked to a wide range of drugs, including Amitriptyline, Methotrexate, and Thalidomide.

Liver abscess NOS is associated with drugs such as Ceftriaxone, Metronidazole, and Linezolid. Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as Acetylsalicylic acid and Levamisole. Tuberculosis is a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with drugs including Isoniazid, Rifampicin, and Pyrazinamide, with lopinavir, phosphorus, and phosphates playing a role in its pathogenesis. It is linked to the tuberculosis pathway.
300: Rabies outbreaks have been reported in multiple instances, including an event in France on August 26, 2004, which resulted in one death. Additional outbreaks were recorded in unspecified locations on May 6, 1997, and April 10, 2024. Rabies is classified as a viral infectious disease. The drug cinnabarin is associated with rabies, indicating its use or study in relation to the condition.
301: Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various countries, including Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Brazil, India, the Netherlands, Egypt, and several nations in Asia, Africa, and South America, with case counts ranging from a few to over half a million. Notable outbreaks occurred in Malaysia in 1997 with 19,544 cases and 50 deaths, in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, in the Netherlands in 2004 with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths, and in Brazil in 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths. More recent outbreaks include a large-scale event in 2024 with 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths reported in an unspecified country, and significant outbreaks in Pakistan (2021, 48,906 cases), Bangladesh (2022, 52,807 cases), and East Timor (2005 and 2022). The disease is caused by the Dengue virus and is classified as a viral infectious disease. Several drugs have been associated with Dengue Fever, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and various plant-derived compounds such as hinokiflavone and podocarpusflavone A. Additionally, dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease.
302: Dengue Fever has been associated with multiple outbreak events across various countries and time periods, including significant outbreaks in Malaysia in 1996 and 1997, with case counts reaching 2,450 and 19,544 respectively, and in 1997 with 11,328 cases and 28 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, in Brazil in 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths, in Pakistan in 2019 with 47,120 cases and 75 deaths, and in 2022 with 25,932 cases and 62 deaths. Other notable events include outbreaks in the Netherlands in 2004 with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths, in Cape Verde in 2009 with 3,367 and 16,744 cases, in East Timor in 2005 and 2022 with multiple reports involving hundreds of cases, and in Burkina Faso in 2016 and 2017 with over 1,000 cases. Outbreaks have also been documented in countries such as Cameroon, El Salvador, India, France, and the Philippines, with varying case numbers and mortality rates. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and hinokiflavone, which are either studied concurrently with the disease or implicated in its pathogenesis. The disease is also linked to several biological pathways and shares characteristics with dengue hemorrhagic fever, which is considered a subtype of dengue disease.
303: An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis. A range of drugs are associated with its treatment, including Allopurinol, Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, Paromomycin, and various other antiprotozoals. Additionally, several compounds such as 2,5-bis(2-(2-propoxy)-4-(2-pyridylimino)aminophenyl)furan, 2-(2''-dichloroacetamidobenzyl)-3-(3'-indolylquinoline), Desmodium gangeticum extract, Isosorbide dinitrate, Ketoconazole, Neopterin, Organotin Compounds, Reactive Oxygen Species, Thalidomide, Val-Glu-Pro-Ile-Gly-Tyr, amarogentin, amphotericin B, deoxycholate drug combination, and liposomal amphotericin B are also linked to the disease.
304:
Meningitis has been documented in multiple outbreaks across various countries, including Ghana in 1997 with 961 cases and 143 deaths, Burkina Faso in 2001 with 10,897 cases and 1,525 deaths, and South Africa (KwaZulu Natal) in 1997 with 41,699 cases and 4,498 deaths. Additional significant outbreaks occurred in Egypt in 1999, with one event reporting 22,000 cases and 1,600 deaths, and another with 16,000 cases and 1,000 deaths. Other notable events include outbreaks in Guinea Bissau (2013, 9,249 cases, 857 deaths), Chad (1996 and 1998, with 2,440 and 2,835 cases respectively), and Nigeria (1996 and 2015, with 8,423 and 652 cases respectively). Meningitis is classified as a central nervous system disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including corticosteroids, ceftriaxone, ampicillin, chloramphenicol, vancomycin, dexamethasone, and others such as ibuprofen, prednisone, and linezolid. The disease is also linked to bacterial meningitis, which shares similar drug associations and is considered a subtype of meningitis.

Salmonellosis has been reported in multiple outbreaks, including in the United States in 2004 and 2015, with 32 and 124 confirmed cases respectively, and in France in 2017 with 35 cases. It is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the drug cinnamaldehyde.

Malaria has been reported in outbreaks in Burundi (1999, 616,034 cases, 51 deaths), Pakistan (2022, 3,400,000 cases), and Ethiopia and the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999. It is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including chloroquine, artemisinin, quinine, mefloquine, and atovaquone-proguanil. Symptoms include fever, chills, jaundice, vomiting, coma, and neurological manifestations. The disease is linked to biological pathways related to malaria and is associated with pathogenic mechanisms involving cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid.

HIV infection-AIDS was reported in Pakistan in 2019 with 876 confirmed cases. HIV infection is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with a broad range of antiretroviral drugs, including zidovudine, lamivudine, tenofovir, efavirenz, and ritonavir-boosted protease inhibitors. It is linked to the pathway of human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection.
305:
An outbreak of HBV and HCV occurred in China on December 29, 2024, and was reported on January 8, 2025. 

Liver cirrhosis is classified as a type of liver disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including 2,5-dimethyl-4-benzoquinone, 3-methylquercetin, Acitretin, Adefovir dipivoxil, Ademetionine, Amiodarone, Andrographolide, Azoxymethane, Betulinic Acid, Cholecalciferol, Cholesterol, Coconut oil, Dimethyl fumarate, Dust, Endotoxins, Eucalyptol, Fenofibrate, Ferulic acid, Fructose, Geraniol, Halofuginone, Halothane, Hyaluronic acid, Isoniazid, Lovastatin, Melitten, Mesalazine, Methionine, Methyltestosterone, Nimodipine, Nizatidine, Orlistat, Resveratrol, Ribavirin, Rosiglitazone, Scopolamine, Serotonin, Silymarin, Sirolimus, Spironolactone, Streptozocin, Tamoxifen, Tempol, Thalidomide, Torasemide, arsenite, corticosteroids, delphinidin, thymoquinone, and trofopar. Glycerin is implicated in the disease's pathogenesis.

306: Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole are associated with this condition, indicating their use or study in relation to filariasis.
307:
Yellow Fever is a viral infectious disease caused by the Yellow Fever virus. Documented outbreaks have occurred in multiple countries across different time periods, including significant events in Nigeria, Ghana, Cameroon, Brazil, Congo, Angola, Ecuador, and other regions. Notable outbreaks include a large-scale event in Nigeria in 2017 with 341 confirmed cases and 45 deaths, another in 2021 with 1,312 cases and 2 deaths, and a severe outbreak in Angola in 2016 with 1,708 cases and 238 deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in Brazil, Congo, and Cameroon, with case counts ranging from single cases to thousands. The disease is associated with symptoms such as fever, jaundice, and hemorrhagic manifestations, and it is classified as a viral infectious disease. The disease is transmitted by mosquitoes, primarily in tropical regions, and remains a public health concern in endemic areas.

Dengue Fever is a viral infectious disease caused by the Dengue virus, with documented outbreaks in numerous countries including Sri Lanka, Malaysia, India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, The Netherlands, Brazil, and several Caribbean and South American nations. Outbreaks have varied in scale, with major events in Sri Lanka in 2017 reporting 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, and in The Netherlands in 2004 with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths. Other significant outbreaks occurred in India, Brazil, and Pakistan, with case numbers reaching into the tens of thousands. The disease is characterized by high fever, severe headache, joint pain, and in severe cases, hemorrhagic fever and shock. It is classified as a viral infectious disease and is transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes. Outbreaks have been reported in both urban and rural settings, often during rainy seasons, and have shown increasing frequency and geographic spread in recent decades.

Typhoid Fever is a primary bacterial infectious disease caused by Salmonella enterica serovar Typhi. Outbreaks have been reported in countries such as Pakistan, Republic of Tajikistan, and Algeria. Notable events include a large outbreak in Tajikistan in 1996 with over 7,500 cases, and another in Pakistan in 2016 with 5,274 confirmed cases. The disease is typically transmitted through contaminated food or water and is associated with symptoms such as prolonged fever, headache, abdominal pain, and constipation or diarrhea. It is linked to the Salmonella infection pathway and is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. Treatment often involves antibiotics such as chloramphenicol and ciprofloxacin, which are commonly used to manage the infection.

Leptospirosis is a primary bacterial infectious disease caused by Leptospira bacteria. Outbreaks have been documented in India, Malaysia, and the United Republic of Tanzania, with notable events including a 1997 outbreak in India reporting 413 cases and 48 deaths, and a 2022 outbreak in Tanzania with 20 cases and 3 deaths. The disease is typically contracted through contact with water or soil contaminated by animal urine, especially in tropical and subtropical regions. It is associated with symptoms such as fever, muscle pain, jaundice, and in severe cases, kidney and liver failure. The disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is often linked to environmental exposure, particularly during flooding or heavy rainfall.

Chikungunya virus disease is a viral infectious disease caused by the Chikungunya virus, with outbreaks reported in multiple countries including the United Republic of Tanzania, Congo, Chad, Reunion Island, France, Italy, Spain, and Egypt. Major outbreaks occurred in 2016 in Tanzania with 1,792 cases, in 2020 in Chad with 27,540 cases, and in 2018 in Egypt with 13,978 cases. The disease is characterized by sudden onset of fever, severe joint pain, rash, and muscle pain, and is transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes. It is classified as a viral infectious disease and has shown increasing global spread, particularly in tropical and subtropical regions. The disease is often associated with urban environments and has been linked to travel-related transmission.

Ebola is a viral infectious disease caused by the Ebola virus, with outbreaks reported in multiple countries including the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Guinea Bissau, Sierra Leone, and Gabon. Notable outbreaks include a large event in the Democratic Republic of the Congo in 2018 with over 500 cases and 296 deaths, and another in 2019 with over 3,000 cases and 2,000 deaths. In Guinea Bissau, multiple outbreaks occurred in 2014, with case counts ranging from 49 to 233 and death tolls from 29 to 157. The disease is characterized by severe hemorrhagic fever, high fever, vomiting, diarrhea, and internal and external bleeding. It is transmitted through direct contact with bodily fluids of infected individuals or animals. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and has been associated with high mortality rates in past outbreaks.

Malaria is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease caused by Plasmodium species, with outbreaks reported in countries such as Pakistan, Burundi, Ethiopia, and the United Republic of Tanzania. A major outbreak in Pakistan in 2022 involved 3.4 million cases, while another in Burundi in 1999 reported over 616,000 cases and 51 deaths. The disease is transmitted by Anopheles mosquitoes and is characterized by symptoms such as fever, chills, headache, and anemia. It is associated with the malaria pathway and is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The disease remains a major public health issue in tropical and subtropical regions, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, and is managed through antimalarial drugs such as chloroquine, artemisinin-based combination therapies, and preventive measures like insecticide-treated bed nets.

Hepatitis C is a viral infectious disease caused by the Hepatitis C virus, with documented associations with various drugs used in its treatment and management. These include antivirals such as ribavirin, interferon alfa, peginterferon alfa-2a, peginterferon alfa-2b, sofosbuvir, ledipasvir, daclatasvir, and simeprevir. The disease is also linked to the Hepatitis C pathway and is classified as a viral infectious disease. It can lead to chronic liver disease, cirrhosis, and liver cancer. The disease is associated with risk factors such as blood transfusions, intravenous drug use, and exposure to contaminated medical equipment. Some drugs are used in combination therapies to inhibit viral replication and improve treatment outcomes.

Hepatitis B is a viral infectious disease caused by the Hepatitis B virus, with symptoms including fever, jaundice, liver dysfunction, and skin manifestations. It is associated with the Hepatitis B pathway and is classified as a viral infectious disease. The disease can resemble liver cancer, membranous glomerulonephritis, and primary biliary cholangitis in its chronic forms. Treatment involves antiviral drugs such as entecavir, tenofovir, lamivudine, adefovir, and interferon alfa. The disease is transmitted through blood, sexual contact, and from mother to child during childbirth. It is also associated with the presence of hepatitis B antigens and can lead to chronic infection, increasing the risk of liver cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.

Toxoplasmosis is a coccidiosis caused by the parasite Toxoplasma gondii, with documented associations with drugs such as pyrimethamine, sulfadiazine, atovaquone, and artemether. The disease is classified as a coccidiosis and is typically acquired through ingestion of contaminated food or water, or through contact with cat feces. It can cause flu-like symptoms in healthy individuals but may lead to severe complications in immunocompromised patients and congenital infections in newborns. The disease is linked to the toxoplasmosis pathway and is managed with combination therapies to control parasite replication.

Pneumonic Plague is a form of plague caused by Yersinia pestis, classified as a type of plague. It is transmitted through respiratory droplets and causes severe pneumonia with symptoms such as high fever, cough, and difficulty breathing. It is a highly contagious and potentially fatal disease, requiring prompt antibiotic treatment.

Fever is a common symptom associated with numerous infectious and inflammatory conditions. It is linked to various drugs, including antipyretics such as acetaminophen, ibuprofen, and aspirin, as well as other agents like antineoplastic drugs, antibiotics, and antihypertensives. Some drugs can induce fever as a side effect, while others are used to manage fever in the context of infections. Fever is also associated with the presence of hydroxyindoleacetic acid, a metabolite of serotonin, and is a key clinical sign in diseases such as malaria, dengue, and viral hepatitis.

Schistosomiasis is a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease caused by blood flukes of the genus Schistosoma. It is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is treated with drugs such as praziquantel and metrifonate. The disease is acquired through contact with freshwater contaminated by parasite larvae, leading to symptoms such as abdominal pain, diarrhea, and liver damage. It is particularly prevalent in tropical and subtropical regions, especially in sub-Saharan Africa, and is associated with poor sanitation and lack of access to clean water.

Intestinal Schistosomiasis is a form of schistosomiasis affecting the intestines, caused by Schistosoma mansoni and other species. It is treated with drugs such as praziquantel, quercetin, and resveratrol. The disease is classified as a type of schistosomiasis and is associated with symptoms such as abdominal pain, diarrhea, and blood in stool. It is transmitted through contact with contaminated freshwater and is prevalent in regions with inadequate sanitation.

Jaundice is a clinical sign characterized by yellowing of the skin and eyes, often due to elevated bilirubin levels. It is associated with various drugs, including anticonvulsants, antibiotics, antipsychotics, and antineoplastic agents. Some drugs can induce jaundice as a side effect, while others are used in the treatment of liver diseases. The condition is linked to liver dysfunction and is a common manifestation in diseases such as hepatitis, malaria, and cirrhosis.
308:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where a significant event in 1997 involved over 11,000 cases and 28 deaths, and another in 1997 with 19,544 cases and 50 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, Brazil in 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths, and the Netherlands in 2004 with multiple events totaling over 140,000 cases and more than 1,000 deaths. Other notable outbreaks were recorded in East Timor, Cameroon, Bangladesh, Mayotte, Barbados, El Salvador, India, France, Pakistan, and South America, with case counts ranging from a few to over 7.6 million in a 2024 outbreak. The disease is caused by the Dengue virus and is classified as a viral infectious disease. Several drugs are associated with its management, including hydroxychloroquine, doxycycline, and various compounds such as 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, and hinokiflavone. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease.

Malaria has been documented in outbreaks in countries including Burundi, where a 1999 event reported over 616,000 cases and 51 deaths, and Pakistan, with a 2022 outbreak involving 3.4 million cases. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania and Ethiopia, with reports indicating ongoing transmission. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It presents with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, coma, diarrhea, vomiting, purpura, retinal hemorrhage, and neurological manifestations. Key drugs used in its treatment include chloroquine, artemisinin, quinine, atovaquone, and proguanil, with some drugs also playing a role in disease pathogenesis, such as cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. The disease is linked to specific biological pathways, including those associated with malaria infection.
309:
An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, and was followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis. It is associated with a range of drugs, including amphotericin B, miltefosine, antimony compounds, paromomycin, and various antiprotozoals. 

Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, and another in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, involving 3,400,000 cases. Additional outbreaks were documented in the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, and in Ethiopia in 2024. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It presents with symptoms such as fever, chills, jaundice, hemoglobinuria, retinal hemorrhage, and neurological manifestations. The disease is associated with numerous drugs, including chloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, atovaquone-proguanil, and various antimalarials. It is also linked to biological pathways involving malaria itself. Malaria resembles conditions such as celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, anemia, and thymus cancer. 

Anemia is categorized as a hematopoietic system disease and shares characteristics with malaria, celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, and thymus cancer. It is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fatigue, pallor, jaundice, fever, abdominal pain, and various forms of hemorrhage and organ dysfunction. Drugs linked to anemia include iron supplements, erythropoietin, interferons, and several chemotherapeutic agents such as doxorubicin, cyclophosphamide, and bortezomib. The drug alanine is implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. 

Splenomegaly is associated with various drugs, including anticonvulsants, antimony sodium gluconate, arsenic trioxide, and several chemotherapeutic agents such as busulfan and vincristine. 

Hearing loss is associated with drugs such as aspirin, gentamicin, cisplatin, and other ototoxic agents, and is linked to the pathogenesis of the condition through the involvement of adenosine 5'-phosphosulfate. 

Peptic ulcer disease is classified as a gastrointestinal system disease and is associated with drugs including proton pump inhibitors, H2 blockers, antibiotics like amoxicillin and clarithromycin, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. 

Malnutrition not otherwise specified is associated with drugs such as arsenic and margarine, and glycerin is implicated in its pathogenesis. 

Tinnitus is associated with a broad range of drugs, including aspirin, antibiotics, antipsychotics, and chemotherapeutic agents. 

Tetanus is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as diazepam, baclofen, and other muscle relaxants used in managing its symptoms.

Chagas disease, also known as American trypanosomiasis, is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including Benznidazole, Nifurtimox, Nitrofural, and various experimental compounds such as 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol and 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol. Chagas disease is associated with heart disease, which in turn is categorized as a cardiovascular system disease. Cardiomyopathy, a condition related to heart disease, is also linked to Chagas disease and shares the same classification. Several drugs are associated with cardiomyopathy, including Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Inhibitors, Beta Blocking Agents, Bisoprolol, and various agents such as Paclitaxel, Quercetin, and Mitoquinone. Some of these drugs, including 99mTc-glucarate, Potassium, and phosphates, are also implicated in the pathogenesis of cardiomyopathy. 

Congenital heart disease is another condition related to heart disease and is associated with a range of drugs such as Alprostadil, Amantadine, Cocaine, Ethanol, and various environmental agents like Bisphenol A and Tetrachlorodibenzodioxin. First-degree atrioventricular block is classified as a type of atrioventricular block. Parasitemia, a condition involving the presence of parasites in the blood, is associated with drugs such as Artesunate, Chloroquine, Quinine, and combinations like fanasil and pyrimethamine, with Creatinine also noted as a factor in its pathogenesis.

Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and dozens of deaths, and in 2022 in Bangladesh and Pakistan, with large case counts and notable fatalities. Additional outbreaks were documented in countries such as India, El Salvador, Egypt, France, the Netherlands, and Thailand, with case numbers ranging from a few to over 58,000 in a single event. Notably, a major outbreak in the Netherlands in 2004 involved more than 58,000 cases and over 600 deaths, while a 2024 outbreak reported in an unspecified country involved nearly 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths. The disease has also been recorded in smaller outbreaks in Barbados, East Timor, Burkina Faso, and other locations across Asia, Africa, and South America. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is further categorized into subtypes such as dengue hemorrhagic fever and dengue shock syndrome, both of which are considered subtypes of dengue disease. Biomedical research indicates that several drugs are associated with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and various flavonoids such as hinokiflavone and podocarpusflavone A, which are studied in relation to the disease.

Chikungunya virus disease has also been documented in multiple outbreaks, particularly in the United Republic of Tanzania, where events occurred in 2016 and 2017 with thousands of cases, and in Chad, where a large outbreak in 2020 involved over 27,000 cases. Other notable outbreaks were reported in Reunion Island, France, Egypt, and Argentina, with case numbers ranging from a few to over 13,000. The disease has also been detected in the United States, Spain, Italy, and other European countries, indicating its global spread. Chikungunya virus disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and the drug chloroquine is associated with its study or treatment.
