It is associated with drugs such as 3,5-diethoxycarbonyl-1,4-dihydrocollidine, 24-norursodeoxycholic acid, bezafibrate, cisplatin, danazol, floxuridine, fluorouracil, Fu-CF protocol, linezolid, methimazole, minocycline, N-formylmethionyl-leucyl-tyrosine, paclitaxel, quinacrine, sulfasalazine, teicoplanin, tobacco smoke pollution, tretinoin, trinitrobenzenesulfonic acid, and ursodeoxycholic acid; calcium is involved in its pathogenesis. It resembles ulcerative colitis. Pancreatitis is a pancreas disease and presents with symptoms such as acute abdomen, abdominal pain, chronic pain, colic, diarrhea, dyspepsia, ecchymosis, edema, hematemesis, hyperalgesia, hypergammaglobulinemia, hypothermia, jaundice, obstructive jaundice, neurogenic inflammation, pain, pain threshold, intractable pain, postoperative pain, purpura, Schoenlein-Henoch, purpura, thrombotic thrombocytopenic, retinal hemorrhage, tetany, visceral pain, and vomiting. It is associated with drugs such as 1-cyano-2-hydroxy-3-butene, 1-methylspermidine, 2-acetoxy-5-(2-4-(trifluoromethyl)phenethylamino)benzoic acid, 3-aminobenzamide, acetaminophen, amiodarone, calcium chloride, carbamazepine, ceftriaxone, cimetidine, cocaine, codeine, contrast media, cyclosporine, cytarabine, danazol, dantrolene, dichlorvos, didanosine, ebselen, edaravone, efavirenz, eluxadoline, ethyl chloride, fatty acids, nonesterified, glycodeoxycholic acid, indomethacin, isoniazid, lipopolysaccharides, meglumine antimoniate, melatonin, mercaptopurine, metolazone, metronidazole, nevirapine, octreotide, oxyphenbutazone, pentamidine, phytoestrogens, piroxicam, prednisone, resveratrol, ricinoleic acids, simvastatin, soil pollutants, sulfasalazine, trimethadione, trinitrobenzenesulfonic acid, ent-1,3,6,11-tetrahydroxykaur-16-ene-15-one 3,11-diacetate, and triglycerides; potassium is involved in its pathogenesis. It is associated with biological pathways including bile secretion, gastric acid secretion, pancreatic secretion, and protein digestion and absorption. It resembles sclerosing cholangitis.
314: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, and July 14, 1999, as well as in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, where 3,400,000 cases were reported. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is also categorized as a parasitic infectious disease and a disease by infectious agent. The condition is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, headache, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, pallor, oliguria, retinal hemorrhage, coma, and neurologic manifestations. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or study of malaria, including chloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, mefloquine, primaquine, atovaquone, and proguanil. Some of these drugs, such as chloroquine and hydroxychloroquine, are also linked to disease pathogenesis through mechanisms involving bilirubin, cholesterol, and glutamic acid. The disease is further associated with the Malaria pathway. Malaria shares similarities with other conditions such as anemia, celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, and thymus cancer, and it is known to resemble malaria caused by *Plasmodium falciparum* and *Plasmodium vivax*, both of which are specific subtypes of the disease.
315:
Elephantiasis is classified as a form of lymphedema. Drugs associated with this condition include quartz, smectite, and mica.

Filarial elephantiasis is a type of filariasis. It is associated with the drugs albendazole, diethylcarbamazine, and ivermectin.

Atherosclerosis is characterized by symptoms such as acute coronary syndrome, albuminuria, amaurosis fugax, angina pectoris, stable and unstable angina, anoxia, body weight changes, intermittent claudication, mobility limitation, abdominal obesity, overweight, and obstructive sleep apnea. Drugs associated with atherosclerosis include 3,4,3',4'-tetrachlorobiphenyl, (+)-JQ1 compound, arsenic trioxide, betahistine, bezafibrate, cadmium, carbon disulfide, carbon dioxide, cholesterol, clopidogrel, dextromethorphan, epinephrine, ethanol, ferulic acid, fexofenadine, fluvastatin, humic substances, isradipine, lacidipine, lipid peroxides, lithium, MF59 oil emulsion, mangifera indica extract, minocycline, naloxone, nicardipine, nifedipine, nitric oxide, olive oil, orlistat, particulate matter, pentoxifylline, phenytoin, prasugrel hydrochloride, propranolol, rosiglitazone, tamoxifen, tobacco smoke pollution, troglitazone, vanadium, zinc, gossypetin, hmg coa reductase inhibitors, methylcholanthrene, myricitrin, pregnadiene-3,16-dione, quercetin 3-O-glucuronide, sodium arsenite, tobacco tar, and xiongshao. Some of these drugs also play a role in the disease's pathogenesis, including 8-epi-prostaglandin F2alpha and po-2. The condition is associated with the biological pathway of lipid and atherosclerosis. Atherosclerosis is classified as a type of arteriosclerotic cardiovascular disease and shares characteristics with abdominal obesity-metabolic syndrome 1, coronary artery disease, and type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Enteritis is associated with a range of drugs, including the 1-UFT protocol, (glycyl-glycyl)GLP-2, anisodamine, brimonidine tartrate, candesartan, carboplatin, ceftriaxone, clonidine, cocaine, croton oil, curcumin, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, Chinese herbal drugs, fluorouracil, hydroxychloroquine, indomethacin, lansoprazole, methotrexate, mitoxantrone, paclitaxel, and sulfasalazine.

Filariasis is a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole.

Autoimmune diseases are linked to a broad range of drugs, including amiodarone, asbestos (amphibole), aurotioprol, azathioprine, bisphenol A, cadmium chloride, carbamates, chlorpromazine, cholecalciferol, cobalt, cocaine, curcumin, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, epigallocatechin gallate, gold compounds, heparin, isoniazid, lead acetate, mercuric chloride, mercury, heavy metals, methotrexate, minocycline, mycophenolic acid, N-(oxo-5,6-dihydrophenanthridin-2-yl)-N,N-dimethylacetamide hydrochloride, niacin, octreotide, particulate matter, pemoline, prednisolone, procainamide, quercetin, quinidine, resveratrol, silicon dioxide, silver, silver nitrate, sodium aurothiomalate, sulfasalazine, sulforaphane, tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, thimerosal, trichloroethylene, vitamin D, hmg coa reductase inhibitors, lipopolysaccharide from E. coli O55-B5, methylmercuric chloride, pesticides, and tremolite.

Autoimmune disease is classified as a type of immune system disease.

Neurodegenerative disease is a central nervous system disease and is associated with drugs such as 2,5-hexanedione, 2-chloropropionic acid, acetaminophen, aluminum, aluminum lactate, amphetamine, ascorbic acid, barium, calcium, chromomycin A3, cianidanol, cupric sulfate, dextroamphetamine, indomethacin, isoflurane, magnesium, manganese, melatonin, memantine, methotrexate, midazolam, minocycline, molybdenum cofactor, muscimol, nicotine, nitric oxide, nitrous oxide, oxidopamine, pentetrazol, peroxynitrous acid, pilocarpine, plant extracts, quercetin, reserpine, resveratrol, rotenone, sodium selenite, sulindac, tauroursodeoxycholic acid, thiotepa, trandolapril, triethylenetetramine, tryptophan, vitamin E, aluminum maltolate, annonacin, catalpol, mancozeb, trimethyltin, and trimethyltin chloride. Some of these drugs contribute to the disease's pathogenesis, including 3-nitro-L-tyrosine, glutathione, and heme.

Onchocerciasis is a form of filariasis and is associated with the drugs abamectin, albendazole, diethylcarbamazine, and ivermectin.

Urinary tract infection is classified as a urinary system disease and is associated with a wide range of antibiotics and antimicrobials, including alendronate, amikacin, amoxicillin, amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate combination, ampicillin, aztreonam, carbenicillin, cefbuperazone, cefixime, cefotaxime, cefpirome, cefradine, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cefuroxime, cephalexin, cilastatin-imipenem combination, cinoxacin, ciprofloxacin, co-trimoxazole, dicloxacillin, doxycycline, flucytosine, fosfomycin, fusidic acid, gentamicins, linezolid, metronidazole, minocycline, mitoxantrone, nalidixic acid, neomycin, netilmicin, nitrofurantoin, ofloxacin, omadacycline, phenazopyridine, piperacillin, pivmecillinam, sparfloxacin, streptomycin, sulfisoxazole, sultamicillin, talampicillin, tazobactam-piperacillin combination, tobramycin, trimethoprim, vancomycin, chlorhexidine gluconate-lidocaine combination, and quinupristin-dalfopristin.

Inflammation is associated with various drugs, including 4,4'-diaminodiphenylmethane, 5-androstene-3,7,17-triol, 12-acetoxyhawtriwaic acid lactone, angiotensin receptor antagonists, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, antigens from Dermatophagoides, blood glucose, CP 31398, caraway oil, doxorubicin, enalapril, erlotinib hydrochloride, ferulic acid, fibric acids, fructose, hydrochloric acid, imiquimod, indinavir, ketanserin, lorazepam, methapyrilene, N-(2(R)-2-(hydroxamidocarbonylmethyl)-4-methylpentanoyl)-L-tryptophan methylamide, oleanolic acid, oxymatrine, picryl chloride, potassium dichromate, progesterone, rosiglitazone, SGA 360, saponins, sitagliptin phosphate, sterigmatocystin, tofacitinib, toluene 2,4-diisocyanate, tretinoin, trichloroacetaldehyde, ursodeoxycholic acid, valpromide, zinc oxide, acanthoic acid, adolapin, aminochrome 1, beta-amyrin, cadmium telluride, helenalin, lipopolysaccharide from E. coli O26-B6, nitrosoperoxycarbonate, sodium boranocarbonate, tcdA protein from Clostridium difficile, and tin sulfide. Some of these drugs are involved in the pathogenesis of inflammation, including alanine and posaconazole.
316:
An outbreak of Crimean-Congo Hemorrhagic Fever (CCHF) was reported in Kosovo on June 14, 2001, with 44 confirmed cases. Additional outbreaks occurred in Kosovo on June 15, 2001 (48 cases), June 19, 2001 (57 cases and 4 deaths), and June 26, 2001 (69 cases and 6 deaths), indicating a significant cluster of cases in the region during that period. The disease has also been documented in other countries, including South Africa (KwaZulu Natal) in November 1996 (32 cases, 1 death), Egypt in March 2003 (30 cases, 6 deaths), Pakistan in March 1998 (19 cases, 12 deaths), June 2000 (25 cases, 15 deaths), and October 2010 (26 cases, 3 deaths), as well as Turkey in August 2006 (242 cases, 20 deaths), and Iraq in May 2022 (212 cases, 27 deaths). CCHF is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with the drug Ribavirin, which is used in its treatment. 

Diarrhoea has been reported in Angola in May 1998, with 560 confirmed cases and 12 deaths. It is categorized as a gastrointestinal system disease. The condition is associated with several drugs, including Loperamide, Omeprazole, and Proton pump inhibitors, and is linked to the pathogenic roles of the amino acids alanine and aspartic acid, as well as chlorides. 

Influenza is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with a range of drugs such as Oseltamivir, Amantadine, Rimantadine, and Acetylsalicylic acid. It is linked to the Influenza A pathway. Other conditions such as infection, shock, viremia, disseminated intravascular coagulation, hemorrhage, and multiple organ failure are also associated with various drugs, some of which play a role in disease pathogenesis, including taurine in shock and alanine in viremia. Disseminated intravascular coagulation is further classified as a thrombophilia.
317: Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and dozens of deaths, and in 2022 in the People's Republic of Bangladesh, which saw over 7,600 cases and 3000 deaths. Additional outbreaks were documented in countries such as Brazil, India, Pakistan, the Netherlands, Cameroon, El Salvador, and East Timor, with case counts ranging from a few to over 58,000 and death tolls reaching up to 658. Outbreaks have also been reported in smaller territories like Rarotonga, Reunion (France), and São Tomé and Príncipe, as well as in multiple countries across Asia, Africa, and South America in 1998. The disease has been associated with severe outcomes in several instances, including high mortality in the Netherlands and Pakistan. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is further categorized as a subtype of dengue disease. It is also associated with dengue hemorrhagic fever, a more severe form of the condition. Several drugs have been observed in association with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, among others. Some of these drugs are noted for their role in disease pathogenesis, such as dextrose, while others are used in the treatment or study of the disease.
318:
An infection has been associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, the COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, clarithromycin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, cytarabine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, doxorubicin, epirubicin, etoposide, everolimus, the FEC protocol, fludarabine, itraconazole, the LNH 87 protocol, the MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, metronidazole, netilmicin, pemetrexed, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, sirolimus, tetrandrine, topotecan, triamcinolone, vancomycin, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin.

Schistosomiasis is associated with the drugs metrifonate and praziquantel. It is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. Intestinal schistosomiasis is also linked to praziquantel, quercetin, and resveratrol, and is considered a subtype of schistosomiasis.

The disease entity is associated with several substances that play a role in disease pathogenesis, including homocysteine, isoprostanes, mannitol, tocopherol, and ubidecarenone.

Asthma presents with symptoms such as alkalosis, respiratory; anoxia; apnea; birth weight; chest pain; cough; cyanosis; dysphonia; dyspnea; paroxysmal dyspnea; fetal hypoxia; hoarseness; hypercapnia; hyperventilation; hypocapnia; hypoventilation; mouth breathing; neurogenic inflammation; psychophysiologic disorders; respiratory sounds; sleep apnea, obstructive; sleep disorders; sneezing; snoring; tremor; and voice disorders. It is associated with drugs including 1-methylphenanthrene, 8-epi-prostaglandin F2alpha, amides, aminophylline, benzo(a)pyrene, benzofurans, budesonide, captan, carbaryl, carbimazole, carbon tetrachloride, chlordan, co-trimoxazole, contraceptives, oral, dexamethasone, dextromethorphan, EPTC, ephedrine, FR 173657, flunisolide, fungicides, industrial, glutathione, ipratropium, ketotifen, lansoprazole, leukotriene C4, loratadine, nedocromil, neuromuscular blocking agents, paraquat, parathion, particulate matter, phosphodiesterase 4 inhibitors, prednisone, propranolol, resveratrol, roflumilast, salbutamol, salmeterol xinafoate, solvents, synephrine, tobacco smoke pollution, toluene 2,4-diisocyanate, vasodilator agents, butylate, cysteinyl-leukotriene, gamma-tocopherol, methylxanthine, phenanthrene, and trimellitic anhydride. Certain substances, such as we 201, are implicated in the pathogenesis of asthma. The condition is associated with the asthma pathway and is classified as a bronchial disease. It resembles atopic dermatitis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease.
319:
Infection is associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, the COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, clarithromycin, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, cytarabine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, doxorubicin, epirubicin, etoposide, everolimus, exisulind, fludarabine, itraconazole, the LNH 87 protocol, lithium, the MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, metronidazole, netilmicin, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, sirolimus, the TAC protocol, tetrandrine, tobramycin, topotecan, triamcinolone, the VIP-E protocol, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin.

Trachoma is classified as a commensal bacterial infectious disease. Similarly, chlamydia is also categorized as a commensal bacterial infectious disease. Chlamydia infection is associated with several drugs, including azithromycin, ciprofloxacin, doxycycline, erythromycin, INP 0341, INP 0400, melitten, mercuric chloride, minocycline, and trovafloxacin. Eye infections are associated with the drug tobramycin.
320:
Typhoid fever, caused by Salmonella, is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. Outbreaks have been documented in multiple regions, including a significant event in the Republic of Tajikistan in August 1996 with 7,516 confirmed cases, and another in the same country earlier that month with 4,000 cases. A later outbreak occurred in Pakistan in November 2016, involving 5,274 cases. Additionally, an outbreak was reported in Algeria in January 1996, with 910 cases and one death. The disease is associated with the use of antibiotics such as Chloramphenicol and Ciprofloxacin, and its pathogenesis is linked to the biological pathway of Salmonella infection.

Hepatitis E is a viral infectious disease that has caused numerous outbreaks across multiple countries and regions. Notable events include a large outbreak in Sudan (W Equatoria) in September 2004 with 3,753 cases and 55 deaths, and another in the same region in 2021 with 1,143 cases and five deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in Egypt in 2004 with over 2,400 cases, in Chad in 2004 with 1,442 cases, and in Niger in 2017 with 282 cases and 27 deaths. A widespread outbreak in 2022 affected 33 countries across five WHO regions, with 920 cases and 18 deaths. Other significant events occurred in Burkina Faso (2020), Angola (2017), and the United Kingdom (2022), with varying case numbers and fatalities. The disease is also associated with outbreaks in multiple countries including China, South Korea, Italy, and Iran in 2022, and in Chad in 2016 and 2022. Hepatitis E is linked to the viral infectious disease category.

Measles, a viral infectious disease, has been responsible for large-scale outbreaks globally. A major outbreak occurred in Madagascar in October 2018 with 19,539 cases and 39 deaths. Another significant event took place in Ethiopia in May 2022, with 16,814 cases and 182 deaths. Outbreaks have also been reported in Pakistan in January 2022 with 35,319 cases and 156 deaths, and in the Cook Islands in October 2019 with 4,217 cases and 62 deaths. Other notable events include outbreaks in the Central African Republic (North) in January 2019 with 7,626 cases, in Sudan (W Equatoria) in March 2022 with 4,339 cases, and in the United States in January 2015 with 121 cases. A widespread outbreak in 2019 affected the European Region with 34,300 cases and 13 deaths, and another in 2019 in the Americas and Africa with 413,308 cases. The disease is associated with the biological pathway of Measles and is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Hepatitis A, also a viral infectious disease, has been linked to outbreaks in the European Region and the Americas, including Chile and the United States, in June 2016, with 1,173 confirmed cases. The disease is associated with several drugs, including Hepatitis A Vaccines, Ribavirin, Glycyrrhizic acid, Pyrazofurin, Chlorine dioxide, and Amantadine.
321: An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis. A range of drugs are associated with its treatment, including Allopurinol, Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Antimony potassium tartrate, Antimony sodium tartrate, Antiprotozoals, Artemisinin, Desmodium gangeticum extract, Isosorbide dinitrate, Ketoconazole, Meglumine, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Neopterin, Organotin Compounds, Paromomycin, Pentamidine, Reactive Oxygen Species, Thalidomide, Val-Glu-Pro-Ile-Gly-Tyr, amarogentin, amphotericin B, deoxycholate drug combination, and liposomal amphotericin B.
322: No disease information is available in the provided knowledge graph, as both the eKG-DONs and iBKH sources are empty.
323:
Measles outbreaks have been reported globally across multiple years and regions, including significant events in Egypt (2018 and 2019), Ethiopia (2022), Madagascar (2018), Pakistan (2022), the Republic of Ireland (2000), the Cook Islands (2019), the Central African Republic (2019), Sudan (2022), Mexico (2020), Brazil (2018), Ecuador (2015 and 2023), Japan (2018), the United States (2015), Thailand (2023), Somalia (2022), South Africa (2022), Kyrgyzstan (2014), Chile (2023), Guadeloupe (1997), and the European Region (2019), with case counts ranging from single cases to over 40,000 in a single outbreak. The disease is caused by the measles virus and is classified as a viral infectious disease. It is associated with specific biological pathways linked to measles. 

Rabies outbreaks have been documented in France (2004), with one reported death, and in unspecified locations in 1997 and 2024. Rabies is also classified as a viral infectious disease. The drug cinnabarin is associated with rabies, though its role is not specified as contributing to pathogenesis.
324:
Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. It is associated with several drugs, including acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole, which are used in its management. Onchocerciasis and loiasis are both subtypes of filariasis. Onchocerciasis is treated with drugs such as abamectin, albendazole, diethylcarbamazine, and ivermectin, while loiasis is managed using diethylcarbamazine and ivermectin.

Acute monocytic leukemia is a subtype of monocytic leukemia, which itself falls under the broader category of myeloid leukemia. This condition is associated with a range of drugs, including 1,3-benzothiazol-2-yl(2-((2-(3-pyridinyl)ethyl)amino)-4-pyrimidinyl)acetonitrile, 2-(2-amino-3-methoxyphenyl)-4H-1-benzopyran-4-one, arsenic trioxide, cytarabine, pyrazolanthrone, resveratrol, and U 0126, which are used in its treatment or studied in relation to its pathogenesis.
325:
Outbreaks of Zika virus have been reported across multiple countries between 2015 and 2021, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, India, Brazil, Argentina, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, and others. Notable events include a large outbreak in French Guiana in November 2015 with 8,750 confirmed cases, and a significant outbreak in Brazil in January 2016 with 3,174 cases and 38 deaths. The virus has also been associated with cases in India, with outbreaks reported in 2016 and 2021 involving 70 and 3 confirmed cases, respectively. Zika fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. In terms of pharmaceutical associations, the drugs chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide have been observed to be associated with the condition.

Outbreaks of Dengue Fever have occurred in numerous countries across Asia, Africa, South America, and the Caribbean, with major events reported in Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Brazil, India, Cameroon, Cape Verde, The Netherlands, and others. Notable outbreaks include a severe event in Sri Lanka in January 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, a large outbreak in Brazil in May 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths, and a significant outbreak in India in 1996 with 8,170 cases and 319 deaths. More recent outbreaks include a major event in 2023 with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths reported in an unspecified country, and a large outbreak in 2024 with 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths. Dengue disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. A related condition, dengue hemorrhagic fever, is considered a subtype of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, F2-isoprostanes, hydroxychloroquine, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, rolitetracycline, silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A.

Outbreaks of Chikungunya virus disease have been documented in countries such as the United Republic of Tanzania, Congo, Reunion, France, Italy, Spain, Argentina, Egypt, and others. Notable events include a large outbreak in Chad in July 2020 with 27,540 cases and one death, and a significant outbreak in Reunion Island in March 2005 with 3,115 cases. Other outbreaks occurred in Tanzania in 2016 and 2017 with 1,792 and 453 cases, respectively, and in Egypt in May 2018 with 13,978 cases. Chikungunya is classified as a viral infectious disease. The drug chloroquine has been associated with this condition.
326:
Outbreaks of Zika virus have been reported across multiple countries and regions, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, Brazil, Argentina, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Cape Verde, India, and others, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 8,700 in a single event. Notable outbreaks occurred in French Guiana in November 2015 with 8,750 cases and in Brazil in January 2016 with 3,174 cases and 38 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological agents associated with Zika virus include chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide.

Outbreaks of Dengue Fever have been documented in numerous countries, including Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Cameroon, India, Brazil, Egypt, The Netherlands, East Timor, France, Pakistan, Thailand, and others, with case numbers ranging from a few to over 7.6 million in a single event. Major outbreaks occurred in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, in Brazil in 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths, and in 2024 with a reported 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths. The disease is also classified as a viral infectious disease, and its severe form, dengue hemorrhagic fever, is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease. Several drugs are associated with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, F2-isoprostanes, hydroxychloroquine, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, rolitetracycline, silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A.

Outbreaks of Chikungunya virus disease have been reported in the United Republic of Tanzania, the United States, Congo, Cameroon, France, Chad, Reunion, St Martin, Italy, Spain, Argentina, and Egypt, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 27,000. A significant outbreak occurred in Chad in 2020 with 27,540 cases and 1 death, while another in Reunion Island in 2005 involved 3,115 cases. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Chloroquine is associated with chikungunya virus disease, and the condition is recognized as a subtype of viral infectious disease.
327: Chagas disease is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, Benznidazole, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Nifurtimox, and Nitrofural, which are studied or used in its treatment. The disease is linked to the Chagas disease pathway.
328:
Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. It is associated with the drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole. Loiasis is a subtype of filariasis and is treated with diethylcarbamazine and ivermectin. Onchocerciasis, another condition within the filariasis group, is also managed using diethylcarbamazine and ivermectin.

Infections are associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, bortezomib, the COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, cytarabine, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, doxorubicin, epirubicin, etoposide, everolimus, exisulind, the FEC protocol, fludarabine, itraconazole, the LNH 87 protocol, lithium, the MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, metronidazole, netilmicin, pemetrexed, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, sirolimus, the TAC protocol, tetrandrine, tobramycin, topotecan, triamcinolone, the VIP-E protocol, vancomycin, asulacrine, and liposomal doxorubicin.

Thromboembolism is associated with drugs such as acetylsalicylic acid, aminocaproic acid, anagrelide, anthracyclines, busulfan, the CMF regimen, the CMFVP protocol, carboplatin, carmustine, chlorotrianisene, cisplatin, clopidogrel, cocaine, combined oral contraceptives, contrast media, cyclosporine, dacarbazine, danaparoid, dexamethasone, dicoumarol, doxorubicin, ethinyl estradiol-norgestrel combination, Femovan, fluorouracil, gestodene, hirudins, hydroxyurea, imetelstat, iobitridol, iohexol, iopamidol, ioxaglic acid, meloxicam, nadroparin, norethindrone acetate, phenprocoumon, pipobroman, piroxicam, prednisone, progestins, pyrroles, steroids, thalidomide, tretinoin, vincristine, warfarin, drospirenone/ethinyl estradiol, fenflumizole, heparin-dihydergot, and triglycerides.

Cancer is classified as a disease of cellular proliferation and is associated with drugs such as 1'-acetoxyestragole, 2'-benzoyloxycinnamaldehyde, 4-demethyl-6-O-methyldoxorubicin, 5,7-dihydroxy-6-methoxy-2-phenylchromen-4-one, (Ni(Py-3')TPP-Ru(phen)2Cl)(PF6), albumin-bound paclitaxel, benzoyl peroxide, carmustine, celecoxib, clofenotane, curcumin, diazinon, EC131 conjugate, etoposide, fish oil, idarubicin, isothiocyanates, melitten, methylnitrosourea, mirex, N-acetylcochinol-O-phosphate, nelfinavir, nimustine, PC protocol, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, polyvinyl chloride, pyridoxine, radiation-sensitizing agents, radioactive pollutants, resveratrol, SC-203048, safflower oil, sirolimus, sunitinib, talabostat, tobacco smoke pollution, topotecan, toxaphene, triptolide, vehicle emissions, antineoplaston A5, aristolochic acid I, atranorin, benzo(a)pyrene-7,8-dihydrodiol-9,10-epoxide-DNA, beta-D-glucosylisophosphoramide mustard, climacostol, cucurbitacin D, deguelin, methylcholanthrene, and schizandrin B. The pathogenesis of cancer involves the drugs amines, glycine, kynurenic acid, ethyl arachidonate, and trimethionine.
329:
An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which itself is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Treatment options for Visceral Leishmaniasis include a range of drugs such as Amphotericin B, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Paromomycin, Pentamidine, and liposomal amphotericin B, among others. Additionally, compounds like amarogentin, amphotericin B, deoxycholate drug combination, and artemisinin have been associated with treatment.

Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Ethiopia in 2024, and Pakistan in 2022 with 3,400,000 cases. Malaria is also categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The disease is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, oliguria, pallor, pruritus, hemoglobinuria, and neurologic manifestations. Key drugs used in the treatment of malaria include chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, pyrimethamine, sulfadoxine, lumefantrine, atovaquone, and proguanil, often used in combination. Some drugs, such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. Malaria is linked to the Malaria pathway and is associated with various insecticides and environmental factors.

Cutaneous leishmaniasis and mucocutaneous leishmaniasis are both subtypes of leishmaniasis, with treatment involving drugs such as Amphotericin B, antimony compounds, meglumine antimoniate, pentamidine, and itraconazole. Leishmaniasis as a whole is associated with pathways related to the disease and is treated with a broad range of antiprotozoals, including miltefosine, ketoconazole, and artemisinin derivatives. Tuberculosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with pathways related to the disease. It is treated with drugs such as isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol, streptomycin, and bedaquiline, among others. Some drugs, including lopinavir, phosphorus, and phosphates, are involved in the disease’s pathogenesis. Hepatomegaly and lymphadenopathy are conditions associated with various drugs, including 2,2,5,7,8-pentamethyl-1-hydroxychroman, alpidem, zidovudine, and AZD8848, respectively. Splenomegaly is linked to drugs such as carbamazepine, prednisone, and environmental pollutants.
330:
Typhoid fever has been associated with multiple outbreak events, including a significant outbreak in the Republic of Tajikistan on August 6, 1996, which reported 7,516 confirmed cases, and another in the same country on August 2, 1996, with 4,000 confirmed cases. A further outbreak occurred in Pakistan on November 1, 2016, involving 5,274 cases, and a separate event in Algeria on January 22, 1996, which recorded 910 cases and one death. Typhoid fever is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway of Salmonella infection. Treatment options include the drugs Chloramphenicol and Ciprofloxacin, which are commonly associated with its management.

Malaria has been reported in several regions, including an outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, and another in the same country on July 14, 1999. A major outbreak occurred in Burundi on May 25, 1999, with 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks were documented in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 cases reported. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is linked to the biological pathway of malaria. The condition is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, vomiting, and neurological manifestations. Several drugs are associated with its treatment or pathogenesis, including Artemether, Artesunate, Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, Quinine, and Mefloquine, as well as combinations such as atovaquone-proguanil and amodiaquine-artesunate. Drugs like Cholesterol, Glutamic acid, and bilirubin are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis.
331: Schistosomiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The condition is associated with several drugs, including Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is also linked to the use of Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol.
332: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths, and another outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998. Additional outbreaks were documented in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with the latter reporting 3,400,000 cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is closely related to other parasitic infections such as leishmaniasis and filariasis. The disease is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, coma, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, pallor, retinal hemorrhage, and neurological manifestations. Key drugs used in its treatment include chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemisinin, artesunate, mefloquine, primaquine, pyrimethamine, atovaquone-proguanil combination, and sulfonamides. Some drugs are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, including cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. The disease is linked to the malaria pathway and shares clinical and pathological similarities with conditions such as anemia, celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, and thymus cancer.
333: Parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease is classified as a type of parasitic infectious disease and is associated with a range of anthelmintic drugs, including Albendazole, Anthelmintics, Closantel, Ivermectin, Levamisole, Mebendazole, Netobimin, Niclosamide, Praziquantel, and Thiabendazole. Schistosoma mansonii infection is linked to several drugs, such as 3,7-dimethyloctan-1-ol, Acetylcysteine, Artemether, IA 4, Nitric Oxide, Oxamniquine, and Praziquantel, with triglycerides playing a role in the disease's pathogenesis. Schistosomiasis is treated with Metrifonate and Praziquantel. The condition known as schistosomiasis is a subtype of parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. Intestinal schistosomiasis is associated with Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol, while urinary schistosomiasis is linked to Metrifonate. Hookworm disease is managed using Albendazole, Dichlorvos, Ivermectin, Oxantel pamoate, and tribendimidine.
334:
Disseminated intravascular coagulation is classified as a thrombophilia. It has been associated with numerous drugs, including acenocoumarol, acetylsalicylic acid, aminocaproic acid, amphetamine, argatroban, atorvastatin, carbamazepine, carmustine, cefoxitin, cisplatin, cladribine, clofibrate, cocaine, colchicine, contrast media, dalteparin, diamorphine, diethylstilbestrol diphosphate, dinoprostone, dronabinol, edaravone, epinephrine, griseofulvin, heparin, heparin calcium, hirudins, lamotrigine, lepirudin, methotrimeprazine, midomafetamine, nafamostat, phenformin, phenmetrazine, phenytoin, piperacillin, polyanetholesulfonate, progesterone, proguanil, pyrimethamine, rifampicin, sulfasalazine, tetracycline, thalidomide, thiopental, tranexamic acid, tranylcypromine, valproic acid, vindesine, estrogens, and propylene dichloride.

Edema is associated with a wide range of drugs, including 1,4-anthraquinone, 2-(4-((dimethylamino)carbonyl)-3-fluorophenyl)-alpha,alpha-dimethyl-N-1,3,4-thiadiazol-2-yl-5H-(1)benzopyrano(2,3-b)pyridin-5-acetamide, adenosine-5'-(N-ethylcarboxamide), anesthetics, azacitidine, benzoyl peroxide, chlorpropamide, cisplatin, clonazepam, clonidine, contraceptives, oral, combined, crack cocaine, dibenzothiophene, edotreotide, estramustine, femovan, fludrocortisone, fluorouracil, fluvoxamine, heparin, iridoid glycosides, local anesthetics, mechlorethamine, metamizole, methysergide, metoclopramide, mizolastine, N,N-diethylcyclophosphamide, N-((4-dimethylaminophenyl)methyl)-N-(4-isopropylphenyl)-7-methoxy-1,2,3,4-tetrahydronaphthalen-1-carboxamide, N-oleylhomovanillamide, nicardipine, nifedipine, nortriptyline, plant extracts, RU24858, ruthenium red, SR 48968, triamcinolone acetonide, vitamin A, ZK 216348, zafirlukast, citronellal, costunolide, hydroperoxyisophosphamide, morin, perfluorooctanoic acid, somatotropin releasing hormone (1-29)-amide, N-Ac-Tyr(1)-Phe(2)-, and sufosfamide.

Vomiting is associated with multiple drugs, including 3-(2-methoxybenzylamino)-2-phenylpiperidine, AVI protocol, alizapride, altretamine, amodiaquine, anticoagulants, buflomedil, bupivacaine, butaclamol, COPE regimen, carbetocin, casopitant, cocaine, cyclizine, cyclosporine, diatrizoate, diatrizoate meglumine, diclofenac, dihydrocodeine, enalapril, ethanol, etoposide, FEC protocol, felbamate, fentanyl, fluorescein, GDC-0152, gemeprost, glycyrrhizic acid, granisetron, halothane, IFL protocol, methotrexate, niridazole, okadaic acid, olanzapine, palonosetron, TER 286, tetracyclines, thioridazine, trovafloxacin, vitamin A, acetaminophen-hydrocodone combination, aluminum phosphide, cannabinoids, deoxynivalenol, digitalis glycosides, indopan, pentamethylmelamine, and triethyl phosphine gold. Hemolytic anemia is classified as a normocytic anemia. It is associated with drugs such as auranofin, cefadroxil, cefotaxime, ceftazidime, cefuroxime, chlorambucil, chlorpropamide, cimetidine, ciprofloxacin, cladribine, cyclophosphamide, dapsone, dextropropoxyphene, diatrizoate meglumine, doxorubicin, fenfluramine, flucloxacillin, gemcitabine, gentamicins, lawsone, methotrexate, methysergide, meticillin, misoprostol, nitrofurantoin, oxaliplatin, peginterferon alfa-2b, penicillamine, pentamidine, phenazopyridine, phenobarbital, piroxicam, prednisone, pyrimethamine, ranitidine, resorcinols, ribavirin, succimer, sulbactam, sulindac, trimethoprim, vitamin A, water pollutants, chemical, beta-lactam antibacterials, penicillins, corticosteroids, eunalgit, lead nitrate, naphthalene, propylene dichloride, and sulfonamides.

Erythema is associated with drugs including aminolevulinic acid, amoxicillin, betamethasone, Break-Free CLP, carmustine, cefuroxime, climbazole, clindamycin, dextran sulfate, diatrizoate, diclofenac, dimethyl sulfoxide, docetaxel, doxorubicin, efavirenz, epoprostenol, erythromycin, everolimus, fullerenes, glycopyrrolate, hydroxyurea, imiquimod, isosorbide, mefloquine, methohexital, methyl nicotinate, methylprednisolone aceponate, naltrexone, nicotine, plant extracts, plant preparations, potassium chloride, propylene glycol, propylthiouracil, sulfasalazine, temozolomide, tetradecanoylphorbol acetate, thiethylperazine, thioridazine, tretinoin, trichloroethylene, trinitrobenzenesulfonic acid, vancomycin, yohimbine, chromium hexavalent ion, delta-aminolevulinic acid methyl ester, esorubicin, nonane, perhexiline maleate, and trimellitic anhydride.

Thrombocytopenia is classified as a blood platelet disease. It is associated with drugs such as 1-arabinofuranosylcytosine-5'-stearylphosphate, 4'-deoxy-4'-iododoxorubicin, ajmaline, amrinone, ancrod, B-CAVe protocol, BOP-CISCA-POMB-ACE regimen, benzylpenicillin, bivalirudin, CF regimen, carbimazole, cephalosporins, cladribine, clarithromycin, danaparoid, desmopressin, diclofenac, didanosine, doxifluridine, doxorubicin, ELF protocol, emitefur, enoxaparin, epirubicin, estramustine, etoposide, Folfox protocol, fondaparinux, furosemide, gefitinib, heparin calcium, ifosfamide, iopromide, JET protocol, lepirudin, leucovorin, mercaptopurine, methotrexate, mitomycin, mycophenolic acid, PC protocol, propylthiouracil, thalidomide, topotecan, valproic acid, vancomycin, venlafaxine hydrochloride, zidovudine, chlorozotocin, and chromium tripicolinate. The drug aspartic acid has been implicated in the pathogenesis of thrombocytopenia, and the condition is associated with the tight junction pathway.

Ecchymosis is associated with drugs including amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate combination, amphetamine, anticoagulants, cefotaxime, dronabinol, heparin, levonorgestrel, mefloquine, midomafetamine, paroxetine, pentoxifylline, and phenylbutazone.

Fever is associated with drugs such as 4,4'-diphenylmethane diisocyanate, alprostadil, aminosalicylic acids, antihypertensives, antitubercular agents, aspartame, atropine, capsaicin, carbenicillin, carbimazole, cefadroxil, cefalotin, clindamycin, daunorubicin, dichloralphenazone, fentanyl, glutathione, interleukin 1 receptor antagonist protein, isoflurane, ketanserin, lamivudine, levamisole, linezolid, lipopolysaccharides, morphine, nevirapine, nicotinamide mononucleotide, nifedipine, nimesulide, olanzapine, omeprazole, paclitaxel, petroleum, phenylbutazone, physostigmine, promethazine, propofol, quetiapine fumarate, risperidone, S-(N,N-diethylaminoethyl) isobutyl methylphosphothiolate, sirolimus, succinylcholine, tegafur, temozolomide, turpentine, carbabenzpyride, dexamyl, kelfiprim, p-chloroamphetamine, and sodium arsenite. The drug hydroxyindoleacetic acid has been implicated in the pathogenesis of fever.
335: Taeniasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. Cysticercosis is a condition that is categorized as a subtype of taeniasis. The treatment of cysticercosis involves the use of drugs including Albendazole, Dexamethasone, Praziquantel, and Steroids.
336:
Outbreaks of Coronavirus have been documented across multiple countries and time periods, including Albania in 1996 with 66 cases and 7 deaths, Angola in 1999 with 661 cases and 41 deaths, and again in 2006 with 34 cases and 7 deaths, as well as in Egypt in 2009, 2013, and 2019, and in Pakistan in 2011 with 84 cases and again in 2011 with 1 case. Additional outbreaks occurred in countries such as Chad, Cameroon, China, Guinea Bissau, Israel, Somalia, Sudan, Yemen, Niger, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, Brazil, Philippines, Madagascar, Ukraine, Mozambique, and the Republic of Tajikistan, with case counts ranging from 1 to 225 and including fatalities in several instances, notably 12 deaths in Tajikistan in 2010 and 10 deaths in Tajikistan in 2010. The virus has been reported in various regions including Central and East Africa, the Middle East, South Asia, and parts of Europe and Southeast Asia.

COVID-19 is classified as a coronavirus infectious disease. It is associated with several drugs, including Remdesivir, Lopinavir, Ritonavir, Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, Baricitinib, and others, some of which are used in combination therapies. The disease is linked to the biological pathway associated with Coronavirus disease - COVID-19. Influenza is categorized as a viral infectious disease and is associated with multiple drugs such as Oseltamivir, Amantadine, Rimantadine, and others, as well as the Influenza A pathway.
337:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and several deaths. Outbreaks have also been documented in Sri Lanka, with a major epidemic in 2017 involving over 80,000 cases and 215 deaths. Other notable outbreaks include those in Brazil (2002 and 2008), India (1996 and 2003), Pakistan (2019 and 2022), the Netherlands (2004), and East Timor (2005 and 2022), with case counts ranging from a few dozen to over 500,000. Additional outbreaks have been reported in countries such as Egypt, Cameroon, Cape Verde, Thailand, Honduras, El Salvador, and Burkina Faso, with varying case numbers and mortality rates. A large-scale outbreak was reported in 2024 affecting unspecified regions, with 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths. The disease has also been recorded in South America, France, Spain, Portugal, Uruguay, and the French Caribbean, with some events involving suspected or confirmed cases and associated fatalities.

Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is further categorized into subtypes including dengue hemorrhagic fever and dengue shock syndrome, both of which are considered forms of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with the condition, including antivirals such as doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and rolitetracycline, as well as compounds like 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, and hinokiflavone. Other associated substances include silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, and podocarpusflavone A. Additionally, various environmental and pharmaceutical agents such as 2,4-dichlorophenol, carbamazepine, dichlorvos, disinfectants, halogens, lithium, lorazepam, mezlocillin, phenol and its derivatives, quaternary ammonium compounds, risperidone, styrene, and valproic acid have been observed in association with the disease.
338: An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway linked to leishmaniasis. Treatment options for the disease include a range of drugs such as Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, and various other antiprotozoals, including liposomal amphotericin B, Artemisinin, and several experimental compounds like 2,5-bis(2-(2-propoxy)-4-(2-pyridylimino)aminophenyl)furan and 2-(2''-dichloroacetamidobenzyl)-3-(3'-indolylquinoline). Additional agents such as Allopurinol, Ketoconazole, Paromomycin, and reactive oxygen species have also been associated with treatment or pathogenic mechanisms.
339:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and several deaths, and in Sri Lanka in 2017 with over 80,000 cases and 215 fatalities. Additional outbreaks were documented in Cameroon, Bangladesh, Mayotte, Barbados, India, the Netherlands, France, Pakistan, and other countries, with case counts ranging from a few to over 500,000 in a single event in 2023. The disease is caused by the Dengue virus and is associated with a range of pharmaceutical agents, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, Doxycycline, Hydroxychloroquine, and several other compounds. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its severe form, dengue hemorrhagic fever, is considered a subtype of dengue disease.

Chikungunya virus disease has been reported in outbreaks across multiple countries, including the United Republic of Tanzania, Congo, Chad, Reunion, France, Italy, Spain, Argentina, Egypt, and the Caribbean region, with case numbers ranging from a few to over 27,000 in Chad in 2020. The disease is also classified as a viral infectious disease. It is associated with the drug Chloroquine, which is used in its treatment or study. The disease shares epidemiological and clinical features with other viral infectious diseases.

Malaria has been documented in outbreaks in the United Republic of Tanzania, Burundi, Ethiopia, and Pakistan, with a particularly large outbreak in Pakistan in 2022 involving 3.4 million cases. The disease is caused by parasitic protozoa and is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, coma, diarrhea, vomiting, pallor, purpura, and neurological manifestations. It is linked to several biological pathways, most notably the malaria pathway itself. Treatment involves a variety of drugs, including Artemether, Artesunate, Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, Quinine, and others, with some drugs also playing a role in disease pathogenesis, such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin.
340: Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and several deaths. Outbreaks have also been documented in countries such as India, Brazil, the Netherlands, Pakistan, Egypt, Cameroon, El Salvador, and the Republic of Panama, with case counts ranging from a few to over 58,000 in a single event. Notably, large-scale outbreaks were recorded in the Netherlands in 2004, with more than 58,000 cases and over 600 deaths, and in Bangladesh in 2022, with over 50,000 cases and more than 200 deaths. Additional outbreaks have occurred in East Timor, Vietnam, Cape Verde, Reunion (France), and other parts of South America, Africa, and Southeast Asia, with some events involving hundreds to thousands of cases and varying mortality rates. A particularly severe outbreak was reported in 2024 with approximately 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths, though the specific country was not specified. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its severe forms include dengue hemorrhagic fever and dengue shock syndrome, both of which are subtypes of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with the condition, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and various other compounds such as hinokiflavone and podocarpusflavone A, which are either studied in relation to the disease or observed in concurrent research.
341:
Lymphedema is classified as a lymphatic system disease. It is associated with the drugs Melphalan and Sirolimus, which are observed in conjunction with the condition, and caprolactone is implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. Biological pathways linked to lymphedema include AKT phosphorylation of nuclear targets, focal adhesion, the RAF/MAP kinase cascade, and signaling to p38 via RIT and RIN.

Filariasis is categorized as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. It is associated with the drugs Acetylsalicylic acid and Levamisole.

Hookworm disease is associated with the drugs Albendazole, Dichlorvos, Ivermectin, Oxantel pamoate, and tribendimidine.
342:
Inflammatory bowel disease is classified as an intestinal disease and is associated with multiple drugs, including Azathioprine, Corticotropin, Curcumin, Dexamethasone, Dextran Sulfate, Glucosamine, Heparin, Leukotriene B4, Mercaptopurine, Mesalazine, Methotrexate, Metronidazole, Nabumetone, Naproxen, Olsalazine, Omeprazole, Polaprezinc, Prednisolone, Prednisone, Rifaximin, Rutin, Sulfasalazine, Tioguanine, Tobacco Smoke Pollution, Trinitrobenzenesulfonic Acid, Zileuton, and capsiate, some of which are also implicated in the disease's pathogenesis through their role in Arachidonic Acid metabolism. The condition is linked to several biological pathways, including Antigen processing and presentation, Autophagy, Cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, Inflammatory bowel disease, and JAK-STAT signaling pathway.

Rectal prolapse is categorized as a rectal disease.

Dysentery is classified as an intestinal infectious disease and is associated with the drug Nalidixic acid.

Trichuriasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with the drugs Dichlorvos and Oxantel pamoate.
343:
An outbreak of HIV infection-AIDS occurred in Pakistan on April 25, 2019, with 876 confirmed cases. HIV infection is associated with the pathway of human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection and is classified as a viral infectious disease. It is also linked to opportunistic infections, which are often treated with drugs such as Dexamethasone and Temozolomide. The disease is further associated with several drugs, including Adefovir dipivoxil, Atazanavir Sulfate, Lamivudine, Tenofovir, and various antiretroviral combinations such as Lopinavir-ritonavir and Emtricitabine/Tenofovir Disoproxil Fumarate. Other drugs associated with HIV infection include Isoniazid, Iron, and Vitamin A, which may play roles in disease management or pathogenesis.

Cryptococcosis and candidiasis are both classified as opportunistic mycoses and are associated with a range of antifungal drugs, including Amphotericin B, Fluconazole, Flucytosine, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, and others such as Anidulafungin, Caspofungin, and Voriconazole. Pneumocystis infections are managed with drugs like Atovaquone, Co-Trimoxazole, Dapsone, and Pentamidine. Tuberculosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with a broad range of drugs, including Isoniazid, Rifampicin, Ethambutol, Pyrazinamide, and newer agents such as Bedaquiline and OPC-67683. Some drugs, such as Lopinavir and Phosphorus, are noted to play a role in the pathogenesis of tuberculosis. Influenza is a viral infectious disease associated with drugs like Oseltamivir, Rimantadine, Amantadine, and various natural compounds such as Quercetin and Resveratrol. The disease is linked to the Influenza A pathway. Splenomegaly and hepatomegaly are conditions associated with a wide array of drugs, including Amiodarone, Methotrexate, Prednisone, Verapamil, and various environmental pollutants, heavy metals, and industrial chemicals. These associations suggest potential drug-induced or toxin-related etiologies for organomegaly.
344:
Rift Valley Fever has been associated with multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Egypt, United Republic of Tanzania, Madagascar, Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Niger, South Africa (KwaZulu Natal), China, Cameroon, and Mayotte. Notable events include a large outbreak in Egypt in 2007 with 601 cases and 211 deaths, another in 2008 with 698 cases and 222 deaths, and a significant outbreak in Tanzania in 2007 with 264 cases and 109 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in 2010 in South Africa and Tanzania, in 2012 in Egypt and Tanzania, and in 2016 in Niger and China. The disease has also been reported in 2019 in Mayotte and in 2020 in Tanzania and Egypt. Rift Valley Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological agents associated with this condition include Galidesivir and Ribavirin.

Hemorrhagic fever has been documented in multiple outbreaks across the United Republic of Tanzania, Congo, Sudan (W Equatoria), Gabon, the Republic of the Congo, Angola, and other regions. Major outbreaks occurred in Tanzania in 2000, 2001, 2007, and 2012, with case counts ranging from 8 to 395 and death tolls from 4 to 150. In Congo, outbreaks were reported in 1996, 2001, 2002, 2003, 2007, 2010, 2012, and 2009, with case numbers from 2 to 60 and deaths from 2 to 50. Other notable events include outbreaks in Sudan (2004), Angola (2005), and Cameroon (1999). The disease is associated with various drugs, including Active Hexose Correlated Compound, Adefovir dipivoxil, Corticosterone, Indinavir, Lamivudine, Mycophenolic acid, Piperonyl butoxide, Ribavirin, Tenofovir, Zidovudine, and resmethrin, some of which are linked to the disease’s pathogenesis.

An outbreak of Myocarditis occurred in Sri Lanka on March 17, 2005, and another in Greece on April 25, 2002, involving 39 confirmed cases and 3 deaths. Myocarditis is classified as an extrinsic cardiomyopathy. Several drugs have been associated with this condition, including amiodarone, amitriptyline, amphetamines, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, anthracyclines, bisphenol A, carvedilol, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, hydroxychloroquine, imipramine, methyldopa, methylprednisolone, prednisolone, prednisone, testosterone, and various antipsychotics, corticosteroids, and other agents such as black widow spider venom, catecholamines, and resveratrol.

Outbreaks of Visceral Leishmaniasis were reported in Pakistan in January 2002, May 2002, and June 2002, with case counts of 738 and 200,000, respectively. Visceral Leishmaniasis is a form of leishmaniasis and falls under the category of parasitic protozoa infectious diseases. It is associated with a range of drugs, including amphotericin B, antimony, antimony sodium gluconate, meglumine antimoniate, miltefosine, pentamidine, paromomycin, and liposomal amphotericin B, as well as other agents such as allopurinol, ketoconazole, and artemisinin. The disease is also linked to the leishmaniasis pathway.

Cutaneous leishmaniasis and mucocutaneous leishmaniasis are both subtypes of leishmaniasis. Cutaneous leishmaniasis is associated with drugs such as allopurinol, amphotericin B, antimony, antimony sodium gluconate, meglumine antimoniate, miltefosine, itraconazole, ketoconazole, pentamidine, pentoxifylline, and various other antiprotozoals and antimicrobials. Mucocutaneous leishmaniasis shares similar drug associations, including amphotericin B, antimony, antimony sodium gluconate, meglumine antimoniate, and pentamidine.

Meningoencephalitis is classified as a central nervous system disease. It is associated with a broad range of drugs, including acyclovir, amoxicillin, ceftriaxone, isoniazid, vancomycin, methylprednisolone, nevirapine, and others such as ibuprofen, chlorambucil, and fibrinolytic agents.

Leishmaniasis is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with multiple drugs, including amphotericin B, antimony, meglumine antimoniate, miltefosine, pentamidine, and various other antiprotozoals, arsenic compounds, and novel compounds such as 5,7-dihydroxy-8-(2-methylbutanoyl)-6-(3-methylbutyl)-4-phenyl-chroman-2-one and mammea A-BB. The disease is also linked to the leishmaniasis pathway.

Chagas disease, also known as American trypanosomiasis, is classified as a trypanosomiasis. It is associated with drugs such as benznidazole, nifurtimox, and nitrofural, and is linked to the Chagas disease pathway. Trypanosomiasis is itself a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with drugs including antimony, arsenic compounds, pentamidine, and borrelidin.

An outbreak of Salmonellosis occurred in the United States on June 2, 2004, with 32 confirmed cases. A second outbreak was reported in France on December 2, 2017, involving 35 confirmed cases. A third outbreak took place in the United States on January 1, 2015, with 124 confirmed cases. Salmonellosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. The compound cinnamaldehyde is associated with this condition. 

Diarrhoea, also referred to as Diarrhea, was reported in Angola on May 26, 1998, with 560 confirmed cases and 12 deaths. Diarrhoea is categorized as a gastrointestinal system disease. The condition is associated with the role of certain substances in disease pathogenesis, including alanine and aspartic acid, and chlorides. Several drugs are also associated with diarrhoea, including amines, bile acids and salts, antibiotics, antivirals, chemotherapeutic agents, and various other compounds such as curcumin, rifampicin, and lipopolysaccharide from Escherichia coli O111 B4.
347: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999, Pakistan in 2022 with 3.4 million confirmed cases, and Ethiopia in 2024. It is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Treatment involves a variety of drugs, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, mefloquine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, atovaquone-proguanil, lumefantrine, and sulfonamides, as well as combinations such as amodiaquine-artesunate and mefloquine-sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine. Certain drugs are noted to play a role in disease pathogenesis, including cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. The disease is linked to the malaria pathway, and its management includes the use of insecticides and other agents such as dapsone, doxycycline, and triazoles.

The disease is associated with a range of drugs, including Acyclovir, Ganciclovir, and Pyrimethamine, and is classified as a brain disease. It is also linked to the TCR signaling pathway and is considered a viral infectious disease. The condition shares characteristics with other diseases such as encephalitis, tremor, gliosis, necrosis, and neurodegenerative disease, and is associated with various drugs that may play a role in its pathogenesis, including calcium, sorbitol, and tryptophan.

Yellow Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various countries, including Congo, Brazil, Cameroon, Angola, Ecuador, Burkina Faso, Egypt, Nigeria, Sudan, Mali, and others, spanning from 1996 to 2022. Outbreaks have varied in scale, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 3,000, and fatalities reported in several instances, including 165 deaths in Egypt in 2012 and 345 deaths in Angola in 2016. The virus has been consistently identified in these events, with confirmed cases and deaths documented in both tropical and subtropical regions. Yellow Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Dengue Fever has been associated with numerous outbreaks globally, particularly in Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, India, the Netherlands, Brazil, and other countries across Asia, Africa, and South America. Notable events include a large outbreak in the Netherlands in 2004 with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths, a significant outbreak in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, and a major outbreak in 2023 reported with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths. The disease has also been documented in countries such as Pakistan, Egypt, and the Philippines, with case numbers ranging from a few to over 100,000. Dengue Fever is categorized as a viral infectious disease, and its severe form, dengue hemorrhagic fever, is considered a subtype of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with Dengue Fever, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, Doxycycline, F2-Isoprostanes, Hydroxychloroquine, Hydroxyeicosatetraenoic Acids, Rolitetracycline, Silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A.

The virus has been detected in both endemic and non-endemic regions, including isolated cases in The Netherlands and France.
354: Abscesses have been associated with a range of drugs, including Ampicillin, Aurothioglucose, Aztreonam, Buprenorphine, Carbenicillin, Cefalotin, Cefmetazole, Ceftazidime, Ceftriaxone, Chlorpromazine, Ciprofloxacin, Daptomycin, Diazepam, Drugs, Chinese Herbal, Floxuridine, Gentamicins, Linezolid, Meticillin, Metronidazole, Nafcillin, Paraldehyde, Phenacetin, Promethazine, Sultamicillin, Tobramycin, Vancomycin, beta-lactam antibacterials, penicillins, cefuroxime axetil, and perilla seed oil. 355: Leptospirosis has been associated with multiple outbreak events across different regions and time periods, including a 2022 outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania with 20 confirmed cases and 3 deaths, a 1997 outbreak in India reporting 413 cases and 48 deaths, a 2000 outbreak in Malaysia with 37 cases, another 2004 outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania involving 141 cases and 6 deaths, and a 2000 outbreak in Canada with 6 confirmed cases. Leptospirosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. It is also associated with kidney-related conditions, particularly nephritis, which is a broader category of kidney disease. Several drugs have been linked to nephritis, including acetaminophen, diclofenac, indomethacin, ibuprofen, and various antibiotics such as ampicillin, gentamicins, and sulfasalazine. Additionally, other drugs such as doxorubicin, pentamidine, and streptomycin are associated with the condition, and some, like creatinine, are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. The condition is also linked to exposure to environmental agents such as cadmium, mercury, and tetrachlorodibenzodioxin.
356:
Obesity is characterized by a range of symptoms including body weight increase, bulimia, hyperphagia, hypoventilation, obesity, obesity hypoventilation syndrome, abdominal obesity, morbid obesity, overweight, pediatric obesity, Prader-Willi syndrome, sarcopenia, obstructive sleep apnea, thinness, weight gain, and weight loss. It is classified as a form of overnutrition and shares clinical and metabolic similarities with polycystic ovary syndrome and type 2 diabetes mellitus. Several drugs are associated with obesity, including 2,5-dichlorophenol, 3,3',4',5,7-pentamethylquercetin, acetylcysteine, aminorex, anticholesteremic agents, bisphenol A, caffeine, cholesterol, dietary sugars, enalapril, excitatory amino acid agents, fenfluramine, fenretinide, fluvoxamine, folic acid, fructose, fumagillin, geraniol, glutathione, glyphosate, linoleic acids, methoxychlor, oleanolic acid, phentermine, phthalic acids, pioglitazone, polychlorinated biphenyls, prednisolone acetate, resveratrol, rimonabant, risperidone, thallium, yohimbine, alpha-naphthoflavone, bis(4-hydroxyphenyl)sulfone, chromium histidinate, davallialactone, deoxynivalenol, evodiamine, fructooligosaccharide, oleoylethanolamide, and raspberry ketone. Additionally, certain compounds such as eltanolone, nitroprusside, nucleotides, and valproic acid are implicated in the pathogenesis of obesity. The condition is associated with key biological pathways including neuroactive ligand-receptor interaction, oleoyl-phe metabolism, PPAR signaling pathway, and regulation of lipolysis in adipocytes.

Scabies is classified as a mite infestation and is associated with several drugs, including benzyl benzoate, ivermectin, lindane, permethrin, piperonyl butoxide, butyl phosphorotrithioate, diethyl maleate, and sulfur.

Dementia is classified as a cognitive disorder.

Impetigo is classified as a form of pyoderma and is associated with the drugs azithromycin and gatifloxacin.

Rheumatic fever is classified as a bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the drugs acetylsalicylic acid, methotrexate, prednisone, and antimacrophage globulin.

Glomerulonephritis is classified as a form of nephritis and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including 3-acetyl-5-methyltetronic acid, adrenomedullin, ampicillin, anti-bacterial agents, antilymphocyte serum, benazepril, calcitriol, candesartan cilexetil, cefuroxime, chlorpropamide, cisplatin, creatinine, daunorubicin, diethylhexyl phthalate, enalapril, enterotoxins, everolimus, fosfomycin, hydralazine, hydrochlorothiazide, irbesartan, levamisole, lithium, mebendazole, methimazole, oxycodone, phentermine, phenylbutazone, pirfenidone, platelet aggregation inhibitors, prednisolone, prednisone, propylthiouracil, puromycin aminonucleoside, rifampicin, sulfadiazine, superantigens, telmisartan, thimerosal, trichlormethiazide, troglitazone, valsartan, vancomycin, vitamin E, XLF-III-43, corticosteroids, staphylococcal enterotoxin B, isoniazid/pyrazinamide/rifampin, mannuronic acid, and ochratoxin A. Certain substances, including amines and malonaldehyde, are also involved in the disease’s pathogenesis.
357: Brucellosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. It is associated with several drugs, including Cholesterol, LDL, HL 004, Ketoconazole, Rifampicin, and Tetracycline, which are often studied or used in conjunction with the condition. Spondylitis and inflammatory spondylopathy are both categorized as subtypes of spondyloarthropathy, with spondylitis specifically linked to the drug Nimesulide. Tuberculosis is also a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with a broad range of drugs, including Amikacin, Bedaquiline, Isoniazid, Rifampicin, and various others such as ethambutol, streptomycin, and pyrazinamide, many of which are used in combination regimens. Additionally, certain drugs like Lopinavir, Phosphorus, and phosphates are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. The condition is linked to the tuberculosis pathway. Leprosy, another primary bacterial infectious disease, presents with a wide array of symptoms including fever, skin and oral manifestations, neuralgia, paralysis, sensory disorders, and vision and gait disturbances. It is associated with drugs such as Chloroquine, Clofazimine, Dapsone, Prednisolone, Rifampicin, and Thalidomide, and also involves reactive oxygen species and pyrroles. The disease is linked to several biological pathways, including antigen processing and presentation, cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, natural killer cell-mediated cytotoxicity, protein processing in the endoplasmic reticulum, and Toll-like receptor signaling. Lepromatous leprosy is a subtype of leprosy and shares drug associations with its parent condition, including Clofazimine, Dapsone, Ethionamide, Ofloxacin, Prednisolone, Prednisone, Rifampicin, and Thalidomide. Brucella abortus, Brucella suis, and Brucella canis brucellosis are all classified as subtypes of brucellosis.
358: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including an event in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, and another in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is also recognized as a subtype of parasitic infectious disease. It is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, pallor, diarrhea, vomiting, coma, and neurological manifestations such as cerebellar ataxia and unconsciousness. The condition is linked to several biological pathways, notably the malaria pathway itself. Treatment involves a variety of drugs, including artemisinin-based combinations such as artesunate and amodiaquine, as well as chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, mefloquine, and pyrimethamine. Other drugs used include atovaquone-proguanil, sulfonamides, and tetracycline. Certain drugs are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, including cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. Malaria shares clinical and pathological similarities with other conditions such as anemia, celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, and thymus cancer. Anemia, which is frequently associated with malaria, presents with symptoms including pallor, reticulocytosis, jaundice, and various systemic manifestations such as abdominal pain, fever, diarrhea, and purpura. It is also linked to hematopoietic system diseases and is treated with drugs like iron dextran, iron sucrose, and cyanocobalamin. Cerebral malaria, a severe form of the disease, is managed with mefloquine and quinine. Other parasitic infections related to malaria include hookworm disease, schistosomiasis, and intestinal parasitism, which are treated with drugs such as albendazole, ivermectin, praziquantel, and oxamniquine.

Tuberculosis shares a classification relationship with other diseases, being categorized as a primary bacterial infectious disease.

Diabetes mellitus is associated with various drugs, including antipsychotics, gliclazide, glipizide, and troglitazone, as well as environmental and metabolic agents such as cadmium, mercury, ozone, and arachidonic acids. Some of these agents, including alanine, chemokine CX3CL1, and pyrimidine, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. Diabetes mellitus is classified as a glucose metabolism disease and is linked to conditions such as hypertension and obesity.

Hypertension is characterized by a range of symptoms, including headache, dizziness, edema, proteinuria, nocturia, obesity, and various cardiovascular and neurological manifestations such as angina pectoris, heart murmurs, and reflex abnormalities. It is associated with drugs like angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, calcium channel blockers, and metoprolol, as well as agents that contribute to its pathogenesis, including 1-naphthaleneacetic acid, prostaglandins A, and phosphatidylinositols. Hypertension is classified as an artery disease and resembles conditions such as abdominal obesity-metabolic syndrome 1, obesity, and type 2 diabetes mellitus.

A subsequent outbreak occurred in Ethiopia in 2000, with no reported fatalities. In 1998, a case was reported in the Soviet Union involving 15 confirmed cases and 2 deaths. The United States experienced a series of anthrax outbreaks in 2001, including events on October 11, October 18, November 5, and November 23, with case counts ranging from 3 to 23 and death tolls from 1 to 5. Most recently, in 2023, an outbreak in Zimbabwe reported 684 confirmed cases and 4 deaths, with the event being officially documented in February 2024.

Anthrax disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. The drugs ciprofloxacin and omadacycline are associated with the condition, often used in its treatment or study. Cutaneous anthrax is recognized as a subtype of anthrax disease.

362: An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan in January 2002, reporting 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each associated with 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which itself is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Other drugs linked to the disease include Allopurinol, Ketoconazole, Artemisinin, and various antiprotozoals. Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and dozens of deaths. Outbreaks have also been documented in Sri Lanka, Brazil, India, the Netherlands, Cameroon, Bangladesh, East Timor, Egypt, France, and other countries, with case counts ranging from a few to over half a million, and death tolls reaching several hundred in some instances. Notably, a large-scale outbreak was reported in 2024 with 7.6 million confirmed cases and 3,000 deaths, while another major outbreak in 2023 involved 500,000 cases and 750 deaths. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its severe forms include dengue hemorrhagic fever and dengue shock syndrome, both of which are subtypes of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with the condition, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, hinokiflavone, podocarpusflavone A, and others, though their specific roles in pathogenesis are not detailed.

Additional outbreaks were documented in Chad, Congo, France, Italy, Spain, Argentina, and Egypt, with case numbers ranging from a few to over 27,000. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and the drug chloroquine has been associated with it.

365: Dengue Fever has been associated with numerous outbreaks across multiple regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with over 2,000 and 11,000 cases respectively, and in Sri Lanka in 2017, reporting more than 80,000 cases and 215 deaths. Outbreaks have also been documented in Brazil, with a major event in 2002 involving over 317,000 cases, and in India, where multiple outbreaks were recorded between 1996 and 2003, including one in 1996 with 8,170 cases and 319 deaths. Other notable outbreaks occurred in the Netherlands in 2004, with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths, and in Pakistan, where outbreaks in 2019, 2021, and 2022 reported tens of thousands of cases and multiple fatalities. Additional outbreaks have been reported in East Timor, Egypt, Honduras, El Salvador, Cambodia, Uruguay, Ecuador, Burkina Faso, and Chad, with varying case counts and mortality rates. A particularly large outbreak was reported in 2024, with over 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths, though the specific country was not specified. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is further categorized as a subtype of dengue disease, with dengue hemorrhagic fever being a more severe form of the condition. Several drugs have been associated with the disease, including hydroxychloroquine, doxycycline, silver, and various cholesterol metabolites such as 24-hydroxycholesterol and 27-hydroxycholesterol. Other compounds linked to the disease include hinokiflavone, podocarpusflavone A, and hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids.
366: Liver disease is associated with a wide range of drugs, including those that are merely observed or studied concurrently, such as 1,4-dioxane, cadmium, chromium, cyclosporine, methotrexate, and various environmental pollutants, as well as drugs that play a role in disease pathogenesis, including hepatitis B e antigens and toluene. It is classified as a type of hepatobiliary disease.
