Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease is linked to drugs like acarbose, allopurinol, arsenic trioxide, fructose, and valproic acid, with some also contributing to pathogenesis, such as desmosterol and pidolic acid. This condition is part of the broader category of fatty liver disease and is associated with biological pathways including glycerolipid metabolism and non-alcoholic fatty liver disease-specific signaling. Obesity, which resembles polycystic ovary syndrome and type 2 diabetes mellitus, is characterized by symptoms such as body weight increase, hyperphagia, obesity hypoventilation syndrome, sleep apnea, and sarcopenia. It is associated with drugs like fenfluramine, sibutramine, metformin, and resveratrol, some of which contribute to pathogenesis through mechanisms involving eltanolone, nitroprusside, nucleotides, and valproic acid. Pathways linked to obesity include neuroactive ligand-receptor interaction, oleoyl-phe metabolism, PPAR signaling, and regulation of lipolysis in adipocytes. Parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease is associated with anthelmintic drugs such as albendazole, ivermectin, praziquantel, and mebendazole, and is classified under parasitic infectious disease. Schistosoma mansonii infection is treated with drugs including praziquantel, oxamniquine, and artemether, and is linked to triglyceride metabolism. Schistosomiasis, which includes intestinal schistosomiasis, is also associated with praziquantel and is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. Infection, in general, is associated with a broad spectrum of drugs including antibiotics, chemotherapeutics, and immunosuppressants such as ceftriaxone, methotrexate, cyclosporine, and vancomycin. Fibrosis is linked to drugs like acetaminophen, asbestos, nitroprusside, and various environmental toxins, with some contributing to pathogenesis through mechanisms involving homocysteine and cyclic adenosine monophosphate. Colitis, a form of inflammatory bowel disease, is associated with drugs such as indomethacin, metronidazole, and irinotecan, and is linked to pathogenesis involving cyclic adenosine monophosphate. Alcoholic fatty liver and alcoholic liver disease are associated with ethanol, acetaldehyde, and drugs like silymarin, vitamin E, and resveratrol, with some contributing to pathogenesis through mechanisms involving oxidative stress and lipid metabolism.
367: Rheumatic diseases are classified as a type of musculoskeletal system disease. Various drugs are associated with the management or study of rheumatic diseases, including Antrafenine, Auranofin, Carbamazepine, Chloroquine, Codeine, Deferoxamine, Diclofenac, Etodolac, Floctafenine, Flufenamic acid, Hydroxychloroquine, Indomethacin, Isotretinoin, Ketoprofen, Methylprednisolone, Niflumic acid, Oxycodone, Plant Extracts, Sulindac, Tetracyclines, and naphthalan.
368:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, Cameroon, Sri Lanka, Mayotte, East Timor, El Salvador, Brazil, Egypt, India, Cape Verde, and the Netherlands, with case counts ranging from a few to over 80,000 and fatalities reaching up to 750 in some instances. Notable outbreaks occurred in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, in the Netherlands in 2004 with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths, and in 2023 and 2024 with large-scale outbreaks reported in unspecified locations involving over 500,000 and 7.6 million cases, respectively. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is further categorized as dengue hemorrhagic fever, a more severe form. Several drugs have been associated with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, among others. Additionally, viremia, a condition involving the presence of virus in the bloodstream, has been linked to drugs such as ribavirin, tenofovir, and zidovudine, as well as to the role of alanine in disease pathogenesis.

Zika virus outbreaks have been documented in multiple countries, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, India, Brazil, Dominican Republic, and others, with case numbers ranging from 1 to over 8,700. Notable outbreaks include a large event in French Guiana in 2015 with 8,750 cases and a significant outbreak in Brazil in 2016 with 3,174 cases and 38 deaths. The virus has also been reported in India in 2016 and 2021, with 70 cases recorded in 2021. The disease has been associated with severe outcomes in some cases, including fatalities in El Salvador and the United States.
369:
Outbreaks of Hemorrhagic fever have been reported in multiple regions, including the United Republic of Tanzania, Congo, Sudan (W Equatoria), Gabon and the neighbouring villages of the Republic of the Congo, Cuvette Ouest region, and other locations across Africa, with documented cases ranging from 1 to 374 and deaths from 1 to 80. Notable outbreaks occurred in 1996, 1997, 2000, 2001, 2002, 2003, 2004, 2007, 2010, 2011, 2012, and 2015, with significant mortality observed in several events, particularly in Congo and Tanzania. The disease is associated with the use of various drugs, including acetaminophen, aspirin, clopidogrel, heparin, warfarin, and others, which are linked to hemorrhage. Additionally, the condition is associated with the TCR signaling pathway and is classified as a type of brain disease. The virus responsible for the outbreaks is consistently identified as Hemorrhagic fever.

Outbreaks of Zika virus have been documented in multiple countries, including Brazil, French Guiana, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Panama, El Salvador, Guatemala, Mexico, Dominican Republic, Honduras, United States, India, Papua New Guinea, Sint Maarten, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Trinidad and Tobago, Cape Verde, the Netherlands, the Maldive Islands, and others, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 8,700. The outbreaks span from 2015 to 2021, with major epidemics reported in French Guiana and Brazil. The virus is associated with drugs such as active hexose correlated compound, adefovir dipivoxil, indinavir, lamivudine, mycophenolic acid, ribavirin, tenofovir, zidovudine, and resmethrin, some of which play a role in disease pathogenesis. The condition is also linked to the presence of alanine in its pathogenic mechanisms.

Outbreaks of Rift Valley fever (RVF) have been recorded in Somalia, with a confirmed case count of 4. The disease is associated with various drugs, including acetaminophen, aspirin, clopidogrel, heparin, warfarin, and others, which are linked to hemorrhage. The condition is also associated with the TCR signaling pathway and is classified as a type of brain disease. The virus responsible for the outbreaks is consistently identified as RVF.
370:
Outbreaks of Yersinia Pestis have been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including Ecuador in 2010 with 17 confirmed cases, and Madagascar, which experienced several outbreaks between 1997 and 2021, with case counts ranging from 14 to over 2,000 and death tolls reaching up to 202. Additional outbreaks occurred in Zambia, Mozambique, Angola, Congo, India, Algeria, and Tanzania, with varying case numbers and mortality rates. The disease has been documented in Madagascar in multiple instances during 2017, including on August 1, August 17, and August 23, with cumulative cases and deaths reported across these events. Other notable outbreaks include one in Zambia in 1997 with 267 cases and 26 deaths, and another in 2001 with 436 cases and 11 deaths.

Yersinia Pestis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with several forms, including bubonic, pneumonic, and septicemic plague, all of which are subtypes of plague. In terms of treatment, the drugs Ciprofloxacin, Doxycycline, and Omadacycline are associated with the management of the disease.
371:
Zika virus has been associated with multiple outbreak events across various regions, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, India, and others, with case counts ranging from 1 to 8,750. These outbreaks were reported between 2015 and 2021, with notable events in French Guiana and Ecuador involving hundreds of cases. Zika fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological agents associated with Zika virus include chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide.

Yellow Fever has been documented in numerous outbreaks across Africa, South America, and other regions, including Ghana, Congo, Brazil, Cameroon, Angola, Ecuador, Egypt, Mali, Burkina Faso, Sudan, United Republic of Tanzania, Sierra Leone, Ethiopia, and multiple countries in 2021. Outbreaks have varied in scale, with case numbers ranging from 1 to over 1,300 and fatalities from 1 to 215. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Dengue Fever has been reported in widespread outbreaks in Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Mayotte, East Timor, Barbados, El Salvador, Brazil, Egypt, The Netherlands, India, Pakistan, Reunion (France), Thailand, Honduras, Cambodia, Uruguay, São Tomé and Príncipe, and others, with case counts ranging from 2 to over 7.6 million. Notable outbreaks occurred in 1996–1997 in The Netherlands and Malaysia, and in 2019–2023 in multiple countries, including a large-scale outbreak in 2023 with 500,000 cases. Dengue disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease. Drugs associated with dengue disease include 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, F2-isoprostanes, hydroxychloroquine, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, rolitetracycline, silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A.

Chikungunya virus disease has been reported in outbreaks in United Republic of Tanzania, United States, Congo, Cameroon, France, Chad, Reunion, St Martin, Italy, Spain, Argentina, and Egypt, with case numbers ranging from 1 to over 27,000. Notable outbreaks occurred in Chad in 2020 and Egypt in 2018, with over 13,000 cases. Chikungunya is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with chikungunya include chloroquine.
372:
Dengue Fever has been associated with multiple outbreak events across various regions, including Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Brazil, India, Cameroon, and several other countries in Asia, Africa, and South America, with case counts ranging from a few to over 80,000 in a single outbreak. Notable outbreaks occurred in Malaysia in 1996 and 1997, with 2,450 and 19,544 cases respectively, and in Sri Lanka in 2017, with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its severe forms, dengue hemorrhagic fever and dengue shock syndrome, are recognized as subtypes of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, among others.

Chikungunya virus disease has been reported in multiple countries, including the United Republic of Tanzania, Congo, Reunion, Chad, and several European nations such as France, Italy, and Spain, with outbreaks documented from 2005 to 2018. The largest outbreak occurred in Chad in 2020, with 27,540 cases and one death. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and chloroquine has been associated with its treatment or study.

Zika virus outbreaks have been recorded in countries across the Americas, including Brazil, Colombia, Ecuador, Panama, and the United States, as well as in parts of Asia and Africa, with notable events in French Guiana, Cape Verde, and India. Outbreaks have been reported from 2015 to 2021, with case numbers ranging from a few to over 8,000 in a single event. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and drugs such as chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide have been associated with it.

West Nile Disease has been documented in the United States, Canada, Israel, and other countries, with significant outbreaks in the U.S. and Canada during 2002, including over 3,500 cases and more than 200 deaths in the U.S. alone. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and drugs such as active hexose correlated compound and minocycline have been associated with it.
373:
Fatty liver disease is classified as a lipid storage disease. It is associated with a wide range of drugs, including 2,2',3,3',4,4',5-heptachlorobiphenyl, 2,4,4'-trichlorobiphenyl, 2,5,2',5'-tetrachlorobiphenyl, 4-methylene-2-octyl-5-oxofuran-3-carboxylic acid, 5-aminoisoquinolinone, 18alpha-glycyrrhetinic acid, acetaminophen, agmatine, cadmium chloride, capsaicin, cholecalciferol, clodronic acid, copper, dietary fats, diethyl phthalate, doxycycline, endotoxins, nonesterified fatty acids, fenofibrate, flame retardants, geraniol, Ginkgo biloba, ibuprofen, isotretinoin, conjugated linoleic acids, lithocholic acid, meldonium, methionine, nitroaspirin, obeticholic acid, olaparib, pectins, pentoxifylline, phenacetin, phenylbutazone, propofol, rutin, streptozocin, sucrose, tetrachloroethylene, thiophanate, turmeric, ziram, caryophyllene, fatostatin, honokiol, oleuropein, quercetin 3-O-glucuronide, sesamol, and sophocarpine. Additionally, the disease is linked to the role of acyl coenzyme A and malonaldehyde in its pathogenesis.

Clonorchiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease.

Hepatobiliary disease is classified as a gastrointestinal system disease.

Cholangitis is classified as a bile duct disease and is associated with numerous drugs, including 1-naphthylisothiocyanate, 1-amino-2,4-dibromoanthraquinone, amiodarone, amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate combination, ampicillin, atorvastatin, azathioprine, carbamazepine, chlorpromazine, cilastatin-imipenem combination, dapsone, dexamethasone, floxuridine, flucloxacillin, gentamicins, hydralazine, ibuprofen, lithocholic acid, N-formylmethionyl-leucyl-tyrosine, naproxen, obeticholic acid, octreotide, phenobarbital, piperacillin, sulfasalazine, sulindac, sulpiride, tenoxicam, trichloroethylene, ursodeoxycholic acid, corticosteroids, and glucocorticoids.

Cholangiocarcinoma is classified as a bile duct adenocarcinoma and is associated with drugs such as 1,2,3-trichloropropane, 2,2-bis(bromomethyl)-1,3-propanediol, 2,3',4,4',5-pentachlorobiphenyl, 2-aminofluorene, 2-nitrotoluene, 3,4,5,3',4'-pentachlorobiphenyl, anabolic agents, azoxymethane, carmustine, chlorodiphenyl (54% chlorine), diethylnitrosamine, dimethylnitrosamine, melatonin, methotrexate, methylene chloride, nitroglycerin, pentachlorophenol, phenobarbital, reactive nitrogen species, reactive oxygen species, tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, thalidomide, thioacetamide, furan, methylethylnitrosamine, nitromethane, and propylene dichloride.

Cholecystitis is classified as a form of cholangitis and is associated with drugs including 2-ethoxyethanol, 2-methoxyethanol, acetylsalicylic acid, ampicillin, busulfan, ceftriaxone, cisplatin, clofibrate, cyclophosphamide, dapsone, Chinese herbal drugs, erythromycin, floxuridine, fluorouracil, gabapentin, heparin, indomethacin, mitomycin, morphine, semustine, and thiazides.

Parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease is classified as a parasitic infectious disease and is associated with anthelmintic drugs such as albendazole, anthelmintics, closantel, ivermectin, levamisole, mebendazole, netobimin, niclosamide, praziquantel, and thiabendazole.

Cholelithiasis is associated with drugs including atorvastatin, ceftriaxone, celecoxib, clofibrate, conjugated estrogens, cyclosporine, cytarabine, Chinese herbal drugs, fenofibrate, fibric acids, gemfibrozil, glyburide, indapamide, indinavir, medroxyprogesterone acetate, morphine, naloxone, octreotide, prednisone, progesterone, rifampicin, sulindac, tacrolimus, and ursodeoxycholic acid.

Cholelithiasis is classified as a gallbladder disease.

Choledocholithiasis is classified as a common bile duct disease and is associated with drugs such as atazanavir sulfate, cefoperazone, ceftriaxone, cytarabine, and exisulind.
374: Schistosoma mansonii infection is associated with several drugs, including 3,7-dimethyloctan-1-ol, acetylcysteine, artemether, IA 4, nitric oxide, oxamniquine, and praziquantel, which are used in its treatment or study. Additionally, triglycerides play a role in the disease's pathogenesis. Schistosomiasis is linked to metrifonate and praziquantel as associated drugs. The condition schistosomiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is associated with praziquantel, quercetin, and resveratrol, and shares a hierarchical relationship with schistosomiasis.
375: An outbreak of diarrhoea occurred in Angola on May 26, 1998, reporting 560 confirmed cases and 12 deaths. Diarrhoea is classified as a gastrointestinal system disease. Several drugs are associated with diarrhoea, including the 1-UFT protocol, Abemaciclib, Acetazolamide, Aliskiren, Alvimopan, Amphotericin B, Amsacrine, Arsenic, Azithromycin, BMS 181339, Benoxaprofen, Camptothecin, Carbachol, Cefixime, Ciprofloxacin, Cisplatin, Cocaine, Colchicine, Copper, Daunorubicin, Dicoumarol, Doxifluridine, Hydroxypropyl betadex, Lamivudine, Lincomycin, MRK 003, Melphalan, Mercuric chloride, Mitomycin, Naloxone, Okadaic Acid, Ondansetron, Paclitaxel, Paroxetine, Pentoxifylline, Prednisolone, Proton pump inhibitors, Quinidine, RN 1734, Rubitecan, SR 48968, Senna Extract, Sirolimus, Telmisartan, Tetracycline, Thiorphan, Ticlopidine, Tioguanine, esculetin, and sangre de grado. Additionally, certain substances are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis, including alanine and aspartic acid, as well as chlorides. Diarrhoea is also associated with other conditions such as malnutrition NOS and cryptosporidiosis, the latter being classified as a type of coccidiosis. Growth disorders are linked to a range of drugs, including various polychlorinated biphenyls, amphetamines, arsenic, ascorbic acid, boric acid, ethanol, lead, lithium, mercury, and several pharmaceuticals, with magnesium identified as a contributing factor in pathogenesis.
376:
Visceral Leishmaniasis has been associated with multiple outbreak events in Pakistan, including a significant outbreak in 2002 with 200,000 confirmed cases, as well as additional events in the same year with 200,000 and 738 cases, respectively. The disease is classified as a type of leishmaniasis. Pharmacological interventions associated with Visceral Leishmaniasis include a range of drugs such as Allopurinol, Amphotericin B, Antimony, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Paromomycin, Pentamidine, and various antiprotozoals, as well as compounds like 2,5-bis(2-(2-propoxy)-4-(2-pyridylimino)aminophenyl)furan, 2-(2''-dichloroacetamidobenzyl)-3-(3'-indolylquinoline), and Desmodium gangeticum extract. Other agents linked to treatment or study include reactive oxygen species, thalidomide, and val-Glu-Pro-Ile-Gly-Tyr, with liposomal amphotericin B and amphotericin B, deoxycholate drug combination also noted.

Meningitis has been documented in numerous outbreaks across multiple countries, including large-scale events in Burkina Faso (1996 and 1997), Egypt (1996–1999), Chad (1996–2011), Nigeria (1996–2015), Ghana (1997, 2017), and others, with case counts ranging from a few to over 40,000 and death tolls reaching several thousand. The disease is classified as a central nervous system disease. Pharmacological agents associated with meningitis include a broad spectrum of drugs such as Amikacin, Ceftriaxone, Cefotaxime, Cefuroxime, Chloramphenicol, Ciprofloxacin, Dexamethasone, Vancomycin, Meropenem, Linezolid, and corticosteroids. Specific drugs noted include Azithromycin, Co-Trimoxazole, Ibuprofen, Indomethacin, and various antibiotics and antifungals. Meningitis is further categorized into subtypes such as bacterial meningitis and aseptic meningitis, both of which are also classified under the broader category of meningitis. Bacterial meningitis is associated with drugs like Ampicillin, Benzylpenicillin, Imipenem, and Vancomycin, while aseptic meningitis is linked to agents such as Methotrexate, Valaciclovir, and various non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. Additionally, meningitis is associated with conditions such as Central Nervous System Infections and Fever, with drugs like Fluconazole, Ketoconazole, and Prednisone also noted in the context of CNS infections, and fever-related treatments including Acetylcysteine, Metamizole, and corticosteroids. Hydroxyindoleacetic acid is noted to play a role in the pathogenesis of fever.
377: Chikungunya virus disease has been associated with multiple outbreak events across various regions, including a significant outbreak in Chad in July 2020 with 27,540 confirmed cases and one death, and another in Congo in February 2019 involving 6,149 cases. Additional outbreaks were reported in the United Republic of Tanzania in 2016 and 2017, with 1,792 and 453 cases respectively, as well as in Reunion Island in 2005 and 2006 with 3,115 and 1,722 cases. Smaller outbreaks occurred in countries such as the United States, France, Italy, Spain, Argentina, India, Egypt, and St Martin, with case counts ranging from 1 to 1,3978. Chikungunya virus disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. The drug chloroquine is associated with this condition, often observed or studied in relation to it.
378:
An infection is classified as a disease by infectious agent and is associated with a range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, the COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, clarithromycin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cytarabine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, doxorubicin, epirubicin, etoposide, everolimus, exisulind, the FEC protocol, fludarabine, itraconazole, the LNH 87 protocol, lithium, the MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, metronidazole, netilmicin, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, sirolimus, tetrandrine, tobramycin, topotecan, triamcinolone, the VIP-E protocol, vancomycin, and chlorhexidine phosphanilate. 

Parasitic diseases are associated with drugs such as abamectin, arsenamide, disintegrins, ivermectin, toosendanin, and dextrose in unspecified form, which plays a role in disease pathogenesis. 

Cholangiocarcinoma is classified as a bile duct adenocarcinoma and is associated with drugs including 1,2,3-trichloropropane, 2,2-bis(bromomethyl)-1,3-propanediol, 2,3',4,4',5-pentachlorobiphenyl, 2-aminofluorene, 2-nitrotoluene, 3,4,5,3',4'-pentachlorobiphenyl, anabolic agents, azoxymethane, carmustine, chlorodiphenyl (54% chlorine), diethylnitrosamine, dimethylnitrosamine, melatonin, methotrexate, methylene chloride, nitroglycerin, pentachlorophenol, phenobarbital, reactive nitrogen species, reactive oxygen species, tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, thalidomide, thioacetamide, furan, methylethylnitrosamine, nitromethane, and propylene dichloride.
379: Schistosomiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The condition is associated with several drugs, including Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is also linked to the use of Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol.
380:
Brucellosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. It includes several subtypes, including Brucella abortus brucellosis, Brucella suis brucellosis, and Brucella canis brucellosis, all of which are considered subtypes of brucellosis. The condition is associated with several drugs, including Cholesterol, LDL, HL 004, Ketoconazole, Rifampicin, and Tetracycline.

COVID-19 is classified as a coronavirus infectious disease. It has been associated with a wide range of drugs, including (2-tert-butoxy-1-(2-cyclohexyl-1-(1-formyl-2-(2-oxopyrrolidin-3-yl)ethylcarbamoyl)ethylcarbamoyl)propyl)carbamic acid benzyl ester, Acalabrutinib, Acetazolamide, Active Hexose Correlated Compound, Azithromycin, Baricitinib, Betulinic Acid, Camostat, Cepharanthine, Chloroquine, Choline, Dactinomycin, Emodin, Galidesivir, Heparin, Low-Molecular-Weight, Hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, Interferon alfa, Ivermectin, Lopinavir, Losartan, Mefloquine, Melatonin, Mercaptopurine, Nifedipine, Remdesivir, Ribavirin, Ritonavir, Rupintrivir, Selamectin, Setrobuvir, Sirolimus, Sofosbuvir, Teicoplanin, Tenofovir, Toremifene, Umifenovir, antivirals, chloroquine diphosphate, and lopinavir-ritonavir drug combination.
381:
Blood coagulation disease is classified as a hematopoietic system disease. Numerous drugs are associated with this condition, including acetylsalicylic acid, heparin, warfarin, argatroban, and various anticoagulants, as well as agents such as arsenic trioxide, estrogens, and dietary fats.  

Edema is associated with a wide range of drugs, including amlodipine, dexamethasone, spironolactone, furosemide, and various antipsychotics, anticonvulsants, and anti-inflammatory agents, as well as environmental factors like endotoxin from Escherichia coli and vehicle emissions.  

Fibrosis is linked to multiple drugs, including amiodarone, bleomycin, cyclophosphamide, methotrexate, and various chemotherapeutic agents, as well as environmental toxins such as asbestos, cadmium, and lipopolysaccharides, and compounds like D-glucose, tetracaine, and sodium arsenite.  

Synovitis is categorized as a connective tissue disease and is associated with drugs such as methotrexate, prednisolone, sulfasalazine, and azathioprine, among others.  

Systemic lupus erythematosus is a connective tissue disease that resembles systemic scleroderma. It presents with a broad range of symptoms, including fever, fatigue, arthralgia, skin manifestations, proteinuria, purpura, neurological and ocular symptoms, abdominal pain, and hypergammaglobulinemia. Drugs associated with this condition include acetylsalicylic acid, hydroxychloroquine, cyclophosphamide, and various anticonvulsants and antipsychotics. Some drugs, such as hydralazine and procainamide, are implicated in its pathogenesis, along with biological agents like prostaglandins, nucleotides, and malonaldehyde. The disease is linked to the systemic lupus erythematosus pathway.  

Cutaneous lupus erythematosus is a subtype of lupus erythematosus and is associated with drugs such as hydroxychloroquine, chloroquine, prednisone, and thalidomide.  

Intracranial hemorrhages are associated with anticoagulants, antiplatelet agents, and drugs such as acetylsalicylic acid, heparin, clopidogrel, and cocaine, as well as substances like ethanol, amphetamines, and various chemotherapeutic agents.  

Cerebral hemorrhage is linked to drugs including heparin, anticoagulants, and various chemotherapeutics, as well as agents like amphetamines, phenprocoumon, and tretinoin. Some drugs contribute to its pathogenesis through mechanisms involving leukotriene B4 and 5-methyltetrahydrofolic acid.  

Chronic disease is associated with drugs such as acetylsalicylic acid, arsenic, asbestos, and phenindione, as well as corticosteroids.  

Rheumatoid arthritis is a type of arthritis that resembles systemic lupus erythematosus and systemic scleroderma. It is characterized by symptoms such as joint pain, fatigue, fever, edema, neurological manifestations, and skin changes. Drugs associated with this condition include methotrexate, hydroxychloroquine, azathioprine, and various nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs. Some drugs, such as calcifediol and pyrrole-2-carboxylate, are involved in its pathogenesis, and the disease is associated with the rheumatoid arthritis pathway.  

Acute kidney failure is classified as a form of kidney failure and is associated with drugs such as acetylsalicylic acid, furosemide, contrast media, and various antibiotics, as well as environmental toxins like nickel and bromodichloromethane.
382: Pulmonary tuberculosis is classified as a type of tuberculosis, which itself is a primary bacterial infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including aminosalicylic acids, arsenic, capreomycin, carbon monoxide, clarithromycin, cycloserine, dihydrostreptomycin sulfate, ethambutol, isoniazid, nitrogen dioxide, ofloxacin, pyrazinamide, pyridoxine, rifabutin, rifampicin, streptomycin, and the combination isoniazid/pyrazinamide/rifampin. Tuberculosis is also linked to several drugs that play a role in its pathogenesis, such as lopinavir, phosphorus, and phosphates. The disease is associated with the tuberculosis pathway. Additionally, abscesses are linked to various drugs, including ampicillin, aurothioglucose, aztreonam, buprenorphine, carbenicillin, cefalotin, cefmetazole, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, chlorpromazine, ciprofloxacin, daptomycin, diazepam, drugs of Chinese herbal origin, floxuridine, gentamicins, linezolid, meticillin, metronidazole, nafcillin, paraldehyde, phenacetin, promethazine, sultamicillin, tobramycin, vancomycin, beta-lactam antibacterials, penicillins, cefuroxime axetil, and perilla seed oil.
383: Scrub typhus is classified as a type of typhus.
384:
Ebola outbreaks have been documented in multiple countries, including the Republic of Congo, Guinea Bissau, Sierra Leone, Angola, and the United Republic of Tanzania, with events recorded from 1997 to 2022. Notable outbreaks occurred in the Republic of Congo in 2014, 2018, 2019, and 2020, with case counts ranging from 24 to over 3,400 and death tolls reaching up to 2,287. Additional outbreaks were reported in Guinea Bissau in 2014 and 2021, and in Sierra Leone during the 2014 epidemic, with significant case and death numbers. Ebola hemorrhagic fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and several drugs have been associated with its treatment, including BH30sucMan, Favipiravir, Galidesivir, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Remdesivir, and Teicoplanin.

Measles outbreaks have occurred globally across multiple regions, including Africa, Europe, the Americas, and Asia, with documented events from 1997 to 2023. Major outbreaks were reported in Madagascar (2018), Ethiopia (2022), Pakistan (2022), the Central African Republic (2019), and the Cook Islands (2019), with case numbers exceeding 10,000 in several instances and deaths reaching up to 182. Smaller outbreaks were also recorded in countries such as Egypt, Brazil, Japan, the United States, and The Netherlands. Measles is classified as a viral infectious disease, and it is associated with the Measles biological pathway.
385: Schistosomiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is further categorized into subtypes such as intestinal schistosomiasis and urinary schistosomiasis, both of which are also subtypes of schistosomiasis. The treatment of schistosomiasis involves the use of drugs including Metrifonate and Praziquantel, with Praziquantel also being used in the management of intestinal schistosomiasis. Additional drugs associated with intestinal schistosomiasis include Quercetin and Resveratrol. The broader category of parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease includes drugs such as Albendazole, Anthelmintics, Closantel, Ivermectin, Levamisole, Mebendazole, Netobimin, Niclosamide, and Thiabendazole. In the context of cattle diseases, several drugs are associated with treatment or management, including Acrylamide, Anti-Bacterial Agents, Baypamun HK, Bufanolides, Butylene glycol, Cardiac Glycosides, Citric acid, Dichlorvos, Famphur, Fenthion, Furazolidone, Levamisole, Metrifonate, Niacin, Phlorhizin, Plant Preparations, and Methidathion, with methane being implicated in the disease pathogenesis.
386: Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole are associated with the condition, often observed or studied in conjunction with it.
387:
Trypanosomiasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including antimony, arsenic compounds, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, pentamidine, and borrelidin.

Chagas disease, also known as American trypanosomiasis, is a condition linked to the same disease category as trypanosomiasis. It is associated with multiple drugs such as benznidazole, nifurtimox, nitrofural, and several experimental compounds including 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, and N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl. The disease is also associated with the biological pathway known as Chagas disease.

Multiple sclerosis is an autoimmune disease of the central nervous system and presents with a wide range of neurological symptoms, including ageusia, aphasia, apraxias, ataxia, blindness, chorea, constipation, diplopia, disorders of excessive somnolence, dizziness, dysarthria, dyslexia, dystonia, fasciculation, gait disorders, hearing disorders, hearing loss, hemianopsia, hemiplegia, hiccup, Huntington disease, hypesthesia, hypothermia, lower urinary tract symptoms, mobility limitation, muscle cramp, muscle weakness, muscular atrophy, myotonia, neuralgia, neurogenic inflammation, ophthalmoplegia, orthostatic intolerance, pain, paraparesis, paraplegia, paresis, postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome, pseudobulbar palsy, reflex abnormalities, scotoma, sensation disorders, sleep disorders, speech disorders, Susac syndrome, torticollis, tremor, urinary bladder dysfunction, and urinary incontinence. It is associated with various drugs, including amantadine, azathioprine, baclofen, cladribine, corticotropin, dalfampridine, dextromethorphan, dimethyl fumarate, fingolimod hydrochloride, fumaric acid, GABA-A receptor agonists, glatiramer acetate, levodopa, methylprednisolone, methylprednisolone hemisuccinate, mitoxantrone, naltrexone, nicotinamide, nitric oxide, pemoline, peroxynitrous acid, prednisolone, prednisone, quinidine, siponimod, teriflunomide, tobacco smoke pollution, vitamin D, cannabinoids, and corticosteroids. Some of these drugs are also implicated in the disease's pathogenesis, particularly hydrogen. The condition is associated with the cell adhesion molecules pathway.

Encephalomyelitis, autoimmune, experimental, is associated with a range of drugs, including 1,25-dihydroxy-16-ene-vitamin D3, 1,25-dihydroxy-24-oxo-16-ene-vitamin D3, 1-(2-(1-adamantyl)ethyl)-1-pentyl-3-(3-(4-pyridyl)propyl)urea, 2-(1'H-indole-3'-carbonyl)thiazole-4-carboxylic acid methyl ester, 2-(2'-(dimethylamino)ethyl)-1,2-dihydro-7-ethoxydibenz(de,h)isoquinoline-1,3-dione, 2-(4-acetoxyphenyl)-2-chloro-N-methylethylamine, 15-deoxy-delta(12,14)-prostaglandin J2, 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, amphetamine, bisphenol A diglycidyl ether, calcitriol, carbamazepine, ciglitazone, clioquinol, cyclophosphamide, dexamethasone, estradiol, estriol, ethinylestradiol, febuxostat, flecainide, Freund's adjuvant, hydrocortisone, indican, lithium, ML 7, methylprednisolone, minocycline, mitoxantrone, N-(oxo-5,6-dihydrophenanthridin-2-yl)-N,N-dimethylacetamide hydrochloride, nitric oxide, octreotide, phenytoin, prednisolone, progesterone, raloxifene hydrochloride, reactive oxygen species, resveratrol, rifampicin, T 0070907, tacrolimus, tamoxifen, arvanil, benzamide, myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein (35-55), myelin proteolipid protein (139-151), nitroflurbiprofen, periodate-oxidized adenosine 5'-triphosphate, and phenanthridone. Some of these drugs are involved in the disease's pathogenesis, particularly glutamic acid.
388:
An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which in turn is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It is associated with a range of drugs including Amphotericin B, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Pentamidine, and various antiprotozoals. Other compounds linked to treatment include allopurinol, ketoconazole, isosorbide dinitrate, thalidomide, and reactive oxygen species. The disease is also associated with the Leishmaniasis pathway.

Malaria outbreaks have been reported in multiple regions, including Burundi in May 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, and in Pakistan in January 2022 with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998 and July 1999, and in Ethiopia in November 2024. Malaria is also classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It presents with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, coma, unconsciousness, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, pallor, and neurological manifestations. The disease is associated with several drugs, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, primaquine, pyrimethamine, atovaquone, and sulfonamides. Some drugs, such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. Malaria is linked to the Malaria pathway and is associated with insecticides and various antimalarial agents.
389: Leptospirosis has been reported in multiple outbreaks across different regions, including a 2022 event in the United Republic of Tanzania with 20 confirmed cases and 3 deaths, a 1997 outbreak in India involving 413 cases and 48 deaths, a 2000 outbreak in Malaysia with 37 cases, another 2004 outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania with 141 cases and 6 deaths, and a 2000 outbreak in Canada with 6 confirmed cases. Leptospirosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease.
390:
An outbreak of HIV infection-AIDS occurred in Pakistan on April 25, 2019, with 876 confirmed cases. HIV infection is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with the human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection pathway. The condition is managed with a range of antiretroviral agents, including Adefovir dipivoxil, Amprenavir, Atazanavir Sulfate, Emtricitabine, Enfuvirtide, Etravirine, Fosamprenavir, Foscarnet, Lamivudine, Maraviroc, Raltegravir potassium, Stavudine, Tenofovir Disoproxil Fumarate, Zidovudine, and various drug combinations such as Lamivudine/Zidovudine and Emtricitabine/Tenofovir Disoproxil Fumarate. Additionally, other substances like Plant Extracts, Chloroquine diphosphate, and Clasto-lactacystin beta-lactone have been studied in relation to the disease.

Chagas disease, also known as American trypanosomiasis, is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including Benznidazole, Nifurtimox, and various experimental compounds such as 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol and 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol. The disease is linked to the Chagas disease pathway.

Cardiomyopathy is classified as a type of heart disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including Acetaminophen, Acetylcysteine, Anthracyclines, Ascorbic acid, Azacitidine, Butylated hydroxytoluene, Chlorpromazine, Coconut oil, Dextroamphetamine, Ebselen, Ethanol, Fluorides, Inosine, Luteolin, Mazindol, Methotrexate, Methylprednisolone, Metoprolol, Minoxidil, N-leucyldoxorubicin, Naringenin, Nitrofurantoin, Norepinephrine, Ofloxacin, Plant Extracts, Prednisolone, Quercetin, Razoxane, Resveratrol, Risperidone, Ritodrine, SB 203580, STAMP-I protocol, Saponins, Sildenafil Citrate, Sorafenib, Spironolactone, Tacrolimus, Thiotepa, Triptolide, Valrubicin, Zidovudine, antineoplastic agents, arachidonyl-2-chloroethylamide, cyclopentenyl cytosine, diallyl trisulfide, estrogens, fructus schizandrae, radix ginseng, radix ophiopogonis drug combination, and necrox-7. Certain substances, such as 99mTc-glucarate, Potassium, and phosphates, are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis.

Hypertension is associated with a broad spectrum of symptoms, including albuminuria, angina pectoris, anoxia, birth weight abnormalities, body weight changes, low cardiac output, decerebrate state, dizziness, edema, eye manifestations, fetal distress, fetal hypoxia, headache, hearing loss, heart murmurs, hemifacial spasm, orthostatic hypotension, intermittent claudication, microvascular angina, nocturia, obesity, oliguria, overweight, polyuria, post-exercise hypotension, proteinuria, psychophysiologic disorders, abnormal reflexes, retinal hemorrhage, sleep apnea syndromes, obstructive sleep apnea, snoring, and Williams syndrome. It is linked to numerous drugs, including Aldosterone, Aminocaproic acid, Amlodipine, Valsartan Drug Combination, Amrinone, Atipamezole, Beta Blocking Agents, Betamethasone, Carbenoxolone, Chlorisondamine, Chlorpromazine, Cloxacillin, Diazepam, Diazoxide, Dietary Carbohydrates, Dihydralazine, Disopyramide, Doxorubicin, Enoxolone, Eprosartan, Ergotamines, Fenfluramine, Flunitrazepam, Gestodene, Glutathione disulfide, Guanfacine, Hemicholinium 3, Hemin, Hydrocortisone acetate, Hydrogen Sulfide, Innovar, Lamivudine, Lorazepam, Mitomycin, Neomycin, Nimodipine, Pancuronium, Perindoprilat, Pirenzepine, Prednisolone, Pseudoephedrine, Ramipril, Tacrine, Teprotide, Tubocurarine, Urethane, Xipamide, corticosteroids, fludrocortisone acetate, praeruptorin A, and trimethaphan camsylate. Substances such as 1-naphthaleneacetic acid, 2,3-epoxypropyl acrylate, 5,6-epoxy-8,11,14-eicosatrienoic acid, 11-dehydro-thromboxane B2, Cotinine, Cytidine-5'-Triphosphate, Phosphatidylinositols, prostaglandins a, and undecaphosphotungstate are involved in disease pathogenesis. Hypertension is classified as a type of artery disease and resembles conditions such as abdominal obesity-metabolic syndrome 1, obesity, and type 2 diabetes mellitus.

Diabetes mellitus is classified as a glucose metabolism disease. It is associated with drugs such as 2,4,4',5-tetrachlorobiphenyl, 2,4,5,2',4',5'-hexachlorobiphenyl, 5-((2,4-dioxo-5-thiazolidinyl)methyl)-2-methoxy-N-((4-(trifluoromethyl)phenyl)methyl)benzamide, 8-(4-benzenesulfonylamino)quinoline, Agent Orange, Aldrin, Antimony, Arsenic compounds, Atrazine, Berberine, Cadmium, Chlorides, Chlorodiphenyl (54% Chlorine), Chlorthalidone, Clozapine, Coumaphos, Dichlorodiphenyl Dichloroethylene, Fonofos, Gallic Acid, Heptachlor, Indapamide, Lead, Meglumine antimoniate, Mercury, Metals, Heavy, Niacin, Omega-3 fatty acids, Pentamidine, Phenformin, Pioglitazone, Plant Extracts, Puerarin, Raloxifene Hydrochloride, Ramipril, Repaglinide, Resveratrol, Selenium, Sodium selenite, Tacrolimus, Uranium, Vanadium Compounds, aminoacetone, aroclor 1260, arsenite, corticosteroids, docosapentaenoic acid, lipoxin A4, metribuzin, osthol, and terbufos. Substances such as Alanine, chemokine cx3cl1, and pyrimidine are involved in disease pathogenesis.

Syphilis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. It is associated with drugs such as Arsenic, Doxycycline, and Metamfetamine.

Heart disease is classified as a cardiovascular system disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including Acebutolol, Amineptine, Amlodipine, Azathioprine, Butylated hydroxyanisole, Caffeine, Carmustine, Cortisone, Crizotinib, Curcumin, DNA-(2-amino-3-methylimidazo(4,5-f)quinoline) adduct, Deferoxamine, Dexamethasone, Didanosine, Digitoxin, Digoxin, Disulfiram, Dopamine, Ergotamine, Glutathione, Grape Seed Proanthocyanidins, Iodixanol, Mannitol, Mibefradil, N-(2-cyclohexyloxy-4-nitrophenyl)methanesulfonamide, Oxymetholone, Phenytoin, Pirlindole, Potassium, Pravastatin, Prednisolone, Prenylamine, Pyrazolanthrone, Risperidone, Sodium bicarbonate, Sodium selenite, Soot, Superoxides, Tetrandrine, Thiamethoxam, Tin, Tobramycin, bis(2-chloroethoxy)methane, chloroquine diphosphate, cyclopentenyl cytosine, damvar, diuretics, fluoroacetamide, pifithrin, and tricarbonyldichlororuthenium (II) dimer.

Arthritis is classified as a bone inflammation disease and is associated with drugs such as 2-(4-isobutylphenyl)propionic acid 4-(diethoxyphosphoryloxy)butyl ester, Acetylsalicylic acid, Allopurinol, Antithyroid Preparations, Azaribine, Cabergoline, Carbamazepine, Carbimazole, Cefotaxime, Chloroquine, Cocaine, Diclofenac, Environmental Pollutants, Etodolac, Freund's Adjuvant, Gemfibrozil, Glucosamine, Gold Compounds, HCT 3012, Indomethacin, Isoniazid, Leflunomide, Levamisole, Licofelone, Lornoxicam, Loxoprofen, Meloxicam, Mesalazine, Methimazole, Methotrexate, Midomafetamine, Minocycline, N-(3-chlorophenyl)-2-(4-((2,4-dioxothiazolidin-5-ylidene)methyl)phenoxy)acetamide, Naproxen, OXT-328, Oxypurinol, Penicillamine, Pentazocine, Phenylbutazone, Pranoprofen, Pyrazinamide, Quinidine, Rifampicin, Sulfasalazine, Sulindac, Tetrandrine, Tolmetin, Tretinoin, phenylhydrazine, and phosphoaspirin. Substances such as Arachidonic Acid and Carbon are involved in disease pathogenesis.

Influenza is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as 1'-methylspiro(adamantane-2,3'-pyrrolidine)maleate, Acetylsalicylic acid, Active Hexose Correlated Compound, Amantadine, Azithromycin, Camostat, Isoquercitrin, Masoprocol, Oseltamivir, Oseltamivir acid, Ozone, Probenecid, Pseudoephedrine, Quercetin, Resveratrol, Rimantadine, Ro 64-0802, Selenium, Sulfur dioxide, Vitamin E, agathisflavone, amentoflavone, benzylsulfonylargininyl-proline-(4-amidinobenzyl)amide, pyrrolidine dithiocarbamic acid, and robustaflavone. The condition is linked to the Influenza A pathway.

Congestive heart failure is classified as a type of heart disease and is associated with drugs such as 5-FU-CDDP protocol, 14-dehydroxy-3-glucodigitoxigenin, Amphotericin B, Amrinone, Anagrelide, Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Inhibitors, Anti-Inflammatory Agents, Non-Steroidal, Atorvastatin, Bendroflumethiazide, Bucindolol, Captopril, Carbocromen, Carubicin, Cephalosporins, Chloroquine, Clindamycin, Denopamine, Dexamethasone, Diclofenac, Dobutamine, Dofetilide, Eplerenone, Fidarestat, Fludrocortisone, Fluvastatin, Indomethacin, LND 623, Meldonium, Methoxyflurane, Metoclopramide, Midodrine, N-(1-methylethyl)-1,1,2-trimethylpropylamine, Nicardipine, Nifedipine, Olprinone, Pentobarbital, Pergolide, Phentolamine, Pirbuterol, Piroximone, Potassium chloride, Razoxane, Rofecoxib, Smoke, Sodium, Testosterone, Tretinoin, aluminum phosphide, calcium channel blockers, and pirinixic acid. Substances such as 6-sulfatoxymelatonin and Hypoxanthine are involved in disease pathogenesis.

Cancer is classified as a disease of cellular proliferation and is associated with drugs such as 1,4-phenylenebis(methylene)selenocyanate, 2'-benzoyloxycinnamaldehyde, 2,4,5,2',4',5'-hexachlorobiphenyl, 2-ethyloestradiol-bis-sulfamate, 2-oxindole, 3-chloro-4-(dichloromethyl)-5-hydroxy-2(5H)-furanone, 4-biphenylamine, 4-demethyl-6-O-methyldoxorubicin, Biphenyl Compounds, Cadmium, Calcium, Capsaicin, Carbamates, Catechols, Coke, Curcumin, Cytarabine, Decitabine, Digoxin, EC145, Epoprostenol, Estradiol, Fish oil, Gemcitabine, Meloxicam, Melphalan, Mitomycin, N-7-(2-(nitrophenyldithio)ethyl)mitomycin C, N-nitrosoguvacoline, Phenethyl Isothiocyanate, Phenobarbital, Plant Preparations, Polysaccharides, Radioactive Pollutants, Razoxane, Salinomycin, Sparfosic acid, Streptozocin, Sulfates, Temsirolimus, Thiamine, U 0126, azinomycin epoxide, benzo(c)phenanthrene, diamminedichlorodihydroxyplatinum IV, echinocystic acid, ergosterol-5,8-peroxide, flavokawain A, liposomal doxorubicin, and tris(4-chlorophenyl)methanol. Substances such as Amines, Glycine, Kynurenic Acid, ethyl arachidonate, and trimethionine are involved in disease pathogenesis.
391:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia, where significant events occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and several deaths, and in Sri Lanka in 2017, which saw over 80,000 cases and 215 fatalities. Outbreaks have also been documented in Brazil, with a major event in 2002 involving over 317,000 cases, and in the Netherlands, where multiple outbreaks between 2004 and 2005 resulted in tens of thousands of cases and hundreds of deaths. Other notable outbreaks occurred in Pakistan, with large-scale events in 2019 and 2022, and in East Timor, where multiple outbreaks between 2005 and 2022 involved hundreds to thousands of cases. Additional outbreaks have been reported in countries such as Egypt, India, Cameroon, Bangladesh, France, and South America, with varying case numbers and mortality rates. The disease has also been recorded in smaller outbreaks in countries including Barbados, Portugal, Uruguay, and Ecuador, and in the French Caribbean region, where it was associated with a concurrent chikungunya outbreak.

Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. It is further categorized as a subtype of dengue disease, with dengue hemorrhagic fever being a more severe form that falls under the same category. Several drugs have been associated with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, F2-isoprostanes, hydroxychloroquine, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, rolitetracycline, silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A, which are either studied in relation to the disease or observed in clinical contexts.
392: Leishmaniasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It encompasses several clinical forms, including cutaneous leishmaniasis and mucocutaneous leishmaniasis, both of which are subtypes of leishmaniasis. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Pentamidine, Miltefosine, and various antiprotozoals. Additional drugs linked to treatment or study include Atovaquone, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, Paromomycin, Pentoxifylline, Omeprazole, Zinc sulfate, and liposomal amphotericin B. Other compounds such as 5,7-dihydroxy-8-(2-methylbutanoyl)-6-(3-methylbutyl)-4-phenyl-chroman-2-one, mammea A-BB, methyl gallate, fenarimol, pistagremic acid, and oil of Chenopodium have also been associated with the disease. Specific agents like Nelfinavir, Nitric Oxide, and WR279396 are noted in relation to leishmaniasis. The biological pathway associated with leishmaniasis is directly linked to the disease itself.
393:
Visceral Leishmaniasis has been reported in multiple outbreaks in Pakistan, with one event in January 2002 involving 738 confirmed cases, another in May 2002 with 200,000 cases, and a third in June 2002 also reporting 200,000 cases. The disease is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is a subtype of leishmaniasis. Treatment options include a range of drugs such as Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, and various antiprotozoals. Other compounds associated with treatment include 5,7-dihydroxy-8-(2-methylbutanoyl)-6-(3-methylbutyl)-4-phenyl-chroman-2-one, Atovaquone, Chromans, Marbofloxacin, Maytansine, Nitric Oxide, and Tafenoquine. The disease is linked to the biological pathway associated with leishmaniasis.

Malaria has been documented in several outbreaks, including one in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998, another in Burundi in May 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, a third in Tanzania in July 1999, and a significant outbreak in Pakistan in January 2022 involving 3,400,000 cases. The disease is also classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is a subtype of leishmaniasis. Symptoms include fever, chills, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, neurological manifestations, pallor, retinal hemorrhage, and others. Treatment involves drugs such as Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, Quinine, Artemisinin, Artesunate, Amodiaquine, Primaquine, Pyrimethamine, and various antimalarial combinations. Additional drugs associated with the disease include Atovaquone, Proguanil, and Dapsone. The disease is linked to the biological pathway associated with malaria and shares characteristics with conditions such as anemia, celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, and thymus cancer.
394:
Ebola has been associated with multiple outbreaks across several countries, including the Republic of Congo, Sierra Leone, Guinea Bissau, Guinea, United Republic of Tanzania, and others, spanning from 1996 to 2022. Notable events include a large outbreak in the Republic of Congo in 2018 with over 160 cases and 106 deaths, another in 2019 with 3,227 cases and 2,154 deaths, and a significant outbreak in 2020 with 3,458 cases and 2,277 deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in Sierra Leone, with over 1,700 cases and 961 deaths in 2014, and in Guinea Bissau, where multiple events occurred between 2014 and 2014, involving hundreds of cases and deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Treatment options under investigation or use include BH30sucMan, Favipiravir, Galidesivir, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Remdesivir, and Teicoplanin.

Dengue Fever has been reported in numerous countries across Asia, Africa, South America, and the Caribbean, with major outbreaks in Malaysia, Sri Lanka, Brazil, India, the Netherlands, and others. Notable events include a large outbreak in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, a significant outbreak in the Netherlands in 2004 with over 40,000 cases and 507 deaths, and a widespread outbreak in 2023 with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths. More recent outbreaks have occurred in 2022 and 2024, with over 7.6 million cases reported in 2024. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is a subtype of dengue disease. Potential therapeutic agents include 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, Doxycycline, F2-Isoprostanes, Hydroxychloroquine, Hydroxyeicosatetraenoic Acids, Rolitetracycline, Silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A.

SARS has been documented in multiple outbreaks, primarily in China, with significant events in 2003, including a major outbreak in March and April with over 3,000 cases and 154 deaths, and another in May with over 8,000 cases and 735 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in Singapore, Canada, the Philippines, and other regions, with cases reported in 2003 and 2004. The disease is associated with a viral infectious disease classification.

COVID-19 is classified as a coronavirus infectious disease and is linked to the biological pathway associated with Coronavirus disease - COVID-19. It is associated with a range of drugs, including Remdesivir, Hydroxychloroquine, Lopinavir, Ritonavir, Baricitinib, Interferon alfa, Ivermectin, Chloroquine, and others, some of which are used in combination therapies.
395: Rabies outbreaks have been reported in multiple instances, including an event in France on August 26, 2004, which resulted in one death. Additional outbreaks were recorded in unspecified locations on May 6, 1997, and April 10, 2024. Rabies is classified as a viral infectious disease. The drug cinnabarin is associated with rabies, indicating its use or study in relation to the condition.
396: Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various countries and time periods, including significant events in Malaysia in 1996 and 1997 with over 2,000 and 11,000 cases respectively, and in Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths. Notable outbreaks also occurred in The Netherlands in 2004, with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths, and in India during the 1990s and 2000s, with multiple events reporting thousands of cases and varying numbers of fatalities. Additional outbreaks were documented in Pakistan, Bangladesh, Brazil, the Philippines, East Timor, El Salvador, and other regions, with case counts ranging from a few to over 7.6 million in a single event reported in 2024. The disease has also been recorded in countries such as Egypt, Portugal, France, Spain, Uruguay, and Chad, with varying case numbers and mortality rates. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is further categorized as a subtype of dengue disease. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with the condition, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, Doxycycline, F2-Isoprostanes, Hydroxychloroquine, Hydroxyeicosatetraenoic Acids, Rolitetracycline, Silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A.
397: Opisthorchiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including anthocyanins, praziquantel, reactive nitrogen species, reactive oxygen species, and turmeric. Hepatitis C is categorized as a viral infectious disease and is linked to multiple drugs, such as interferon alfa, peginterferon alfa-2a, peginterferon alfa-2b, ribavirin, sofosbuvir, ledipasvir, daclatasvir, simeprevir, and various antivirals, including interferons and combinations like ledipasvir/sofosbuvir. The disease is associated with the hepatitis C pathway and has a role in disease pathogenesis involving alanine. Carcinogenesis is linked to a range of drugs, including 1,3-butadiene, benzo(a)pyrene, diethylnitrosamine, chromium hexavalent ion, and reactive oxygen species, with carbon identified as a contributing factor in disease development. Neoplastic processes are associated with curcumin, phytochemicals, and reactive oxygen species. Bile duct cancer presents symptoms including abdominal pain, colic, jaundice, obstructive jaundice, and pruritus, and is classified as a biliary tract cancer; it resembles duodenum cancer, gallbladder cancer, liver cancer, muscle cancer, pancreatic cancer, pancreatitis, primary biliary cholangitis, and sclerosing cholangitis. Bile duct carcinoma is a subtype of bile duct cancer and is associated with drugs such as azathioprine, bile acids and salts, danazol, dinitrobenzenes, and methyldopa. Cholangiocarcinoma, classified as a bile duct adenocarcinoma, is associated with drugs including azoxymethane, methotrexate, thalidomide, methylene chloride, nitroglycerin, pentachlorophenol, phenobarbital, furan, and various reactive species. Cancer, as a disease of cellular proliferation, is associated with a broad range of drugs such as curcumin, melatonin, arsenic trioxide, resveratrol, and numerous agents including polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, heavy metals, and antineoplastons, with disease pathogenesis involving amines, glycine, kynurenic acid, and other compounds. Inflammation is associated with drugs including anti-inflammatory agents, omega-3 fatty acids, melatonin, and various compounds like doxorubicin, ebselen, and tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, with alanine and posaconazole implicated in disease mechanisms. Schistosomiasis, classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease, is associated with metrifonate and praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is linked to praziquantel, quercetin, and resveratrol.
398:
Liver disease is associated with a wide range of drugs, including 1,2,4-trichlorobenzene, 1,4-dioxane, 2,4,5,2',4',5'-hexachlorobiphenyl, AO-8 formulation, acetaminophen, acrylamide, Agent Orange, anethole, azathioprine, benzo(a)pyrene, carmustine, cephalexin, chlorpropamide, cimetidine, clindamycin, contraceptives, oral hormonal, diethylhexyl phthalate, disopyramide, doxorubicin, enalapril, ethanol, ethosuximide, flutamide, idarubicin, levothyroxine, lincomycin, melatonin, N-(oxo-5,6-dihydrophenanthridin-2-yl)-N,N-dimethylacetamide hydrochloride, N-vinyl-2-pyrrolidinone, nicardipine, nitrofurantoin, PCB 180, patulin, plant extracts, prednisone, rotenone, sodium fluoride, sulfapral, ursodeoxycholic acid, vincristine, vitamin E, zidovudine, zinc, zinc sulfate, clentiazem, clometacin, fanasil/pyrimethamine drug combination, propiconazole, pyrazinobutazone, and triadimefon. Additionally, hepatitis B e antigens and toluene are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis. Liver disease is classified as a type of hepatobiliary disease.

Infection is associated with numerous drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, clarithromycin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cytarabine, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, doxorubicin, epirubicin, etoposide, everolimus, exisulind, FEC protocol, fludarabine, itraconazole, lithium, MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, metronidazole, netilmicin, pirarubicin, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, sirolimus, TAC protocol, tetrandrine, tobramycin, topotecan, triamcinolone, vancomycin, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin.

Filariasis is associated with the drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole, and it is classified as a type of parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease.

Cholangiocarcinoma is associated with drugs such as 1,2,3-trichloropropane, 2,2-bis(bromomethyl)-1,3-propanediol, 2,3',4,4',5-pentachlorobiphenyl, 2-aminofluorene, 2-nitrotoluene, 3,4,5,3',4'-pentachlorobiphenyl, anabolic agents, azoxymethane, carmustine, chlorodiphenyl (54% chlorine), diethylnitrosamine, dimethylnitrosamine, melatonin, methotrexate, methylene chloride, nitroglycerin, pentachlorophenol, phenobarbital, reactive nitrogen species, reactive oxygen species, tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, thalidomide, thioacetamide, furan, methylethylnitrosamine, nitromethane, and propylene dichloride. It is classified as a type of bile duct adenocarcinoma.

Onchocerciasis is associated with the drugs abamectin, albendazole, diethylcarbamazine, and ivermectin, and it is classified as a type of filariasis.

Schistosomiasis is associated with the drugs metrifonate and praziquantel.

Intestinal schistosomiasis is associated with the drugs praziquantel, quercetin, and resveratrol, and it is classified as a type of schistosomiasis.
399:
Outbreaks of Zika virus have been reported across multiple countries and regions, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, India, Brazil, Argentina, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Sint Maarten, and others, spanning from 2015 to 2021. These outbreaks varied in scale, with case counts ranging from single cases to over 8,700 in French Guiana in 2015, and included reports of deaths in Panama and the United States. Zika virus is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological agents associated with Zika virus infection include Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and Niclosamide, which are often studied or used in conjunction with the disease.

Dengue Fever has been documented in numerous countries, including Malaysia, Sri Lanka, East Timor, Barbados, El Salvador, Brazil, Egypt, India, The Netherlands, Cape Verde, France, Pakistan, Burkina Faso, Bangladesh, Uruguay, and others, with outbreaks recorded from 1995 to 2024. Notable events include a large outbreak in Brazil in 2002 with over 317,000 cases, a severe outbreak in Sri Lanka in 2017 with over 80,000 cases and 215 deaths, and a massive outbreak in 2024 affecting multiple countries with over 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths. Dengue Fever is also classified as a viral infectious disease, and its severe form, dengue hemorrhagic fever, is considered a subtype of dengue disease. Drugs associated with Dengue Fever include 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, Doxycycline, F2-Isoprostanes, Hydroxychloroquine, Hydroxyeicosatetraenoic Acids, Rolitetracycline, Silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A.

Chikungunya virus disease has been reported in countries such as the United Republic of Tanzania, United States, Congo, Cameroon, France, Chad, Reunion, St Martin, Italy, Spain, Argentina, and Egypt, with outbreaks occurring between 2005 and 2018. The disease has been associated with significant case numbers, including over 27,000 cases in Chad in 2020 and over 13,000 cases in Egypt in 2018. Chikungunya virus disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with this condition include Chloroquine. Additionally, the disease has been linked to outbreaks in the French Caribbean and other regions, with some events involving suspected or confirmed cases in multiple islands.

Infection, as a general category, is associated with a broad range of drugs, including antibiotics such as Benzylpenicillin, Cefalotin, Cefapirin, Cefotiam, Cefoxitin, Ceftazidime, Cephalexin, Ciprofloxacin, Clarithromycin, Clindamycin, Vancomycin, and Tobramycin, as well as immunosuppressants like Azathioprine, Cyclophosphamide, Cyclosporine, and Sirolimus, chemotherapeutic agents such as Carboplatin, Docetaxel, Epirubicin, and Cytarabine, and other compounds including Silver sulfadiazine, Lithium, and various corticosteroids.

Guillain-Barré syndrome is classified as an autoimmune disease of the peripheral nervous system. Drugs associated with this condition include Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Inhibitors, Antilymphocyte Serum, Cisplatin, Cladribine, Colchicine, Cyclophosphamide, Cyclosporine, Cytarabine, Fibrinolytic Agents, Gemcitabine, Penicillamine, Pravastatin, Simvastatin, and corticosteroids.
400: Rabies has been reported in multiple outbreaks, including one in France on August 26, 2004, which resulted in one death. Additional outbreaks were documented in unspecified locations on May 6, 1997, and April 10, 2024. Rabies is classified as a viral infectious disease. The drug cinnabarin is associated with rabies, indicating its use or study in relation to the condition.
401:
Hendra virus outbreaks have been reported across multiple countries and time periods, including a significant event in 1995 in an unspecified location with 367 confirmed cases and 5 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in Malaysia on April 7, 1999, involving 11 cases and 1 death; in Kosovo on August 30, 1999, with 1 confirmed case; in the United States on September 4, 2012, with 6 cases and 2 deaths; and on September 12, 2012, with 8 cases and 3 deaths. A more recent outbreak was reported in Argentina on December 19, 2018, with 29 confirmed cases and 11 deaths. Another outbreak was documented in Colombia, Honduras, and Brazil on October 2, 1997, though specific case and death counts were not provided.

                        Hendra virus is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. The drug 3-(5-tert-butylisoxazol-3-yl)-2-((3-chlorophenyl)hydrazono)-3-oxopropionitrile and the antiviral agent Favipiravir are associated with tick-borne diseases, which may include or relate to Hendra virus in the context of broader infectious disease management.
402: Schistosomiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The condition is associated with several drugs, including Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is also linked to the use of Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol.
403: Trachoma is classified as a commensal bacterial infectious disease.
404: Tick-borne diseases are associated with a range of drugs, including 3-(5-tert-butylisoxazol-3-yl)-2-((3-chlorophenyl)hydrazono)-3-oxopropionitrile and Favipiravir, which are often studied or used in conjunction with the condition. Inflammation, a key feature of these diseases, is linked to multiple drugs that are either associated with the condition or play a role in its pathogenesis, such as Alendronate, Prednisolone, and various compounds including cycloheximide, formaldehyde, and thalidomide. Notably, alanine and posaconazole are implicated in the disease's pathogenic mechanisms. Tick-borne diseases are classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and are also categorized under disease by infectious agent. The condition known as tick-borne relapsing fever is a subtype of relapsing fever, which itself falls under the broader category of primary bacterial infectious diseases.
405:
Rabies outbreaks have been reported in multiple instances, including one in France on August 26, 2004, which resulted in one death. Additional outbreaks were recorded in unspecified locations on May 6, 1997, and April 10, 2024.  

Rabies is classified as a viral infectious disease. The drug cinnabarin is associated with rabies, indicating its use or study in relation to the condition.
406: Brucellosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. It is associated with several drugs, including Cholesterol, LDL, HL 004, Ketoconazole, Rifampicin, and Tetracycline, which are often observed or studied in conjunction with the condition. The disease is caused by various species of Brucella, including Brucella melitensis, Brucella abortus, Brucella suis, and Brucella canis, all of which are subtypes of brucellosis. Additionally, certain substances such as Homocysteine, Isoprostanes, Mannitol, Tocopherol, and Ubidecarenone are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis.
407: Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole are associated with the condition, often observed or studied in conjunction with it.
408: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, and July 14, 1999, as well as in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases, and in Ethiopia on November 1, 2024. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway known as Malaria. The condition presents with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, hemoglobinuria, vomiting, diarrhea, coma, neurological manifestations, oliguria, pallor, purpura, retinal hemorrhage, and unconsciousness. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or study of malaria, including Artemether, Artesunate, Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, Quinine, Mefloquine, Primaquine, Atovaquone, Proguanil, and Amodiaquine. Some of these drugs, such as Chloroquine and Quinine, are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis through their influence on cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. Malaria is known to resemble other conditions such as celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, and thymus cancer, and it is also associated with hematopoietic system diseases.
409: An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which in turn is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, and liposomal amphotericin B, among others. Additional therapeutic agents include Allopurinol, Ketoconazole, Paromomycin, and various experimental compounds such as amarogentin and Val-Glu-Pro-Ile-Gly-Tyr. The disease is also linked to biological pathways involving Leishmaniasis. Other related diseases include cutaneous leishmaniasis and mucocutaneous leishmaniasis, both of which share the same classification under leishmaniasis and are similarly associated with drugs like Amphotericin B, Antimony, and Pentamidine.
410:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple countries across various time periods, including Malaysia, where outbreaks occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and several deaths, and in The Netherlands, where significant outbreaks were documented in 2004, involving tens of thousands of cases and hundreds of fatalities. Additional outbreaks have been recorded in India, Pakistan, Brazil, Cameroon, El Salvador, and other regions, with case counts ranging from a few to over 7.6 million in a single event reported in 2024. The disease has also been observed in East Timor, Bangladesh, France, Thailand, and several Caribbean and South American locations, with varying case numbers and mortality rates. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with a range of drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, among others. It is also linked to specific symptoms such as fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, purpura, and neurological manifestations. The condition is further associated with the broader category of dengue hemorrhagic fever, which is a subtype of dengue disease. 

Malaria has been documented in multiple outbreaks, including in Burundi in 1999 with over 616,000 cases and 51 deaths, and in the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999. A large-scale outbreak was reported in Pakistan in 2022, involving 3.4 million cases. More recent reports indicate ongoing outbreaks in Ethiopia and other regions. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with symptoms such as fever, chills, jaundice, hemoglobinuria, diarrhea, vomiting, coma, and neurological disorders. It is linked to several drugs, including chloroquine, artemisinin, quinine, and various antimalarial combinations, as well as agents that play a role in disease pathogenesis such as cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. The disease is also associated with the biological pathway known as Malaria.
411: An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis. Several drugs are associated with the disease, including Allopurinol, Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Antimony potassium tartrate, Antimony sodium tartrate, Antiprotozoals, Artemisinin, Desmodium gangeticum extract, Isosorbide dinitrate, Ketoconazole, Meglumine, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Neopterin, Organotin Compounds, Paromomycin, Pentamidine, Reactive Oxygen Species, Thalidomide, Val-Glu-Pro-Ile-Gly-Tyr, amarogentin, amphotericin B, deoxycholate drug combination, and liposomal amphotericin B.
412:
Rift Valley Fever has been associated with multiple documented outbreaks across various regions, including Somalia in 1998, Madagascar in 2008, and repeated occurrences in the United Republic of Tanzania, Egypt, Yemen, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Niger, China, Cameroon, and Mayotte between 1996 and 2022. These outbreaks have involved case counts ranging from 1 to over 600 and have resulted in significant mortality, with death tolls reaching up to 109 in some instances. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological studies have identified Galidesivir and Ribavirin as drugs associated with Rift Valley Fever.

Hemorrhagic fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across Central and East Africa, including the United Republic of Tanzania, Congo, Gabon, Sudan, Angola, and other regions, with documented events spanning from 1996 to 2012. These outbreaks have involved case numbers ranging from 1 to over 395 and have resulted in death tolls as high as 176. The disease is categorized as a sensory system disease.
413:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including Malaysia in 1996 and 1997 with outbreaks resulting in thousands of cases and dozens of deaths, Sri Lanka in 2017 with over 80,000 cases and 215 deaths, and India in multiple years including 1996, 2003, and 2022, with case counts ranging from several thousand to over 50,000. Outbreaks have also been documented in Pakistan, Bangladesh, the Netherlands, Cape Verde, El Salvador, the Philippines, and other regions, with varying case numbers and mortality rates. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its management involves the use of several drugs, including hydroxychloroquine, doxycycline, silver, and various hydroxycholesterols. Additionally, it is associated with other conditions such as dengue hemorrhagic fever, which is considered a subtype of dengue disease.

Chikungunya virus disease has been reported in multiple countries, including Tanzania, Congo, Reunion, Italy, Spain, and Egypt, with outbreaks occurring between 2005 and 2020. Notable events include a large outbreak in Chad in 2020 with over 27,000 cases and a death, and significant outbreaks in Reunion and Tanzania. The disease is also categorized as a viral infectious disease, and its treatment includes the use of chloroquine. The condition shares epidemiological and clinical features with other arboviral diseases, particularly dengue, though it is distinct in its pathogenesis and symptomatology.

Zika virus outbreaks have been documented in multiple regions, including Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, French Guiana, the United States, Mexico, Panama, Ecuador, and India, with major events occurring between 2015 and 2016. The virus has been associated with severe outcomes such as congenital syndrome and neurological complications. Treatment includes the use of chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, and other antiviral agents. Zika fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and the virus has been linked to outbreaks in both tropical and temperate regions, often in association with travel or local transmission in endemic areas.

Other diseases such as encephalitis, cerebrovascular disease, diabetes mellitus, and nervous system diseases are associated with a wide range of drugs, including antivirals, anti-inflammatory agents, and metabolic modulators. Encephalitis is linked to the TCR signaling pathway and is classified as a brain disease. Cerebrovascular disease is a type of vascular disease, while diabetes mellitus is a glucose metabolism disorder. Nervous system diseases are associated with neurotoxic agents and psychiatric medications, and some are linked to specific metabolic pathways. Myelitis and Guillain-Barré syndrome are classified as spinal cord and autoimmune peripheral nervous system diseases, respectively, and are associated with various immunomodulatory and neurotoxic drugs.
414:
An outbreak of HIV infection-AIDS occurred in Pakistan on April 25, 2019, with 876 confirmed cases. HIV infection is associated with the biological pathway of Human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection.

Urticaria is classified as a skin disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, non-steroidal anti-inflammatory agents, antihistamines such as cetirizine and loratadine, antibiotics like cephalexin and roxithromycin, anticoagulants such as enoxaparin, and various other substances including pesticides, plant extracts, and dyes like tartrazine.

Angioedema, which is also categorized as a skin disease, is linked to numerous drugs, including acetylsalicylic acid, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors such as lisinopril and ramipril, calcium channel blockers, corticosteroids, and other agents like ibuprofen, hydrochlorothiazide, and estrogens.
