Tuberculosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including isoniazid, rifampicin, pyrazinamide, ethambutol, and streptomycin, often used in combination regimens. Additional drugs associated with tuberculosis include bedaquiline, moxifloxacin, and prednisolone. Certain drugs, such as lopinavir, phosphorus, and phosphates, are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis.

Shock is associated with a broad spectrum of drugs, including amiodarone, cyclosporine, antihypertensives like verapamil and nifedipine, and various other agents such as cocaine, morphine, and antipsychotics. Drugs contributing to shock pathogenesis include taurine.

Serum sickness is linked to drugs such as antilymphocyte serum, minocycline, and phenytoin.

Necrosis is associated with a diverse group of compounds, including industrial chemicals, pesticides, heavy metals, and pharmaceuticals such as docetaxel, tamoxifen, and cyclophosphamide. Drugs implicated in the pathogenesis of necrosis include magnesium.
415: An outbreak of myocarditis occurred in Sri Lanka on March 17, 2005, and another in Greece on April 25, 2002, which involved 39 confirmed cases and 3 deaths. Myocarditis is classified as an extrinsic cardiomyopathy. It is associated with a range of drugs, including Amiodarone, Amphetamines, Amphotericin B, Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme Inhibitors, Azathioprine, Carvedilol, Clozapine, Curcumin, Dapsone, Daunorubicin, Desipramine, Dobutamine, Epinephrine, Haloperidol, Idarubicin, Imipramine, Lithium carbonate, Minocycline, Particulate Matter, Perphenazine, Phenelzine, Pimobendan, Pranlukast, Prednisolone, Prednisone, Primidone, Propylthiouracil, Simendan, Sodium Chloride Symporter Inhibitors, Sodium Potassium Chloride Symporter Inhibitors, Sulfasalazine, Testosterone, Tetracycline, Ticlopidine, Tretinoin, Zidovudine, antidepressants, calycosin-7-O-glucopyranoside, catecholamines, corticosteroids, fructus schizandrae, radix ginseng, radix ophiopogonis drug combination, and methyllycaconitine. Additionally, the condition has been linked to the presence of 4-hydroxy-2-nonenal, Malonaldehyde, and other compounds such as cytokines and methyllycaconitine.
416: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998 and July 1999, and in Ethiopia in November 2024. A further outbreak was reported in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, hemoglobinuria, and neurological manifestations. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or study of malaria, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, quinine, pyrimethamine, atovaquone-proguanil, and lumefantrine. Some of these drugs, such as chloroquine and artemisinin derivatives, are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. Malaria is linked to the biological pathway associated with malaria infection.
417: Elephantiasis is classified as a type of lymphedema and is associated with the use of drugs such as quartz, smectite, and mica. Filarial elephantiasis, a form of elephantiasis, is linked to the drugs albendazole, diethylcarbamazine, and ivermectin, and is categorized as a type of filariasis. Filariasis itself is a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with the drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole. Lymphedema, in turn, is a type of lymphatic system disease and is associated with the drugs melphalan and sirolimus; caprolactone plays a role in its disease pathogenesis. Biological pathways associated with lymphedema include AKT phosphorylation of nuclear targets, focal adhesion, RAF/MAP kinase cascade, and signaling to p38 via RIT and RIN. Hydrocele is classified as a male reproductive system disease and is associated with the drugs phenol and tetracycline. Fever is associated with a broad range of drugs, including albendazole, alcohols, aminoglutethimide, amiodarone, amoxicillin, artesunate, bifenthrin, bupivacaine, buprenorphine, buspirone, carbamazepine, carmofur, cefadroxil, ceftazidime, cetirizine, cocaine, cyclopentolate, cypermethrin, dantrolene, dinoprost, glycine betaine, imipramine, indomethacin, interleukin 1 receptor antagonist protein, isoniazid, lamotrigine, M-VAC protocol, meprobamate, meticillin, minocycline, minoxidil, N-phenethylpiperidine oxalate, nifedipine, nizatidine, omeprazole, parecoxib, phenylbutazone, phosphatidylinositols, prajmaline, tacrolimus, tegafur, thiothixene, topotecan, trifluoperazine, valganciclovir, vinblastine, vinorelbine, sodium arsenite, ukrain, and xanthurenic acid; hydroxyindoleacetic acid is involved in its disease pathogenesis. Podoconiosis is classified as a type of elephantiasis.
418:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia in 1997 with over 11,000 cases and 28 deaths, Sri Lanka in 2017 with 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, Brazil in 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths, and the Netherlands in 2004 with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in countries such as Cameroon, East Timor, Egypt, India, Pakistan, and the Republic of Panama, with case counts ranging from dozens to hundreds of thousands and death tolls varying from a few to over 750. Notably, a large-scale outbreak was reported in 2023 with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths, and another in 2024 involving 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and hinokiflavone. It is also linked to dengue hemorrhagic fever, a more severe form of the disease.

Typhoid fever has been documented in outbreaks in the Republic of Tajikistan in 1996, with over 7,500 cases reported, and in Pakistan in 2016 with 5,274 cases. The disease is categorized as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the Salmonella infection pathway. Treatment options include chloramphenicol and ciprofloxacin.

Typhus has been reported in Burundi in 1997 with 23,889 cases. It is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and includes subtypes such as epidemic typhus and endemic typhus. Treatment involves azithromycin and prednisolone.

Malaria has been observed in outbreaks in the United Republic of Tanzania, Burundi, and Ethiopia, with one notable event in Pakistan in 2022 involving 3.4 million cases. The disease is associated with a range of symptoms including fever, chills, jaundice, coma, diarrhea, and neurological manifestations. It is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is linked to the malaria pathway. Treatment includes drugs such as chloroquine, artemisinin, quinine, and various antimalarial combinations. Cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin are also implicated in its pathogenesis.

Other conditions such as splenomegaly, hepatomegaly, and lymphadenopathy are associated with a wide range of drugs, including ciprofloxacin, methotrexate, arsenic trioxide, and various environmental pollutants, though these are not directly linked to the primary diseases in this context.
419:
Epilepsy is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including seizures, amnesia (both anterograde and retrograde), transient global amnesia, aphasia (including Wernicke's aphasia), ataxia, dystonia, chorea, coma, stupor, syncope, sleep disorders, spasm, muscle hypotonia, and various neurological conditions such as cerebral palsy, Rett syndrome, and Kearns-Sayre syndrome. It also presents with systemic manifestations like fever, apnea, and hypoxia. The condition is classified as a brain disease and shares clinical similarities with migraine. Several drugs are associated with epilepsy, including anticonvulsants such as carbamazepine, valproic acid, gabapentin, topiramate, and clonazepam, as well as agents like ACTH, adenosine, and lithium. Some drugs, such as choline, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. Additionally, certain medications like amphetamine, methylphenobarbital, and anticholinesterases are noted for their association with epileptic episodes.

Onchocerciasis is classified as a type of filariasis and is treated with drugs including ivermectin, diethylcarbamazine, albendazole, and abamectin. Filariasis, in turn, is categorized as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. Mansonelliasis is also recognized as a form of filariasis. 

COVID-19 is classified as a coronavirus infectious disease. It is associated with a broad spectrum of drugs, including antivirals such as remdesivir, lopinavir-ritonavir, ribavirin, and sofosbuvir, as well as repurposed agents like chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, baricitinib, and ivermectin. Other drugs associated with the condition include acalabrutinib, azithromycin, and various immunomodulators and anticoagulants. Choline is also noted as playing a role in the disease’s pathogenesis.

Stroke is associated with a variety of drugs, including anticoagulants (e.g., acenocoumarol, warfarin), antiplatelets (e.g., clopidogrel, aspirin), statins (e.g., pravastatin), and agents used for blood pressure control (e.g., doxazosin, indapamide-perindopril combination). Some drugs, such as ethinylestradiol and oral contraceptives, are linked to increased stroke risk. Additionally, inositol and malonaldehyde are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis.
420: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi in May 1999 with 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths, and another in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998. Additional outbreaks occurred in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan in January 2022, with the latter reporting 3.4 million cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, and neurological manifestations such as cerebellar ataxia and consciousness disorders. Several drugs are used in its treatment, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemisinin-based combinations, quinine, mefloquine, and primaquine. Some drugs are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, including cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. The disease is linked to the Malaria pathway and is associated with other conditions such as fetal hypoxia, hemoglobinuria, and retinal hemorrhage.
421: Parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease is classified as a type of parasitic infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including Albendazole, Anthelmintics, Closantel, Ivermectin, Levamisole, Mebendazole, Netobimin, Niclosamide, Praziquantel, and Thiabendazole. Schistosomiasis, which is a subtype of parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease, is associated with the drugs Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a form of schistosomiasis, is linked to the drugs Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol. Hookworm disease is associated with the drugs Albendazole, Dichlorvos, Ivermectin, Oxantel pamoate, and tribendimidine.
422:
Influenza is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with the Influenza A pathway. Several drugs are linked to influenza, including amantadine, rimantadine, oseltamivir, oseltamivir acid, pseudoephedrine, quercetin, resveratrol, vitamin E, selenium, and various other compounds such as 1'-methylspiro(adamantane-2,3'-pyrrolidine)maleate, acetylsalicylic acid, and active hexose correlated compound. 

Hepatitis C is also categorized as a viral infectious disease and is associated with the Hepatitis C pathway. It is linked to a broad range of drugs, including interferon alfa, peginterferon alfa-2a, peginterferon alfa-2b, ribavirin, sofosbuvir, ledipasvir, daclatasvir, simeprevir, telaprevir, nelfinavir, and others such as 2'-deoxy-2'-fluoro-2'-C-methylcytidine, azathioprine, cyclosporine, tacrolimus, and mycophenolic acid. Additionally, certain compounds like platycodin D, platycodigenin, and deapi-platycodin D are associated with hepatitis C, and alanine is noted to play a role in the disease's pathogenesis.

Viral infectious disease is further classified as a disease by infectious agent and is associated with the drug tilorone. The broader category of Virus Diseases includes drugs such as cidofovir, melatonin, 6-phosphonylmethoxyethoxy-2,4-diaminopyrimidine, tobacco smoke pollution, ergosterol-5,8-peroxide, proxigermanium, and putranjivain A.
423:
Japanese Encephalitis is classified as a viral infectious disease. Documented outbreaks have occurred in multiple countries, including Malaysia in 1999 with 133 confirmed cases and 54 deaths, India in 1999 (Andhra) with 965 cases and 200 deaths, India in 2005 with 1,145 cases and 296 deaths, Thailand in 1997 with 247 cases and 24 deaths, and Australia in 2022 with 37 cases and 3 deaths. The disease is caused by the Japanese Encephalitis virus and is associated with severe neurological manifestations, including coma, unconsciousness, and consciousness disorders.

Rabies is classified as a viral infectious disease. Outbreaks have been reported in France in 2004 with one death, and in unspecified locations in 1997 and 2024. The disease is associated with the drug cinnabarin, which is observed in concurrent studies or treatments.

H5N1, also known as the Type A (H5N1) flu strain, is a subtype of influenza. Outbreaks have been reported in numerous countries, including Egypt, China, Vietnam, Thailand, The Netherlands, India, Azerbaijan, Germany, Turkey, Kazakhstan, Pakistan, and others, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 120 and death tolls from 1 to 112. Notable outbreaks include a 2007 event in The Netherlands with 109 cases and 88 deaths, and a 2008 event in The Netherlands with 124 cases and 101 deaths. The disease is associated with the drug chloroquine, which is studied in relation to its effects on the condition.

Rift Valley Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. Outbreaks have occurred in Madagascar in 2008 with 418 cases and 17 deaths, United Republic of Tanzania in 2006, 2007, 2018, and 2020 with multiple case counts and deaths, Niger in 2016 with 64 cases and 23 deaths, Egypt in 2007, 2008, 2012, 2020, and 2022 with varying case and death numbers, Yemen in 2000 with 321 cases and 32 deaths, Saudi Arabia in 2000 with 405 cases and 76 deaths, South Africa in 1999 and 2010 with 3 and 172 cases respectively, and China in 2016 with one case. The disease is associated with the drugs Galidesivir and Ribavirin, which are studied in relation to its treatment.

SARS is classified as a coronavirus infectious disease. Outbreaks occurred globally in 2003, with major events in China, Hong Kong, Canada, Vietnam, and other regions, resulting in over 8,000 cases and more than 700 deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in the United Kingdom in 2020 and Denmark in 2020. The disease is associated with multiple drugs, including Remdesivir, Chloroquine, Lopinavir-Ritonavir, Niclosamide, and others, which are studied for their potential therapeutic effects.

Avian influenza is classified as an influenza. Outbreaks have been reported in China, Egypt, Vietnam, The Netherlands, Cambodia, and other countries, with case counts ranging from 1 to over 160 and death tolls from 1 to 141. Notable events include a 2007 outbreak in The Netherlands with 163 cases and 135 deaths, and a 2010 outbreak with 1 case and 141 deaths. The disease is associated with the drug Active Hexose Correlated Compound and is linked to the Influenza A biological pathway.

Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, and neurological manifestations. Outbreaks have been reported in Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, and Pakistan in 2022 with 3,400,000 cases. The disease is associated with numerous drugs, including Chloroquine, Quinine, Mefloquine, Artemisinin, Atovaquone, and others, some of which play a role in disease pathogenesis through mechanisms involving cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. The disease is also linked to the Malaria biological pathway.

Cerebral malaria is a severe form of malaria and is associated with the drugs Mefloquine and Quinine.

Leishmaniasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Antimony, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, and others. It is linked to the Leishmaniasis biological pathway.

Trypanosomiasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as Antimony, Arsenic compounds, Pentamidine, and Borrelidin.

Chagas disease, also known as American trypanosomiasis, is classified as a form of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with drugs such as Benznidazole, Nifurtimox, and Nitrofural, and is linked to the Chagas disease biological pathway.
424:
Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with the drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole. 

Hookworm disease is associated with the drugs albendazole, dichlorvos, ivermectin, oxantel pamoate, and tribendimidine.

Anemia is characterized by a wide range of symptoms, including acute abdominal pain, fatigue, pallor, fever, diarrhea, jaundice, hematemesis, proteinuria, purpura, retinal and vitreous hemorrhage, and other systemic manifestations. It is categorized as a hematopoietic system disease and shares clinical similarities with celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, malaria, and thymus cancer. The condition is associated with multiple drugs, including ferrous sulfate, iron dextran, cyanocobalamin, and phenylhydrazine, and is linked to the role of alanine in disease pathogenesis. Additional drugs associated with anemia include 5-benzylacyclouridine, 5-FU-CDDP protocol, amphetamine, analgesics, bicalutamide, dapsone, dasatinib, dexamethasone, and many others, some of which are used in cancer therapy or have toxic effects.

Ascariasis and ascaridiasis are both classified as parasitic helminthiasis infectious diseases. Ascariasis is associated with drugs such as albendazole, dichlorvos, fenbendazole, mebendazole, oxibendazole, pyrantel pamoate, and drugs of Chinese herbal origin.

Trichuriasis is also classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with dichlorvos and oxantel pamoate.

Infection is associated with a broad range of drugs, including antibiotics such as ciprofloxacin, ceftriaxone, and vancomycin, chemotherapeutic agents like methotrexate, doxorubicin, and cyclophosphamide, immunosuppressants such as cyclosporine and sirolimus, and other agents including arsenic, cocaine, and lithium.
425: Critical illness is associated with the use of androgens, simendan, and estrogens, which are commonly observed or studied in the context of this condition.
426:
Dengue Fever has been associated with multiple outbreaks across various regions, including Malaysia in 1996 with 2,450 cases, Cameroon in 2017 with 623 cases and 2 deaths, Sri Lanka in 2017 reporting 80,732 cases and 215 deaths, East Timor in 2005 with 95 cases and 11 deaths, Barbados in 2019 with 339 cases and 6 deaths, Brazil in 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths, Egypt in 2019 with 1,197 cases and 5 deaths, India in 2003 with 1,723 cases and 4 deaths, and multiple countries across Asia, Africa, and South America in 1998 with 16,647 cases and 55 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in Rarotonga in 1997 with 123 cases, Cape Verde in 2009 with 3,367 cases, and in 2023 with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths reported in an unspecified location. A more recent outbreak in 2024 involved 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths, also reported without a specific country. Other notable events include outbreaks in Pakistan in 2019 with 47,120 cases and 75 deaths, and in 2022 with 25,932 cases and 62 deaths, as well as in Bangladesh in 2022 with 52,807 cases and 230 deaths. The disease has also been reported in Spain, Uruguay, Ecuador, and other locations with smaller case counts. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its related conditions include dengue hemorrhagic fever and dengue shock syndrome, both of which are subtypes of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with the disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, hinokiflavone, podocarpusflavone A, and silver.

Ebola has been documented in multiple outbreaks, primarily in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, with significant events in 2018, including a large outbreak beginning in August 2018 with 78 cases and 44 deaths, and another in October 2018 with 194 cases and 122 deaths. Subsequent outbreaks in 2019 included 628 cases and 383 deaths in January, 186 cases and 2,052 deaths in September, and 3,227 cases and 2,154 deaths in October. A major outbreak in 2020 involved 3,463 cases and 2,280 deaths, and another in 2021 with 11 cases and 9 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in 2014 in Guinea Bissau with 224 cases and 143 deaths, and in Sierra Leone with 163 cases and 61 deaths, as well as 888 cases and 539 deaths in July 2014. Other events include outbreaks in Gabon in 2001 with 29 cases and 20 deaths, in United Republic of Tanzania in 2000 with 71 cases and 35 deaths, and in Angola in 2014 with 7 cases and 2 deaths. The disease has also been reported in the Cuvette Ouest region in 2003 and in multiple other locations with varying case numbers. Ebola is classified as a viral infectious disease, and its severe form, Ebola hemorrhagic fever, is a recognized subtype. Several drugs have been associated with the disease, including favipiravir, galidesivir, remdesivir, teicoplanin, and BH30sucMan. The condition is also linked to viremia, a state of virus presence in the bloodstream, which is associated with drugs such as ribavirin, zidovudine, and adefovir dipivoxil, and in some cases, alanine may play a role in disease pathogenesis. Thrombocytopenia, a condition characterized by low platelet count, is also associated with Ebola and is linked to various drugs and the tight junction pathway, and is classified as a blood platelet disease.
427: Onchocerciasis is classified as a type of filariasis and is associated with several drugs, including Abamectin, Albendazole, Diethylcarbamazine, and Ivermectin. Tinea pedis, a fungal infection of the feet, is categorized as a form of dermatophytosis and is treated with drugs such as Fluconazole, Griseofulvin, and Itraconazole. Tinea capitis, a fungal infection affecting the scalp, is also a subtype of dermatophytosis and is managed using Griseofulvin, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, Methoxsalen, and Terbinafine. Filariasis is a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with Acetylsalicylic acid and Levamisole. Eczema is linked to a wide range of drugs and environmental agents, including alclometasone dipropionate, betamethasone benzoate, betamethasone-17,21-dipropionate, diazolidinylurea, diisobutyl phthalate, diisodecyl phthalate, diisononyl phthalate, dinocap, mancozeb, methyldithiocarbamate, mono-(2-ethylhexyl)phthalate, mono-isobutyl phthalate, monoethyl phthalate, propachlor, propineb, and various other compounds such as Agent Orange, Air Pollutants, Alitretinoin, Amcinonide, Benomyl, Bromothalonil, Carbamazepine, Carbaryl, Chloral hydrate, Dexamethasone, Diethylhexyl Phthalate, Dimethoate, Dinitrobenzenes, Formaldehyde, Halcinonide, Hydrocortisone, Lead, Maneb, Methoxsalen, Nickel sulfate, Nitrogen Dioxide, Nitrogen Oxides, Particulate Matter, Phenylmercuric acetate, Phylloquinone, Picryl Chloride, Polymyxin B, Prednicarbate, Tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, Thiram, Tretinoin, Trioxsalen, Vehicle Emissions, Zineb, and others. Osteomyelitis is a type of bone inflammation disease. Unspecified osteomyelitis NOS is associated with a broad range of drugs, including Cefazolin, Cefepime, Cefotetan, Cefpirome, Ceftazidime, Ceftriaxone, Chloramphenicol, Ciprofloxacin, Co-Trimoxazole, Cocaine, Colistin, Daptomycin, Diclofenac, Fusidic acid, Gentamicins, Ibuprofen, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, Linezolid, Methotrexate, Meticillin, Metronidazole, Nafcillin, Neomycin, Ofloxacin, Pefloxacin, Rifampicin, Teicoplanin, Telavancin, Tobramycin, Vancomycin, and Zoledronic acid. Scabies is a mite infestation and is treated with Benzyl benzoate, Ivermectin, Lindane, Permethrin, Piperonyl butoxide, butyl phosphorotrithioate, diethyl maleate, and sulfur. Yaws and Buruli ulcer disease are both classified as primary bacterial infectious diseases. Pityriasis versicolor is a superficial mycosis and is managed with Fluconazole and Ketoconazole. Trypanosomiasis, a parasitic protozoa infectious disease, is associated with drugs such as Antimony, Arsenic compounds, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, Pentamidine, and borrelidin. Leprosy, a primary bacterial infectious disease, presents with a range of symptoms including ageusia, blindness, edema, eye manifestations, facial paralysis, fever, gait disorders, hyperesthesia, hypesthesia, muscle weakness, muscular atrophy, neuralgia, neurologic manifestations, oral manifestations, paralysis, paresthesia, abnormal reflexes, sensation disorders, skin manifestations, somatosensory disorders, and vision disorders. It is treated with Chloroquine, Clofazimine, Dapsone, Prednisolone, Reactive Oxygen Species, Rifampicin, Thalidomide, and Triptolide. The disease is associated with biological pathways such as antigen processing and presentation, cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity, protein processing in the endoplasmic reticulum, and Toll-like receptor signaling pathway. Lepromatous leprosy, a subtype of leprosy, is managed with Clofazimine, Dapsone, Ethionamide, Ofloxacin, Prednisolone, Prednisone, Rifampicin, and Thalidomide. Dracunculiasis and schistosomiasis are both parasitic helminthiasis infectious diseases. Intestinal schistosomiasis is a form of schistosomiasis and is treated with Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol. Schistosomiasis is managed with Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Trachoma is classified as a commensal bacterial infectious disease.
428: Multiple outbreaks of MERSCoV have been reported across several countries, with the most frequent occurrences in Saudi Arabia, followed by North Korea, Italy, Germany, Jordan, Kuwait, Sultanate of Oman, Egypt, Thailand, and the United States. Outbreaks have been documented from 2013 to 2021, with significant case counts and fatalities recorded in Saudi Arabia, including events with over 75 cases and 23 deaths, and in North Korea, where one outbreak reported 186 cases and 36 deaths. The virus has also been detected in the United States, where a notable outbreak in 2014 involved 632 cases and 193 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. In terms of pharmaceutical interventions, several drugs have been associated with MERSCoV, including BH30sucMan, Favipiravir, Galidesivir, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Remdesivir, and Teicoplanin.
429: An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which itself is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, and liposomal amphotericin B, among others. These drugs are used in the treatment or management of the disease. The biological pathways linked to Visceral Leishmaniasis include the general Leishmaniasis pathway.
430: Brucellosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including Cholesterol, LDL, HL 004, Ketoconazole, Rifampicin, and Tetracycline. Brucella abortus brucellosis, Brucella suis brucellosis, and Brucella canis brucellosis are all subtypes of brucellosis. The condition is linked to various drugs that are either associated with its treatment or play a role in its pathogenesis, such as Acetaminophen, Ibuprofen, Amoxicillin, and others. Fever, a common symptom, is associated with a broad range of drugs, including Alprazolam, Azlocillin, Barbiturates, Bromocriptine, Caffeine, Carbamazepine, and many others, some of which are involved in disease pathogenesis, such as Hydroxyindoleacetic Acid. Foot-and-mouth disease is associated with the drugs Poly I-C and UNC 0638. The broader category of disease includes drugs that contribute to pathogenesis, such as Homocysteine, Isoprostanes, Mannitol, Tocopherol, and Ubidecarenone.
431: Leptospirosis has been reported in multiple outbreaks across different regions, including a 2022 event in the United Republic of Tanzania with 20 confirmed cases and 3 deaths, a 1997 outbreak in India involving 413 cases and 48 deaths, a 2000 outbreak in Malaysia with 37 cases, a 2004 outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania with 141 cases and 6 deaths, and a 2000 outbreak in Canada with 6 confirmed cases. The disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease.
432:
An outbreak of HIV infection-AIDS occurred in Pakistan on April 25, 2019, with 876 confirmed cases. 

HIV infection is associated with the biological pathway of Human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection. Lymphadenopathy is linked to the drug AZD8848. Cryptococcal meningitis, which is classified as a type of fungal meningitis, is associated with several drugs including Amphotericin B, Azathioprine, Dexamethasone, Fluconazole, Flucytosine, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, Miconazole, Prednisolone, Temozolomide, and various formulations of amphotericin B such as amphotericin B, deoxycholate drug combination and liposomal amphotericin B, as well as soybean oil, phospholipid emulsion. Additionally, Dextrose, unspecified form, plays a role in the disease pathogenesis of cryptococcal meningitis. Pneumocystis infections are associated with the drugs Atovaquone, Co-Trimoxazole, Dapsone, and Pentamidine.
433:
An outbreak of pneumonia occurred in Viet Nam and Indonesia on March 12, 2003, with 305 confirmed cases and 5 deaths. Pneumonia is classified as a lung disease and is associated with several drugs, including amikacin, amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate combination, azithromycin, benzylpenicillin, cefixime, cefotaxime, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, dexamethasone, enoxacin, fluoxetine, prednisone, vancomycin, and corticosteroids. Additionally, arginine is implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. The condition is also linked to the presence of environmental factors such as smoke and silicon dioxide.

Meningitis has been reported in multiple locations across Africa, Asia, and Europe, including outbreaks in Congo, Ghana, Burkina Faso, Guinea Bissau, Mozambique, Egypt, Sudan, Saudi Arabia, Nigeria, South Africa, Ethiopia, Chad, Philippines, Hungary, and others, with case counts ranging from 18 to over 40,000 and death tolls from 3 to over 4,000. Meningitis is classified as a central nervous system disease and is further categorized as bacterial meningitis, which is a subtype of meningitis. It is associated with a wide range of drugs, including amikacin, amphotericin B, azithromycin, cefotaxime, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, chloramphenicol, ciprofloxacin, co-trimoxazole, dexamethasone, erythromycin, hydrocortisone, ibuprofen, indomethacin, methotrexate, methylprednisolone acetate, metronidazole, prednisolone, prednisone, rifampicin, vancomycin, and corticosteroids. Eosinophilic meningitis is a subtype of chronic meningitis, and optic neuritis, which is a type of optic nerve disease, is associated with drugs such as amiodarone, isoniazid, methylprednisolone, prednisone, and tamoxifen.
434:
An outbreak of typhus occurred in Burundi on May 6, 1997, resulting in 23,889 confirmed cases. Typhus is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is further categorized under broader disease types such as scrub typhus, epidemic typhus, and endemic typhus. The condition is associated with the use of drugs including Azithromycin and Prednisolone. 

Typhus shares characteristics with other diseases, including pulmonary atelectasis, which is linked to drugs such as Hydrochloric acid, KL4 surfactant, and Silanes. Conjunctivitis is associated with a wide range of drugs including Auranofin, Brimonidine Tartrate, Calcium, Carbamazepine, Celecoxib, Cytarabine, Dexamethasone, Docetaxel, Doxorubicin, and others, and is itself classified as a conjunctival disease. Splenomegaly is associated with numerous drugs such as Amiodarone, Azathioprine, BCG vaccine, Bortezomib, Busulfan, and various environmental and chemical agents including Cadmium chloride, Mercury, and Nickel chloride. 

Infection is linked to a broad spectrum of drugs including Azathioprine, Cyclophosphamide, Methotrexate, Vancomycin, and others, many of which are used in chemotherapy or immunosuppressive regimens. Meningoencephalitis is associated with drugs such as Acyclovir, Amoxicillin, Ceftriaxone, and Methylprednisolone, and is classified as a central nervous system disease. Vasculitis and systemic vasculitis are associated with drugs including Methotrexate, Cyclophosphamide, Heparin, Warfarin, and Tacrolimus, and are categorized as vascular diseases. 

Interstitial nephritis is associated with drugs such as Amoxicillin, Cefotaxime, Diclofenac, and Prednisolone, and is classified as a type of nephritis. Thrombocytopenia is linked to drugs including Dexamethasone, Heparin, Methotrexate, and Clopidogrel, and is classified as a blood platelet disease; it is also associated with the pathogenic role of Aspartic acid. Acute kidney failure is associated with drugs such as Cytarabine, Doxorubicin, and Streptozocin, and is classified as a kidney failure condition. Nephritis is associated with drugs including Dexamethasone, Cyclosporine, and Glucocorticoids, and is classified as a kidney disease. 

Additionally, acute disease is associated with drugs such as Carbamazepine, Lithium, and various disinfectants, while the tight junction pathway is implicated in thrombocytopenia.
435: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998 and July 1999, and in Ethiopia in November 2024. A further outbreak was reported in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. The disease is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, coma, diarrhea, vomiting, pallor, purpura, retinal hemorrhage, cerebellar ataxia, consciousness disorders, and neurological manifestations. It is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is linked to the biological pathway known as Malaria. Treatment options include drugs such as chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, quinine, pyrimethamine, sulfonamides, atovaquone-proguanil combination, and amodiaquine-artesunate combination. Certain drugs, including chloroquine diphosphate, desmethylastemizole, and ferroquine, are also noted for their role in disease pathogenesis. Additionally, the disease is associated with elevated levels of bilirubin, cholesterol, and glutamic acid.
436:
Infection is associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, the COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, clarithromycin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, cytarabine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, doxorubicin, epirubicin, etoposide, everolimus, exisulind, the FEC protocol, itraconazole, the LNH 87 protocol, lithium, the MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, netilmicin, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, the TAC protocol, tetrandrine, topotecan, triamcinolone, the VIP-E protocol, vancomycin, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin.

Trypanosomiasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as antimony, arsenic compounds, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, pentamidine, and borrelidin.

Sleeping sickness is a subtype of trypanosomiasis and is associated with drugs including melarsoprol, miltefosine, pafuramidine, pentamidine, phenylbutazone, piclamilast, and octadecyl-(2-(N-methylpiperidino)ethyl)phosphate. It is linked to the biological pathway of African trypanosomiasis.
437: A series of outbreaks of Crimean-Congo hemorrhagic fever (CCHF) occurred in Kosovo during June 2001, with multiple events reported between June 14 and June 26. The first outbreak, recorded on June 14, involved 44 confirmed cases. A subsequent event on June 15, though the country was not specified, reported 48 cases. On June 19, another outbreak in Kosovo was documented with 57 confirmed cases and 4 deaths. The most severe event occurred on June 26, with 69 confirmed cases and 6 deaths. All outbreaks were associated with the CCHF virus. No additional biomedical relationships were identified in the iBKH data for this disease.
438: Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with the drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole. Trypanosomiasis is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is linked to a range of drugs including antimony, arsenic compounds, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, pentamidine, and borrelidin. Dracunculiasis is also classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. Trachoma is recognized as a commensal bacterial infectious disease. Onchocerciasis, which is a form of filariasis, is associated with the drugs abamectin, albendazole, diethylcarbamazine, and ivermectin. Schistosomiasis is treated with metrifonate and praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is associated with praziquantel, quercetin, and resveratrol. Schistosomiasis itself is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease.
439:
An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which in turn is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, Antimony, and various antiprotozoals. Biological pathways linked to the disease include the Leishmaniasis pathway. 

HIV infection-AIDS was reported in Pakistan on April 25, 2019, with 876 confirmed cases. HIV infection is classified as a human immunodeficiency virus infectious disease and is associated with the pathway of Human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection. 

Anemia is a condition that shares symptoms with Visceral Leishmaniasis and includes manifestations such as fever, pallor, jaundice, diarrhea, abdominal pain, purpura, retinal hemorrhage, and other systemic and neurological signs. It is classified as a hematopoietic system disease and resembles conditions such as celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, malaria, and thymus cancer. Drugs associated with anemia include iron, aspirin, and various chemotherapeutic agents, with alanine implicated in its pathogenesis. 

Lymphopenia, a condition characterized by low lymphocyte counts, is classified as a type of leukopenia. It is associated with drugs such as benzene, antimony sodium gluconate, and various chemotherapeutics, with creatinine and zinc playing roles in its pathogenesis. 

Cancer is classified as a disease of cellular proliferation and is associated with drugs such as cisplatin, doxorubicin, and various chemotherapeutic agents, with amines, glycine, and kynurenic acid implicated in its pathogenesis. 

Leukopenia, a disease of leukocytes, is associated with drugs including acetaminophen, chloramphenicol, and various immunosuppressants. 

Inflammation is associated with drugs such as aspirin, berberine, and various anti-inflammatory agents, with alanine and posaconazole implicated in its pathogenesis.
440: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998 and July 1999, and in Ethiopia in November 2024. A major outbreak was also documented in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is closely related to other conditions such as anemia, celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, and thymus cancer, sharing similar clinical characteristics with these diseases. The symptoms associated with malaria include fever, chills, jaundice, pallor, vomiting, diarrhea, neurological manifestations, coma, and retinal hemorrhage, among others. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or management of malaria, including chloroquine, artemisinin, artesunate, mefloquine, primaquine, and pyrimethamine, with some combinations such as atovaquone-proguanil and mefloquine-sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine being used clinically. Certain drugs, such as chloroquine diphosphate and fanasil-pyrimethamine, are noted for their role in disease pathogenesis, particularly through interactions with metabolites like bilirubin, cholesterol, and glutamic acid. The disease is also linked to the malaria pathway, and its causative agents, including Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax, are associated with specific drug regimens and biological mechanisms.
441: Schistosomiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The condition is associated with several drugs, including Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is also linked to the use of Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol.
442:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including Malaysia in 1996 with 2,450 cases, Cameroon in 2017 with 623 cases and 2 deaths, Mayotte in 2020 with 3,533 cases and 16 deaths, East Timor in 2005 with multiple outbreaks involving up to 215 cases and 20 deaths, Barbados in 2019 with 339 cases and 6 deaths, El Salvador in 2002 with 1,200 cases and 101 deaths, Brazil in 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths, Egypt in 2019 with 1,197 cases and 5 deaths, Rarotonga in 1997 with 123 cases, Cape Verde in 2009 with 3,367 cases, and Pakistan in 2019 with 47,120 cases and 75 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in India in 1996 and 2003, with 8,170 and 2,185 cases respectively, and in the Netherlands in 2004 with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths. A large outbreak was reported in 2023 in an unspecified country with 500,000 cases and 750 deaths. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease and is a subtype of dengue disease. It is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and various flavonoids such as hinokiflavone and podocarpusflavone A. The disease is also linked to biological pathways related to viral infection and immune response.

Malaria has been documented in several regions, including the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, and Ethiopia in 2024. A significant outbreak occurred in Pakistan in 2022 with 3,400,000 cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with symptoms such as fever, chills, jaundice, vomiting, diarrhea, pallor, purpura, and neurological manifestations. Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Ethiopia in 2024, and Pakistan in 2022 with 3,400,000 cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with the Malaria biological pathway. It presents with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, coma, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, purpura, retinal hemorrhage, unconsciousness, and neurological manifestations. The disease is linked to several drugs, including Artemisinin, Chloroquine, Quinine, Mefloquine, Atovaquone, and Proguanil, with some drugs also playing a role in disease pathogenesis, such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin. Dengue Fever has been associated with multiple documented outbreaks across various regions and time periods. Notable events include a large outbreak in Malaysia in 1997 with 11,328 cases and 28 deaths, and another in 1996 with 2,450 cases. Additional significant outbreaks occurred in Sri Lanka in 2017, reporting 80,732 cases and 215 deaths; in Brazil in 2002 with 317,787 cases and 57 deaths; in The Netherlands in 2004 with over 58,000 cases and 658 deaths; in India in 1996 with 8,170 cases and 319 deaths; in Pakistan in 2019 with 47,120 cases and 75 deaths; in Bangladesh in 2022 with 52,807 cases and 230 deaths; and in Thailand in 2022 with 28,109 cases and 38 deaths. A particularly severe outbreak was reported in 2024 with 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths, though the country was not specified. Other outbreaks were recorded in countries such as East Timor, Barbados, Egypt, Vietnam, and the Republic of Panama, with varying case counts and mortality rates. Dengue Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. In terms of pharmacological associations, several drugs have been linked to the condition, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and various flavonoids such as hinokiflavone and podocarpusflavone A. Additionally, dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease.

Zika virus has been reported in multiple outbreaks globally, primarily between 2015 and 2016. Notable events include an outbreak in French Guiana in 2015 with 8,750 cases, and in Brazil in 2015 with 14 cases, followed by a larger outbreak in 2016 with 3,174 cases and 38 deaths. Other outbreaks were documented in countries such as El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, the Netherlands, the United States, India, and the Dominican Republic, with case counts ranging from 1 to 10. A significant outbreak occurred in Cape Verde in 2015 with 165 cases. The virus has also been reported in the French Caribbean and other island nations, including Saint Lucia, Martinique, and Guadeloupe, with a combined total of 2,818 confirmed and probable cases. Zika fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. Pharmacological studies have associated chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide with the condition.

Middle East Respiratory Syndrome Coronavirus (MERSCoV) has been reported in multiple outbreaks, primarily in Saudi Arabia, with additional cases in Jordan, Egypt, Kuwait, Oman, and other countries. Notable events include a large outbreak in Saudi Arabia in 2014 with 571 cases and 171 deaths, and another in 2013 with 22 cases and 10 deaths. Other significant outbreaks occurred in 2015, 2016, and 2017, with case numbers ranging from 1 to 25 and death tolls from 1 to 193. A particularly severe outbreak in 2020 reported 2,538 cases and 871 deaths, though the country was not specified. MERSCoV has also been documented in Italy, Austria, the United Arab Emirates, and North Korea, with varying case numbers and mortality rates. The virus is associated with high fatality rates in some instances, such as a 2013 outbreak in Saudi Arabia with 71 deaths from just 2 cases. MERSCoV is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Encephalitis has been linked to a range of drugs, including acyclovir, amoxicillin, ceftriaxone, chlorambucil, co-trimoxazole, erythromycin, and various other agents such as endocannabinoids, lipopolysaccharides, and mannitol. It is classified as a brain disease and is associated with the TCR signaling pathway. Meningoencephalitis, a related condition, is categorized as a central nervous system disease and has been associated with drugs such as acyclovir, amoxicillin, ceftriaxone, and methylprednisolone. Powassan encephalitis is recognized as a subtype of tick-borne encephalitis, which itself is classified as a viral infectious disease.

450: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999, Pakistan in 2022 with 3.4 million confirmed cases, and Ethiopia in 2024. The disease is caused by Plasmodium species, with Plasmodium falciparum and Plasmodium vivax being recognized subtypes. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Common symptoms associated with malaria include fever, chills, headache, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, pallor, retinal hemorrhage, coma, unconsciousness, neurological manifestations, oliguria, hemoglobinuria, and in severe cases, fetal hypoxia and birth weight reduction. The disease is linked to several biological pathways, primarily under the category of malaria. A wide range of drugs are associated with malaria treatment, including chloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, mefloquine, primaquine, atovaquone, proguanil, sulfadoxine, pyrimethamine, and various combinations such as amodiaquine-artesunate and mefloquine-sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine. Some drugs, such as chloroquine diphosphate and ferroquine, are noted for their role in disease pathogenesis. Additionally, certain substances like glucose-6-phosphate, cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin are implicated in the disease’s biological mechanisms.

Thrombocytopenia, a blood platelet disease, is associated with a range of drugs including Ancrod, Cefoxitin, Ceftriaxone, Chloramphenicol, Chromium, Cimetidine, Co-Trimoxazole, Dacarbazine, Diclofenac, Dinoprostone, Diquat, Doxorubicin, Flunitrazepam, Fluorouracil, Fondaparinux, Fosbretabulin, Fotemustine, Gefitinib, Haloperidol, Heme, Heparin, Ifosfamide, Linezolid, Lomustine, M-VAC protocol, Mefenamic acid, Mefloquine, Melphalan, Metenolone, Navitoclax, Nedaplatin, Oprelvekin, PC protocol, Pentosan Sulfuric Polyester, Phenobarbital, Phenoxymethylpenicillin, Phenytoin, Streptozocin, TP protocol, Tanespimycin, Temozolomide, Thiazides, Thiotepa, Thymidine, Tioguanine, Vinorelbine, Zidovudine, cefuroxime axetil, estrogens, and losoxantrone, with aspartic acid implicated in its disease mechanism. The tight junction pathway is associated with thrombocytopenia.

Fever is linked to multiple drugs such as 5-(2-aminopropyl)indole, Acetylsalicylic acid, Amantadine, Aminoglutethimide, Amphetamine, Anticonvulsants, Antihypertensives, Arachidonic Acid, Arsenic, Artesunate, Atropine, BEP protocol, Bifenthrin, CHOP protocol, Carbamazepine, Cefpodoxime, Ceftriaxone, Citalopram, Clomipramine, Contraceptives, Oral, Synthetic, Dapsone, Dinoprostone, Docetaxel, Fluorouracil, Foscarnet, Gentamicins, Ifosfamide, Iohexol, Irinotecan, Lamotrigine, Linezolid, Lithium, Lithium carbonate, Mefenamic acid, Mexiletine, Nimodipine, Nizatidine, OPEC OJEC protocol, Omeprazole, Phenytoin, Phosphatidylinositols, Prednisolone, Tacrolimus, Turpentine, Valganciclovir, benzamide, carbabenzpyride, fanasil, pyrimethamine drug combination, p-Chloroamphetamine, and xanthurenic acid, with hydroxyindoleacetic acid playing a role in its pathogenesis.

Cough is associated with a variety of substances including 2-(1,3-dimethyl-2,6-dioxo-1,2,3,6-tetrahydro-7H-purin-7-yl)-N-(4-isopropylphenyl)acetamide, 4-(3-trifluoromethylpyridin-2-yl)piperazine-1-carboxylic acid (5-trifluoromethylpyridin-2-yl)amide, 6-chloronicotinic acid, Alfentanil, Allyl isothiocyanate, Amiodarone, Amlodipine, Beta Blocking Agents, Budesonide, Bupivacaine, Camphor, Cefpodoxime, Cilazapril, Citric acid, Clozapine, Crotonaldehyde, Dextromethorphan, Dihydrocodeine, Dropropizine, Drotebanol, Drugs, Chinese Herbal, Enalapril, Enflurane, Epinephrine, Fentanyl, Guaifenesin, Hydrocodone, Insecticides, Isoflurane, Lidocaine, Losartan, Mustard Gas, Nitrogen Oxides, Noscapine, Perindopril, Petroleum, Plant Extracts, Propanidid, Propylene glycol, Pyrrolidines, Spirapril, Telmisartan, Terbutaline, Tetraethyl Lead, Zafirlukast, azabicyclane, corexit 9500, moguisteine, naringin, and tobacco tar.

Bacterial pneumonia is classified as a type of pneumonia and is associated with drugs such as Amoxicillin, Azithromycin, Cefodizime, Cefotaxime, Cefpirome, Ceftazidime, Ceftobiprole, Ceftriaxone, Ciprofloxacin, Clarithromycin, Clindamycin, Co-Trimoxazole, Delafloxacin, Dust, Erythromycin, Flucloxacillin, Fosfomycin, Gentamicins, Linezolid, Ofloxacin, Omadacycline, Pefloxacin, Pristinamycin, Procaine benzylpenicillin, Solithromycin, Sulfadiazine, Sultamicillin, Teicoplanin, Telavancin, Vancomycin, and erythromycin stearate.
456:
Lassa Fever is a viral infectious disease that has been reported in multiple outbreaks across West Africa and other regions. Notable outbreaks occurred in Nigeria, Sierra Leone, Angola, Benin, Ghana, and Guinea Bissau, with significant case counts and fatalities recorded in Nigeria, including events in 2015, 2017, 2018, 2019, 2020, 2022, and 2023, with one outbreak in 2023 reporting 4,702 cases and 152 deaths. Additional outbreaks were documented in Sierra Leone, Angola, and Benin during the 1990s and 2010s, with varying case numbers and mortality rates. Cases have also been reported in non-endemic countries such as Germany, the United Kingdom, Sweden, and the Netherlands, typically linked to travel or imported cases. The disease is associated with the drug Ribavirin, which is used in its treatment. Lassa Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Leptospirosis is a primary bacterial infectious disease that has been reported in outbreaks in India, Malaysia, Canada, and the United Republic of Tanzania. Notable events include a 1997 outbreak in India with 413 cases and 48 deaths, and outbreaks in Tanzania in 2004 and 2022, with 141 and 20 cases respectively, including fatalities. The disease has also been reported in Malaysia and Canada with smaller case numbers. These outbreaks are typically linked to environmental exposure, such as contact with contaminated water or soil.

Hantavirus Pulmonary Syndrome is a viral infectious disease that has been documented in a single outbreak in the Republic of Panama in 2000, involving 12 confirmed cases and 3 deaths. The disease is associated with the role of nitrates in its pathogenesis, suggesting a potential influence on disease progression or severity. It is classified as a viral infectious disease.
457:
Outbreaks of Zika virus have been reported across multiple countries and regions, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Republic of Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, India, Brazil, Argentina, Honduras, Dominican Republic, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Sint Maarten, Cape Verde, Bonaire and Aruba (Kingdom of the Netherlands), Trinidad and Tobago, Costa Rica, Colombia, Venezuela, France, and others, with case counts ranging from single cases to over 8,000 in French Guiana. Notable events include a large outbreak in French Guiana in November 2015 with 8,750 cases, a significant outbreak in Brazil in January 2016 with 3,174 cases and 38 deaths, and a cluster in India in July 2021 with 70 cases. The virus has also been associated with severe outcomes, such as deaths in Panama and El Salvador.

Zika virus infection is classified as a viral infectious disease. The condition is associated with several drugs, including Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and Niclosamide, which are studied or used in the context of the disease. Additionally, the virus is linked to a range of other health conditions, including fetal diseases and microcephaly, which is itself categorized as a congenital nervous system abnormality. The disease is also associated with nervous system disorders, which are classified as diseases of anatomical entities. Certain drugs have been implicated in the pathogenesis of related conditions, such as Dihydroxyacetone in fetal diseases and Sorbitol and Tryptophan in nervous system diseases.
458: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania in May 1998 and July 1999, as well as in Ethiopia in November 2024 and Pakistan in January 2022, where 3,400,000 cases were reported. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, jaundice, coma, neurological manifestations, oliguria, retinal hemorrhage, vomiting, pallor, and purpura. It is also linked to complications such as cerebral malaria, anemia, pulmonary edema, shock, and parasitemia. The disease shares characteristics with conditions including celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, thymus cancer, and hematopoietic system disease. Several drugs are associated with malaria treatment, including chloroquine, quinine, mefloquine, artemisinin, artesunate, primaquine, and atovaquone-proguanil. Other drugs are involved in disease pathogenesis, such as cholesterol, bilirubin, glutamic acid, and glucose-6-phosphate. The disease is also associated with the malaria pathway. Specific forms of malaria, such as Plasmodium falciparum malaria and Plasmodium vivax malaria, are recognized subtypes with distinct drug associations, including amodiaquine, sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine, and artemether. Anemia related to malaria is associated with symptoms like abdominal pain, diarrhea, fever, and hypergammaglobulinemia, and is influenced by drugs such as iron sucrose, hydroxyurea, and aspirin, with alanine implicated in its pathogenesis. Pulmonary edema and shock, which can occur as complications, are linked to various drugs, including norepinephrine, mannitol, and taurine, with lactic acid and norepinephrine playing roles in pathogenesis.
459: Cutaneous leishmaniasis, mucocutaneous leishmaniasis, and leishmaniasis are all classified as types of leishmaniasis, with leishmaniasis further categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a range of drugs, including Amphotericin B, Antimony, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Meglumine antimoniate, Pentamidine, Pentoxifylline, Miltefosine, Itraconazole, Ketoconazole, Paromomycin, and various antiprotozoals. Additional drugs linked to treatment or study include Allopurinol, Indomethacin, Omeprazole, Zinc sulfate, WR279396, liposomal amphotericin B, mammea A-BB, methyl gallate, oil of Chenopodium, pistagremic acid, and several experimental compounds such as 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, and 5,7-dihydroxy-8-(2-methylbutanoyl)-6-(3-methylbutyl)-4-phenyl-chroman-2-one. The biological pathway associated with leishmaniasis is the leishmaniasis pathway itself.
460: Cholera has been documented in multiple outbreaks across various countries, including Burkina Faso, Republic of Panama, Pakistan, South Africa (KwaZulu Natal), Cap Verde, Congo, Iraq, Angola, Algeria, Cameroon, Ghana, United Republic of Tanzania, Comoros, Rumonge, multiple countries in Asia, Africa, and South America, Benin, Mozambique, Ecuador, Rwanda, and others, with case counts ranging from a few to over 79,000 and death tolls reaching several thousand in some instances. The causative agent, *Vibrio cholerae*, has also been associated with outbreaks in Cameroon, Zambia, Mexico, Niger, Congo, Sierra Leone, United Republic of Tanzania, Egypt, Philippines, Iraq, Pakistan, Azerbaijan, Madagascar, Somalia, and Comoros, with similar patterns of high case numbers and fatalities in certain events. Cholera is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease, and its pathogenesis is associated with the biological pathway of *Vibrio cholerae* infection. The cholera vaccine CVD 103-HgR is associated with the disease, indicating its use in prevention or study.
461:
Outbreaks of Crimean-Congo Hemorrhagic fever have been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including South Africa (KwaZulu Natal) in November 1996 with 32 cases and 1 death, Egypt in March 2003 with 30 cases and 6 deaths, and Pakistan in 1998 with 19 cases and 12 deaths, as well as in 2000 with 25 cases and 15 deaths, in 2010 with 26 cases and 3 deaths, and again in 1998 with 4 cases and 2 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in Kosovo in June 2001, in Turkey in August 2006 with 242 cases and 20 deaths, and in Iraq in May 2022 with 212 cases and 27 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and the drug Ribavirin is associated with its treatment.

Outbreaks of West Nile Disease have been documented in various regions, including the United States and Canada in 2002, with multiple events reporting high case counts—such as 3,587 cases and 211 deaths in the United States on November 13, 2002, and over 1,000 cases in Canada during the same year. Other notable outbreaks occurred in the United States in 1999 with 50 cases and 5 deaths, in 2002 with 1,460 cases and 66 deaths, and 1,963 cases and 94 deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in the Republic of Moldova in 1996 and 1996, with 168 and 450 cases respectively, and in Israel in 2000 with 151 cases and 12 deaths. Smaller outbreaks occurred in Brazil, Portugal, Romania and Turkey, and Canada in 2002, with varying case numbers and one death reported in several instances. West Nile Disease is also classified as a viral infectious disease.
462:
Trypanosomiasis is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It is associated with several drugs, including antimony, arsenic compounds, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, pentamidine, borrelidin, melarsoprol, miltefosine, pafuramidine, phenylbutazone, piclamilast, and octadecyl-(2-(N-methylpiperidino)ethyl)phosphate. The condition is linked to the biological pathway of African trypanosomiasis.

Sleeping sickness is a subtype of trypanosomiasis and shares the same classification. It is associated with the same set of drugs as trypanosomiasis, including melarsoprol, miltefosine, pafuramidine, pentamidine, phenylbutazone, piclamilast, and octadecyl-(2-(N-methylpiperidino)ethyl)phosphate. The disease is also associated with the biological pathway of African trypanosomiasis.
463: Schistosomiasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease and is associated with the drugs Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Intestinal schistosomiasis, a subtype of schistosomiasis, is linked to the drugs Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol. Portal vein thrombosis is categorized as a hepatic vascular disease, while splenomegaly is associated with a wide range of drugs, including Amiodarone, Aniline, Anticonvulsants, Antimony Sodium Gluconate, Arsenic trioxide, Azathioprine, BCG vaccine, Cadmium Chloride, Carbamazepine, Cefaclor, Cefodizime, Ciprofloxacin, Curcumin, Diflunisal, Diuron, Environmental Pollutants, Geraniol, Guanidines, Hydroxyacetylaminofluorene, INCB018424, Lead acetate, Manganese chloride, Mercury, Methotrexate, Penicillamine, Pentachlorophenol, Phenacetin, Plant Preparations, Propylthiouracil, Protein kinase inhibitors, Pyrazoles, Quinidine, Resveratrol, Sulfasalazine, Tetrachlorodibenzodioxin, Tioguanine, Trinitrobenzenesulfonic Acid, Trinitrotoluene, Triptolide, Urethane, Vincristine, Vitamin E, bromfenvinphos, ceric oxide, chromium trioxide, dimethylglycine, kappa-casein glycomacropeptide, pristimerin, sodium arsenite, and sodium tungstate(VI). Hypersplenism is classified as a splenic disease and is associated with the drug Isoniazid. Liver disease is categorized as a hepatobiliary disease and is linked to numerous drugs, including 1,1,1,2-tetrafluoro-2-chloroethane, 2,4,5,2',4',5'-hexachlorobiphenyl, 3 beta-hydroxy-delta 5-cholenic acid, 3,4,3',4'-tetrachlorobiphenyl, ABVD protocol, Ademetionine, Agent Orange, Antitubercular Agents, Azathioprine, Barbital, Benzo(a)pyrene, Beta carotene, Blood Glucose, Cephalexin, Clindamycin, Contraceptives, Oral, Cyanocobalamin, Cytarabine, Defoliants, Chemical, Docosahexaenoic Acids, Enalapril, Fluorouracil, Fosinopril, Homocysteine, Lincomycin, Melatonin, N-(2-hydroxyethyl)retinamide, Nefazodone, Nickel sulfate, Nifedipine, Patulin, Phenytoin, Piroxicam, Prednisone, Propofol, Quercetin, Rifampicin, Rosiglitazone, Safflower oil, Simvastatin, Thiotepa, Tizanidine, Ursodeoxycholic acid, Zidovudine, cardamonin, cobaltiprotoporphyrin, mancozeb, rosmarinic acid, sodium bisulfide, and vinyl chloride, with Hepatitis B e Antigens and Toluene implicated in its disease pathogenesis. Liver cirrhosis, a form of liver disease, is associated with drugs such as 15-deoxy-delta(12,14)-prostaglandin J2, Allyl isothiocyanate, Amiodarone, Andrographolide, Anti-Bacterial Agents, Azathioprine, Chlordecone, Clodronic acid, Colchicine, Cyclophosphamide, Diethylnitrosamine, Diethylstilbestrol, Doxepin, Drugs, Chinese Herbal, Ferulic acid, Furosemide, Gallic Acid, Glatiramer Acetate, Halofuginone, Hesperidin, Lovastatin, Melitten, Methyldopa, Mitogens, Nandrolone decanoate, Nimodipine, Nitrofurantoin, Obeticholic acid, Oleanolic Acid, Olmesartan Medoxomil, Papaverine, Phenobarbital, Polyphenols, Polyvinyl Chloride, Silymarin, Terbinafine, Thalidomide, Thiabendazole, Thiamethoxam, Thioacetamide, Titanium dioxide, Vitamin A, Zinc protoporphyrin, allyl alcohol, anandamide, chromium hexavalent ion, corticosteroids, isoprostane F2alpha III, leukadherin-1, and perfluorooctanoic acid, with Glycerin contributing to its pathogenesis. Hepatic encephalopathy, a brain disease, is associated with drugs including Acetylcysteine, Acetylsalicylic acid, Amitriptyline, Amoxicillin-Potassium Clavulanate Combination, Azoxymethane, Benzbromarone, Buthionine Sulfoximine, Carbamazepine, Carbimazole, Carnitine, Chrysin, Co-Trimoxazole, Cocaine, Cyproterone acetate, Goserelin, Halothane, Isoniazid, Itraconazole, Lactulose, Levetiracetam, Lisinopril, Methazolamide, Methionine, Methionine Sulfoximine, Methoxyflurane, Methyldopa, Metronidazole, Minocycline, Morphine, Ofloxacin, Paroxetine, Phenobarbital, Phenprocoumon, Phenytoin, Prednisolone, Ranitidine, Rifampicin, Ritonavir, Sodium benzoate, Sorafenib, Spironolactone, Thioacetamide, Thiopental, Topiramate, Valproic acid, Vancomycin, Zafirlukast, Zolpidem, benzarone, and methylcholanthrene. Portal hypertension, a hepatic vascular disease, is associated with drugs such as Amiodarone, Anti-Retroviral Agents, Azathioprine, Busulfan, Candoxatrilat, Carbon Tetrachloride, Carvedilol, DC 015, Didanosine, Flurbiprofen, Glutathione, Glyburide, Histamine, Hydrochloric acid, Isoniazid, Isoprenaline, Isosorbide mononitrate, Malonaldehyde, Methotrexate, Methyldopa, Mirtazapine, Nifedipine, Nitric Oxide, Octreotide, Polaprezinc, Propranolol, Spironolactone, Thalidomide, Thioacetamide, Tioguanine, Vitamin A, Y-27632, Zidovudine, Zinc, bilirubin, ginkgolide-B, nitroflurbiprofen, perhexiline maleate, and stronger neominophagen C. Varicose veins are classified as a vein disease and are associated with the drug Sodium tetradecyl sulfate. Hemolysis is linked to drugs including 2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid, 2-Methoxyethanol, Aflatoxins, Allopurinol, Asbestos, Serpentine, Azathioprine, Benoxaprofen, Benzo(a)pyrene, Benzylpenicillin, Carboplatin, Ceftazidime, Cephalexin, Chlorpromazine, Ciprofloxacin, Cupric Chloride, Curcumin, Cysteine, Dextropropoxyphene, Diclofenac, Eugenol, Ferrous sulfate, Hydrogen peroxide, Lead acetate, Mercuric chloride, Micafungin, Penicillamine, Phenazopyridine, Potassium Dichromate, Ribavirin, Rifampicin, Rutin, Silver, Sucrose, Sulfasalazine, Sunitinib, Tolmetin, alpha-Tocopherol, alpha-terthienyl, aluminum phosphide, bromochlorodifluoromethane, chloroaluminum tetrasulfophthalocyanine, isopropylethanediol, nickel monoxide, notoginsenoside R1, polyethylene glycol monostearate, polyphyllin D, potassium bromate, propylene dichloride, saikosaponin, and styrofoam. Kidney failure is categorized as a kidney disease and is associated with drugs such as Amifostine, Axitinib, Betamethasone, Bortezomib, Chromium, Contraceptives, Oral, Cyclosporins, Demeclocycline, Dexamethasone, Dextropropoxyphene, Dihydroergotamine, Diltiazem, Etoposide, Exenatide, Fibric Acids, Flucytosine, Hesperidin, Ivermectin, Ketorolac Tromethamine, Mercaptopurine, Metformin, Methotrexate, Methylprednisolone, Mibefradil, Mitoxantrone, Nafcillin, Netilmicin, Nicergoline, PD 150606, Peginterferon alfa-2b, Penicillamine, Pioglitazone, Piperacillin, Pranoprofen, Propofol, Puromycin Aminonucleoside, Quinine, Ranitidine, Riluzole, Risperidone, Sodium fluoride, Thiazides, Thioridazine, Triamterene, Uranium, Valsartan, Vancomycin, chloroquine diphosphate, chromium tripicolinate, ethyl 6-(N-(2-chloro-4-fluorophenyl)sulfamoyl)cyclohex-1-ene-1-carboxylate, with 8-epi-prostaglandin F2alpha and Calcium Phosphate implicated in its pathogenesis. Ascites is associated with drugs such as 2-(4-morpholinyl)-8-phenyl-4H-1-benzopyran-4-one, Amiodarone, Amitriptyline, Azathioprine, Azoxymethane, Carbon Tetrachloride, Carboplatin, Ciprofloxacin, Clomifene, Cloxacillin, Contraceptives, Oral, Curcumin, Cyanocobalamin, Dactinomycin, Daunorubicin, Diclofenac, Doxorubicin, Drugs, Chinese Herbal, Enalapril, Epoprostenol, Fluorouracil, Flutamide, Furazolidone, Indomethacin, Ketoconazole, Leucovorin, Mecobalamin, Mercuric chloride, Methotrexate, Methylprednisolone, Paroxetine, Phenprocoumon, Plant Extracts, Potassium Dichromate, Prednisolone, Propranolol, Puromycin Aminonucleoside, Pyrrolizidine Alkaloids, Quercetin, Ritonavir, Spironolactone, Tioguanine, Tocopherol, Torasemide, Triamterene, Ursodeoxycholic acid, Vitamin A, liquiritigenin, liquiritin, and perhexiline maleate. Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a subtype of diabetes mellitus and presents with symptoms including Acute Coronary Syndrome, Albuminuria, Birth Weight, Body Weight, Flushing, Gastroparesis, Microvascular Angina, Obesity, Obesity, Abdominal, Overweight, Proteinuria, Urinary Bladder, Overactive, Weight Gain, and Weight Loss. It is associated with drugs such as 1,5-anhydroglucitol, 2-dodecyl-6-methoxycyclohexa-2,5-diene-1,4-dione, 2-palmitoylglycerol, 3-((4-chlorobenzyl)oxy)-N-((1S)-1-phenylethyl)-2-thiophenecarboxamide, 8-epi-prostaglandin F2alpha, Acarbose, Air Pollutants, Amino acids, Angiotensin Receptor Antagonists, BLX 1002, Benzoic acid, Bezafibrate, Bisphenol A, Blood Glucose, Bromine, Canagliflozin, Chlorpropamide, Cholesterol, HDL, Dexfenfluramine, Dichlorodiphenyl Dichloroethylene, Dietary Sugars, Diethylnitrosamine, Drugs, Chinese Herbal, Efavirenz, Enalapril, Fibric Acids, Gliclazide, Heme, Indium, L 783281, Levonorgestrel, Liraglutide, Magnesium, Malathion, Malonaldehyde, Mercury, Metformin, Nitrendipine, Panax notoginseng extract, Polychlorinated Biphenyls, Rosiglitazone, Saxagliptin, Silver, Superoxides, hypotaurine, mangiferin, neomangiferin, perfluorooctane sulfonic acid, platyconic acid, and triaquaoxohexakis(propionato)trichromium(III), with Spermine implicated in its pathogenesis. The disease is associated with biological pathways including Cell Cycle, Mitotic, Insulin secretion, PPAR signaling pathway, Pancreatic secretion, Protein processing in endoplasmic reticulum, Signaling by NOTCH4, Signaling by WNT, TGF-beta receptor signaling in EMT (epithelial to mesenchymal transition), Type II diabetes mellitus, and p53 signaling pathway. Type 1 diabetes mellitus 2 is classified as a subtype of type 1 diabetes mellitus. Jaundice is associated with drugs such as 4,4'-diaminodiphenylmethane, Acetaminophen, Adefovir dipivoxil, Amiodarone, Amitriptyline, Amodiaquine, Amoxicillin-Potassium Clavulanate Combination, Benoxaprofen, Benzbromarone, Bupivacaine, Buprenorphine, Cefoxitin, Ceftriaxone, Celecoxib, Cephalexin, Chlorpromazine, Clozapine, Colchicine, Diethylstilbestrol diphosphate, Disulfiram, Dydrogesterone, Etretinate, Floxuridine, Ganciclovir, Glyburide, Isosorbide, Lamotrigine, Lovastatin, Methotrexate, Methyldopa, Midomafetamine, Nalidixic acid, Octreotide, Phenazopyridine, Phenylbutazone, Prajmaline, Propylthiouracil, Rosuvastatin Calcium, Simvastatin, Sulfasalazine, Telbivudine, Ticlopidine, Troglitazone, Troleandomycin, Vitamin E, Zafirlukast, aluminum phosphide, benzarone, cefuroxime axetil, and stronger neominophagen C.
464:
Ebola has been associated with multiple outbreak events across several countries, including repeated occurrences in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, Sierra Leone, Guinea Bissau, and the United Republic of Tanzania. Outbreaks have been documented from as early as 1996 to 2022, with significant case counts and high mortality rates, particularly in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, where outbreaks in 2018, 2019, and 2020 involved thousands of cases and hundreds to over two thousand deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in 2014 in Guinea Bissau, Sierra Leone, and the United Republic of Tanzania, with case numbers ranging from tens to over three thousand and death tolls reaching several hundred. Ebola is classified as a viral infectious disease. Several drugs have been associated with Ebola, including BH30sucMan, Favipiravir, Galidesivir, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Remdesivir, and Teicoplanin.

Malaria has been reported in multiple regions, including the United Republic of Tanzania, Burundi, Ethiopia, and Pakistan, with documented outbreaks occurring as early as 1998 and as recently as 2022. Notably, a large outbreak in Pakistan in 2022 involved three million confirmed cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. The condition is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal and eye hemorrhage, pallor, pruritus, purpura, oliguria, hypergammaglobulinemia, cerebellar ataxia, and fetal hypoxia. Several drugs are associated with malaria treatment, including Artemether, Artemisinin, Artesunate, Atovaquone, Chloroquine, Hydroxychloroquine, Mefloquine, Primaquine, Quinine, Pyrimethamine, and various combinations such as atovaquone-proguanil and amodiaquine-artesunate. Some drugs, such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. The biological pathway associated with malaria is directly linked to the disease itself.
465:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple outbreaks across various countries, including Malaysia, Cameroon, Sri Lanka, Bangladesh, Mayotte, East Timor, Barbados, El Salvador, Brazil, India, Egypt, Pakistan, Burkina Faso, and others, with case counts ranging from a few to over 80,000 and death tolls reaching up to 260 in some instances. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Associated drugs include 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and several other compounds, some of which are under investigation for their potential role in disease management. The condition is also linked to the broader category of viral infectious diseases.

Zika virus outbreaks have occurred in multiple regions, including El Salvador, Guatemala, Ecuador, Panama, Mexico, Papua New Guinea, The Netherlands, St Lucia, United States, Viet Nam, French Guiana, Dominican Republic, Brazil, Honduras, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Cape Verde, India, and others, with case numbers ranging from 1 to over 8,700. The virus is associated with drugs such as chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, IDX184, and niclosamide. It is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Yellow Fever has been documented in outbreaks in Angola, Sierra Leone, Brazil, Cameroon, The Netherlands, South Africa, Congo, Ecuador, China, United Republic of Tanzania, Mali, Guinea Bissau, Belgium, Ghana, Egypt, and other countries, with case counts varying from 1 to over 1,300 and fatalities reaching up to 215. The disease is categorized as a viral infectious disease.

West Nile Disease has been reported in Canada, the United States, Republic of Moldova, Israel, Brazil, Portugal, Romania, Turkey, and other locations, with case numbers ranging from 1 to over 3,500 and deaths reaching up to 211. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Drugs associated with it include Active Hexose Correlated Compound and minocycline.

Hepatitis C is a viral infectious disease linked to biological pathways involving hepatitis C and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including interferons, ribavirin, sofosbuvir, ledipasvir, daclatasvir, and others, some of which are used in treatment regimens. The disease is also associated with the amino acid alanine in its pathogenesis.

Microcephaly is classified as a congenital nervous system abnormality and is associated with drugs such as acitretin, valproic acid, ethanol, cocaine, and various environmental toxins like bisphenol A and mercury, which may contribute to its development.

Meningoencephalitis is categorized as a central nervous system disease and is associated with drugs including acyclovir, amoxicillin, ceftriaxone, methylprednisolone, and others used in the treatment of infections and inflammatory conditions.

Guillain-Barré syndrome is classified as an autoimmune disease of the peripheral nervous system and is associated with drugs such as cisplatin, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, and corticosteroids, which may play a role in its pathogenesis.

Newcastle disease and dengue hemorrhagic fever are both classified as viral infectious diseases, with the latter being a subtype of dengue disease.
466:
Infection is associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, the COPP protocol, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, clarithromycin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, cytarabine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, docetaxel, epirubicin, etoposide, everolimus, exisulind, FEC protocol, fludarabine, itraconazole, LNH 87 protocol, MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, methylprednisolone, netilmicin, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, sirolimus, TAC protocol, tetrandrine, tobramycin, topotecan, triamcinolone, VIP-E protocol, vancomycin, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin. 

Hookworm disease is associated with the drugs albendazole, dichlorvos, ivermectin, oxantel pamoate, and tribendimidine.
