Anemia is characterized by a broad spectrum of symptoms, including acute abdominal pain, acute pain, respiratory alkalosis, anoxia, apnea, asthenia, ataxia telangiectasia, low birth weight, high and low cardiac output, chest pain, color vision defects, Cri-du-Chat syndrome, cyanosis, infantile diarrhea, edema, emaciation, eye manifestations, fatigue, fetal distress, fetal hypoxia, low fetal weight, fever, fever of unknown origin, sensorineural hearing loss, heart murmurs, hematemesis, hydrops fetalis, hypergammaglobulinemia, hyperoxia, jaundice, obstructive jaundice, Kearns-Sayre syndrome, livedo reticularis, X-linked mental retardation, monoclonal gammopathy of undetermined significance, mucopolysaccharidosis II, neurologic manifestations, pain, pallor, paresthesia, proteinuria, purpura, hyperglobulinemic purpura, thrombocytopenic purpura, thrombotic thrombocytopenic purpura, reticulocytosis, retinal hemorrhage, vitreous hemorrhage, and Waterhouse-Friderichsen syndrome. 

The condition is associated with drugs such as the 1-UFT protocol, 1-arabinofuranosylcytosine-5'-stearylphosphate, 4-chloronitrobenzene, anabolic agents, androgens, benzylpenicillin, CPDD protocol, calcium, carboplatin, carmustine, cefodizime, cefotetan, codeine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, DHAP protocol, diphenhydramine, EPOCH protocol, Emitefur, fenretinide, fluorouracil, flutamide, gefitinib, gentamicins, hydrochlorothiazide, isotretinoin, K 7174, LCS101, lenalidomide, leucovorin, lomustine, mecobalamin, methyldopa, omeprazole, PC protocol, peginterferon alfa-2b, prednisone, primaquine, razoxane, TER 286, temozolomide, uranium, VIP-E protocol, vitamin A, vitamin E, zidovudine, gemcitabine-oxaliplatin regimen, granulocyte colony-stimulating factor, phenylhydrazine, and rentiapril. Additionally, alanine plays a role in the pathogenesis of anemia.

Anemia is classified as a hematopoietic system disease and shares characteristics with celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, malaria, and thymus cancer.
467: Outbreaks of Chikungunya virus disease have been reported across multiple regions, including Tanzania, the United States, Congo, Cameroon, France, Chad, Reunion, St Martin (French part of the Caribbean isle), Italy, Spain, Argentina, India, and Egypt, with case counts ranging from 1 to 27,540. Notable outbreaks occurred in Chad in July 2020 with 27,540 cases and 1 death, in Congo in February 2019 with 6,149 cases, and in Tanzania in May 2016 with 1,792 cases. The disease has also been documented in Reunion Island in 2005 and 2006, with 3,115 and 1,722 cases respectively, and in Italy and Spain in 2015 and 2017, with smaller case numbers. Chikungunya virus disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. The drug Chloroquine is associated with this condition.
468:
An outbreak of Visceral Leishmaniasis occurred in Pakistan on January 16, 2002, with 738 confirmed cases, and was followed by two additional outbreaks on May 22 and June 28, 2002, each reporting 200,000 confirmed cases. Visceral Leishmaniasis is classified as a type of leishmaniasis, which in turn is a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Treatment options include a range of drugs such as Amphotericin B, Antimony, Meglumine antimoniate, Miltefosine, Pentamidine, and various antiprotozoals. The disease is associated with the biological pathway of leishmaniasis.

Malaria outbreaks have been reported in multiple regions, including the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, Ethiopia in 2024, and Pakistan in 2022 with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. Malaria is also classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Common symptoms include fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice, coma, and retinal hemorrhage. Treatment involves drugs such as Artemisinin, Chloroquine, Quinine, Primaquine, Atovaquone, and various sulfonamides and antimalarial combinations. The disease is associated with the biological pathway of malaria, and its pathogenesis is linked to factors such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin.
469: Chagas disease is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, Benznidazole, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Nifurtimox, and Nitrofural. The disease is linked to the Chagas disease pathway. Trypanosomiasis, in turn, is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as Antimony, Arsenic compounds, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, Pentamidine, and borrelidin.
470: Filariasis is classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. The drugs acetylsalicylic acid and levamisole are associated with the condition, often observed or studied in conjunction with it.
471: Urinary schistosomiasis is classified as a type of schistosomiasis, and the drug Metrifonate is associated with its treatment. Intestinal schistosomiasis is also categorized under schistosomiasis, and treatment includes the drugs Praziquantel, Quercetin, and Resveratrol. Schistosomiasis as a whole is associated with the drugs Metrifonate and Praziquantel. Schistosomiasis is further classified as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease. Schistosoma mansonii infection is associated with multiple drugs, including 3,7-dimethyloctan-1-ol, Acetylcysteine, Artemether, IA 4, Nitric Oxide, Oxamniquine, and Praziquantel; additionally, triglycerides play a role in the disease's pathogenesis.
472:
Tuberculosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway of tuberculosis. Several drugs are linked to tuberculosis, including 9,9-dimethylcarbazine, amikacin, aminosalicylic acid, amithiozone, bedaquiline, capreomycin, chenodeoxycholic acid, ciprofloxacin, cycloserine, ethambutol, ethionamide, gentamicins, iron, isoniazid, kanamycin, menaquinone, OPC-67683, ofloxacin, pitolisant, prednisolone, pyrazinamide, pyridoxine, pyrroles, rifampicin, streptomycin, terizidone, tretinoin, verapamil, viomycin, isofon, and the combination isoniazid/pyrazinamide/rifampin. Additionally, lopinavir, phosphorus, and phosphates are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. 

Leprosy, also categorized as a primary bacterial infectious disease, presents with a range of symptoms including ageusia, blindness, edema, eye manifestations, facial paralysis, fever, gait disorders, hyperesthesia, hypesthesia, muscle weakness, muscular atrophy, neuralgia, neurologic manifestations, oral manifestations, paralysis, paresthesia, abnormal reflexes, sensation disorders, skin manifestations, somatosensory disorders, and vision disorders. It is associated with the drugs chloroquine, clofazimine, dapsone, prednisolone, reactive oxygen species, rifampicin, thalidomide, and triptolide. The disease involves several biological pathways, including antigen processing and presentation, cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity, protein processing in the endoplasmic reticulum, and Toll-like receptor signaling pathway. 

Lepromatous leprosy is a subtype of leprosy and is associated with the drugs clofazimine, dapsone, ethionamide, ofloxacin, prednisolone, prednisone, rifampicin, and thalidomide. 

In addition, several compounds including homocysteine, isoprostanes, mannitol, tocopherol, and ubidecarenone are involved in the pathogenesis of the disease.
473: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, and July 14, 1999, as well as in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 cases reported, and in Ethiopia on November 1, 2024. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, headache, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, hemoglobinuria, retinal hemorrhage, neurologic manifestations, oliguria, pallor, and unconsciousness. The condition is also linked to anemia, which presents with symptoms such as fatigue, abdominal pain, jaundice, purpura, and various systemic manifestations including cardiac output abnormalities, hypoxia, and skin and eye changes. Several drugs are associated with the treatment or management of malaria, including Artemisinin, Chloroquine, Mefloquine, Primaquine, Artesunate, Atovaquone, Pyrimethamine, and Quinine. Some of these drugs, such as Primaquine and Chloroquine, are also associated with hemolysis, while others like Artemisinin and Amodiaquine are used in combination therapies. The disease is further linked to biological pathways involving malaria itself, and its pathogenesis is influenced by factors such as bilirubin, cholesterol, and glutamic acid. Malaria resembles other conditions such as celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, thymus cancer, and anemia, and is considered a subtype of hematopoietic system disease.
474:
Tuberculosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway of tuberculosis. Multiple drugs are linked to its management, including 9,9-dimethylcarbazine, Amikacin, Aminosalicylic acid, Amithiozone, Bedaquiline, Capreomycin, Chenodeoxycholic acid, Ciprofloxacin, Cycloserine, Ethambutol, Ethionamide, Gentamicins, Iron, Isoniazid, Kanamycin, Menaquinone, OPC-67683, Ofloxacin, Pitolisant, Prednisolone, Pyrazinamide, Pyridoxine, Pyrroles, Rifampicin, Streptomycin, Terizidone, Tretinoin, Verapamil, Viomycin, isofon, and the combination drug isoniazid/pyrazinamide/rifampin. Additionally, Lopinavir, Phosphorus, and phosphates are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis.

Leprosy, also categorized as a primary bacterial infectious disease, presents with a range of symptoms including ageusia, blindness, edema, eye manifestations, facial paralysis, fever, gait disorders, hyperesthesia, hypesthesia, muscle weakness, muscular atrophy, neuralgia, neurologic manifestations, oral manifestations, paralysis, paresthesia, abnormal reflexes, sensation disorders, skin manifestations, somatosensory disorders, and vision disorders. It is associated with several drugs such as Chloroquine, Clofazimine, Dapsone, Prednisolone, Reactive Oxygen Species, Rifampicin, Thalidomide, and Triptolide. The disease involves key biological pathways including antigen processing and presentation, cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity, protein processing in the endoplasmic reticulum, and Toll-like receptor signaling pathway.

Lepromatous leprosy is a subtype of leprosy and is managed with drugs including Clofazimine, Dapsone, Ethionamide, Ofloxacin, Prednisolone, Prednisone, Rifampicin, and Thalidomide.
475:
Yellow Fever is classified as a viral infectious disease. Documented outbreaks have occurred in multiple countries across different time periods, including significant events in Angola (2000, 2016), Brazil (1996, 2008, 2016, 2017), Cameroon (2001, 2002, 2011, 2012, 2013, 2014), Congo (2008, 2009, 2010, 2016, 2018, 2020), Federal Republic of Nigeria (1996, 2000, 2017, 2019), and others such as Ecuador, Mali, The Netherlands, and South Africa. Outbreaks have varied in scale, with case counts ranging from single cases to over 1,700 in a single event, and fatalities reported in several instances, including 238 deaths in Angola in 2016 and 215 deaths in Brazil in 2016.

Dengue disease is also categorized as a viral infectious disease. It has been associated with a range of drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, F2-Isoprostanes, hydroxychloroquine, hydroxyeicosatetraenoic acids, rolitetracycline, silver, cholest-5-en-3 beta,7 alpha-diol, hinokiflavone, and podocarpusflavone A. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease. Outbreaks of Dengue Fever have been reported globally, including in Malaysia (1996), Sri Lanka (2017), Bangladesh (2022), Brazil (2002, 2008), India (1996, 2003, 2005, 2016, 2019), and numerous other countries across Asia, Africa, South America, and Europe. Notable outbreaks include one in France in 2018 with 6,942 cases and 14 deaths, and another in the Netherlands in 2004 with over 23,000 cases and 367 deaths. A large outbreak in 2024 was reported with 7.6 million cases and 3,000 deaths.

Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. It is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, pallor, purpura, retinal hemorrhage, coma, unconsciousness, and neurological manifestations. The disease has been linked to several biological pathways, primarily under the category of Malaria. Treatment involves a variety of drugs such as chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemether, artesunate, quinine, mefloquine, atovaquone, proguanil, and others, including antimalarial combinations like atovaquone-proguanil and mefloquine-sulfadoxine-pyrimethamine. Additionally, cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. Outbreaks have been documented in countries such as Burundi, Pakistan, Ethiopia, and the United Republic of Tanzania, with one outbreak in Pakistan in 2022 involving 3.4 million cases.

Chikungunya is recognized as a viral infectious disease. It has been associated with the drug chloroquine. Outbreaks have been reported in various regions, including Reunion Island (2005, 2006), the United Republic of Tanzania (2016, 2017), Chad (2020), Egypt (2018), France (2014, 2017), Italy (2017), Spain (2015), and several Caribbean islands. Notable events include a large outbreak in Chad in 2020 with 27,540 cases and one death, and a significant outbreak in Reunion in 2006 with 1,722 cases.
476: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999, Pakistan in 2022 with 3.4 million confirmed cases, and Ethiopia in 2024. The disease is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, coma, unconsciousness, jaundice, diarrhea, vomiting, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, oliguria, pallor, pruritus, hypergammaglobulinemia, hemoglobinuria, cerebellar ataxia, consciousness disorders, neurologic manifestations, fetal hypoxia, birth weight abnormalities, eye hemorrhage, and jet lag syndrome. The condition is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is linked to the biological pathway known as Malaria. Treatment involves a variety of drugs, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, lumefantrine, pyrimethamine, proguanil, atovaquone-proguanil combination, and sulfonamides. Some drugs are also implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis, such as cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid. Additionally, several medications are associated with malaria treatment, including amodiaquine, artesunate, and various antimalarial combinations.
477:
An outbreak of pneumonia occurred in Viet Nam and Indonesia on March 12, 2003, with 305 confirmed cases and 5 deaths. Pneumonia is classified as a lung disease and is associated with several drugs, including amoxicillin-potassium clavulanate combination, ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, and various antibiotics, as well as environmental agents such as asbestos, coal ash, and particulate matter. The condition is also linked to the role of arginine in disease pathogenesis. Additionally, drugs such as amiodarone, dapsone, and corticosteroids are associated with pneumonia, and the disease has been observed in association with exposure to lipopolysaccharide from E. coli O26-B6 and other microbial components.

An outbreak of myocarditis was reported in Sri Lanka on March 17, 2005, and another in Greece on April 25, 2002, involving 39 confirmed cases and 3 deaths. Myocarditis is classified as an extrinsic cardiomyopathy and is associated with a range of drugs, including amitriptyline, amphetamines, cyclophosphamide, methyldopa, and antipsychotics, as well as environmental toxins such as endosulfan and particulate matter. The condition is also linked to the role of catecholamines and malonaldehyde in disease pathogenesis. Drugs such as prednisolone, prednisone, and corticosteroids are associated with myocarditis, and the disease has been observed in conjunction with exposure to lipopolysaccharide from E. coli O55-B5 and black widow spider venom.

Hepatitis is classified as a liver disease and is associated with numerous drugs, including azathioprine, methimazole, amiodarone, and various antivirals such as ledipasvir/sofosbuvir. Other drugs linked to hepatitis include antipsychotics, anticonvulsants, and environmental agents such as benzene and copper. Viral hepatitis is also associated with drugs like foscarnet, iron, and tylophorine, and is itself classified as a type of hepatitis. The condition is further linked to the use of corticosteroids and other immunosuppressive agents.

Pericarditis is classified as a pericardium disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including antipsychotics, antihypertensives, antineoplastics, and antibiotics such as amikacin and vancomycin. Drugs like methotrexate, cyclophosphamide, and sulfasalazine are also linked to pericarditis, and the condition has been observed in association with exposure to bisphenol A, antimony, and other environmental toxins.

Q fever is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as ketoconazole, imipramine, and lovastatin. The condition is linked to bacterial pathogens and is part of the broader category of infectious diseases.

Parainfluenza infection is associated with antiviral drugs such as galidesivir, remdesivir, and ribavirin. These agents are used in the context of viral respiratory infections and are studied for their potential therapeutic effects.

Fever is associated with a broad range of drugs, including antipyretics, antivirals, and psychotropic agents such as amantadine, risperidone, and lithium. The condition is also linked to the role of hydroxyindoleacetic acid in disease pathogenesis and is associated with exposure to substances such as lipopolysaccharide from E. coli O111 B4 and various toxins.

Nervous system disease is associated with drugs such as antipsychotics, anticonvulsants, and neurotoxic agents including aluminum, arsenic trioxide, and methylmercury compounds. The condition is linked to the role of sorbitol and tryptophan in disease pathogenesis and is classified as a disease of anatomical entity.

Endocarditis and infective endocarditis are classified as endocardium diseases and are associated with antibiotics such as vancomycin, rifampicin, and ampicillin, as well as antipsychotics and other drugs like cocaine and diamorphine. These conditions are linked to bacterial infections and are part of the broader category of endocardial disorders.
478:
Infection is associated with a wide range of drugs, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, ciprofloxacin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, cytarabine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, epirubicin, etoposide, FEC protocol, fludarabine, itraconazole, LNH 87 protocol, lithium, MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, metronidazole, netilmicin, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, sirolimus, TAC protocol, tetrandrine, tobramycin, topotecan, triamcinolone, VIP-E protocol, vancomycin, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin.

Scabies is classified as a type of mite infestation and is associated with drugs such as benzyl benzoate, ivermectin, lindane, permethrin, piperonyl butoxide, butyl phosphorotrithioate, diethyl maleate, and sulfur.

Contact dermatitis is categorized as a form of dermatitis and is associated with numerous substances, including 2-mercaptobenzothiazole, 4-nitrobenzyl bromide, 4-toluenesulfonamide-formaldehyde resin, (4-chloro-2-((2-methyl-5-(propylsulfonyl)phenyl)ethynyl)phenoxy)acetic acid, amcinonide, anileridine, benzoic acid, benzyl benzoate, bromothalonil, bronopol, CRA1000, capsaicin, captan, chromates, cinnamaldehyde, clobetasol, clorophene, dibutylphthalate, dinitrochlorobenzene, eugenol, fluocortolone, formaldehyde, glutathione, grape seed proanthocyanidins, lipoic acid, mercuric chloride, nickel, niclosamide, organic chemicals, oxazolone, peppermint oil, permethrin, phenol, picryl chloride, plant oils, polyisobutylene, propyl gallate, safrole, tixocortol pivalate, trichloroethylene, trinitrobenzenesulfonic acid, alpha-pinene, benzanthrone, bu-zhong-yi-qi-tang, captafol, corticosteroids, folpet, n-butyl glycidyl ether, phenylglycidyl ether, and quinone.
479:
Cholera has been documented in multiple outbreaks across various regions, including significant events in the United Republic of Tanzania, Mozambique, Congo, Zambia, and South Africa, with case counts ranging from a few to over 60,000 and death tolls reaching several thousand. Outbreaks have occurred in diverse locations such as Viet Nam, Angola, Malaysia, Ecuador, and the Republic of Panama, with notable events in 1996, 1997, 1998, 2000, 2003, 2008, 2010, 2012, 2017, 2021, 2022, and 2023. The disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the biological pathway of Vibrio cholerae infection. The cholera vaccine CVD 103-HgR is recognized as a drug associated with the disease.

Hepatitis E has been reported in numerous outbreaks across Africa, the Middle East, and parts of Asia, including Angola, Sudan, Chad, Niger, Egypt, Burkina Faso, and the United Kingdom, with case numbers ranging from dozens to over 3,000 and fatalities reaching up to 55 in a single event. Outbreaks have occurred in 2004, 2016, 2017, 2020, 2021, 2022, and 2023, including a multi-country event involving China, South Korea, Italy, and Iran. Hepatitis E is classified as a viral infectious disease.

Jaundice is associated with a wide range of drugs, including acetohexamide, acitretin, ajmaline, amineptine, anabolic agents, anticoagulants, atazanavir sulfate, azithromycin, bupivacaine, busulfan, chlordiazepoxide, chlorpromazine, cinnarizine, ciprofloxacin, contraceptives, oral, cyproterone acetate, dapsone, diflunisal, ethambutol, ethanol, fibrinolytic agents, floxuridine, flupirtine, flutamide, isoflurane, isoniazid, lovastatin, methazolamide, methimazole, midomafetamine, naproxen, nitrofurantoin, ofloxacin, omeprazole, papaverine, paroxetine, prednisolone, pyrazinamide, ranitidine, rifampicin, simvastatin, thioridazine, ticlopidine, ursodeoxycholic acid, vitamin A, zafirlukast, benzarone, bilirubin, and combinations such as difebarbamate, febarbamate, and phenobarbital. It is also linked to the biological pathway of human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection.
480: Outbreaks of V. cholerae have been documented across multiple countries and time periods, including significant events in Cameroon, Zambia, Mexico, Congo, Niger, Sierra Leone, United Republic of Tanzania, Egypt, Philippines, Iraq, Pakistan, Dagestan, South East Asia, Azerbaijan, Republic of Panama, Madagascar, Somalia, Comoros, and others, with case counts ranging from a few to over 20,000 and death tolls reaching up to 303 in a single outbreak. These outbreaks have occurred in diverse geographic regions and spanned from the late 1990s to the 2020s, indicating persistent transmission of the pathogen in areas with varying levels of public health infrastructure. Cholera, caused by the same bacterium, has also been reported in numerous outbreaks in countries such as Pakistan, Cap Verde, Somalia, Angola, Mozambique, Brazil, Congo, Madagascar, Benin, Burkina Faso, South Africa, Republic of Tajikistan, Zambia, and others, with some events involving tens of thousands of cases and hundreds of deaths. Notable large-scale outbreaks occurred in the United Republic of Tanzania (1998, 2004, 2008), Pakistan (1998, 2000, 2002), and Republic of Panama (2010), highlighting recurring challenges in controlling the disease in vulnerable regions. The disease is associated with the biological pathway of Vibrio cholerae infection and is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease. The cholera vaccine CVD 103-HgR is associated with the disease, indicating its use in prevention and control efforts.
481: SARS outbreaks were documented across multiple countries during 2003, with significant events in China, Hong Kong, Singapore, Canada, and other regions. Notable outbreaks included one in China on March 24, 2003, with 456 confirmed cases and 17 deaths, and another on March 28, 2003, with 1,485 cases and 53 deaths. Hong Kong experienced a major outbreak on April 5, 2003, with 2,416 cases and 89 deaths, and a later event on April 26, 2003, involving 4,836 cases and 293 deaths. Singapore reported an outbreak on May 12, 2003, with 58 cases and 1 death, and another on April 22, 2003, with 186 cases and 16 deaths. Canada had multiple outbreaks, including one on June 11, 2003, with 90 confirmed cases, and another on May 26, 2003, with 8 cases and 3 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in China in 2003 and 2004, including a large event on May 27, 2003, with 8,221 cases and 735 deaths, and smaller incidents in 2004. A later outbreak was reported in Denmark on June 1, 2020, with 214 confirmed cases. SARS is classified as a coronavirus infectious disease, and several drugs have been associated with its treatment, including chloroquine, lopinavir-ritonavir, remdesivir, ribavirin, and glucocorticoids. The disease is also linked to the pathogenic role of catecholamines.
482: Leptospirosis has been reported in multiple outbreaks across different regions, including an event in the United Republic of Tanzania on August 8, 2022, with 20 confirmed cases and 3 deaths, another in India on October 27, 1997, involving 413 cases and 48 deaths, a third in Malaysia on September 3, 2000, with 37 confirmed cases, a fourth in the United Republic of Tanzania on June 17, 2004, resulting in 141 cases and 6 deaths, and a fifth in Canada on September 21, 2000, with 6 confirmed cases. Leptospirosis is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease.
483:
Dengue Fever has been associated with multiple outbreak events across various countries and time periods, including large-scale epidemics in Malaysia (1996 and 1997), Sri Lanka (2017), Brazil (2002 and 2008), India (1996 and 2003), Pakistan (2019 and 2022), and the Netherlands (2004), as well as significant outbreaks in East Timor, Cameroon, Cape Verde, Bangladesh, and other regions. Notably, a severe outbreak in 2023 reported 500,000 cases and 750 deaths, while another in 2024 recorded 7,600,000 cases and 3,000 deaths. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with several drugs, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, and silver, among others. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of Dengue Fever.

Zika virus outbreaks have been documented in multiple countries, including Brazil, Colombia, Venezuela, Ecuador, Panama, Mexico, French Guiana, the United States, India, and several Caribbean islands, with events reported between 2015 and 2021. Notable outbreaks include a large cluster in French Guiana in 2015 with 8,750 cases and a significant outbreak in India in 2021 involving 70 cases. The virus has been linked to cases in El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, and the Dominican Republic, with some events reporting fatalities.
484: Rabies outbreaks have been reported in France on August 26, 2004, resulting in one death, and in unspecified locations on May 6, 1997, and April 10, 2024. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease. Several drugs are associated with rabies, including cinnabarin. Additionally, a broad range of drugs are associated with infection, including arsenic, azathioprine, azlocillin, benzylpenicillin, bortezomib, COPP protocol, carboplatin, cefalotin, cefapirin, cefotiam, cefoxitin, ceftazidime, ceftriaxone, cephalexin, clarithromycin, clindamycin, cocaine, cyclophosphamide, cyclosporine, cytarabine, daunorubicin, desoxycortone, dexamethasone, docetaxel, doxorubicin, epirubicin, etoposide, FEC protocol, fludarabine, itraconazole, LNH 87 protocol, lithium, MOPP-ABV protocol, melphalan, methotrexate, metronidazole, netilmicin, pemetrexed, pirarubicin, prednisone, silver sulfadiazine, sirolimus, TAC protocol, tetrandrine, topotecan, triamcinolone, VIP-E protocol, asulacrine, chlorhexidine phosphanilate, and liposomal doxorubicin.
485:
Outbreaks of Chikungunya virus disease have been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including a significant event in Chad in July 2020 with 27,540 confirmed cases and one death, and another in Congo in February 2019 with 6,149 cases. Additional outbreaks occurred in Reunion Island in 2005 and 2006, with 3,115 and 1,722 cases respectively, and in Tanzania in 2016 and 2017 with 1,792 and 453 cases. Smaller outbreaks were documented in the United States, France, Italy, Spain, Argentina, India, Egypt, and St Martin, with case counts ranging from 1 to 14. Chikungunya virus disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and the drug Chloroquine has been associated with its management.

Ebola outbreaks have occurred in multiple countries, with the most extensive and prolonged events recorded in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, including outbreaks in 2018, 2019, and 2020 with case counts ranging from 32 to over 3,000 and death tolls exceeding 1,000 in several instances. Notable outbreaks also occurred in Guinea Bissau in 2014, with over 200 cases and more than 100 deaths, and in Sierra Leone in 2014, with over 1,000 cases and more than 600 deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in South Africa (1996), Mali (2014), Cameroon (1996), and Angola (2014), with varying case numbers and fatalities. Ebola hemorrhagic fever is classified as a viral infectious disease, and several drugs, including Favipiravir, Galidesivir, Remdesivir, and Teicoplanin, have been associated with its treatment.

Nipah virus outbreaks have been documented in Malaysia (1999), Bangladesh (2001, 2004, 2023), India (2018, 2021, 2023), and Pakistan (2024). The most severe outbreak occurred in Kerala State, India, in 2018, with 19 confirmed cases and 17 deaths. Other notable events include a 2023 outbreak in Bangladesh with 11 cases and 8 deaths, and a 2024 outbreak in Pakistan with 2 cases and 2 deaths. The virus is known to cause severe neurological symptoms and high mortality.

Microcephaly is classified as a congenital nervous system abnormality, and its occurrence has been associated with exposure to various drugs and environmental agents, including Acitretin, Valproic acid, Ethanol, Cocaine, Mercury, and Bisphenol A, among others. Arthritis is categorized as a bone inflammation disease and is associated with a wide range of drugs, including nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, anticonvulsants, immunosuppressants, and certain environmental pollutants. The pathogenesis of arthritis is linked to substances such as Arachidonic Acid and Ethanol. Myositis is classified as a myopathy and is associated with drugs such as statins (e.g., Atorvastatin, Simvastatin), anticonvulsants, and immunosuppressants, with some agents contributing to disease development. Tenosynovitis is recognized as a type of arthropathy. HIV infection is associated with the pathway of Human immunodeficiency virus 1 infection. Influenza is classified as a viral infectious disease and is linked to the Influenza A pathway, with several antiviral and symptomatic drugs, including Oseltamivir, Amantadine, and Quercetin, being associated with its treatment.
486: Leprosy, multibacillary is associated with a range of symptoms including ageusia, blindness, edema, eye and oral manifestations, facial paralysis, fever, gait disorders, hyperesthesia, hypesthesia, muscle weakness, muscular atrophy, neuralgia, neurologic and sensation disorders, paralysis, paresthesia, abnormal reflexes, skin and vision disorders, and other neurologic complications. The condition is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is a subtype of leprosy. It is also categorized as a glucose metabolism disease, indicating a potential link to metabolic disturbances. Treatment involves several drugs, including clofazimine, dapsone, prednisolone, rifampicin, and thalidomide. Additional drugs associated with the condition include azathioprine, doxorubicin, fluorouracil, foscarnet, lidocaine, metronidazole, oxaliplatin, paclitaxel, tetracycline, and perhexiline maleate. The disease is linked to the Toll-like receptor signaling pathway, antigen processing and presentation, cytokine-cytokine receptor interaction, natural killer cell mediated cytotoxicity, and protein processing in the endoplasmic reticulum. Leprosy is also associated with delayed hypersensitivity reactions, which can be triggered by a wide array of agents such as amoxicillin, cefaclor, cefixime, dexamethasone, formaldehyde, phenytoin, prednisone, mercury, and various heavy metals and environmental chemicals. Furthermore, leprosy is connected to diabetes mellitus, with certain drugs and compounds—such as alloxan, streptozocin, clozapine, metformin, and thiazolidinediones—implicated in both conditions, and with alanine, chemokine CX3CL1, and pyrimidine playing roles in disease pathogenesis.
487:
Outbreaks of Rabies have been reported in multiple locations, including France on August 26, 2004, resulting in one death, and in unspecified locations on May 6, 1997, and April 10, 2024. Rabies is classified as a viral infectious disease. The drug cinnabarin is associated with the condition, though its role in disease pathogenesis is not specified.

Multiple outbreaks of Ebola have occurred across several countries, primarily in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, with significant events recorded in 1996, 1997, 2000, 2014, 2018, 2019, 2020, and 2021. Notable outbreaks include one in 2014 in Guinea Bissau with 226 confirmed cases and 149 deaths, another in Sierra Leone with 2,252 cases and 1,244 deaths, and a large outbreak in the Democratic Republic of the Congo in 2019 with over 3,000 cases and more than 2,000 deaths. Additional outbreaks were reported in Guinea, Mali, United Republic of Tanzania, and other regions, with case counts ranging from single cases to over 3,000 and death tolls exceeding 2,000 in some instances. Ebola is also classified as a viral infectious disease.
488:
Parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease is classified as a parasitic infectious disease and is associated with multiple drugs, including Albendazole, Anthelmintics, Closantel, Ivermectin, Levamisole, Mebendazole, Netobimin, Niclosamide, Praziquantel, and Thiabendazole. Strongyloidiasis, which is a subtype of parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease, is also associated with several drugs such as Abamectin, Dichlorvos, Levamisole, Metrifonate, and Thiabendazole. Onchocerciasis, a form of filariasis, is treated with Abamectin, Albendazole, Diethylcarbamazine, and Ivermectin. Infection, a broad category, is associated with a wide range of drugs including antibiotics such as Benzylpenicillin, Cefotiam, Cefoxitin, Ceftazidime, Ceftriaxone, Cephalexin, Ciprofloxacin, Clindamycin, and Vancomycin, as well as chemotherapeutic agents like Cyclophosphamide, Methotrexate, Doxorubicin, and Cytarabine, and immunosuppressants such as Cyclosporine and Prednisone. Additionally, substances like arsenic, cocaine, and silver sulfadiazine are also linked to infection.

Type 2 diabetes mellitus is a subtype of diabetes mellitus and is characterized by symptoms such as acute coronary syndrome, albuminuria, birth weight changes, body weight fluctuations, flushing, gastroparesis, microvascular angina, obesity, abdominal obesity, overweight, proteinuria, overactive urinary bladder, weight gain, and weight loss. It is associated with numerous drugs, including Acarbose, Glipizide, Pioglitazone, Rosiglitazone, Liraglutide, and various agents such as folic acid, resveratrol, thiazolidinediones, and triglycerides, with spermine playing a role in disease pathogenesis. The condition is linked to several biological pathways, including insulin secretion, PPAR signaling pathway, pancreatic secretion, protein processing in the endoplasmic reticulum, cell cycle regulation, signaling by NOTCH4, signaling by WNT, TGF-beta receptor signaling in epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, p53 signaling pathway, and the type II diabetes mellitus pathway.

Type 1 diabetes mellitus 2 is classified as a subtype of type 1 diabetes mellitus. Rheumatic fever, a bacterial infectious disease, is associated with drugs such as Acetylsalicylic acid, Methotrexate, Prednisone, and antimacrophage globulin. HTLV-I Infections are associated with Arsenic trioxide and Niclosamide. Glucose intolerance, a form of hyperglycemia, is linked to a variety of drugs including Alitretinoin, Fenofibrate, Fructose, Resveratrol, Rosiglitazone, and Valproic acid, with guanosine-5'-diphosphate, malonaldehyde, and bilirubin implicated in its pathogenesis.
489: Chagas disease is classified as a type of trypanosomiasis. It is associated with several drugs, including 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-methoxypropyloxy)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol, Benznidazole, N-pip-phenylalanine-homophenylalanine-vinyl sulfone phenyl, Nifurtimox, and Nitrofural. The disease is linked to the Chagas disease pathway. Trypanosomiasis, in turn, is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with drugs such as Antimony, Arsenic compounds, N,N'-diphenyl-4-methoxybenzamidine, Pentamidine, and borrelidin.
490: Chagas cardiomyopathy is a condition associated with the use of drugs such as digoxin and diuretics. It is classified as a form of heart disease and is also recognized as a subtype of trypanosomiasis, reflecting its etiological link to the parasitic infection caused by Trypanosoma cruzi. The disease is associated with several therapeutic agents, including benznidazole, nifurtimox, nitrofural, and other experimental compounds such as 3-((2-benzyl-6-(3-hydroxy-4-methoxypyrrolidin-1-yl)pyridin-3-yl)ethynyl)quinuclidin-3-ol and its analogs. Additionally, various drugs have been linked to the condition either through clinical use or involvement in disease pathogenesis, including amrinone, angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors, betamethasone, cardiotonic agents, celiprolol, cerivastatin, cocaine, cyclic GMP, dantrolene, darusentan, dexamethasone, digitoxin, dipyridamole, disopyramide, doxazosin, etoposide, famotidine, fosinopril, hydralazine, idarubicin, indomethacin, isosorbide dinitrate, labetalol, MDL 72567, mitomycin, moricizine, N-(1-methylethyl)-1,1,2-trimethylpropylamine, N-((2'-(((4,5-dimethyl-3-isoxazolyl)amino)sulfonyl)-4-(2-oxazolyl)(1,1'-biphenyl)-2-yl)methyl)-N,3,3-trimethylbutanamide, NSP 804, omega-3 fatty acids, ozone, phentermine, potassium chloride, prednisone, procarbazine, razoxane, ritodrine, streptozocin, superoxides, thiamine, thiotepa, timolol, tizanidine, zinc, aluminum phosphide, amiloride-hydrochlorothiazide combination, astragaloside A, beta-adrenergic receptor kinase inhibitory peptide, and liposomal doxorubicin. Certain substances, such as 6-sulfatoxymelatonin and hypoxanthine, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. The condition is also associated with the biological pathway of Chagas disease.
491: Cystic echinococcosis is classified as a type of echinococcosis. Echinococcosis is associated with the use of drugs including Dexamethasone, Hydrogen peroxide, and Mebendazole. Additionally, echinococcosis is categorized as a parasitic helminthiasis infectious disease.
492: No disease information is available in the provided knowledge graph, as both the eKG-DONs and iBKH sources are empty.
493: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including an event in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, and another in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks were documented in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, headache, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, pallor, retinal hemorrhage, oliguria, hemoglobinuria, coma, and neurologic manifestations. The disease is also linked to elevated levels of bilirubin, cholesterol, and glutamic acid, and is associated with the Malaria pathway. Several drugs are used in its treatment or study, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, mefloquine, pyrimethamine, atovaquone-proguanil, and sulfonamides. Some drugs, such as chloroquine diphosphate and amodiaquine-artesunate combinations, are specifically noted for their role in disease pathogenesis. Malaria resembles other conditions such as celiac disease, chronic kidney disease, anemia, and thymus cancer in clinical presentation or pathophysiology.
494:
Measles outbreaks have been reported across multiple countries and time periods, including significant events in Egypt (2018 and 2019), Ethiopia (2022), Madagascar (2018), Brazil (2018), Mexico (2020), Ecuador (2015 and 2023), Japan (2018), the United States (2015), Thailand (2023), the Cook Islands (2019), the Central African Republic (2019), Sudan (2022), Somalia (2022), Pakistan (2022), the Republic of Ireland (2000), Gaza Strip (2019), South Africa (2022), Guadeloupe (1997), Kyrgyzstan (2014), Chile (2023), and the European Region (2019). These outbreaks have involved case counts ranging from a single case to over 40,000, with notable mortality in several instances, including 182 deaths in Ethiopia, 156 in Pakistan, and 62 in the Cook Islands. Measles is classified as a viral infectious disease, and the virus responsible has been identified in all reported outbreaks. 

Japanese Encephalitis has been documented in Malaysia (1999), India (2005 and 1999), Thailand (1997), and Australia (2022), with case numbers ranging from 24 to 1145 and associated deaths from 3 to 296. The disease is categorized as a brain disease and a viral infectious disease. It is associated with the TCR signaling pathway, and various drugs have been observed to be associated with its treatment or study, including Acyclovir, Ganciclovir, and others.

Typhoid fever outbreaks have occurred in the Republic of Tajikistan (1996), involving over 7,500 cases, and in Pakistan (2016), with 5,274 confirmed cases, as well as in Algeria (1996), with 910 cases and one death. The disease is classified as a primary bacterial infectious disease and is associated with the Salmonella infection pathway. Drugs such as Chloramphenicol and Ciprofloxacin are associated with its treatment.
495:
Outbreaks of Coronavirus have been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including Albania in 1996 with 66 cases and 7 deaths, Egypt in 2009, Somalia in 2013 with 1 case, Zambia in 2022 with 1 case, Iraq in 1999 with 16 cases, Israel in 2013 with 27 cases, Pakistan in 2011 with 84 cases, Niger in 2013 with 1 case, Cameroon in 2014 with 7 cases, China in 2011 with 4 cases, Guinea Bissau in 2014 with 3 and 5 cases on separate dates, Chad in 2003 and 2011, Papua New Guinea in 2018 with 26 cases, Yemen in 2005 with 4 cases, Republic of Tajikistan in 2010 with 171 cases and 12 deaths, Angola in 2006 with 34 cases and 7 deaths, and in 1999 with 661 cases and 41 deaths, Brazil in 2014, the Philippines in 2019, Madagascar in 2015 with 8 cases, Ukraine in 2015 with 2 cases, Federal Republic of Nigeria in 2016 with 3 cases, Sudan (W Equatoria) in 2013 with 3 cases, Mozambique in 2022 with 1 case, and The Netherlands in 2005 with 20 and 225 cases. The disease is classified as a coronavirus infectious disease. In terms of treatment, the drug Remdesivir has been associated with effects on the condition.

Malaria outbreaks have been documented in various regions, including the United Republic of Tanzania in 1998 and 1999, Burundi in 1999 with 616,034 cases and 51 deaths, Ethiopia in 2024, and Pakistan in 2022 with 3,400,000 cases. The disease is categorized as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Symptoms associated with malaria include fever, chills, diarrhea, vomiting, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal hemorrhage, purpura, pallor, and neurological manifestations. Treatment involves a range of drugs such as chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, artemisinin, artesunate, quinine, mefloquine, primaquine, pyrimethamine, atovaquone, and sulfonamides. Some drugs, including cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin, are implicated in the disease’s pathogenesis. The condition is linked to the malaria biological pathway.

Visceral Leishmaniasis has been reported in Pakistan in 2002 with 200,000 cases on two separate occasions and 738 cases in January 2002. The disease is a subtype of leishmaniasis and is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Treatment includes drugs such as amphotericin B, miltefosine, meglumine antimoniate, antimony compounds, pentamidine, and other antiprotozoals. The disease is associated with the leishmaniasis biological pathway.
496:
Dengue Fever has been reported in multiple countries across different time periods, including Malaysia, where outbreaks occurred in 1996 and 1997 with thousands of cases and several deaths, and in 2022 with over 7,000 cases. Significant outbreaks were also documented in India, with events in 1996, 2003, and 2022, involving thousands of cases and varying numbers of fatalities. Other notable outbreaks occurred in Brazil (2002 and 2008), Pakistan (2019 and 2021), the Netherlands (2004), and the People's Republic of Bangladesh (2022), with case counts ranging from several hundred to over half a million. Additional outbreaks were reported in Cameroon, East Timor, France, Reunion, Thailand, and other regions, with some events involving high case numbers and fatalities. The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease, and it is further categorized as a subtype of dengue disease. Several drugs have been associated with dengue disease, including 24-hydroxycholesterol, 27-hydroxycholesterol, doxycycline, hydroxychloroquine, silver, and various other compounds. Dengue hemorrhagic fever is recognized as a subtype of dengue disease.

Chikungunya virus disease has been reported in multiple regions, including the United Republic of Tanzania (2016 and 2017), Congo (2019), Chad (2020), Reunion (2005 and 2006), and France (2014 and 2017), with case counts ranging from a few to over 27,000. Outbreaks were also documented in Italy (2017), Spain (2015), Argentina (2016), and Egypt (2018). The disease is classified as a viral infectious disease and is associated with the drug chloroquine. It is also linked to other viral infectious diseases, and the broader category of virus diseases includes associations with drugs such as cidofovir, melatonin, and ergosterol-5,8-peroxide.
497: An outbreak of Diarrhoea occurred in Angola on May 26, 1998, resulting in 560 confirmed cases and 12 deaths. Diarrhoea is classified as a gastrointestinal system disease. It is associated with several drugs, including Acetic acid, Atropine, Azithromycin, Ceftriaxone, Cisplatin, Clozapine, Dipyridamole, Etoposide, Gemcitabine, Hydrocortisone, Imipenem, Lithium carbonate, Moxifloxacin, Pantoprazole, Tetracycline, and Tetracyclines, some of which are implicated in the disease's pathogenesis through mechanisms involving Alanine, Aspartic acid, and Chlorides. Diarrhoea is also linked to the drug GDC-0152, which has been observed in association with the condition. The disease shares characteristics with other gastrointestinal disorders and is part of a broader category of diseases affecting the digestive system.
498: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including an event in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, and another in Burundi on May 25, 1999, which involved 616,034 cases and 51 deaths. Additional outbreaks occurred in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with 3,400,000 confirmed cases reported in the latter. Malaria is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease and is associated with a range of symptoms, including fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal and eye hemorrhage, oliguria, pallor, pruritus, purpura, purpura fulminans, hypergammaglobulinemia, cerebellar ataxia, fetal hypoxia, birth weight abnormalities, consciousness disorders, and neurologic manifestations. The disease is linked to the Malaria pathway. Treatment involves several drugs, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, artemisinin, artesunate, amodiaquine, lumefantrine, atovaquone-proguanil, mefloquine, pyrimethamine, sulfonamides, doxycycline, and tetracycline, as well as insecticides. Some drugs are associated with the disease's pathogenesis, including cholesterol, bilirubin, and glutamic acid.
499: Malaria has been reported in multiple outbreaks across several countries, including a significant event in Burundi on May 25, 1999, with 616,034 confirmed cases and 51 deaths, and another outbreak in the United Republic of Tanzania on May 19, 1998, followed by a subsequent outbreak in the same country on July 14, 1999. Additional outbreaks were documented in Ethiopia in November 2024 and in Pakistan on January 1, 2022, with the latter reporting 3,400,000 cases. The disease is associated with a wide range of symptoms, including fever, chills, vomiting, diarrhea, jaundice, coma, unconsciousness, retinal and eye hemorrhage, pallor, purpura, oliguria, hypergammaglobulinemia, cerebellar ataxia, and neurologic manifestations. It is classified as a parasitic protozoa infectious disease. Several drugs are associated with malaria treatment or pathogenesis, including chloroquine, hydroxychloroquine, quinine, mefloquine, artemisinin, artesunate, atovaquone-proguanil combination, and amodiaquine-artesunate combination. Some drugs, such as chloroquine diphosphate, are noted for their role in disease pathogenesis, while others like amodiaquine, artemether, and dapsone are used in treatment. The disease is linked to biological pathways involving malaria itself, and its pathogenesis is influenced by factors such as cholesterol, glutamic acid, and bilirubin.
